To ask the Minister of Justice, pursuant to AQW 43079/22-27, to detail (i) the languages for which an interpreter was required; and (ii) the number of instances where an interpreter was required for each language, in each of the last five years.
To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether Translink's financial reserves will be impacted by the freezing of public transport fares.
answer_text: Budgets for 2026-27 and beyond have not yet been agreed, therefore it is not possible at this stage to determine the impact of the fares freeze on Translink’s financial reserves.The decision to freeze public transport fares was taken in the context of continuing cost of living pressures and to provide certainty to passengers. With fuel prices remaining high, I did not believe it would be right to increase fares and place further pressure on households who rely on buses and trains every day.Keeping fares stable helps support people at a challenging time and ensures public transport remains an affordable option. I will continue to engage with Translink as budgets are developed to manage financial pressures responsibly within the funding available.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the number of defendants (i) proceeded against; and (ii) convicted in Northern Ireland, for offences arising from the unlawful use of privately owned electric scooters, in each of the last five financial years.
answer_text: Offences relating to the use of electronic scooters on public roads or in public spaces may be prosecuted under the Road Traffic (Northern Ireland) Order 1995.  However, there is no offence under this legislation that specifically relates to electronic scooters, and prosecutions datasets held by the Department do not specify the type of vehicle involved, where a prosecution was brought for a relevant offence under this legislation.   Therefore, from information held, it not possible to identify the number of individuals that have been prosecuted or convicted for unlawful use of privately owned electric scooters, as requested.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of visitors from outside the UK and Ireland who have received emergency care, in each of the last ten years.
To ask the Minister of Health when he will answer AQW 36922/22-27.
To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any outstanding repair works to Cairn Wood.
answer_text: I am advised that the entirety of the path network in Cairn Wood is currently open following clearance work commissioned by Forest Service and Ards and North Down Borough Council in its role as recreation provider. Clearance of small areas of windblow, in proximity of the path network is outstanding, and is due to be cleared during 2026. Regeneration of cleared areas will follow in 26/27 planting season. Additionally, enrichment planting is planned to supplement the replacement of individual trees lost in the Cairn Wood area due to storm Eowyn.  Also, the area adjacent to the reservoir, opposite Cairn Wood carpark, which has now been cleared is due to be replanted in the 2026/27 planting season.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of GP practices which handed back their contracts, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: GP Contract Handbacks have declined markedly in recent years, falling from 13 in 2022/23 and 11 in 2023/24 to 5 in 2024/25 and 1 in 2025/26, indicating a downward trend compared to earlier years. Despite the pressures on General Practice and the number of practices that have handed back their contracts in each of the last five years, my Department has been able to secure arrangements/new contractors to ensure continuity of General Medical Services.   Only one of these contract hand-backs has resulted in a practice closure.  In this case the patient list was dispersed to ensure continuity of General Medical Services. The table below presents the number of GP practices that have handed back their contract, in each of the last five years: Financial Year Number of Contract handbacks2021/202222022/2023132023/2024112024/2025*52025/20261*Includes one practice closure where the patient list was dispersed to ensure continuity of General Medical ServicesWith regards to the contract handback in 2025/2026 my officials are actively working through the process to seek a new contractor.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the number of meetings held by her Department with representatives of driving instructors, regarding the introduction of Graduated Driver Licensing (i) prior to; and (ii) after the announcement on 27 January 2026.
answer_text: Prior to my announcement on 27 January 2026 that GDL would be implemented from 1 October 2026, officials met representatives of the driving instruction industry on at least 30 occasions where GDL was specifically mentioned.  These included meetings of the GDL Stakeholder Group, of which NIAIC was a formal member, as well as meetings between officials and NIAIC. In addition, updates on the progress of GDL were included in at least thirteen editions of the Update Newsletter issued by my officials to all approved instructors.Since my announcement there have been four GDL meetings between officials and NIAIC representatives, including one joint meeting with NIAIC and the Driving Instructors Association.  Engagement has also continued between NIAIC and the Registrar, comprising more than 20 emails and phone calls, and a mass industry correspondence regarding GDL.  Officials delivered a GDL presentation on 19 February 2026, and together with the GDL technical provider, delivered a demonstration of an example supervising driver/Approved instructor GDL user journey, including example module content, to NIAIC representatives on 5 March 2026.  Furthermore, officials have scheduled monthly meetings with NIAIC, the first of which took place on 12 February 2026, to update them on the practicalities of GDL implementation. NIAIC has also been invited to attend the wider stakeholder group meeting about GDL in April 2026.  To ensure broad and representative engagement across the approved instructor industry, regional information workshops will also be held in the coming months.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether her Department has received an Enhanced Drainage Area Plan for Bangor from NI Water, as outlined in the Northern Ireland Flood Risk Management Plan 2021-27.
answer_text: NI Water is currently developing an Enhanced Drainage Area Plan (DAP) for the Bangor catchment. I am advised that additional investigations have been necessary which have delayed the overall programme. NI Water now anticipate the outputs of the Enhanced DAP will be available in Autumn 2026 when they will be shared with my Department.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the York Street Interchange Project.
answer_text: In October 2022, my predecessor published a Placemaking and Active Travel Review report and asked officials to take forward further work to refine the options within it. Officials have completed this work and have engaged with key stakeholders on the York Street Interchange project, to gather their assessment of the report.Officials have presented the findings of this work to me, and I am taking the time to consider the next steps for this key infrastructure project.As the scheme is not funded through City and Growth deals and is not identified as an Executive Flagship project, I have asked my officials to consider possible alternative funding methods which could facilitate the scheme progression. This work is at an early stage and will take several months to complete.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether his Department has destroyed any records, in the last five years.
answer_text: The Department’s Retention and Disposal schedule details the retention and disposal of records. The Department ensures that relevant records are handled in compliance with the Schedule.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the expected timescale for the full implementation of the voluntary developer contributions scheme for wastewater infrastructure.
answer_text: Introducing a voluntary developer contributions scheme requires primary legislation. My officials are working at pace to progress the legislation and develop the necessary administrative arrangements around guidance, oversight and governance of the scheme.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the rationale for not providing an oral statement to the Assembly, regarding the voluntary developer contributions scheme for Wastewater Infrastructure.
answer_text: Developer contributions for wastewater infrastructure is at an early stage, with ongoing policy development and stakeholder engagement.  Making a written statement was, therefore, the more proportionate and appropriate format by which to announce my decision to progress a voluntary scheme and continue to explore the future potential of a levy.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether her Department will prioritise the adoption of residential developments that participate in the voluntary developer contributions scheme.
answer_text: Adoption proceeds in line with statutory requirements, technical standards and the stage at which a development is ready to be considered for adoption.Participation in the voluntary developer contributions scheme currently under policy development is not a factor in the established processes NI Water applies to the adoption of residential developments.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the anticipated cost of the Artificial Intelligence sensor project, as referenced in the Road Safety Strategy for Northern Ireland to 2030, Action Plan to 2027.
answer_text: My Department has installed sensor technology around Belfast to collect real time data on traffic volumes, vehicle type and movement patterns, as part of the of the Artificial Intelligence Project referenced in the Strategy.  The initial cost is in the region of £200,000 which includes the sensor infrastructure; preparatory ground works; and maintenance and reporting across a three-year period. This same smart sensor technology is being used in other cities across these islands.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the number of vehicle damage claims received by her Department, in each of the last 12 months, broken down by local council area.
answer_text: The management information my Department holds in relation to roads related public liability claims can be found online at: https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/publications/road-asset-maintenance-directorate-management-information-roads-related-claims-period-20202021-20242025. Please note that this information is recorded in financial years and by Roads Section Office which broadly align with Council areas. In addition, members should be cognisant of the caveats associated with each of the tables of management information available via this link.Table 3 in the management information provides details the number of public liability claims received for the last five full financial years broken down by Section Office and claim type. The Department for Infrastructure maintains its records by Financial Year. Therefore, the figures presented in following tables are the full financial years from 2020/2021 to 2024/2025.Please note that as cases progress updated information is obtained on defect type, accident location and other matters pertaining to a case. Therefore, claim data recorded on DfI's Claim Management System is ‘live’ data which may be subject to change during a claim’s lifecycle. In addition, routine IT housekeeping exercises can impact on previously reported claim data. The details proved here are correct at the time of publication.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the expected timescale for the completion of the Industrial Road Workers recruitment process.
answer_text: I was pleased to note the advertisement of my Department’s recruitment competition for Industrial Road Workers which launched on Monday 16th March 2026.This new innovative approach to recruitment aims to hold interview open days in Ballymena on 27th and 28th April 2026 with appointments likely to be in June 2026 following successful completion of the pre-employment process.Industrial road workers undertake vital operational services and this recruitment campaign demonstrates my commitment to improving our network for the benefit of all road users.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 41378/22-27, to detail the spending areas the £2.5 million was reallocated from.
answer_text: I am committed to doing all I can to improve our roads and was pleased to confirm additional funding of £2.5m which was prioritised for roads maintenance in this financial year. This allocation was possible following scrutiny and challenge of all spending forecasts right across my Department, combined with a review of available workforce capacity.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 41741/22-27, to detail the number of applicants who received a taxi driving licence, without providing a Certificate of Good Conduct.
answer_text: Information from the Driver and Vehicle Agency’s (DVA) Commercial Licensing System shows that, since the system went live in September 2020, no taxi driver licences have been issued without a Certificate of Good Conduct (COGC). Information prior to 2020 is not available. Any application for a taxi driver licence where a COGC has been requested but not provided is cancelled, and no licence is granted.  If you have concerns about a specific case, I would encourage you to contact the DVA’s Passenger Transport Licensing Division at taxiandbuslicensing@infrastructure-ni.gov.uk.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance for an update on the open-book review of departmental spending.
To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, pursuant to AQW 41496/22-27, to detail the reasons for the reduction in deployments.
answer_text: The Queen of Ulster, Fisheries Protection Vessel (FPV), is currently out of vessel certification and cannot be deployed for fisheries protection duties until recertification is completed.		This situation has arisen due to issues such as new Maritime and Coastguard Agency requirements, under the new Workboat Code 3 which have come into force.  These new requirements, in relation to vessel construction, equipment, and operational standards, mean that a programme of work is needed to bring the FPV back into certification.		A significant amount of progress has been made to date, and it is anticipated that certification will be achieved by the end of summer 2026.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for an update on the sentencing review.
To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the cost of operating the fisheries protection vessel, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: The operational costs for DAERA’s Fisheries Protection Vessel, the Queen of Ulster, over the last five years are as follows: 2021 - £99,527; 2022 - £100,057; 2023 - £66,832; 2024 - £44,731; and 2025 - £14,790.Operational costs are not limited to deployments at sea but are also incurred whilst in berth and during annual maintenance cycles. These costs cover port overheads, fuel, communications and navigation, and servicing and maintenance.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any steps her Department is taking to protect consumers from increasing energy and fuel costs.
answer_text: I am very concerned about the impact of the rise in heating oil prices on households and businesses here, particularly on lower income and vulnerable households. I am meeting regularly with the Ministers from the Department for Energy Security and Net Zero, Michael Shanks and Martin McCluskey to press for urgent additional support. While the £17 million announced on Monday 16 March is clearly inadequate to help those who are struggling with rising energy prices, I am committed to engaging with Executive colleagues to ensure those most in need get access to this support as quickly as possible.  I met with the Competition and Markets Authority on 19 March and welcome its formal study of the oil retail market. My officials will provide data to support this work, and I will carefully consider any recommendations arising from its findings.Trading Standards plays a key role in protecting consumers from illegal trading practices, including unfair pricing, while supporting legitimate businesses. It also provides guidance to oil retailers on their obligations and advice to consumers on raising complaints.The Consumer Council provides invaluable information for consumers by monitoring heating oil prices and publishing weekly average prices. Consumers are encouraged to use its website to compare suppliers and find information on oil buying clubs and Fuel Stamp schemes.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the number of occasions her Department has requested a taxi driver licence applicant, who lived overseas, for a Certificate of Good Conduct issued by the relevant embassy.
answer_text: Since the Driver and Vehicle Agency’s online Commercial Licensing System went live in September 2020, 170 taxi driver licence applicants have been asked to provide a Certificate of Good Conduct.  Information prior to this date is not available.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 40569/22-27, to detail the amount of the £3.3 million, allocated to the Southern Division, that was allocated to the Ards and North Down Section Office.
answer_text: As you are aware, £3.3m was allocated to Southern Division from the recent Winter Recovery Roads Fund and almost £2m of additional funding issued to the same division from the £2.51m I announced on 24th February from reallocated departmental funds. This represents a total investment of almost £5.3m in the Southern division area.Local officials are best placed to identify areas of greatest need in which to utilise this targeted funding.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail how the £40 million, allocated to road maintenance since December 2025, will be allocated to each DfI Roads Section Office.
answer_text: Through December Monitoring I was pleased to welcome additional funding of more than £30 million to support road maintenance, including £4 million targeted specifically at the rural road network. This funding will enable an enhanced programme of works across the road network, including resurfacing schemes, street lighting improvements, drainage works, and improvements to road markings and signage.In recognition of the severity of the impact of the winter months on our road network, I established the £7.85m Winter Recovery Road Fund, which together with the additional £2.51m identified for reallocation from my departmental budget, represents a further investment of over £10m in road infrastructure.Each division has been made an allocation based on assessed need and capacity to deliver.  The breakdown of allocation is provided in the table below:DivisionAllocation (£m)Northern8Southern15Eastern6Western11Total40

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the number of times that the fisheries protection vessel has been deployed, in each of the last five years.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance for an update on his Department's engagement with the UK Government regarding the Local Growth Fund.
answer_text: On 22 January 2026, the Executive wrote to Steve Reed, Secretary of State for Housing, Communities and Local Government, calling on him to work with his Ministerial colleagues to rectify the profile of the Local Growth Fund here with a more favourable Resource allocation. In addition, on 25 February 2026, I, alongside my Welsh and Scottish counterparts, wrote to the Chief Secretary to the Prime Minister, Darren Jones, to raise our shared concerns on the British Government’s approach to the Local Growth Fund.  A response was received to the Executive’s letter on 2 March 2026. Disappointingly, the Secretary of State, Steve Reed, has not provided any change to the Capital/ Resource Split on the Local Growth Fund. The Executive subsequently wrote to Hilary Benn on 19 March 2026 to collectively urge the British Government, even at this late stage, to reverse and correct its fundamentally unfair approach in respect of the Local Growth Fund.In parallel, the NIO are currently developing an Investment plan for the Local Growth Fund, and my officials continue to engage with them on that so that the funding that is available, can be allocated as soon as possible.

answer_text: The Creative Innovation Hub (known as ‘The Shed’) being developed by Ards & North Down Borough Council will be located at Queen’s Hall, Sullivan Close, Holywood, BT18 9HL.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) the amount of the £2.5 million, allocated to road maintenance, that has been reallocated from other areas of her Department’s budget; and (ii) from which areas of the budget it has been reallocated from.
answer_text: In line with my commitment to do all I can to improve our roads here, I was able to reprioritise funding from various spending areas across my Department to provide an additional £2.5m towards road maintenance to carry out essential repairs across our roads divisions.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any consideration her Department has given to introducing mandatory drug testing, to those involved in serious or fatal road traffic collisions.
answer_text: The PSNI already have powers to administer preliminary drug tests in a variety of circumstances, including when a driver has been involved in a road traffic collision.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether a response has been received to the joint letter sent by the Executive to the Secretary of State for Ministry of Housing, Communities and Local Government, regarding the Local Growth Fund.
answer_text: A response was received on 2 March 2026. Disappointingly, the Secretary of State, Steve Reed, has not provided any change to the Capital/ Resource Split on the Local Growth Fund. I will now engage with my Executive Colleagues to consider the implications of this.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any recommendations that have been enacted by her Department ,following the 2024 Northern Ireland Audit Office report, Road Openings by Utilities.
answer_text: There were 7 recommendations in the NIAO report on Road Openings by Utilities. Progress on the recommendations are detailed below. Recommendation 1 – Article 18 Notifications An improvement plan has been initiated within my Department to improve notification of resurfacing works to utilities. Recommendation 2 & 4 – Potential Costs of Road Openings and Warranty PeriodDuring the PAC hearing my officials pointed out that it is very difficult to attribute the costs of road deterioration to any one specific cause. Any estimate would be of limited value and subjective due to the many variables that can impact the condition of a road. The age of the road, the materials used, weather conditions, traffic loads, chemical / oil spills, as well as the presence of utility reinstatements can all play a part in the on-going deterioration of our road assets.However, my Department agrees that measuring and reporting the proportion of structural maintenance costs attributable to road openings by utilities, if reliable, is an important step in improving transparency and cost management. As highlighted during the PAC session my Department has now commissioned a full digital survey of the road network. This will provide a significantly greater understanding of our infrastructure and allow a more meaningful analysis of how it is performing. Consultants have been engaged to use this data to bring forward a piece of work to better understand the determinates that affect road condition and deterioration.My officials have also commissioned work to consider the length of the warranty period applied to utility reinstatements. The new digital survey will help support this work and allow my Department to develop an evidence base to justify any potential change to the warranty period.  Recommendation 3 - Review of Sampling and TestingMy Department can carry out up to a 30% sample inspection of utility work as per the Code of Practice. The number of sample inspections for any year is based on the actual number of units of inspection averaged over the previous three financial years for that undertaker. The sample must be representative in order that it can be extrapolated.My Department’s Statistics Branch has reviewed the level of assurance that could be inferred from the current sample sizes.There are three different sample inspection types (A, B, C) so these must be considered as three independent samples and it is assumed that the samples are drawn randomly. The calculations used a confidence level of 95% i.e. we can be 95% confident that the true value in the population lies within these ranges.Sample A inspections - had a confidence interval of +/- 1.85%Sample B inspections - had a confidence interval of +/- 1.41%Sample C inspections - had a confidence interval of +/- 1.38%Even with the lower 22% level of inspection in 2024 / 2025, the sample approach adopted provides my Department with a very high level of assurance that the inspection results reflect the situation acr

answer_text: The Road Safety Action Plan had already been prepared for publication by the Department’s graphic design team before the date for introducing Graduated Driving Licensing had been finalised. The date of 1 October 2026 was subsequently confirmed and announced on the 27 January 2026.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) when the decision was taken, to introduce the latest Road Safety Action Plan to 2027, rather than annual plans, as had been previously been the case; and (ii) the rationale for this decision.
answer_text: The development of any Road Safety Action Plan requires careful consideration and working closely with our road safety partners to reflect emerging trends and updates in road safety.  As in previous years and before publication, all road safety partners; departments, emergency services and other organisations, took time to consider their budget position.  The durations of action plans therefore reflect the political and budgetary positions at that time.  Many interventions in the current plan will progress beyond the scope of a one-year action plan and therefore it makes better sense to monitor them into 2027.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the number of illegally erected signs removed by her Department, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: My Department does not hold records of the information requested in a readily accessible format and any attempt to do so would require an extensive exercise to be conducted across the Department which might only be expected to produce data with a limited degree of exactitude, and this exercise itself would very likely involve disproportionate costs.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail any efforts undertaken by her Department, to obtain criminal record information, when an individual applies for an enhanced disclosure certificate, and their country of origin does not have formal arrangements in place with AccessNI, for the provision of such information.
answer_text: When AccessNI processes an enhanced disclosure for an individual from a country that does not have formal criminal record datasharing arrangements with the UK, AccessNI is limited to checks of UK Police and Disclosure & Barring Service (DBS) databases. No additional international checks can be undertaken.The establishment of international criminal record datasharing arrangements is a reserved matter and falls within the responsibility of the UK Government.An enhanced disclosure certificate is only one component of safe recruitment. For applicants who have lived overseas, employers should seek additional assurances, such as a Certificate of Good Conduct issued by the relevant embassy.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister, following the unveiling of a memorial plaque at Parliament Buildings to victims and survivors of Historical Institutional Abuse, for an update on any further implementation of the inquiry’s recommendations.
answer_text: The unveiling of the memorial on 20 February marked an important milestone for many victims and survivors.At the event, we reaffirmed our commitment to honouring victims and survivors and ensuring their experiences help build a safer future for every child.Other recommendations – including the redress scheme, the public apology, the appointment of the Commissioner for Survivors of Institutional Childhood Abuse and specialist services – have been implemented.We continue to engage with institutions on contributions to redress and support services. Payments have been received from the Good Shepherd Sisters, Barnardo’s, the De La Salle Order and the Diocese of Down and Connor in respect of De La Salle-run homes. Constructive discussions have been held with the Irish Church Missions, the Sisters of Nazareth and the Sisters of Saint Louis.Support continues through the HIA Support Service until 2030, with the COSICA role extended to 2027.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the number of cases examined by the Tuberculosis and Brucellosis Valuation Appeals Panel, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: There have been 21 appeal cases examined by the Tuberculosis and Brucellosis Valuation Appeals Panel over the last five years. Of the 21 appeal cases, there were 4 appeals in 2025, 2 in 2024, 8 in 2023, 2 in 2022 and 4 in 2021. There was also one further appeal, which due to the large numbers of animals involved, was split into five tranches.  These tranches were heard over 5 separate hearing dates from 2023-2025, these hearings took place over 2 or 3 days.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether his Department is considering any adjustments to the closed period for slurry spreading, in light of the unprecedented levels of rain in recent weeks.
answer_text: My Department has given careful consideration to the suggestion to regulate slurry spreading based solely on weather conditions rather than fixed dates. The dates of the closed period are based on local data and scientific research by the Agri Food and Biosciences Institute (AFBI). DAERA commissioned AFBI to undertake a comprehensive review of the underpinning science in 2020 and again in 2024 and both reviews concluded that there is no scientific or agronomic basis for changing the closed period. The AFBI analysis concluded that “The current evidence base supports the closed period as the most effective strategy for minimising the risks associated with slurry spreading from 15 October to the 31 January. Removal of the closed period and reliance solely on the current NAP restrictions on weather and soil conditions, (i.e. restrict applications based on soil moisture and rainfall conditions), will not sufficiently mitigate the risk associated with slurry applications during this period.” During these months, low temperatures and limited daylight restrict crop growth, meaning minimal nutrient uptake and nutrients vulnerable to run off in any subsequent wet weather.  For this reason, the Nutrients Action Programme Regulations require farms to have at least 22 weeks of slurry storage to allow flexibility until spreading conditions improve in the spring.Ultimately, the aim is to protect our watercourses from pollution at a time when major improvements are urgently needed.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

answer_text: There were 4,825 cancelled planned elective surgeries in the South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust over the last five years, a breakdown of which is provided in the table below.YearNo. of Scheduled SurgeriesNo. of Elective Surgeries Cancelled202110,022626202212,099888202314,3921,276202413,0871,053202513,878982Source: South Eastern Health and Social Care TrustThis includes both patient and hospital cancellations on the day of surgery across the main hospital, day procedure unit and paediatric theatres at the Ulster, Downe and Lagan Valley Hospitals. To note, the impact of COVID-19 in 2021 and 2022 resulted in fewer scheduled elective surgeries, resulting in fewer cancellations during this period.My Department has provided recurrent funding to Health and Social Care Trusts to support a dual notification system for appointment reminders. In addition to issuing appointment letters, Trusts also use text message reminders across most services to notify patients of upcoming appointments, helping to minimise inconvenience.Furthermore, the Encompass system is now fully operational across Northern Ireland. Through the My Care application, all patients and service users can securely access a subset of their health and social care record, including appointment letters, clinic letters, medication information and some test results.  Users can also view upcoming appointments and track the progress of their treatment.

To ask the Minister of Health when he will answer AQW 36922/22-27.
To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any arrangements her Department has in place to ensure contractor repaired potholes have been properly sealed.
answer_text: My Department identifies actionable defects for repair through routine highway safety inspections and through public reporting via the online Report a Fault system.Actionable defects are instructed for repair either through our internal or external contractor.  On completion of the repair, inspections are undertaken on a percentage of completed road defect repairs by external contractors before payments are processed. Checks on the work of the Department’s internal contractor are also undertaken.In addition to the contractual checks above, issues will also be recorded at the next routine inspection.If a repair fails to satisfy the required standards, officials will request that it be rectified and subjected to further inspection. This process will continue until the repair fully complies with all stipulated requirements.Officials use DEM 160/21 Road Maintenance Standards for Safety – Limited Service when determining actionable defects and the Manual of Contract Documents for Highways Works when determining a repair to be satisfactory.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for an update on the development of the Ards and North Down digital hub.
answer_text: An Outline Business Case for the Creative Innovation Hub in Holywood was approved by my Department in December 2024. The Project Promoter- Ards & North Down Borough Council had been in negotiations on an initial preferred site but these were discontinued when agreement on a final purchase price could not be reached with the landowner. The Council subsequently reviewed a range of alternative location options to advance the project. As of February 2026, it has secured an approved site in Holywood that meets the requirements set out in the business case and which is already in Council ownership. Initial meetings between my Department and key stakeholders, including the Belfast Region City Deal Advisory Board, Central Procurement Delivery (CPD) and Invest Northern Ireland, have been arranged to review the business case including costs, and to help progress the project as quickly as possible.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the work of the RAISE programme in the Donaghadee and Millisle locality.
answer_text: There has been considerable progress in delivering the RAISE Programme in the Donaghadee and Millisle locality.Firstly, there has been a programme of detailed and extremely positive engagement with stakeholders across the locality through engagement events, focus groups, individual meetings and workshops.  There has also been engagement with parents and with children and young people led by Parenting Focus and Stranmillis University College respectively, and their findings have helped to inform the development of the Strategic Area Plan for the Donaghadee and Millisle locality.A Locality Reference Group has also been established, bringing together key stakeholders including representatives from local schools, community and voluntary organisations, the Council and the Health and Social Care Trust. This Group has played an important role in shaping and developing the Strategic Area Plan for the Donaghadee and Millisle Locality. The Plan was published on 5 September 2025 and identifies the key issues affecting educational outcomes and sets out four priority objectives for the locality. These include improving literacy and numeracy, transitions and pathways.   A call notice issued in September 2025 inviting applications to deliver against objectives in the Strategic Area Plan. Two proposals for the Donaghadee and Millisle locality were identified to progress to the Letter of Offer stage and my officials are currently working to finalise and agree these to enable these projects to commence their important work. A second call for projects is planned for early 2026, and this will include seeking applications to deliver against the objectives from the Strategic Area Plan not yet funded.I have allocated £183,000 to help deliver the RAISE programme in Donaghadee and Millisle, and to ensure that the needs of children and young people, identified in the locality’s Strategic Area Plan, are met.In addition to the locality led projects I have announced a series of Cross Cutting Initiatives.  Two schools serving the locality are also involved in RAISE Cross-Cutting Initiatives, with Clifton Special School taking part in the Reading with AI project and Movilla High School taking part in the Holiday Revision Scheme.  As further Cross Cutting Initiatives are rolled out in the coming months, schools in the locality will have the opportunity to participate in these.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for an update on the Mobile Action Plan for Northern Ireland, including the Northern Ireland Barrier Busting Taskforce.
answer_text: The implementation of the Mobile Action Plan (MAP) is nearing completion. The Barrier Busting Taskforce (BBT) has been leading on delivery of the MAP actions and the vast majority of the work associated with the actions has been completed. The remaining outstanding workstreams will be taken forward by the relevant departments and organisations.I remain committed to encouraging the rollout of mobile connectivity and my officials will continue to work with the mobile industry and other partners to ensure that citizens and businesses throughout the North can fully avail of the benefits that wireless technology can offer.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for an update on Royal Courts of Justice modernisation programme.
answer_text: The Royal Courts of Justice (RCJ) is one of the most important civic buildings in Northern Ireland, both in terms of its architectural significance, and because of its role in accommodating the High Court and Court of Judicature.NICTS is progressing a major capital construction project at RCJ to modernise the building and to improve User Experience, Resilience, Value for Money and Sustainability, in line with the Aims of its Estate Strategy.Following approval of the first Outline Business Case in August 2024, a consultancy team has been progressing through the early design stages and engaging with stakeholders and statutory organisations such as Historic Environment Division (HED).  The Stage 3 Spatial Coordination Stage is currently ongoing, which will allow the submission of a Planning Application before the end of the year.This is a long-term and complex construction project and, subject to further business case approval, funding provision and the procurement of decant facilities, the anticipated completion date is 2033, when the building will celebrate its centenary.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail any steps her Department is taking to improve the AccessNI application process.
answer_text: AccessNI continually aims to provide service improvements designed to enhance the effectiveness, accuracy, and accessibility of the disclosure process for citizens, employers, and volunteer organisations.In September 2025, Northern Ireland became the first region within UK and Ireland to implement a fully digital disclosure service. This transition has strengthened safeguarding arrangements by reducing the risk of certificates being fraudulently altered, misdirected by post or mislaid.  It has also helped drive costs down and streamline the process for the applicant and the business.t.Over the past year, further enhancements have been introduced, including the use of verified Northern Ireland Identity Assurance (NIDA) accounts and digital ID upload. These measures provide greater security, ease of use and improve the overall accuracy and integrity of disclosure applications.Legislative changes coming into effect on 17 February 2026 extended eligibility for Enhanced disclosure checks to selfemployed individuals and personal employees engaged in regulated activity with vulnerable adults or children. AccessNI has implemented the necessary technological developments to ensure that selfemployed roles can be captured effectively within the system.A new Disputes function has also been developed, enabling applicants to more easily dispute or appeal information disclosed on their certificate. This will significantly streamline the process between the submission of a dispute and its resolution.As part of AccessNI’s ongoing engagement with service users, AccessNI maintains a Stakeholder Forum comprising representatives from a range of public, private, and voluntary and community sector organisations. All proposed service improvements are considered in consultation with this group. This is a forum where customers can raise concerns and issues that are affecting their business.  A customer survey is currently open to gather feedback and assist with future development priorities.AccessNI also works in close collaboration with counterparts in England, Wales, Scotland, and the Republic of Ireland to follow progress and service enhancements.It has long been an objective of AccessNI, to provide customers with the option of a portable disclosure product.  Officials continue to explore this within financially sustainable delivery models and are actively monitoring and researching developments in other police databases that may offer a viable solution.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any engagement her Department has had to regulate private car park management companies.
answer_text: Whilst my Department regularly engages with relevant stakeholders to discuss parking matters and potential new parking initiatives, it has no responsibility for the regulation of private car parks, and I currently have no plans to regulate private car park management companies.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any actions taken to increase the provision of NHS dentists, in North Down.
answer_text: To improve access to Health Service dentistry across Northern Ireland, an additional £7 million has been invested in General Dental Services in 2025/26. The investment has been directed towards stabilising services, including the continuation of the Enhanced Child Dental Examination Scheme and a 30% enhancement on fees paid to dentists for Health Service fillings, extractions and root canal treatments. A further £1.6 million has also been allocated to support dental practitioners who continue to provide Health Service dental care to their patients.While these investment measures are necessary for short-term stabilisation, achieving long-term sustainability will require broader reform, and preparatory work in this area has now begun. It is important to note however, that under current General Dental Services Regulations, dental practitioners operate as self-employed, independent contractors who determine both the amount of time they dedicate to delivering Health Service care, and the location from which they provide it.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of times that the nurse-to-patient-ratio within the Emergency Department of the Royal Victoria Hospital fell below the guidelines set by his Department, in the last five years.
answer_text: Nurse to attendance ratios for Emergency Departments (EDs) were established under Phase 2 of the Delivering Care Framework in 2017. While these ratios were not fully funded across the system, the Royal Victoria Hospital (RVH) was an exception, as full funding was provided at the time of its redevelopment. A review of Phase 2 had been planned; however, the wider Delivering Care process is currently paused pending publication of the new Framework, which is expected to be launched at the end of February.The Delivering Care Phase 2 staffing model for EDs was not expressed as a nurse to bed ratio but was instead based on nurse to attendance ratios. Under this model, the recommended range is one nurse per 700 to 850 attendances.My Department does not hold information on nurse to patient ratios in the RVH ED over the past five years. However, data held by my Department indicates that in 2025 the RVH achieved a nurse to attendance ratio of 1:621, which is better than the ratio set out in Delivering Care. For context, the median ratio for Northern Ireland is 1:727, and the median across all Emergency Departments in England, Wales and Northern Ireland is 1:814.Based on this data and benchmarking against EDs in Northern Ireland, England and Wales, the RVH demonstrates a stronger nurse to attendance ratio than that envisaged under Delivering Care and performs favourably against peers regionally and nationally.My Department recognises the significant pressures experienced by EDs. To help address these challenges and improve patient flow and waiting times, a range of system-wide measures is being implemented alongside the increased consultant workforce. These include:•	The expansion of alternatives to ED attendance, such as urgent care centers, Phone First services and minor injury units;•	Implementation of Winter Plan measures, including enhanced discharge processes, rapid access clinics and increased community-based capacity through Hospital at Home services;•	Continued development of Same Day Emergency Care and ambulatory pathways across specialties including frailty, respiratory, cardiology, surgical and acute medicine; and,•	Improvements in diagnostics and discharge coordination to support timely assessment, treatment and earlier discharge.These actions form part of a broader programme to ensure patients receive timely, safe and effective care. My Department remains committed to supporting ED staff, who continue to work under very challenging circumstances, and to sustaining emergency care services through both immediate measures and longer-term reform.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the number of animals slaughtered as part of TB control measures, in each of the last five years, broken down by constituency.
answer_text: My Department does not collate statistics by constituency.  Therefore, the numbers of animals subject to compulsory removal as part of bovine tuberculosis (bTB) control measures have been broken down in the tables below, where available, by areas covered by DAERA Divisional Veterinary Offices (DVO), for each of the last five calendar years. The numbers of reported bovine cases have been further broken down into those cases reported due to a reaction to the Comparative Intradermal Tuberculin Test (CITT) known as the skin test; those which were reported due to a positive result from the Interferon gamma (IFN-g) blood test which is carried out in addition to the skin test in some bTB breakdown herds; and those subject to removal as negative in contact (NIC) animals considered to be at high risk of disease even though they may has tested negative. NIC animals may be animals in a batch of cattle where there has been a high percentage of reactor cattle, or animals taken as part of a herd depopulation in severe disease outbreaks. The test positive animals are recorded by date of the positive test, and the NIC animals are recorded by date of death. For 2025 the numbers of CIIT positive animals are up to date until the end of November 2025 as December figures have not yet been validated. question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any consideration his Department has given to supporting additional 13-15 years tractor driving courses.
answer_text: My Department, through the College of Agriculture, Food and Rural Enterprise (CAFRE) has committed to delivering a programme of tractor driving for 13-15 year-olds training courses, consistent with that delivered during 2025. No additional tractor driving for 13-15 year-olds training courses can be delivered by CAFRE during 2026 due to resource constraints.These tractor driving for 13-15 year-olds training courses will be available during the summer period at CAFRE’s Greenmount and Enniskillen campuses. Details for enrolling on the Tractor Driving for 13-15 year olds training courses will be added to the CAFRE website at www.cafre.ac.uk in the coming months. Further details on other training providers in Northern Ireland delivering these courses are available from Lantra.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the anticipated cost of introducing the Graduated Driver Licensing app and website.
answer_text: The capital costs of developing the Graduated Driver Licensing app and website (the digital Programme of Training and Logbook) is £2 million, which will be incurred over the 2025-2026 and 2026-2027 financial years.  The £17 charge to the learner for the Programme of Training and Logbook will cover the resource running costs and support for the digital solution.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any engagement her Department has had with the PSNI, in relation to enforcement related to the introduction of a Graduated Driver Licensing scheme.
answer_text: During the development of the Graduated Driver Licensing (GDL) proposals, my Department was in contact with the PSNI in their capacity as a GDL stakeholder.  Their input helped shape the GDL provisions within the Road Traffic (Amendment) Act (Northern Ireland) 2016.My Department continues to engage with the PSNI, through the Road Safety Forum; meetings with senior officers in the Driver and Vehicle Agency (DVA) and Roads Policing; and monthly meetings with the DVA PSNI Liaison Officer.  The February 2026 meeting will have a specific update on GDL.In terms of enforcing GDL, that would be for the PSNI to respond to.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail her Department's legal aid spending on legacy cases, in each of the last ten years.
answer_text: The Legal Services Agency records and administers legal aid cases on the basis of specific Natures and Matters.  In turn, and due to the resultant formatting of data within the legal aid case management system, it is not possible to identify or extract cases which may constitute ‘legacy’ without necessitating a manual case-by-case review of all paper (pre-2019) and electronic files, together with supporting documents, as such a generic categorisation does not exist in the database.  Accordingly, it is not possible to attribute legal aid expenditure exclusively to these case types.However, it is possible to extract costs of legacy inquests, which can be objectively identified from a list of cases originally drawn up by the former Lord Chief Justice, and which continues to be maintained. Under this definition, Table 1 details annual legal aid expenditure on legacy inquests since 2019/20.  This reflects the period in which the Legal Service Agency’s digital case management system has been in operation and the data can be extracted. For the current financial year (2025/26), I have included spend accrued during the first three quarters (April to December 2025).  There will be legacy inquests not yet complete, so further payments are still to be made. Table 1: Legal Aid Expenditure on ‘legacy’ cases, since 2019/20YearExpenditure2019/20£859,966.172020/21£963,054.872021/22£738,930.732022/23-£227,711.862023/24£541,314.132024/25£2,882,324.23April – December 2025£659,926.17Source: LAMS, Legal Services Agency NI.Notes:Expenditure figures presented in Table 1 constitute Management Information and are subject to change.The negative (net) total in 2022/23 can be attributed to a large recoupment of legal aid funds following settlement of civil cases arising from legacy inquest cases.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any plans her Department has to increase park and ride provision in the North Down constituency.
answer_text: Enhancing connectivity is a key priority of the Department through public transport and active travel, including opportunities for advanced park and ride schemes. As such, funding requirements for park and ride schemes throughout the province continue to be submitted on an annual basis. My Department has been operating in difficult financial environment for a number of years due to underfunding and austerity and as the capital budget for the Department continues to be less than required, available funding, including that for Park and Ride, must be prioritised accordingly. At present we are progressing Park & Ride proposals at Comber and Newtownards.The preferred site in Comber is located along the A22 Belfast Road corridor immediately adjacent to the Comber Greenway with the potential to provide parking for 410 vehicles in the first phase with scope for further extension. Appraisal and design works are ongoing to enable the submission of a planning application.I can advise that my Department is also progressing the provision of the Newtownards Park and Ride facility at William Street.  Since the land was vested, the Department, with assistance from the Departmental Solicitor’s Office (DSO), has spent a considerable amount of time addressing complex legal title issues in conjunction with Ards and North Down Borough Council.I understand that these issues have largely been resolved with the matter having recently been referred to Land and Property Services (LPS) with a view to determining formal valuation and compensation. On receipt of the Valuer’s Report the Department will issue further instructions to DSO to address any remaining legal matters and to complete the purchase of the land.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to his statement of 27 January 2026, to detail a breakdown of the 70,000 additional elective activities, in terms of Health and Social Care Trust areas, in which they took place.
answer_text: A major priority in the Northern Ireland Executive’s Programme for Government was to reduce waiting lists by treating an additional 70,000 patients by 2027. This initial target of additional elective activities has been exceeded with approximately 189,000 assessments, diagnostic tests and treatments already completed as at the end of November 2025, and projections indicate that the final number will exceed 200,000. The number of additional elective activities delivered across the region between 1 April 2025 and 30 November 2025, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust, is indicated below.Table 1HSC TrustOutpatientsTreatmentsDiagnosticsTotal PatientsBelfast42,2061,11312,46355,782Northern5,4303,83122,68931,950South Eastern5,1032,43647,12954,668Southern5,5822,47510,48618,543Western3,1511,85322,92927,933Total Patients61,47211,708115,696188,876Source: PSSID Performance TeamActions which have been taken to drive down our waiting lists include the expansion of mega clinics, enhancing diagnostic capacity, expanding pre-operative assessment capacity and introducing a new waiting list reimbursement scheme. The continued use of waiting list initiatives, using both in-house and independent sector capacity, are also supporting the reduction in our waiting lists.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any engagement her Department has had with Ards and North Down Borough Council, in relation to promoting the additional tourism potential in Bangor, once the Queen's Parade Regeneration Scheme is complete.
answer_text: Department for Communities is the lead department for the Queen’s Parade regeneration.Tourism NI engages regularly with Council officials to provide support and advice on relevant tourism aspects of the Bangor Waterfront Redevelopment. The £70+ million Bangor Waterfront Project is a complementary but separate project to the Queen’s Parade regeneration and is part of the Belfast Region City Deal (BRCD), again led by the Department for Communities.Tourism NI looks forward to offering further advice and support to Council as both projects develop and progress to market readiness. Tourism NI currently promotes all regions of the North through seasonal marketing campaigns each year, including the Ards and North Down area. This year’s extensive Spring Campaign, which launches on 9th February, will feature both Pickie Fun Park and Exploris Aquarium.As both the Queen’s Parade and Bangor Waterfront redevelopment projects progress to delivery, Tourism NI will continue to collaborate with Ards and North Down Borough Council to develop and align promotional plans for Bangor. Tourism NI will work closely with Council and other stakeholders to identify key visitor markets, a communication strategy, and to develop supporting assets to effectively promote the area.Promotional support from Tourism NI will include inclusion of Bangor in appropriate marketing campaigns, social media, email, and PR activities.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the number of international media visits, facilitated by Tourism NI, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: Tourism NI’s International Media Visits team deliver an annual portfolio of media familiarisation programmes that showcase the breadth and quality of our tourism offering to key international audiences.  		The number of international media visits facilitated by Tourism NI per financial year in the last five years are as follows:	2021-2022: 	123 2022-2023: 	2502023-2024: 	2682024-2025: 	2642025-2026: 	267 delivered to date (a further 30 programmes are due to be delivered in February and March)

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail any engagement he has had with the banking sector, in relation to branch closures, since taking office.
answer_text: I am deeply concerned about the impact of bank branch closures on local communities.  This is an issue I directly raised with the banking sector at my Banking Roundtable on 3 December 2025. I pressed on them the importance of maintaining the branch network, access to cash and essential banking services here, particularly for our most vulnerable.   My officials are now preparing a report summarising the key issues raised at the Roundtable, which I will share with Treasury and the Financial Conduct Authority to seek appropriate action, given their respective responsibilities for banking and financial services.Furthermore, the banking hub concept is something I have been pushing on the sector to roll out at speed here in areas impacted by bank closures. This at least provides some level of in-person banking presence for effected customers.I also encourage communities to request a cash access review directly with LINK if they face difficulties accessing cash locally, and to raise awareness of that process, I invited all MLAs to attend a LINK hosted drop-in event at Parliament Buildings on 2 December 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail her Department's priorities for tourism growth, in the North Down constituency, over the next five years.
answer_text: My 10 Year Tourism Vision & Action Plan sets out as a key objective the doubling of tourism spend to £2bn by 2035. Councils, such as Ards & North Down Borough Council (ANDBC) will play a key role in reaching that ambitious target. Tourism NI has developed a good working relationship with the council over many years, and their new CEO visited the Borough last month. Along with the council team, she visited the new Bangor Waterfront project and discussed the Council’s future plans.  She also met industry stakeholders in the area. Promotion of tourism experiences from the area is ongoing. This year’s Spring Campaign, which launches on 9th February and will feature both Pickie Fun Park and Exploris Aquarium. Currently, ten local experience providers in the ANDBC area are part of Tourism NI’s Embrace a Giant Spirit Brand Experience Portfolio, all of which feature on Tourism NI’s consumer website – discovernorthernireland.com and participate in Tourism NI’s many business support programmes.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the number of Housing Executive properties, in North Down, that have been vacant for more than 12 months.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Housing Executive. I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“As of 28th January 2026, there were 11 domestic properties in North Down area that had been void for more than 12 months. These are all non-actionable voids which are not immediately available for allocation and are held vacant for operational reasons i.e. decanting purposes (where they are being used to house a tenant on a temporary basis pending works to their home), planned maintenance schemes, and properties which have been identified for sale/transfer or have approval for demolition.”

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any engagement she has had with the hospitality sector in relation to the impact of NI Reval2026.
answer_text: You will be aware that the Minister of Finance has stopped the Reval 2026 process, and I know this has been welcomed by our tourism and hospitality industries. His decision followed careful consideration of the concerns raised after the draft values were published, particularly from hotels, pubs and other hospitality businesses.  Like Minister O’Dowd, I am in listening mode and will continue to engage with the sector on their concerns.In recent weeks, I have met with Hospitality Ulster and the NI Food To Go Association along with the Minister of Finance to discuss their proposal for a reduced VAT pilot scheme here given the VAT differential with the south. I will continue to advocate, alongside the Minister of Finance, for a fairer tax regime for our local businesses, allowing them to compete on an all-island basis. We have committed to seek a meeting with Treasury to discuss the proposal for a reduced VAT pilot scheme.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether her Department is considering an additional tranche of the part-time 20mph speed limit zones at schools, during 2026-27.
answer_text: Road safety is a high priority for my Department, and we are committed to working proactively to make our roads safer and to address the needs of all road users. We are acutely aware that children and young people are amongst the most vulnerable groups using our roads, particularly around schools.My Department is currently taking forward a third tranche of part-time 20mph zones at 40 schools across the North which will bring the total delivered since 2020/21 to 273 schools https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/publications/20mph-speed-limit-schools-tranche-3.This initiative is a significant intervention that will help to ensure that children, parents and staff feel safer as they travel to and from school on a daily basis.My Department is also taking forward a review on a number of speed related matters, including 20mph speed limits, and I plan to undertake a public consultation exercise as part of this review in the new year.  Further consideration on the use of 20mph zones will be taken on completion of the consultation.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy, pursuant to AQW 37416/22-27, to detail the locations under consideration to be part of the first focused Stewardship Plan.
answer_text: The first phase of the Destination Stewardship Framework project is nearing completion, following a period of extensive stakeholder engagement and state of the industry research. A draft Destination Stewardship Framework has been produced, setting out the principles for success.Tourism NI and its Board are reflecting on the outworkings of the process to date and are also considering the arrangements to select a Destination for the first focused Stewardship Plan.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the electrification of the Bangor to Belfast railway line.
answer_text: An initial technical study into electrification of the entire NI railway network, including the Bangor line, was completed in 2022.  Further feasibility studies will be required to assess the available options and determine the optimum solution for the Bangor to Belfast line.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail any engagement his Department has had with the UK Government in relation to issues associated with the launch of the new Civil Service Pension Portal.
answer_text: This new portal is not for members of the NICS Civil Service Pension Scheme.My Department therefore has had no engagement with the Westminster Government in relation to issues associated with the launch of the new Civil Service Pension Portal.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 37687/22-27, to detail when the review is anticipated to be completed.
answer_text: A desktop site assessment has been completed and the results have been shared with the SEN Placement Team. Once a schedule of the required accommodation has been identified, the Education Authority will prepare a feasibility report to confirm the site’s suitability.Given the pressures on existing SEN accommodation, everyone is working to reach a conclusion as quickly as possible, however I am unable to confirm a specific timescale for the completion of this work.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance for an update on his engagement with the UK Government in relation to the future of the Shared Prosperity Fund.
answer_text: I refer the Member to answers provided to previous questions AQW 33970/22-27, AQW 31249/22-27, AQO 2452/22-27, AQO 2458/22-27, AQW 32531/22-27, AQW 33970-22-27, AQW 36740/22-27, AQW 37509/22-27 and AQW 38270/22-27, which detail the substantial engagement I have had with British Government Ministers on this matter.I have also kept my Executive colleagues fully appraised of the situation. Local Growth was discussed at the Executive meeting last week, after which, on 22 January, the Executive jointly wrote to the Secretary of State for Housing Communities and Local Government, Steve Reed.  We collectively detailed the devastating impact the current approach to Local Growth will have in the North, and with one voice, called on Mr Reed to work with his Ministerial colleagues to immediately rectify the profile of the Local Growth Fund here with a more favourable Resource allocation.
