question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for an update on the Ards and North Down Local Economic Partnership.
answer_text: The Ards and North Down Local Economic Partnership has met every month since August 2025 to develop its action plan. The partnership anticipates that the action plan will be finalised in the coming weeks.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the average ambulance turnaround time at the Ulster Hospital Emergency Department, in each of the last 12 months.
To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of missed appointments in the South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust, in 2024-25.
answer_text: The number of missed appointments within the South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust from 1 April 2023 to 31 January 2026, broken down by fiscal year, is outlined below. Fiscal YearMissed New First AppointmentsMissed Review AppointmentsTotal Missed AppointmentsTotal % of Missed Appointments2023-247,90314,87122,7749.2%2024-257,12512,70419,8297.4%Source: South Eastern Health and Social Care TrustThe information provided refers to consultant led outpatient appointments for the Acute Programme of Care across the South Eastern Trust, including face to face and virtual appointments. This includes patients who did not attend (DNA) their scheduled appointment, as well as those who cancelled on the day of their appointment.To help reduce the number of DNAs and cancellations, and reduce the associated cost to the health service, Health and Social Care Trusts have implemented text messaging reminders for upcoming appointments. These reminders not only prompt patients to attend, but also provide an opportunity for patients to cancel or reschedule appointments where they are unable to attend. This ensures valuable appointment slots can be offered to other patients.Going forward, with Encompass now fully operational across Northern Ireland, all patients and service users will be able to securely access a subset of their health and social care record via the My Care application, which will include information such as appointment letters, medication details and test results.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 24289/22-27, to detail any actions progressed following the winter planning workshops.
answer_text: The ‘Big Discussion’ and Ambulance Handover/Emergency Department (ED) Trust workshops, focused on a whole systems approach, bringing together teams from across the HSC system. They analysed existing data, explored the drivers of demand, considered what the potential solutions might be to improve pathways, optimise flow, and utilise resources in the most effective way. This activity not only supports the system during the winter period but all year round. The key actions progressed as part of the winter planning workshops include:continuation of the work to address the delayed discharge that exacerbates the pressure on our hospital services, including EDs;the development or enhancement of a range of pathways to reduce unwarranted variation and improve focused activity in identified key areas, for example, in the identification of frailty and provision of early responsive treatment in the over 65 age range;running a range of pilot exercises in key areas to identify and share learning and gather data for evidence-based decision making;embedding key issues, such as frailty in those over 65 into staff training across professions, including within undergraduate and post graduate training;providing more access to support for people to receive their care in the place they call home, including palliative and end of life care;Clinical Advance Care Planning is being progressed in the short term by optimising the current use of Medical Care Plans in residential and nursing homes through providing additional assistance for GP practices; andwork continues with Digital Health and Care Northern Ireland to develop relevant improvement metrics and assessment tools on Encompass and support a consistent, system wide approach in key areas such as frailty.In addition to the above, to support improved flow and reduce ambulance handover delays, my Department expanded Hospital at Home, increased shortstay and intermediate care beds, enhanced frailty and multidisciplinary assessment at the front door, expanded Same Day Emergency Care, and strengthened early review and discharge co-ordination. I want to conclude by acknowledging that many of these actions rely on our dedicated staff across the HSC and wider care system and I want to take the opportunity to commend their ongoing commitment.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the roll-out of multidisciplinary teams in practices in North Down.
answer_text: The MDT rollout commenced in 2021 in the North Down Federation area. To date 11 GP Practices in the North Down Federation area have an element of MDT physiotherapy, mental health or social work staff in post.As per my Implementation Plan published July 2025, the North Down Federation are due to complete MDT rollout by March 2029, (Implementation Plan link provided below).  This will see all 12 GP Practices in the North Down Federation area with a full complement of MDT physiotherapy, mental health or social work staff in post.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for her assessment of the extent to which the Proposed Draft Budget for 2026–29/30 will enable delivery against the targets set out in the Living With Water in Belfast Plan.
answer_text: The Draft Budget 2026-29/30 is out for consultation, and this will be used to inform the Executive’s consideration and decisions on a Final Budget 2026-29/30. The proposed allocations for my Department, within the Draft Budget 2026-29/30, would go some way towards addressing the level of investment required for our infrastructure and for maintaining service delivery. However, difficult decisions would be required in prioritising capital schemes for deliveryProjects in the Living with Water in Belfast Plan will be taken forward at a scale and pace achievable within agreed available budgets.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 35542/22-27, to detail the number of staff who worked on the development of the new Road Maintenance Strategy.
answer_text: My Department has a dedicated core team of four officials responsible for the development and implementation of the new Road Maintenance Strategy. This core team is supported by several cross-functional working groups, which bring together expertise from different areas to address specific elements of the strategy. This collaborative approach ensures that the strategy is comprehensive, effective, and aligned with best practice.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for their assessment of the findings of the Ending Violence Against Women and Girls: Experiences and attitudes of adults in Northern Ireland in 2024 report.
answer_text: Behind every statistic are the lives and experiences of women and girls.The recently published report is valuable in understanding how women and girls experience violence, abuse and harm.Violence and the threat of violence, abuse and harm has a real and tangible impact on how women and girls live their daily lives. This report shows just how deeply it impacts their experiences and limits their ability to participate fully within school, work, sport, public life and online, as well as the negative impact on their health and wellbeing.  This is why ending violence against women and girls is an Executive priority.We have invested over five million pounds into communities, working with partners across society to raise awareness on violence against women and girls and equip everyone to take action to prevent it.  Violence against women and girls is unacceptable, and we will continue to do everything we can to stop it.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the expenditure by her Department on the (i) purchase; and (ii) supply, of salt and grit used for winter road maintenance, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: My Department purchases salt and grit which is used in supplying its winter service delivery. The expenditure on salt and grit usage for the past 5 financial years is detailed in the table below.2020-21£m2021-22£m2022-23£m2023-24£m2024-25£mSalt/grit Usage Expenditure3.12.33.63.03.7

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on his Department's plans for the site of the former Cottown Primary School, Bangor.
answer_text: The former Cotton Primary School is being retained by the Education Authority for potential alternative educational use for SEN provision.The review on its potential usage is currently ongoing.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether salt barns and stockpiles were at maximum capacity, in each DFI Roads Division, on 31 December 2025.
answer_text: As the delivery of Winter Service has been ongoing, the grit levels are constantly fluctuating with use and replenishment, so it would not be possible to give an accurate level of stocks held on this specific date.I can confirm that our salt reserves were filled to capacity over the summer months in preparation for the beginning of Winter Service, with additional re-orders available throughout the winter as required. Re-orders are set to 90% of full capacity to ensure stock levels are maintained and from 1st January onwards, salt stocks are maintained in a linear fashion against a minimum holding for each depot of 6 days’ supply for two actions per day. This policy was adhered to, and the supply of salt has been sufficient for the actions required over this recent period of adverse weather.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when he will answer AQW 28335/22-27.

To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the number of individuals who have been issued a taxi driver licence, without receiving an enhanced disclosure certificate from Access NI, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: In the past five years, no taxi driver licences have been issued without an Enhanced Disclosure Certificate (EDC) from Access NI. Applications for a taxi driver licence submitted without an EDC are cancelled, and no licence is granted.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for an update on the creation of the Destination Stewardship Plan, as part of her Department's Tourism Vision and Action Plan 10 Year Plan.
answer_text: The first phase of the Destination Stewardship Framework project is nearing completion, following a period of extensive stakeholder engagement and state of the industry research. A draft Destination Stewardship Framework has been produced, setting out the principles and proposed structures for success. 		The strategic approach was considered by Tourism NI’s Board in September. A further phase of engagement with Councils and other stakeholders is ongoing to discuss the proposed approach. Tourism NI are also considering the arrangements to select a Destination for the first focused Stewardship Plan.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure when she will answer AQW 35542/22-27.
answer_text: I answered AQW 35542/22-27 on 18 December 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail when she will answer AQW 35743/22-27.
answer_text: I answered AQW 35743/22-27 on 18 December 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail how the £13.2 million, provided for roads maintenance and safety improvements as part of the 2025-26 December Monitoring Round, will be allocated within DFI Roads divisions.
answer_text: As detailed in my recent press release, the £13.2m forms part of a larger allocation of over £30m towards road maintenance.Work is ongoing to allocate this funding which will look to address an additional programme of works, including resurfacing schemes, street lighting improvements, drainage works, road marking and signs.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the number of exemptions granted by AccessNI, for applicants unable to provide overseas criminal-record certificates for roles requiring enhanced disclosures, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: AccessNI provides criminal history information to help employers/voluntary organisations to make safer and more-informed recruitment decisions; the employer, in understanding the specific nature of the role, is best placed to make these decisions.  AccessNI has published guidance to assist employers in considering such matters, including the option of asking a job applicant from overseas to obtain a certificate of good conduct from an Embassy or High Commission.  It is not the role of AccessNI to decide on the relevancy of such information or whether it is appropriate to grant an exemption in relation to such matters.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on her engagement with the Minister of Justice, regarding the introduction of Caoimhé's Law.
answer_text: I am keen to voice support for the introduction of Caoimhè’s Law. I have agreed to attend a roundtable meeting, organised by Road Victim Support Northern Ireland-Donegal (RSVNID), that will include representatives from several departments, including the Department of Justice and other relevant policing and communications bodies.  A meeting date will be agreed in early 2026.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when he will answer AQW 30343/22-27.
To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the (i) current; and (ii) maximum, prison occupation at each prison site.
answer_text: On 12 December 2025 1,440 prisoners were being held in Maghaberry Prison, 505 in Magilligan Prison and 171 at Hydebank Wood.The actual capacity of each prison at any point in time varies dependent upon a range of factors including the residential accommodation open and in use, the size and configuration of cohorts within the population, appropriate use of cell sharing, and maintenance work, etc.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 33190/22-27, to detail (i) the total value of unrecovered costs; and (ii) any efforts being made to recover the outstanding costs.
answer_text: The total amount of unrecovered costs from 2018/19 to 2024/25 currently sits at £1,217k across Belfast, Northern, Southern and Western Trusts.South Eastern Trust were unable to confirm the unrecovered costs.Trusts pursue all debt owing to the Trust and follow their debt recovery process, seeking Business Services Organisation (BSO) Directorate of Legal Services (DLS) advice and support as required.  Recovery of this debt can be challenging as the individuals are non-ordinary residents and are in different legal jurisdictions.  In relation to debt owed by individuals residing in the Republic of Ireland (ROI), Trusts will engage with a legal team in ROI in relation to the pursuit and recovery of this debt. The Trust does not engage with European or international debt collection agencies, however in relation to international debt, the Home Office will be notified and individuals wishing to re-enter NI are refused entry until the debt is paid.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 33190/22-27, for his assessment of the causes of the increase in cost between 2023-24 and 2024-25.
answer_text: The costs associated with providing care to visitors is driven by activity. Where a Trust is in a border county, they tend to have a high number of visitors accessing services.  During the Covid period for a few years, activity dropped but is now on the rise again.Additionally, some Trusts have an Access to Healthcare team with more established processes and systems, particularly following the implementation of the Encompass electronic patient record system, which now helps identify these types of patients.Specifically, for Belfast Trust, 2024/25 costs include cost of renal transplants where patients began treatment in 2023/24 but finished in 2024/25.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the number of Penalty Charge Notices issued in Holywood, in each of the last ten years.
answer_text: The number of Penalty Charge Notices (PCNs) issued for on-street and off-street contraventions in Holywood, since 2018, are available at: https://www.opendatani.gov.uk/dataset/penalty-charge-notice-dataAs per the Department’s Retention and Disposal schedule, older PCN data is not retained.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 33190/22-27, whether the figures include costs of dental care.
answer_text: I can confirm that the costs included in the response to AQW 33190/22-27 did include the costs of dental care for Belfast Trust and Northern Trust. Western Trust and South Eastern did not have any costs of dental care to recover during this period.Southern Trust did not include the costs of dental care.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether his Department will consider subsidising the cost of bluetongue virus vaccines for farms within the Temporary Control Zone.
answer_text: My department has no plans at this stage to provide funding support for Bluetongue vaccine rollout in Northern Ireland at this time.  It should be noted that it is not mandatory to have your livestock vaccinated for BTV-3, it is a decision for the livestock keeper to make in conjunction with advice from their private veterinarian.  It should also be noted that BTV vaccines do not provide cross protection for other serotypes of the virus.  For further information, including what Bluetongue is and helpful information on BTV-3 vaccination please visit: https://www.daera-ni.gov.uk/articles/bluetongue
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when he will answer AQW 33190/22-27.
To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 26562/22-27, to detail (i) when the Road Safety Action Plan 2025-26 will be published; and (ii) the reasons for the delay in publication.
answer_text: The Road Safety Strategy itself is published and runs until 2030.  The associated action plan is in the final stages of development and is to be finalised shortly.  The development of the Action Plan involves cooperation and discussion across several departments and organisations, including the emergency services. Producing a high-level strategic plan takes time and I will be pleased to announce its publication in due course.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for her assessment of the effectiveness of the Substance Misuse Court.
answer_text: The Substance Misuse Court is an alternative approach that is used in helping people who have offended, when substance abuse is the underlying problem in their life. It aims to tackle the root causes of their criminal behaviour and provides them with the support, via intensive treatment programmes, to help them turn their lives around.Two evaluations of the Substance Misuse Court have shown positive outcomes, with many participants demonstrating significant reductions in ‘problem scores’ for drug and alcohol misuse.  A further evaluation is underway for completion by the end of March 2026. I fully endorse their use and effectiveness.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when he will answer AQW 33191/22-27.
To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 35543/22-27, to detail when this strategy is expected to be completed.
answer_text: I can advise that the strategy for improving accessibility across the rail network is due to be completed in Summer 2026.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 32268/22-27, to detail the rationale for his Department continuing to provide funding for Physician Assistant university places when there are no vacancies for Physician Assistants within each of the Health and Social Care Trusts.
answer_text: My Department’s commitment to supporting the Ulster University programme reflects the evidence that this profession, working alongside medics and other healthcare staff in multi-disciplinary teams, can have a significant role in stabilising and enhancing medical teams. My officials will continue to liaise with HSC Trusts to try to secure posts for the highly qualified and motivated individuals graduating from this programme.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when he will answer AQW 32625/22-27.
To ask the Minister of Justice to detail (i) the current percentage of the prison population on remand awaiting trial; and (ii) how this compares to the previous five years.
answer_text: The following table sets out the (i) percentage of the prison population being held on remand and awaiting trial as at midnight on 27th November 2025, (ii) together with figures for the same date in each of the previous five years.DateProportion of Prison Population on Remand/Awaiting Trial27 November 202036.1%27 November 202138.3%27 November 202237.5%27 November 202334.9%27 November 202437.1%27 November 202539.8%* Source: PRISM – Management InformationNote: Information is snapshot data, correct as at midnight on the dates provided.Individuals are classified as ‘Remand’ or ‘Awaiting Trial’ depending on what Court Tier (Magistrates or Crown) the holding charges are being heard in. When the Northern Ireland Prison Service refers to ‘Remand’ prisoners this usually combines those who are on ‘Remand’ and ‘Awaiting Trial’.An individual serving a custodial sentence, may also have had separate holding charges active in either a Magistrates and/or Crown Court. They are not classified on PRISM as ‘Awaiting Trail’ or ‘Remand’ due to serving a custodial sentence.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQO 2745/22-27, to detail any engagement his Department has had with Ulster University in relation to the reasons for the reduced number of applications.
answer_text: Staff at the university can only correspond with those applying for courses, and would have no means of identifying or corresponding with those who do not. Any reasoning for a lowering of applications can only, therefore, be speculative, but is likely to include a perception of poorer employment prospects across the UK and uncertainty following the publication of the Leng Report in England.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the cost of operating the Waiting List Reimbursement Scheme since 2 June 2025.
answer_text: From Scheme launch, 2 June 2025 to the present date, 24 November 2025, the current committal total is £4.02 million.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any plans his Department has to upgrade the Haematology Unit within Belfast City Hospital.
answer_text: On 10 December 2025, the Department of Health granted conditional approval to the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust for the expenditure associated with refurbishment of a level of the Belfast City Hospital Tower to create 32 exclusively single room en-suite accommodations (of which 12 will be isolation rooms).  This project will enable the permanent transfer of the specialist haematology inpatient service, which will utilise all 32 rooms and will represent an upgrade to the existing Unit.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the number of individuals that have applied for the electric vehicle cross pavement charging trial, broken down by constituency.
answer_text: On 30 September 2025 I announced two solutions for the electric vehicle cross pavement charging trial.  Since the trial’s launch, the department has received over 50 enquiries about the scheme.  Six applications have been received in relation to the channel solution, and six for the use of the cable cover. My department does not hold this information broken down by constituency.Officials continue to provide advice and guidance to electric vehicle owners interested in solutions to cross pavement EV charging as well as engaging with charge point operators and solution providers.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, pursuant to AQW 7002/22-27, to detail any progress his Department has made in addressing the legislative gap around Coastal Change Risk Management.
answer_text: The Coastal Forum, which I co-chaired with the Minister for Infrastructure, met on 19 November 2025 and considered progress that has been made on coastal management issues.  I am pleased to report there has been significant progress in addressing the evidence gaps that had previously hindered policy development and coastal management decisions.  However, the Coastal Forum agreed that it would not be feasible to develop policy proposals and introduce a Bill to the Assembly in this mandate. It is important the Coastal Forum continues preparatory work so that a Bill can be introduced early in the next mandate and the Coastal Forum working group has been asked to develop an indicative timetable for the development of policy proposals and introducing a Bill. As outlined in response to AQW 7002/22-27, Coastal Change Risk Management is not the responsibility of any Department.  The Executive will therefore be responsible for agreeing which Department will be responsible for this matter and the timetable for introducing legislation.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail when her officials first began work on a new Road Maintenance Strategy.
answer_text: I launched an ambitious draft road maintenance strategy for public consultation on 2nd December 2025. The draft strategy is aligned to the ‘Better Roads’ pillar within my seven foundations, and has been developed by a range of officials involved in the planning and delivery of road maintenance. Its focus will be on the delivery of high quality, targeted and sustainable road maintenance interventions using modern technology to direct our investments where they are needed most and ensuring smarter maintenance decisions.  The strategy will future proof our roads to meet the changing needs of our society including active travel, reducing the carbon footprint of our operations, and ensuring that the network is resilient after adverse weather events.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the Civil Service's spending on agency staff in 2024-2025.
answer_text: The total expenditure for all Departments on the 2021 Framework Agreement for agency workers from April 2024 to March 2025 was £135,326,352. Individual Departments are responsible for pay bill costs associated with agency workers deployment.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the number of vacancies within her Department, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: My Department is taking action to tackle long-standing staff resourcing challenges that have affected our capacity to deliver essential works and services. In collaboration with NICS HR, we are implementing a strategic approach to attempt to address these issues. For example, in this year alone, recruitment initiatives to fill critical vacancies have resulted in just over 200 successful appointments by the end of October 2025. NICS HR has also indicated that approximately 300 additional candidates are either in the process of being appointed or will receive offers to join my Department in the near future.NICS HR, part of the Department of Finance (DoF), are responsible for all NICS recruitment. When a department identifies a vacant post and where the funding for the post has been approved, the request is referred to the Department of Finance, NICS HR to initiate the recruitment process.The table below gives the number of vacancies within the Department for Infrastructure at the beginning of each of the last five financial years.Financial YearNumber of vacancies declared to NICSHR2021/224182022/233062023/242592024/253042025/26580

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the Translink Rail Halt Accessibility Assessment project.
answer_text: I can advise that Translink is undertaking a project to develop a strategy for improving accessibility across its rail network. This initiative will involve an assessment of accessibility features at all locations across the rail network. The project has included stakeholder engagement with Disabled People’s Organisations to ensure the work being undertaken reflects their needs and priorities. This project will help to inform future investment in the rail network, subject to the availability of future funding.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance for an update on the Back in Business rate relief scheme.
To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department will continue to provide funded Physician Assistant university places.
answer_text: My Department has consistently supported the two-year MSc in Physician Associate—now referred to as Physician Assistant—studies at Ulster University since the programme was first introduced in 2018. This support has been both financial and strategic. Each year, the Department funds tuition fees for up to 20 students per intake, covering the full two-year duration of the course. In addition, we provide financial assistance to Health and Social Care (HSC) Trusts and General Practitioners to enable them to deliver high-quality clinical placements, which are an essential component of the curriculum and ensure students gain the practical experience required for their future roles. Our commitment to this programme is grounded in evidence demonstrating that Physician Assistants, working collaboratively with medical professionals, can make a significant contribution to stabilising clinical teams and improving overall service delivery. By integrating Physician Assistants into multidisciplinary teams, we aim to enhance efficiency, reduce pressure on doctors, and ultimately improve patient outcomes. Recently, Ulster University informed my officials that, due to a reduced number of applications for the 2026 intake, the start date for the academic year will be deferred from January to September 2026. While this adjustment addresses immediate recruitment challenges, decisions regarding subsequent years remain under consideration. These decisions will be informed by two key pieces of work: Professor Gillian Leng’s review of the Physician Assistant role in England and my Department’s own evaluation of the role within the local HSC system. I expect to receive advice on these matters shortly, which will guide the next steps in shaping this important workforce initiative.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any outstanding repairs for storm damage within Cairn Wood.
answer_text: Forest Service has some tree clearance works to complete within Ballysallagh forest resulting from wind damage caused by Storm Éowyn. The trees still to be cleared are not directly impacting on the recreational path network, which has been re-opened by Ards and North Down Borough Council under the management agreement in place.  Replanting in the area adjacent to the Ballysallagh reservoir is planned for spring 2026.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any support his Department is providing to veterinary businesses in relation to the supply of veterinary medicines from Great Britain after the end of the grace period on 31 December 2025.
answer_text: In line with the Windsor Framework (Implementation) Regulations 2024, the supply of veterinary medicines to Northern Ireland (NI), including support to veterinary businesses after the end of the grace period on 31 December 2025, falls under the Direction and Control of the Secretary of State for Environment, Food and Rural Affairs (Defra).I will continue to engage with the UK Government on the need to ensure the continued supply of veterinary medicines to NI beyond the end of the grace period on 31 December 2025.I welcome the UK Government’s publication of the Protecting Animal Health policy in June 2025, and its clear commitment to supporting industry as it adapts to the new veterinary medicines legislation. These changes will require adjustments from Marketing Authorisation Holders and other stakeholders operating in NI. Encouragingly, UK Government data indicates that the majority of Marketing Authorisation Holders intend to continue supplying most of the veterinary medicinal products currently available in the NI market.Looking ahead, I will maintain close engagement with both the UK Government and the Veterinary Medicines Working Group to help safeguard the continued supply of veterinary medicines to NI.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 33517/22-27 to detail the proposed location for the South Eastern Mental Health Unit.
answer_text: The proposed location for the South Eastern Mental Health Unit in the outline Business Case is at the Ulster Hospital site, Dundonald. However, work is still progressing with the Trust and the commissioners about different aspects of the Business Case including bed numbers and future-proofing. Given the current financial position it has not been possible to allocate funding in 25/26 due to the existing scale of capital budget requirements, as noted in AQW 33517/22-27. The draft Budget Review outcome when received, will inform the Capital Investment Plan. This will set out the specific projects to be taken forward over the next ten years.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the status of the current Local Development Plans, broken down by local council area.
answer_text: Councils set their own timetables for the preparation of their Local Development Plans, taking into consideration their own resourcing, evidence gathering processes and governance structures, and these are forwarded to my Department for agreement.To date nine out of the eleven councils have published development plan documents (Fermanagh & Omagh District Council, Belfast City Council, Mid & East Antrim Borough Council, Lisburn & Castlereagh City Council, Derry City & Strabane District Council, Antrim & Newtownabbey Borough Council, Mid Ulster District Council, Newry, Mourne & Down District Council, Ards & North Down Borough Council). Six councils have adopted Plan Strategies (PS) and are working towards their Local Policies Plan (LPP) publication. These are Fermanagh & Omagh District Council, Belfast City Council, Mid & East Antrim Borough Council, Lisburn & Castlereagh City Council, Derry City & Strabane District Council, Antrim & Newtownabbey Borough Council. Council timetables indicate that the remaining two councils (Armagh, Banbridge & Craigavon Borough Council & Causeway Coast & Glens Borough Council) are due to publish the first of their development plan documents next year.My officials continue to engage and support those councils as they work towards their draft PS and draft LPP publication. question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the number of Invest NI staff involved in developing the review of Invest NI Investments report, published on 16th October 2025.
answer_text: A total of 18 Invest NI staff members contributed to the development of the Review of Investments report, published on 16 October 2025. These individuals were not permanently assigned to the report but provided input based on their subject matter expertise.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the cost of developing the review of Invest NI investments report, published on 16th October 2025.
answer_text: A total of 18 Invest NI staff members contributed to the development of the Review of Investments report, published on 16 October 2025. These individuals were not permanently assigned to the report but provided input based on their subject matter expertise. The costs incurred were primarily internal staff costs, supplemented by some external legal expenses. Invest NI does not routinely record the costs associated with individual activities. Therefore, it is not possible to provide a specific costing for the development of this report.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for an update on the actions her Department is taking forward, following the conclusion of the joint public consultation with the Department for Communities on anti-social behaviour.
answer_text: As noted in my previous response to AQW 28077/22-27 in June 2025, my Department, together with the Department for Communities (DfC), concluded a joint consultation on Anti-Social Behaviour which identified strong support for updating the legislative framework to address issues such as drinking in public. As this is likely to be a cross-cutting issue between my Department and DfC, engagement between officials in both Departments has continued, with DfC officials also engaging with local councils on this issue. I am also considering recommendations arising from a report on the Anti-Social Behaviour Order, with any potential amendment to the legislation on either or both of these issues being subject to Executive and Assembly approval. As previously outlined in AQW 28077/22-27, it is anticipated that no legislative change will occur until the next Assembly mandate.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the 14 capital healthcare projects currently paused due to a lack of funding.
answer_text: The projects have not been paused. At the start of the 2025-26 financial year there were 16 projects that were classified as being partially committed due to the progress that they had made through the approvals process.Funding has subsequently been allocated to 4 of these projects in the 2025/26 financial year; these are the Haematology Unit in the Belfast Health & Social Care Trust, Antrim Area Hospital Electrical Infrastructure Project, Cushendall Fire Station and Fintona Fire Station.It has not been possible to allocate funding to the remaining Partially Committed Projects in 2025/26 because of the scale of their capital budget requirements in future years and uncertainty in respect of the level of funding available.  These projects are as follows:Altnagelvin Paediatrics Newry Community Care and Treatment Centre Cityside Health and Care Centre Regional Radio-pharmacy FacilityGlenmona Separated Minors Unit (SMU)Imaging AcademyMother and Baby Unit (Peri-Natal Mental Health)Craigavon Area Hospital Redevelopment - Initial PhaseAntrim Area Hospital Mental Health Inpatient Unit South Eastern Mental Health UnitCentre for Mental Health, OmaghAltnagelvin 5.2 Emergency DepartmentThe ability of the Department of Health to commit funding for these projects for the duration of the investment, will depend on the Department’s capital allocation from the Budget process and the identification of sufficient additional recurrent funding to meet any additional Resource DEL costs associated with the capital projects. Following the announcement of the outcome of the UK wide 2025 Spending Review in June, the Department of Finance has commissioned a Budget exercise that will determine the capital budgets for Northern Ireland Department’s for the years 2026-27 to 2029-30.  The draft Budget outcome is not expected to be confirmed until later this year and will inform the development of the Department of Health capital investment plan which will set out the projects to be taken forward over the next ten years.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the projects to be progressed in 2025-26 as part of the Living With Water in Belfast Plan.
answer_text: Projects being progressed by NI Water during the 2025-26 financial year include:£7m Upper Falls Storm Storage Project in West Belfast which will provide capacity to facilitate the connection of 1,200 homes;£0.9m project at Park Manor in Mallusk, that will resolve two Unsatisfactory Intermittent Discharges (UIDs) and will release capacity in the sewer network for 450 housing units;£0.5m project at Shore Road, Whiteabbey to resolve one UID and remove 1.3 hectares of impermeable area that is currently draining into its network; work in the Carrickfergus, Greenisland and Seahill catchments to reduce spills to Belfast Lough from sewerage infrastructure; andcontinued development of the Catchment Delivery Plan for the drainage area that is served by the Whitehouse Wastewater Treatment Works to inform future investments for NI Water as part of the PC21 and PC28 periods.In addition, my Department continues to take forward a flood alleviation feasibility study along the Knock and Loop rivers in south and east Belfast.  As part of this feasibility study, my officials are working in partnership with the National Trust to take forward a Natural Flood Management pilot scheme in its Lisnabreeny and Cregagh Glen property.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for an update on the potential changes to Access NI to allow disclosure certificates to be used across multiple organisations.
answer_text: The answer to AQW 28339/22-27 refers.  The Department considers that utilising updated data fields on the recently enhanced Police National Database (PND) is key to the development of necessary functionality that will enable AccessNI disclosure certificates to be continuously monitored, and thereby portable across defined sectors.  Work has commenced with Home Office, with the aim of onboarding AccessNI to PND by spring 2026.  However, the PND is a large and complex programme with many conflicting priorities and progress on the necessary technical adjustments will largely be dependent upon the availability of Home Office resources to engage and deliver the necessary changes within the PND infrastructure.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the multi-storey car park works ongoing at the Ulster Hospital.
answer_text: A programme of works at the Ulster Hospital multi-storey car park, including steelwork painting, resurfacing, parking bay delineation and the installation of new lighting, commenced in February 2024.  The project is expected to be completed by May 2026.The projected cost of the multi-storey carpark project is £1.3million which is being funded from the South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust’s general capital allocation.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the locations that will be part of the electric vehicle cross pavement charging trial.
answer_text: I can confirm that this trial is available to all EV owners throughout the North who are without access to a driveway. The trial will include ongoing monitoring and evaluation to inform a permanent way forward for both solutions, and is open to applicants by contacting DfI Transport Decarbonisation unit via email to TDU@infrastructure-ni.gov.uk.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail what support his Department provides to patients travelling to England to receive CAR T-cell therapy.
answer_text: Patients from Northern Ireland have access to CAR-T treatments as per circular “HSC(SQSD) 12-22 – NICE Technology Appraisals – Process for Endorsement, Implementation, Monitoring and Assurance in NI” which supports the timely introduction of effective new medicines.If CAR-T treatments are approved by NICE then patients in NI will be granted access to them on an equitable basis with other nations through the arrangements in place for referral to trusted centres in the wider NHS.As patient’s receiving CAR-T treatment currently have to travel outside NI, financial support is provided by my Departments ECR team in line with the current policy which includes:Pay approved treatment costs direct to the provider.Book flights or ferries.Reimburse accommodation expenses up to a maximum allowance. In exceptional circumstances accommodation may be booked on behalf of the patient.Provide a subsistence allowance for meals.Provide assistance towards other transport expenses.The full policy is available at the link below.Transfer for treatment by the Strategic Planning and Performance Group - DOH/HSCNI Strategic Planning and Performance Group (SPPG)Access to the Air Ambulance for transfer to the treating centre may also offered where this is deemed clinically necessary.  This is determined on the basis of advice from the patient’s referring consultant and their recommendation in respect of clinical need.

answer_text: Patients from Northern Ireland have access to CAR-T treatments as per circular “HSC(SQSD) 12-22 – NICE Technology Appraisals – Process for Endorsement, Implementation, Monitoring and Assurance in NI” which supports the timely introduction of effective new medicines.If CAR-T treatments are approved by NICE then patients in NI will be granted access to them on an equitable basis with other nations through the arrangements in place for referral to trusted centres in the wider NHS.As patient’s receiving CAR-T treatment currently have to travel outside NI, financial support is provided by my Departments ECR team in line with the current policy which includes:Pay approved treatment costs direct to the provider.Book flights or ferries.Reimburse accommodation expenses up to a maximum allowance. In exceptional circumstances accommodation may be booked on behalf of the patient.Provide a subsistence allowance for meals.Provide assistance towards other transport expenses.The full policy is available at the link below.Transfer for treatment by the Strategic Planning and Performance Group - DOH/HSCNI Strategic Planning and Performance Group (SPPG)Access to the Air Ambulance for transfer to the treating centre may also offered where this is deemed clinically necessary.  This is determined on the basis of advice from the patient’s referring consultant and their recommendation in respect of clinical need.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any action his Department has taken following publication of the Leng review.
answer_text: The Leng review makes a number of recommendations across many aspects of the Physician Associate role. My officials are currently considering this report, in conjunction with my Department’s own review into the PA role locally, to ensure that the HSC in Northern Ireland is supported to make the best and safest use of an appropriate skills mix across all clinical settings. Some of the recommendations in this review will take time to implement, and will require close collaborative working with all stakeholders. I can, however, confirm that my Department has adopted the proposed name change to “Physician Assistant” with immediate effect.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any plans his Department has to incorporate outdoor learning into the school curriculum.
answer_text: In December 2024, I was pleased to announce my Curriculum-led Capital Investment Programme with a specific focus on Physical Education. Following this, the Outdoor Learning Project launched in early 2025, which provided funding for outdoor play equipment to all nursery (93), primary (776) and special (40) schools. The Education Authority (EA) also supports the inclusion of outdoor play and outdoor learning through a range of strategic initiatives, providing professional learning opportunities and providing direct advice and guidance to individual schools and practitioners.As part of this provision to develop and support outdoor learning, the EA makes a range of services available to schools and teachers through the Magilligan Field Centre, with programmes catering for learners from pre-school to post-16. The Magilligan Field Centre also contributes to the EA’s overall teacher professional learning programme and recently collaborated with Apple Education, Academia, and C2k to deliver three major outdoor learning Teacher Professional Learning events showcasing the use of digital tools to enhance outdoor learning experiences. Further support for schools and teachers wishing to develop the use of outdoor learning is available through Professional Learning resources hosted on the EA’s Supporting Learning website. These resources provide practical guidance and exemplars for educators seeking to embed outdoor approaches within their teaching practice.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice, pursuant to AQW 30572/22-27, to detail the number of individuals to have served a custodial sentence as a result of breaching their Enhanced Combination Order.
answer_text: AQW 30572/22-27 outlined that Courts may deal with breaches of Enhanced Combination Orders by permitting the individual to re-engage with the requirements of their Order; and the Court may or may not convict the person of a breach of the Order. Of those who have been convicted of an offence relating to a breach of an Enhanced Combination Order in the period 2018 – 2024, an immediate custodial sentence was imposed in 5 cases and a suspended custodial sentence in a further 63 cases.Note:Figures relate to cases where there was a conviction for at least one offence of breach of combination order, where that related to an Enhanced Combination Order. Figures relate to cases where the main disposal imposed was an immediate custodial sentence or a suspended custodial sentence.

To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on her Department's consultation on biennial MOT testing.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail plans his Department has to introduce a 24-7 thrombectomy service.
answer_text: The current thrombectomy service operates from 8am-5pm. It is estimated that just over £5 million recurrent funding is required to expand to a 24/7 service.Following approval of the final budget for 2025/26, my Department was left with a funding gap of over £600 million.  In this context, and while I remain committed to the establishment of 24/7 thrombectomy service, I am currently unable to allocate the required additional funding to support a 24/7 service.In the interim I am committed to ensuring that as many people as possible benefit from the service within the current hours of operation. To this end, it should be noted that the thrombectomy rate in Northern Ireland in 2024/25 was 5.2%, higher than the regional rates in England, Scotland and Wales.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the number of individuals who have participated in the Substance Misuse Court, who have subsequently been convicted of a drug-related offence, since 2018.
answer_text: Figures held by the Department indicate that, from 2018, there were 276 people who participated in the Substance Misuse Court and who had a case completed by end of 2024.  However, of these, 117 went on to be convicted of a further drugs offence.  Of these 117, 78 had been removed from the Substance Misuse Court programme during their period of involvement and 39 had completed, or partially completed, the programme.Note:People who participated in the Substance Misuse Court have been defined as those who completed or partially completed the SMC programme.  This number includes those who engaged with the programme for a protracted period and who received significant intervention but were removed prior to completion.  Those who were referred but deemed not suitable at screening or through non-attendance have been excluded.Figures relate to initial conviction at court. Appeals are not included.Figures for those convicted of a subsequent drugs offence relate to cases where there was a conviction for a drugs offence up to the end of 2024, where the defendant had participated in Substance Misuse Court in a previous case.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the (i) average; and (ii) longest waiting time for an MOT appointment, broken down by test centre.
answer_text: The average waiting time for a full MOT test appointment for a private car across the Driver and Vehicle Agency’s (DVA) network of test centres, from 1 April to 23 September 2025, was 33 days.  This compares to the business plan target for 2025–26 of five weeks, or 35 days, or less, and represents a significant improvement from around 100 days in May 2024.The average waiting time for an MOT at each test centre from 1 April 2025 to 23 September 2025 for all full tests booked during that period is set out in the table below:  Test CentreAverage Waiting time (days)Armagh		37Ballymena35Balmoral39Coleraine25Cookstown35Craigavon36Downpatrick39Enniskillen38Larne32Lisburn38Mallusk33Newbuildings18Newry41Newtownards27Omagh35These average waiting times represent unvalidated management information taken from the DVA’s booking system on 24 September 2025. Waiting times regularly fluctuate based on customer demand and the availability of staff resources.The DVA has confirmed that MOT appointments are currently available across its network of test centres for the forthcoming three-month period.  Around 30,000 vehicles have booked tests up to 16 December 2025.  However, as customers are not required to book the earliest available slot, some may choose a later appointment that may be more convenient for them.The DVA publish monthly provisional headline statistics in the publication ‘Driver and Vehicle Agency Monthly Tests Conducted Statistics’ which are available at Driver and Vehicle Agency Monthly Tests Conducted Statistics | Department for Infrastructure. Table 1.3 provides a breakdown of the number of vehicle tests conducted by test centre, while Table 1.4 details the number of full test applications received, broken down by month.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on his Department’s phone-free pilot in nine schools.
answer_text: The mobile phone-free pilot to trial the use of magnetic pouches that securely lock pupils’ phones during the school day is up and running across a number of the pilot schools.  Training has been provided, unlocking points have been installed, and pouches have been distributed to pupils and are in use. We are in the early stages of the pilot but so far feedback has been positive. I was delighted to attend St Ronan’s College last week to see for myself how the pilot is working on the ground and I am pleased to report that, so far, it is rolling out successfully. Pupils and staff shared their experiences and their views with me on this innovative pilot which gives the schools involved the opportunity to test this particular approach and participate in an evaluation which will inform future policy decisions in this area. I would like to put on record my sincere thanks to the schools involved in the pilot, firstly for stepping forward, taking the lead and volunteering to implement this initiative, and for the work they have done to date to prepare their school communities, ensuring staff, pupils and parents are well informed and on board, and enabling this pilot to launch successfully.Five of the nine schools have begun using the pouches, and there are a further four remaining schools who needed a little more lead in time and who are planning to start in the next few weeks. The five schools who have commenced the pilot are:Friends School, LisburnGlenola Collegiate, BangorSt Fanchea’s College, EnniskillenSt Genevieve’s HS, BelfastSt Ronan’s College, LurganThe four schools that will commence in the coming weeks are:Hazelwood Integrated College, NewtownabbeyLarne High School Markethill High School, ArmaghSt Joseph’s Boys’, Londonderry The mobile-phone free pilot will be evaluated by an independent research team from King’s College London.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the amount spent on agency staff by the South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: Trust expenditure on agency staff has been incurred to ensure that safe and effective services are sustained and contributes importantly to maintaining service provision to patients and clients. HSC Trusts employ locum staff for several reasons, for example, cover for sickness and maternity/paternity leave; cover for existing vacancies; and when demand increases over the winter months.The Department monitors Health and Social Care Trusts’ expenditure on agency and bank staff on a quarterly basis for specific staffing categories, across financial years broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.  Expenditure on Agency staff by the South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust, for the last five years is set out below. A Regional Agency Reduction Implementation and Planning Group has been established to co-ordinate the improvement of staff utilisation, with the aim of reducing unsustainable agency and locum spend. The main function of the Group is to develop and implement a plan, with local and regional commitment and coordination, to safely facilitate, and manage the service impact of, reducing unsustainable agency and locum spend.Significant progress has already been made across HSC as a whole since the implementation of the new agency frameworks with expenditure of ‘Off-Framework’ agency use falling from £134m in 22/23 to £19m in 24/25 across Nursing & Midwifery and Social Workers. For these two staff groups, the continued progress being made has yielded an overall reduction in agency expenditure in 2024/25 compared to the base year (2022/23), of £34m. Alongside this work our strategic approach to workforce development, supported by sustained increased investment in pre-registration education and training, has facilitated a 17.7% (+10,063) increase in whole time equivalent (wte) staff in post across the HSC in Northern Ireland between March 2018 (56,802) and March 2025 (66,865).   This includes a 24.2% (+1,018 wte) increase in medical and dental staff, a 19.4% (+2,938 wte) increase in registered nursing and midwifery staff and a 24.9% (+2,026 wte) increase in professional and technical staff in post.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for an update on the Criminal Justice Inspection Northern Ireland's inspection of the PSNI's use of Community Resolution Notices.
answer_text: I have recently responded to a request from the Chief Inspector of Criminal Justice regarding the inspection report on the PSNI’s use of Community Resolution Notices (CRNs) to indicate that I am content for the report to be published.  I anticipate it will be available shortly.Since they were introduced in 2016, CRNs have become one of the most commonly used out of court disposals to divert cases of lower-level offending away from prosecution through the courts and I welcome the report by Criminal Justice Inspection Northern Ireland (CJINI) to help strengthen the existing measures which were expanded by PSNI in 2024.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail those offences which are eligible for a Community Resolution Notice.
answer_text: Community Resolution Notices (CRNs) were introduced by the Police Service of Northern Ireland (PSNI) in 2016 to divert suitable cases involving low-level offending away from prosecution through the courts.  The CRN arrangements, including the offences for which a CRN may be issued, are a matter for the Chief Constable of the PSNI.   PSNI has, however, provided the Department with details of the offences which are outlined below.  It is important to note that not all cases involving these offences will be suitable to be disposed through a CRN, and PSNI officers will assess each case on its merits subject to additional operational guidance.Offences for which a CRN may be issued: -Allowing an animal to wanderAttempted abstracting electricity (magnet on meter etc.)Arson - up to £300 (excluding endangering life Articles 3 (1) & (3) Criminal Damage (NI) Order 1977)Assault, resist or obstruct policeBegging Burglary non-dwellingBehaviour likely to cause a breach of the peace.Common assaultConsume or purchase intoxicating liquor in a place other than a private house when under 18Counterfeit Currency – up to £100Criminal Damage – up to £300Dishonestly using electricity - up to £300Disorderly behaviour (incl. on licensed premises)Drunk in a public place.Fireworks offencesFraud (Section 1 - fraud, Section 2 - fraud by false rep, Section 6 - possession of articles for use in fraud and Section 11 - obtaining services dishonestly) – up to £300 involving one transaction only.Going Equipped for theftHandling Stolen GoodsHarassment provided - course of conduct proved Indecent behaviour – limited to street urination, ‘mooning’ and ‘streaking’ provided no intent to cause alarm or distress Liquor licensing / licensed premises offences –Consuming Intoxicating liquor while a Minor, Purchasing intoxicating liquor for a Minor, Selling intoxicating liquor outside hours, Minor in Licensed Premises representing himself as 18 years of ageMaking Off Without Payment - up to £300 (excluding ‘drive offs’)Possession of Drugs (only the named drugs and quantity limits shown are suitable)Class ADMT – N, N-Dimethyltryptamine – 5gCocaine – 5g 34 Methylenedioxymethamphetamine (MDMA powder) – 5g  34 Methylenedioxymethamphetamine (MDMA tablets/Ecstasy) – 10 tablets Methamphetamine – 5gLysergic acid diethylamide (LSD) – 10 dosesMagic mushrooms – 5gClass BCannabis – 30g – All types THC Edibles – 1 pacet to a maximum of 30gTHC Vape – 5 vialsButane hash oil/shatter/budder other similar derivative – 5gCannabis cultivation – 5 plantsKhat – 28gKetamine – 7gAmphetamine – 7g Mephedrone – 7gClass CDiazepam (or other benzodiazepine) – 28 tablets Alprazolam (Xanax) – 28 tablets Pregabalin/Gabapentin – 28 tablets Zopiclone – 28 tabletsSending of false communications Section 179 of The Online Safety Act 2023Sending letters etc. with intent to cause distress or anxiety - Article 3 Malicious Communications (Northern Ireland) Order 1988 Threatening Communications - Section 181 of The Online Safety Ac

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail if Road Safety is included in school curriculum.
answer_text: The curriculum provides a range of opportunities for schools to highlight road safety awareness. At primary level for example, the Personal Development and Mutual Understanding (PDMU) Area of Learning requires teachers to enable pupils to develop knowledge, understanding and skills in keeping themselves healthy and safe.  At post-primary level, the Learning for Life and Work (LLW) Area of Learning requires that pupils should have opportunities to “develop preventative strategies in relation to accidents in the home, school and on the road”. Other topics include responsible road use, peer influence and risk-taking behavioursAdditionally, the Education Authority (EA) encourages schools to follow the Department for Infrastructure (DfI) road safety guidance which provide a range of educational road safety resources which are available to both primary and post-primary schools.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the new technologies that will be part of her Department's new Road Maintenance Strategy.
answer_text: The new Road Maintenance Strategy, which is in development, will include the use of the latest technology to establish the condition of the public road network and allow resources to be targeted for most efficient and effective use.The new Road Maintenance Strategy aims to deliver a targeted programme of intelligent maintenance investment guided by digitally supported procedures to inform decision making.The new technology will also allow my department to gauge the impact of investment in road maintenance. Details of the technology to be employed will be available when the new Road Maintenance Strategy is published.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any engagement her Department has had with Artemis Technologies, in relation to (i) promotion of the proposed Belfast to Bangor ferry route; and (ii) a timescale for its introduction.
answer_text: My Department has met Artemis Technologies on its proposed Belfast to Bangor ferry route on a number of occasions, the most recent meeting being May 2024. Artemis Technologies would be best placed to provide you with an update on any proposed timescales for the introduction of this service.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the hotel occupancy rates in North Down, for each of the last five years.
answer_text: Data released by the Northern Ireland Statistics & Research Agency on hotel occupancy for Ards & North Down Local Government District indicate: Hotel room and bed occupancy was 43% and 35% respectively for January-April 2025, the most up-to-date data available for the area.  Full year (January-December) 2024 data for Ards & North Down Local Government District show hotel room occupancy of 53% and bed occupancy of 46%.Northern Ireland Statistics & Research Agency hotel occupancy statistics are not available for Ards & North Down Local Government for the previous four years (2020-2023) due to sample sizes being too small to provide reliable estimates.

answer_text: Translink are developing a business case which is expected to be submitted to my Department by Spring 2026.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail whether the Bangor Flood Alleviation Scheme feasibility study has commenced.
answer_text: I can confirm that my Department has commissioned a feasibility study to assess the river and tidal flood risk to properties within the Bangor Area of Potential Significant Flood Risk. This work will identify if there are any economically viable schemes to reduce the impacts of river and/or coastal flooding in the area. Ongoing work includes information gathering, including completing a topographical survey, for the study area. It is currently anticipated that the feasibility study should be complete in late 2026.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for an update on her Department's consultation on the proposal to increase the existing maximum sentences for the offence of causing or allowing a child or vulnerable adult to die or suffer serious physical harm.
answer_text: My officials are currently considering the responses to the consultation on theproposal to increase the existing maximum sentences for the offence of causing or allowing a child or vulnerable adult to die or suffer serious physical harm.The consultation opened on 19 June 2025 on NI Direct, Citizen Space and closed after eight weeks on 13 August 2025. question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail whether her Department is considering any changes to its policy on the use of e-scooters.
answer_text: It is currently illegal to use e-scooters on public roads and public spaces here and I have noplans to change that position. Anyone using an e-scooter illegally may be subject toenforcement action by the PSNI.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail any contingency plans her Department has in place, should the occupational capacity be reached at both HMP Maghaberry and HMP Magiligan.
answer_text: The Prison Service remains vigilant to changes in the prison population and the action required in response.The contingency arrangements that may apply if Maghaberry and Magilligan reached capacity are dependent upon context, timing, resources and operational decision making etc.I do not intend to detail those arrangements in the hypothetical, or in a way that might undermine sensitive operational information.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for an update on the Ards and North Down Local Economic Partnership.
answer_text: Ards and North Down Local Economic Partnership is progressing well. The Partnership held a workshop on 16th September, during which the Partnership identified potential projects that will help shape the Action Plan. I am meeting with Ards and North Down Council this week to hear more about the Partnership, and I look forward to seeing a well-developed Action Plan over the coming months.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the percentage number of prisoners sharing cells at each prison site.
answer_text: A partnership approach is central to addressing crime, including agricultural crime, in all regions. The Department of Justice chairs the Rural Crime Partnership (RCP) with members including the Police Service of Northern Ireland (PSNI), the Ulster Farmers’ Union, NFU Mutual, the Young Farmers’ Club of Ulster, the Federation of Small Businesses, the Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (DAERA) and Policing and Community Safety Partnerships (PCSPs). The RCP shares information on emerging and/or re-occurring crimes, to support a coordinated response and preventative approach to addressing rural crime. RCP members regularly engage with the public via social media to raise awareness of rural crime issues and to encourage rural communities to report such crimes. My Department recently participated in Rural Crime Week NI, a local media campaign led by the PSNI, which provided an opportunity to raise awareness of the diverse issues faced in our rural communities and help provide information and advice to people who may need or want support.At a local level, PCSPs, which are funded jointly by my Department and the Northern Ireland Policing Board, play a key role in addressing crime, including agricultural crime, and building confidence locally, through engagement and consultation with communities, including rural areas. PCSPs play a pivotal role in supporting communities in identifying solutions to issues of local concern.  They also deliver a range of initiatives to raise awareness and support rural communities and businesses to protect their property and goods.  These initiatives include trailer/equipment marking events and such actions have helped to continue the downward trend in recorded rural and agricultural crime.There are also existing robust mechanisms in place for law enforcement agencies to work collaboratively through the Joint Agency Task Force (JAFT).  Tackling rural crime remains one of the six key priorities of the JATF. The JATF is a highly effective model for tackling cross-border organised crime, with strong practical and strategic collaboration between law enforcement agencies, including PSNI and An Garda Síochána. Notwithstanding the above, the operational detection and investigation of agricultural crime, including how resources are deployed, is an operational matter for the Chief Constable, who is accountable to the Northern Ireland Policing Board.  I am committed to respecting the operational independence of the Chief Constable and the role of the NI Policing Board, and you may therefore wish to also direct your question to the PSNI.
