question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any action her Department is taking to improve the availability of wheelchair accessible vehicles for those seeking to access higher education.
answer_text: Having an inclusive transport network improves access to goods and services for many in our community including those are going through higher education.My Department funds Translink which works to ensure services and facilities are accessible to all. This means creating a consistent customer experience for everyone, ensuring accessible public transport for people with all levels of abilities.The public transport fleet is fully compliant with accessibility regulations.  However, I recognise that higher standards of accessibility are needed and Translink is continuing to improve the levels of accessibility where possible on its vehicles and also within stations.My Department also provides funding to support the demand responsive rural Dial-a-Lift and urban Disability Action Transport Scheme transport services.  Those providing these services take account of the specific needs of their members when allocating vehicles to support journeys.I announced in March of this year a phased review of taxi policy and legislation, commencing with a review of Class C Taxi Licensing. In the second phase of the review of taxi policy and legislation, access to wheelchair accessible vehicles will be reviewed.  I recognise this is another significant and important issue affecting those who need to use this service.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail whether her Department is considering an additional tranche of the part-time 20mph speed limit zones at schools.
answer_text: Road safety is a high priority for my Department and I am committed to working proactively to make our roads safer for everyone. I am acutely aware that children and young people are amongst the most vulnerable groups using our roads.My Department is currently taking forward a third tranche of part-time 20mph zones at 40 schools across the North and are listed on my Department’s website, link; 20mph speed limit at schools - Tranche 3 | Department for Infrastructure.  This third tranche focuses on rural schools that are located on roads where the national speed limit applies while considering the safety implications of introducing lower speed limits.  This will bring the total delivered across the North since 2020/21 to 273 schools.This initiative is a significant intervention that will help to ensure that children, parents and staff feel safer as they travel to and from school on a daily basis.My Department is also currently carrying out a review on a number of speed related matters including 20mph speed limits in general and I plan to undertake a public consultation exercise as part of this review.  Further consideration on the use of part time speed limits at schools will be taken on completion of the consultation.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail (i) the number of letters issued by her Department to sub-postmasters and sub-postmistresses, in relation to the quashing of convictions in the Horizon scandal; and (ii) the number of letters still to be issued.
answer_text: To date, my Department has issued letters to 22 individuals whose convictions have been quashed by the Post Office (Horizon System) Offences Act 2024 (‘the Act’).Currently, the Department has zero letters still to be issued.  I would encourage anyone who believes that their convictions may be within scope of the Act to contact my department, so that I can be assured that all relevant convictions have been identified.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any aspects of the UK Government's new Defence Industrial Strategy 2025 that will involve North Down.
answer_text: The British Government’s Ministry of Defence has the primary responsibility for delivering the Defence Industrial Strategy. Requests for further information regarding Strategy should therefore be forwarded directly to the Ministry of Defence.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the number of jobs created by foreign direct investment during 2024-25, broken down by local council area.
answer_text: Invest NI proactively publishes a full breakdown of support offered to businesses over the past 5 years on the NI Open Data online portal. The portal contains the following information which will facilitate access to the data requested:	Portal | Invest NI Total Tables 1 April 2020 and 31 March 2025	Provides a complete picture of Invest NI support through summary tables that aggregate both published and non-published data (indirect support, like advice and guidance, and offers that cannot be released due to data protection issues). This information is broken down into several different variables, including council and constituency areas.	Portal | Invest NI Financial Offers of Assistance (Detail)	Provides detail on Individual offers of direct financial assistance issued by Invest NI to businesses between 1 April 2020 and 31 March 2025. Does not include indirect support, like advice and guidance, or offers that cannot be released due to data protection issues.	Portal | Invest NI Open Data Guidance	Provides guidance on the data contained in 1 and 2 above.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on her Department's consultation on Developer Contributions for Wastewater Infrastructure.
answer_text: On 21 March I launched a public consultation on developer contributions for wastewater infrastructure.  The consultation ran for 14 weeks, closing on 27 June 2025, and sought views on the possible options for a pathway to introduce developer contributions - either on a voluntary or compulsory basis, which may help to fund wastewater upgrades to unlock capacity going forward.  293 responses to the consultation were received.  These responses are currently being considered, with a view to publishing a Consultation Responses Report in the coming weeks.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the (i) maximum; and (ii) minimum, sentences delivered for death by dangerous driving, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: The offence of ‘causing death by dangerous driving’ is indictable only and the maximum penalty is fourteen years imprisonment.  I plan to increase the maximum penalties for the offences of causing death by driving in my upcoming Sentencing Bill. This will include up to 20 years imprisonment, unless the perpetrator has previously been convicted of a similar offence, in which case they will be eligible for a maximum custodial sentence of discretionary life.In the period 2020 to 2024, there were 17 convictions at courts for the offence of ‘causing death by dangerous driving’.  In 14 of these cases, the main penalty imposed was a custodial one and, in 3 of the cases, the main penalty was a suspended sentence.  The highest and lowest custodial penalty imposed in each of the years concerned has been detailed in the following table.Highest and lowest custodial penalties imposed following a conviction at courts for a causing death by dangerous driving offence, 2020 – 2024YearHighest custodial sentenceLowest custodial sentence2020ECS 13 yearsDCS 4 years2021DCS 7 yearsDCS 1year 3 months2022DCS 6 yearsDCS 1 year2023DCS 9 years 6 monthsDCS 3 years2024DCS 3 years 4 monthsDCS 1 year 4 monthsNote:1. Figures relate to initial disposals at courts.  Appeals are not included.2. Figures relate to cases where there was a conviction for at least one count of the offence specified.3. Figures for disposals relate to the main disposal imposed following conviction for the offence specified.4. DCS relates to a Determinate Custodial Sentence; ECS relates to an Extended Custodial Sentence.  Figures provided for these sentences relate to time to be spent in custody and on licence.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to the Public Health Agency's publication of advice on the use of illegal drugs on social media, for his assessment of the impact of this on the enforcement of legislation concerning the possession of illegal drugs.
answer_text: The Executive’s Substance Use Strategy ‘Preventing Harm, Empowering Recovery’ has a vision that people in Northern Ireland are supported in the prevention and reduction of harm and stigma related to the use of alcohol and other drugs, have access to high quality treatment and support services, and will be empowered to recover.The strategy recognises the importance of reducing availability of, and accessibility to, illegal substance and we work collaboratively with the PSNI, the Organised Crime Taskforce Drug sub-group, and the Department of Justice in relation to several key actions in this respect.The Public Health Agency (PHA) are a key partner in the delivery of the strategy. The PHA takes a public health approach to the prevention, and reduction of harms associated with substance use. This approach enables the PHA to work with partners across Northern Ireland to promote key prevention messages to individuals, families, and communities so that the population are aware of the inherent dangers of substance use and can make informed choices to either delay personal engagement in substance use and or chose not to use substances at all.The PHA messaging often notes the health and legal risks of taking any illegal or illicit drug. However, the PHA also acknowledge the fact that illegal substances are available within Northern Ireland and a proportion of the population actively use these substances. Therefore, in line with public health approaches, the PHA use a range of methods to engage individuals that use substances to ensure they are aware of harm reduction methods. This reduces the risks of non-fatal overdose, deaths, sharing of equipment, the spread of blood born viruses and discarding drug paraphernalia in local communities, which actively protects the entire population. Alongside the harm reduction approaches individuals are actively supported to engage in treatment and recovery focused services.The PHA’s social media substance use messaging, alongside harm reduction advice, plays a critical supporting role as part of the overall prevention approach in Northern Ireland by enhancing public understanding of substance use and related risks, encouraging safe behaviours, and facilitating community-led prevention efforts.While the PHA does not have a role in enforcing the legislation concerning the possession of illegal drugs, they actively engage with the Police Service of Northern Ireland (PSNI) to ensure that both organisations support a joint policing and public health approach to substance use across Northern Ireland. Additionally, PHA also engage with the UK Border Force as appropriate in support of regional prevention activities.You may also wish to note that in the Northern Ireland Audit Office report on Addiction Services in NI in 2020 notes that there is “clear evidence that harm reduction projects are a cost-effective way of tackling the harms related to alcohol and drug misuse. The Department should ensure the further development of co

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the creation of a new Primary Care Centre in Bangor.
To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the number of areas added to the winter gritting network, in each of the last five years, broken down by local council area.
answer_text: At the start of each winter season a review is undertaken and additional roads added to the salted network if they meet the current policy. Updates to the gritting schedule are managed at a local level, with changes made to the publicly available map for the gritted network.  Given the availability of this information on a live basis, a list of roads added to the schedule each year is not held.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the George Best Belfast City Airport Rail connectivity feasibility project.
answer_text: Translink submitted the Belfast City Airport Connectivity report earlier this month. This is currently under consideration in line with the established procedures for all capital investment projects.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the current (i) average; and (ii) longest, waiting time for treatment at the Chronic Pain Department within the Ulster Hospital.
To ask the Minister of Health to detail the current (i) average; and (ii) longest, waiting time for treatment at the Acute Pain Service within the Ulster Hospital.
answer_text: Acute pain is defined as short term pain such as a sprained ankle.  The South Eastern Trust has confirmed there is no waiting list for the acute pain service.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on her Department’s new road maintenance strategy.
To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the York Street Interchange scheme.
answer_text: As part of the Major Projects Prioritisation which I announced on 19th June 2025, Placemaking work on the York Street Interchange Project, commissioned by my predecessor, has been completed, and my officials engaged in consultation with local elected representatives to present the findings of the Placemaking Report and gain necessary feedback from these important stakeholders.This work was completed in May 2025, and a detailed report has been presented to my office for consideration. I have scheduled a meeting with my officials to discuss the next steps for the York Street Interchange project.The estimated cost of the York Street Interchange scheme is in the region of £120m - £160m (based on 2015 prices) however this estimate would be subject to the high levels of inflation and cost increases experienced by the construction sector in recent years. The cost of the scheme is therefore expected to be significantly higher than the 2015 estimate.The scheme is not funded through City and Growth deals and is not identified as an Executive Flagship.  Therefore, I have asked my officials to review possible alternative funding methods which could facilitate scheme progression. This work is at an early stage.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail her Department's anticipated spending on road safety advertising in 2025-26.
answer_text: Road safety continues to be a key priority, and I remain firmly committed to ensuring that all road users can travel safely and with confidence. In support of this, I have reinstated the road safety advertising budget, approving an allocation of £1,946,000 to Road Safety Promotion for the 2025/26 financial year. In addition, the Northern Ireland Road Safety Partnership (NIRSP) has allocated £500,000 in sponsorship, bringing the total budget to £2,446,000.I have launched a new Road Safety Strategy, focused on protecting communities and reducing harm across our transport network.While it is not currently possible to provide a detailed breakdown of anticipated expenditure, I have prioritised the reinstatement of pedestrian and motorcyclist safety campaigns in the first instance.  Further work is underway to assess where best to target the budget to maximise impact and help reduce deaths and serious injuries on our roads.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the percentage of the prison population, made up of prisoners being held on remand awaiting trial, in each of the last 12 months.
answer_text: MonthProportion of Prison Population on Remand/Awaiting Trial1 October 202435.9%1 November 202436.8%1 December 202436.9%1 January 202536.6%1 February 202537.6%1 March 202538.8%1 April 202536.7%1 May 202537.3%1 June 202538.6%1 July 202537.4%1 August 202539.4%1 September 202541.8%The following table sets out the percentage of the prison population being held on remand and awaiting trial on the first day of each of the last 12 months.Source: PRISM – Management InformationNote: Information is snapshot data, correct as at midnight on 1st day of each month.Individuals are classified as ‘Remand’ or ‘Awaiting Trial’ depending on what Court Tier (Magistrates or Crown) the holding charges are being heard in. When the Northern Ireland Prison Service refers to ‘Remand’ prisoners this usually combines those who are on ‘Remand’ and ‘Awaiting Trial’.An individual serving a custodial sentence, may also have had separate holding charges active in either a Magistrates and/or Crown Court. question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for an update on the creation of the Destination Stewardship Plan, as part of her Department's Tourism Vision and Action Plan 10 Year Plan.
answer_text: Work on the Destination Stewardship Framework is progressing well, following a period of extensive stakeholder engagement.   A draft Destination Stewardship Framework has been produced, setting out the principles for a destination stewardship approach and the proposed structures for success in the North. The strategic approach will be considered by Tourism NI’s Board in September.Subject to any further advice from the Board, the next stage will involve further engagement with all Councils and key stakeholders to discuss the strategic approach and the establishment of a process to select the preferred destination with which to develop a Pilot Destination Stewardship Plan.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail her Department's road maintenance budget for 2025-26, broken down by local council area.
answer_text: My Department does not allocate budget by local council area, however, I can advise that the capital Structural Maintenance budget for 2025-26 is £68million.  This budget is allocated to each of the four Roads Divisions on the basis of need using a range of weighted indicators tailored to each maintenance activity (e.g., resurfacing, patching).

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure when she will answer AQW 29652/22-27.
answer_text: I can advise the Member that AQW 29652/22-27 was answered on 30 September 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the (i) current; and (ii) maximum prison occupation at each prison site.
answer_text: ANSWEROn 3 September 1,477 prisoners were being held in Maghaberry Prison, 502 in Magilligan Prison and 173 at Hydebank Wood.  The operational capacity of Maghaberry is 1,631, Magilligan is 502 and Hydebank Wood is 353.  Whilst these figures represent the capacity at a point in time, the actual number of spaces available to accommodate individuals can vary dependent upon a range of factors including the size and configuration of cohorts within the population, appropriate use of cell sharing, maintenance work etc.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether her Department will introduce increased grass cutting in Ards and North Down, in light of the changes to grass cutting in Mid and East Antrim.
answer_text: There are no plans for additional grass cutting in any council area. The Department is committed to protecting wildlife and promoting biodiversity when managing roadside verges and our approach aims to maintain the right balance between road safety and the control of grass and weeds while achieving a greater focus on environmental protection.In compliance with the policy, a single swathe is cut along verges on the strategic road network, urban roads, on heavier trafficked rural roads and at sight lines and junctions twice per year for road safety purposes. On lighter trafficked roads the cutting frequency is reduced to once per year and is scheduled towards the end of the growing season. There is no planned review of the policy, but I continue to work with my officials to improve the service we deliver. Officials have advised Mid & East Antrim Council that the Department can carry out additional cuts at sightlines at bends and junctions for road safety reasons in line with our existing policy.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the number of Custody Prison Officers who were part of recruitment exercises in the last three years have subsequently left their position.
answer_text: Since 1 January 2023, 7 Custody Prison Officers who were part of recruitment exercises in the last three years (1 January 2023 to September 2025) have subsequently left their position.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail any engagement her Department has had with the Independent Rape Gang Inquiry.
answer_text: ANSWER This Inquiry announced by the UK Government applies to England and Wales, reflecting the Home Office’s responsibilities for policing and crime within that jurisdiction. In line with the recommendation made in Baroness Casey’s National Audit on Group-Based Child Sexual Exploitation and Abuse, the scope of the inquiry does not extend to Northern Ireland.However, following a commitment made by the Safeguarding Minister to engage on this matter with the devolved administrations, officials from my Department held an initial introductory meeting with the Home Office over the summer. It was agreed that further engagement would be scheduled to ensure that devolved administrations are kept informed as this work develops.These meetings will provide an opportunity to consider any issues of relevance to Northern Ireland and to respond accordingly.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail his Department's plans for the former Rathgael House site.
answer_text: As part of its ambitious Office Estate Strategy, my Department has been carrying out phased works at Rathgael House. Phase 1 was the demolition of the Rathgael Tower and Balloo Annex, which was completed on 18 December 2024. Phase 2 works, which will refurbish the front access and adjoining car park, began on 12 May 2025 and are to be completed by the end of October.  Phase 3 works, to refurbish the reception area, were tendered in June 2025. All proposed works are planned to be completed by the end of the year.My Department is also considering longer term accommodation requirements and options in the area. As part of this, discussions are ongoing with Ards and North Down Borough Council on the co-location of NICS Staff located in the Bangor Area at the new civic offices planned at Queen’s Parade in Bangor.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to outline their engagement with the Commissioner for Victims and Survivors.
answer_text: On the 4th of August 2025, we were pleased to announce the appointment of Joe McVey as the new Commissioner for Victims and Survivors. He will formally take up the post on the 6th of October 2025, for a term of four years.Joe brings a wealth of senior leadership experience from the voluntary and community sector, and we are confident that he will be a committed and effective advocate for victims and survivors, ensuring their voices are heard and their needs prioritised.Victims and survivors deserve recognition, support, and services that are trauma-informed and centred on their needs. We are committed to ensuring these services empower individuals and contribute to a shared, peaceful future.We wish Joe well as he undertakes this important role and look forward to engaging with him as he works to bring about real improvements to the lives of those who have suffered because of our past.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (i) for his assessment of the Northern Ireland Audit Office report Waste Crime in Northern Ireland; and (ii) to detail any action his Department will take in light of this report.
answer_text: The Member will be aware of the guiding principle that it is inappropriate to comment in detail on reports from the Northern Ireland Audit Office in advance of consideration of the report by the Assembly’s Public Accounts Committee.What I can, though, confirm is that I welcome the publication of this report and can provide assurance that its recommendations will be considered very carefully by officials in my Department.  Reflecting the requirements now in place, my Department will publish its response to the recommendations in the report within the eight week timeframe that applies.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on his Department’s ten-year capital plan.
answer_text: Following my response to your previous request in February 2025 for an update on the Department of Health’s (DoH’s) ten-year capital investment plan (AQW 22158/22-27), the Chancellor of the Exchequer announced the outcome of the UK wide Spending Review 2025 (SR25) in June. The Department of Finance (DoF) subsequently commissioned a budget information gathering exercise to inform the NI Executive’s consideration of the next local Budget process which is expected to cover the years 2026-27 to 2029-30.My ability to commit to funding any new capital projects will depend both on the amount available in my Department’s Capital DEL budget in future years, which will only be confirmed as part of the Budget process later this year, and the identification of sufficient additional recurrent funding to meet the Resource DEL costs associated with the capital projects. Following confirmation of the budget outcome for DoH, the capital investment plan will be developed to provide an assessment of the projects that can be funded over the next ten years.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for an update on the launch of the Delivery Unit.
answer_text: A Delivery Unit has been established to drive reform and transformation across government and wider public services. It will work across departments to help ensure that the Executive’s top priorities receive the scrutiny, support and attention required to make the greatest possible impact on people’s lives. The Executive has agreed that the Delivery Unit will focus initially on our shared goal of reducing waiting times. We are working with the Health Minister to identify how the Unit can complement and add value to the work his department is taking forward in this area.  In addition, all Ministers have been invited to suggest other priority areas where they consider the Delivery Unit can help to drive meaningful change and improvement.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for an update on the creation of the Destination Stewardship Plan, as part of her Department's Tourism Vision and Action Plan 10 Year Plan.
answer_text: Tourism NI has procured a contractor to co-create the Destination Stewardship Framework. The contractor is collaborating with Tourism NI, all councils and relevant stakeholders to gather research, data and evidence to develop a framework. This work will inform the basis on which a destination is selected for the pilot Destination Stewardship Plan. The contractor is nearing completion on its consultation and is currently preparing an engagement report. In accordance with the proposed timeline, my Department anticipates that Tourism NI will have completed its work on the initial stage in September 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any engagement her Department has had with the Shared Rural Network, in relation to investment in additional upgrades for mobile phone masts.
answer_text: The Shared Rural Network (SRN) is a project developed by the Mobile Network Operators (MNOs) and the British Government. My Department had no role in the design or monitoring of the SRN project. DfE’s role, along with other local Departments, was to engage with the British Government and MNOs on the SRN Working Group to receive project updates and to help address localised issues. Ofcom have reported that SRN coverage targets have been met in the North. My officials will remain in contact with British government and Ofcom during the post project evaluation of the project.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the number of individuals who have participated in the Substance Misuse Court, for each year since 2018.
answer_text: The number of individuals who have participated in the Substance Misuse Court since 2018 are detailed in the table below.Table 1. Figures are presented in alignment with the two evaluation periods over this timeline.2 Figures have been collated for the business year since April 2023.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the number of offenders who have received a reduced sentence as a result of their participation in the Substance Misuse Court, for each year since 2018.
answer_text: The Northern Ireland Department of Justice (DoJ) has implemented a programme of problem-solving initiatives as part of an evidence-informed approach which aims to reduce offending and reoffending rates by tackling the root causes of offending behaviour and reducing harmful behaviour within families and the community. One of these initiatives is the Substance Misuse Court which has been running at Belfast Magistrates Court since April 2018.However, no records are held on what sentence might have been imposed by the Judge in a case, if the individual hadn’t participated in Substance Misuse Court, so, from data held, it is not possible to provide the information requested.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the funding provided to Ards and North Down's Local Economic Partnership to date.
answer_text: Local Economic Partnerships will be supported with a £45m fund over 3 years. This funding will issue to Councils in arrears, at specific points in the year based on activity undertaken in its area following agreement of an action plan and issue of a Letter of Offer. Ards and North Down Local Economic Partnership held its inaugural meeting on 8 July, my Department and Invest NI will continue to work with the Partnership to develop its action plan.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail how their Department will utilise the additional £2.2 million in Resource DEL allocated in the June Monitoring Round to tackle Violence Against Women and Girls in North Down.
answer_text: Work is ongoing to determine the detail of how the additional allocation will be spent. We are already working in partnership with each local council, including Ards and North Down, to equip communities across cities, towns, villages and rural areas to prevent and respond to violence against women and girls. The additional £2.2m will enable TEO to deliver on a larger scale and across a broader spectrum of platforms, at both local and regional levels.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the Bangor Station Refurbishment project.
answer_text: Translink are currently undertaking a feasibility design review for the Bangor Station Refurbishment project. Upon completion, they plan to submit a business case in Spring 2026 and, subject to the availability of funding, develop a detailed design by the end of 2026. My Department has been operating in a difficult financial environment for a number of years due to underfunding and austerity by the British Government. The value of our infrastructure cannot be overstated, and I will continue to work with Executive colleagues to ensure appropriate investment is made.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for an update on Project Gigabit.
answer_text: The procurement for Project Gigabit commenced on 11 November 2024, with an invitation to interested suppliers to complete a selection questionnaire to demonstrate their capacity and capability to deliver such a contract. The procurement is seeking a supplier to address an initial scope of approximately 11,000 premises. The deadline for the submission of bids for Project Gigabit closed in May, and the technical and financial evaluation of bids is ongoing. My Department expects to award a contract for Project Gigabit in August, subject to compliant bids being made, bids meeting the published criteria, and all necessary approvals being agreed.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail her Department's anticipated spending on road safety advertising in 2025-26.
answer_text: I have not taken any decisions on my Department’s 2025-26 budget, as I am considering the responses to my Department’s Draft 2025-26 Budget Equality Impact consultation.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the road maintenance budget allocation for 2025-26, broken down by local council area.
answer_text: I have not yet finalised my decisions on my Department’s 2025-26 budget outcome.  In doing so I will, of course, consider the responses received on the Equality Impact Consultation on my Department’s Draft 2025-26 Budget which closed on 5th June 2025.This will involve making challenging choices, and careful management of costs will be necessary in order for my Department to maintain public service delivery in relation to the road network.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail her Department's spending on legal costs in relation to the A5 Western Transport Corridor scheme, in each of the last 10 years.
answer_text: My Department has made payment for legal costs in respect of legal services received in relation to the A5 Western Transport Corridor scheme, as set out in the table below:YearAmount2014/201537,7002015/20164062016/201759,0002017/201802018/201923,7002019/20203,2002020/202151,2002021/202202022/202320,2002023/202479,100Total287,668Legal services have been engaged in 2024/25 and this financial year however payments have not yet been made.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail any action her Department plans to take, following the publication of the legal paper and interim information by the Equality Commission for Northern Ireland, regarding the Supreme Court decision in For Women Scotland Ltd v The Scottish Ministers.
answer_text: Careful consideration is being given to the Legal paper and information published by the Equality Commission for Northern Ireland (ECNI) - The meanings of “sex”, “men”, “women” and “gender reassignment” in equality and allied legislation in Northern Ireland and interim information for employers, service providers and public authorities.The Equality Commission has also written to the Permanent Secretary to offer a meeting with officials to discuss the approach by the Commission and the Department’s interim arrangements.   That meeting is being scheduled and will inform any action taken in response to the ECNI’s Legal paper and information.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any action that will be taken by his Department following the publication of a legal paper and interim information by the Equality Commission for Northern Ireland regarding The Supreme Court decision in For Women Scotland Ltd v The Scottish Ministers.
answer_text: In light of the recent Supreme Court decision in For Women Scotland Ltd v The Scottish Ministers, I have asked my officials to bring me proposals for policy consistent with the judgment.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail any action that will be taken by his Department following the publication of a legal paper and interim information by the Equality Commission for Northern Ireland regarding The Supreme Court decision in For Women Scotland Ltd v The Scottish Ministers.
answer_text: The Equality Commission has written to my Department, inviting engagement with them on their strategy for producing the Guidance. Officials from my Department will attend a meeting to be arranged in the coming weeks and collaborate with the Commission to understand the impact of this judgment in our jurisdiction. This issue must be approached with compassion, understanding and dignity for all.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any action that will be taken by his Department following the publication of the legal paper in June 2025 by the Equality Commission for Northern Ireland following the Supreme Court decision in For Women Scotland Ltd v The Scottish Ministers.
answer_text: In April 2025, I requested that all Health and Social Care (HSC) Trusts pause the introduction of any new policy or guidance relating to single-sex spaces for HSC staff, pending the completion of a region-wide audit by the Department of Health. This audit has now been completed. Following this, I have asked HSC organisations to recommence their paused reviews of the wider HSC Gender Expression & Identity Policy. These reviews should take full account of the recent UK Supreme Court ruling and the legal paper published by the Equality Commission for Northern Ireland (ECNI). The Department of Health will take time to carefully consider the implications of both the ruling and the Commission’s guidance. The Department’s Equality and Human Rights Unit has already initiated engagement with the Equality Commission for Northern Ireland and will continue to liaise closely with ECNI to ensure that any future policy development is legally robust and promotes an environment of respect and dignity for all staff and service users.I remain fully committed to upholding equality of opportunity and fostering a workplace culture where everyone is treated with fairness and respect.

answer_text: Substance Misuse Liaison Nurse Nursing (SMLN) provision is configured differently across the 5 Health and Social Care Trusts. The variation in provision relates to whether HSCTs have an integrated model of liaison nursing which includes mental health and substance use liaison nursing staff, or whether SMLN provision is seen as a distinct service within the HSCT.The detail below sets out the detail of current provision across all 5 Health and Social Care Trust areas.Southern Health & Social Care TrustSHSCT operates an Integrated Liaison Nursing Service.The number and Whole Time Equivalent (WTE) Nursing Staff in SHSCT Mental Health Integrated Liaison Team during the last 5 years;(Note: Figures exclude Bank Staff on Career Break)31/05/2021 - 14 Nurses WTE=13.6031/05/2022 - 24 Nurses WTE=22.1031/05/2023 - 22 Nurses WTE=20.9831/05/2024 - 22 Nurses WTE=20.88 31/05/2025 - 21 Nurses WTE=19.88There is recurrent funding for 2 band 7 Substance Misuse Liaison Nurses in the Integrated Service in SHSCT, however, there are no dedicated staff in these roles. All staff within the Integrated Liaison Service offer assessment and appropriate intervention based on individual assessed needs. This may include brief psychosocial, educational, motivational and harm reduction interventions, and onwards referrals or signposting to supports within the community. In addition to the above, there was one ‘Alcohol Liaison Midwife’ employed as at 31 May 2021 and 31 May 2022. question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for her assessment of the causes of Ards and North Down's low ranking, as outlined in Invest NI Activity by District Council Area 2019-2020 to 2023-2024, in terms of the (i) number of investments; (ii) total assistance offered; (iii) total investment; and (iv) jobs to be created.
answer_text: Within the Ards and North Down Borough Council, Invest NI offered assistance of £18m to 219 businesses during this period. This support, combined with company investment, has the potential to unlock £88m of investment in the area, and support the creation of 636 jobs. Of the 11 Councils, Ards and North Down has the 10th largest business base rate, yet received the 8th highest level of financial assistance, leveraged the 9th highest level of total investment and the 9th highest level of jobs created. An increase in the business base rate, and specifically the creation of more export focused manufacturing and tradeable services companies within the Council area, may help to enhance the Council’s ranking in this context. The creation of the Local Economic Partnership within Ards and North Down also provides an opportunity to address barriers to growth and to increase productivity within the borough.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs what action his Department is taking to improve the effectiveness of TB testing in cattle.
answer_text: The rapid accurate identification and removal of infected animals is the cornerstone of tuberculosis (TB) control in cattle and other species. There are currently two tests approved for use in EU Approved TB Eradication Programmes and therefore suitable for use in the Northern Ireland (NI) Eradication Programme to detect bovine tuberculosis (bTB) in live cattle, the tuberculin skin test, and the Interferon Gamma (IFNg) assay. Routine testing is carried out using the Comparative Intradermal Tuberculin Test (CITT) commonly referred to as the “skin test”. The IFNg blood test is used on a voluntary basis in certain circumstances as a supplementary test in addition to the skin test to improve detection of infection in herds infected with bTB.All herds are tested at least once a year and more often if infection is suspected or revealed in the herd. Herds considered at risk to infected herds will also test more frequently. Herd keepers are discouraged from allowing tests to go overdue through the imposition of movement restrictions both into and out of the herd. Furthermore, overdue tests can result in financial penalties.In April, I was pleased to endorse the TB Partnership Steering Group’s agreed Bovine TB in Northern Ireland: Blueprint for Eradication. This document can be viewed at https://www.daera-ni.gov.uk/publications/tb-partnership-steering-group-bovine-tb-northern-ireland-blueprint-eradication. Internally a new Bovine Tuberculosis Eradication Transformation Programme has also been established to deliver the actions set out in the Blueprint. Initial priority workstreams include work to ensure that TB testing is carried out to the required standard, and work to expand the use of the supplementary IFN-g blood test, along with making the use of this test compulsory (where deemed necessary).  The bTB testing programme is largely delivered by Private Veterinary Practitioners (PVPs) under a contract. As good quality testing is vital in an effective and efficient eradication programme, a review of the criteria used to monitor testing performance has been initiated ensuring learning and good practice is availed of from other Competent Authorities across the British Isles. Other actions related to TB testing within the Blueprint include the development of a policy to support herd keepers who wish to use private/supplementary tests. For the Department to adopt a new test, even as a supplementary test, it would have to be World Organisation of Animal Health (WOAH) approved and satisfied that there is proven evidence in relation to its test characteristics and that its use would benefit the bTB Programme in NI. However, it may be possible to allow herd keepers to avail of some of these tests at their own expense should they wish to use them to aid detection of potentially at-risk animals in the herd and inform decisions about the retention of these animals in the herd. I am committed to ensuring that cattle testing for bTB is carried out as efficiently as
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for an update on efforts to (i) fully reopen all areas of Cairn Wood; and (ii) replace lost tree coverage at Cairn Wood.
answer_text: I understand that the majority of the path network in Cairn Wood is fully operational, however, a section of the Blue path currently remains closed as a result of the storm damage. Work is continuing to reopen this section as soon as possible and the wind damaged trees have already been removed. Upturned tree roots caused by the storm presented public safety concerns and these have now been re-sited. Following these necessary works, the path requires resurfacing of the impacted areas and work has been commissioned by Ards and North Down Borough Council for the repairs. I am advised that the section of path currently closed is expected to be fully operational later this summer, which will provide full public access throughout the path network in Cairn Wood. Harvesting of storm damaged trees is currently ongoing at an area of Ballysallagh forest in proximity to the reservoir, which was extensively damaged by Storm Éowyn, and this area is due to be replanted in the 2026 planting season. Within the wider forest area, including Cairn Wood, where other trees have been lost due to the storm, these will be replaced through natural regeneration.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for her assessment of the additional investment required to move away from a limited service of road maintenance.
answer_text: My Department has been operating in a difficult financial environment for a number of years due to underfunding and austerity by the British GovernmentIn response, my Department has had to reduce its routine road maintenance activities to a limited service, which prioritises delivery of essential services for maintaining public safety and protecting the transport network. Until such times as the funding for road maintenance can be stabilised, it is not possible to determine the full extent of the investment required to return to normal Essential Maintenance Service levels, but it is estimated to be in the region of an additional £20m above the average annual investment provided to deliver the reduced 'Limited Service' that has been in place since 2015. This estimate does not include any element to address the deterioration of the network that has occurred as a result of this sustained underfunding.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any plans her Department has to replace street lights with LED bulbs during 2025-26.
answer_text: To date, the Department has installed a total of 248,320 LED units across the north of Ireland, representing approximately 85% of the total network.Apart from those sodium street lights that are identified as needing replacement during routine maintenance, my Department has no other plans to install LED street lighting units during 2025-2026.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the amount spent on agency staff by the South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust in each of the last five years.

To ask the Minister for the Economy for her assessment of the findings in the Ulster University Economic Policy Centre report, Delivering balanced regional growth in Northern Ireland, in relation to Ards and North Down.
To ask the Minister of Finance for an update on the delivery of a successor to the Shared Prosperity Fund.
answer_text: Unfortunately, despite repeated requests to do so, the British Government has so far released limited detail or clarity on the delivery of “Local Growth” the trailed successor to the Shared Prosperity Fund (SPF). The allocation for Local Growth here will be a flat £46m per year for three years. This represents a real terms loss in relation to SPF and compares extremely poorly to equivalent EU Structural Funds. The spending review notes that the NIO will work with the Ministry of Housing, Communities and Local Government (MHCLG) to implement the new Local Growth Fund, in partnership with the Executive. It is unclear what this partnership will look like or what role the Executive can take. Direct delivery of funding programmes in areas of devolved competence risks duplication and missed opportunities to align with our Programme for Government and other plans and strategies. This could lead to the realisation of poor value for money and could increase administrative burdens through the creation of duplicative structures. This is not a good use of finite resources, ours or those of the Voluntary and Community sector.In absence of detail, my department has been proactively leading cross-departmental engagement and workshops with partners including the Voluntary and Community Sector to try and craft a Local Growth proposal which can deliver. This should ensure that when the British Government release substantive detail on their plans we will be ready get funding to where it is needed.I have written to Minister Norris to outline my concerns and to seek urgent clarity. I have offered to work constructively with him on this issue and have asked for a meeting to discuss this issue at the earliest opportunity.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether there is a policy in place between her Department and utility companies regarding street works, particularly in cases where a road has been recently resurfaced by her Department and is subsequently disturbed by utility work shortly thereafter.
answer_text: The Street Works (NI) Order 1995 (“the Order”), as amended by The Street Works (Amendment) (NI) Order 2007, provides a legislative framework for street works activities by statutory undertakers. The Department has issued a number of codes of practice under the Order which were prepared jointly by the N I Road Authority and Utilities Committee (NIRAUC) and the Department.The N I Street Works Register and Notification System (NISRANS) provides an essential tool for communication of works notices between the utility companies and the Department. NISRANS is a vital component of the coordination process and is used to coordinate works by utility companies and resurfacing works carried out by my Department. The Department has powers under Article 18 of the Street Works (N I) Order 1995 to restrict street works for 12 months following substantial road works. Road restriction notices are placed on NISRANS when resurfacing schemes are planned to allow the utility companies opportunity to detail any planned works.  The system will also alert utility companies when a road restriction is in place with the intention of avoiding road openings in a recently surfaced street whenever possibleThere are however a number of exemptions when the twelve month restriction following resurfacing works does not apply:emergency or urgent works; works not involving breaking up the street, repairing, resetting and replacing manhole or chamber covers and frames, resurfacing up to 20 square metres, pole, lamp, column or sign replacement, pole testing, and works of a similar nature;works required to respond to a request for a new service or supply to a customer which was not received at a time when it was practicable for the works to be done before the period of restriction began; and other works to which my department has given its consent (which must not be unreasonably withheld).NISRANS contains a full noticing history allowing manual checks to be made on individual works should any investigation be required as to whether works have been undertaken during a road restriction.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for an update on the development of the Right Care, Right Person policy, in conjunction with the Department of Health.
answer_text: Pursuant to the responses previously provided in relation to AQW 21551/22-27 and AQW 27198/22-27, the Right Care Right Person policy is a PSNI-led initiative. You may wish to contact the PSNI for a more detailed update.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for an update on the Royal Courts of Justice modernisation programme.
answer_text: The Royal Courts of Justice (RCJ) is one of the most important civic buildings in Northern Ireland, both in terms of its architectural significance, and because of its role in accommodating the High Court and Court of Judicature.NICTS is progressing a major capital construction project at RCJ to modernise the building and to improve User Experience, Resilience, Value for Money and Sustainability, in line with the Aims of its Estate Strategy.Following approval of the first Outline Business Case in August 2024, a consultancy team has been progressing through the early design stages.  Stage 2 Concept Designs have recently completed, and the Stage 3 Spatial Coordination Stage will commence later this summer. This is a long-term and complex construction project and, subject to further business case approval and funding provision, the anticipated completion date is 2033, when the building will celebrate its centenary.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for an update on efforts to tackle bovine TB.
answer_text: Bovine Tuberculosis (bTB) rates in Northern Ireland remain much too high, with herd incidence currently standing at 10.60%.  Therefore, in April, I was pleased to endorse the TB Partnership Steering Group’s agreed Bovine TB in Northern Ireland: Blueprint for Eradication. This document can be viewed at https://www.daera-ni.gov.uk/publications/tb-partnership-steering-group-bovine-tb-northern-ireland-blueprint-eradication. The Blueprint is founded on the principle that eradication bTB requires a unified, multisectoral approach.  It sets out work needed in almost 40 actions across three key themes – People, Cattle and Wildlife.  It builds upon the Chief Veterinary Officer (CVO) review of bTB in Northern Ireland, published in November 2024, which itself took account of the actions within the 2022 bTB Eradication Strategy.Internally a new Bovine Tuberculosis Eradication Transformation Programme has also been established to deliver the actions set out in the Blueprint, some of which will need further consultation; new or amending legislation; and additional resources before implementation.  Initial priorities include work on wildlife intervention options and I expect to have advice on this before the summer.  While it is important to note that this is only one part of the solution and any proposals will be fully consulted upon before I can make a fully informed decision on this matter.  However, I am determined that this consultation will take place as soon as possible and enable me to make an informed, science-led decision on the way forward.Other priority workstreams include work to ensure TB testing in NI is carried out to the required standard; work to progress a potential regionalisation pilot; work to enhance the provision of biosecurity advice; and work to expand the use of the supplementary interferon-gamma (IFN-g) blood test along with making the use of this test compulsory (where deemed necessary). Additional funding has been provided to enable more interferon gamma testing this year.  16,987 IFN-g tests took place in 2024-25 and it is planned that this figure will rise to around 24,000 this year.I am committed to progressing new measures to tackle this disease as quickly as possible and recognise that only by working together to tackle all of the factors that contribute to the spread and endurance of this costly disease will progress be made.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail what action her Department is taking to mitigate the potential impact on bus and rail services as a result of industrial action by members of the Transport Salaried Staffs' Association.
answer_text: The responsibility for negotiating and agreeing pay awards with staff is between Translink and trade union groups representing the workforce. I would encourage the Transport Salaried Staffs' Association union and Translink to continue their discussions to reach agreement.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any projects from the Living With Water Programme being progressed during the 2025-26 financial year.
answer_text: My Department’s review of the affordability of the Living With Water in Belfast Plan concluded that the original 12-year delivery timescale was no longer achievable.  Delivery of the projects in the Belfast Plan are now being taken forward by NI Water and my Department as normal business, at a pace and scale achievable within allocated budgets rather than through a formal programme.  Projects in the Belfast Plan being progressed by NI Water during the 2025-26 financial year include:a £7m Upper Falls Storm Storage Project in West Belfast which will provide capacity to facilitate the connection of 1,200 homes;a £0.9m project at Park Manor in Mallusk, that will resolve two Unsatisfactory Intermittent Discharges (UIDs) and will release capacity in the sewer network for 450 housing units;a £0.5m project at Shore Road, Whiteabbey to resolve one UID and remove 1.3 hectares of impermeable area that is currently draining into its network; work in the Carrickfergus, Greenisland and Seahill catchments to reduce spills to Belfast Lough from sewerage infrastructure; andcontinued development of the Catchment Delivery Plan for the drainage area that is served by the Whitehouse Wastewater Treatment Works to inform future investments for NI Water as part of the PC21 and PC28 periods.During 2025-26, my Department is also taking forward a flood alleviation feasibility study along the Knock and Loop rivers in south and east Belfast.  As part of this feasibility study, my officials are working in partnership with the National Trust to take forward a Natural Flood Management pilot scheme in its Cregagh Glen property.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for her assessment of the findings of the Northern Ireland Fiscal Council's Sustainability Report 2025: special focus - Water.
answer_text: I welcome the report by the NI Fiscal Council. The report recognises that changing the governance model, such as with the introduction of mutualisation, will not have any significant benefit to NI Water’s funding shortfall, and will involve water charging. While the report points to water charging through privatisation or rates increases, I have been clear that I will not implement any measures that will lead to household water charges on already hard-pressed families. The recent experience of water companies in England and Wales has demonstrated that privatised and mutualised water companies do not necessarily provide the better levels of services that people expect, and the report acknowledges this. I recognise that there is a need for additional funding for all public services and NI Water is not immune to this. Water and sewerage infrastructure has suffered from years of historic underinvestment as a result of the austerity policies implemented by the British government and we are dealing with the legacy of this.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail his Department's spending on methadone for addiction services in each of the last five years.
answer_text: The actual ingredient cost for dispensing methadone prescription items in primary care over the past five calendar years is as follows:YearCost2020£112,527.802021£120,418.202022£95,065.342023£90,513.802024£75,547.93The Actual Cost is the estimated cost to the Health Service in Northern Ireland for ingredients and appliances and takes account of the discounts applied to relevant products. The figure does not include dispensing, coding or other fees paid to contractors. Note that as methadone is a controlled drug (schedule 2), in accordance with the Northern Ireland Drug Tariff, it is listed as zero discount, so no discount is applicable.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy (i) for her assessment of the findings from the Ulster University Economic Policy Unit's report, Delivering balanced regional growth in Northern Ireland, in relation to Ards and North Down local council area's economic performance; and  (ii) to detail any action her Department will take in light of these findings.
answer_text: Findings in the UUEPC’s report ‘Delivering Balanced Regional Growth’ report echo the findings detailed in my Department’s Sub-regional Economic Plan - that there has been uneven economic growth across the North. The UUEPC research highlights that Ards & North Down performs poorly on indicators such as GVA per capita and Business Expenditure on R&D. However, there are other indicators against which it performs more strongly when compared to other Council areas.  For example, Ards & North Down had amongst the highest stock of residents with RQF 4+ qualifications in 2023 and has experienced growth in Gross Domestic Household Income higher than the North’s average since 1998. Different levels of performance against different indicators help explain why I believe a local response is needed to improve economic performance. My officials and officials from Invest NI continue to work alongside Ards and North Down council on the establishment of the Local Economic Partnership supported through the Regional Balance fund.  The focus of this partnership will be on local priorities and identifying solutions to the unique challenges of the area.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the development of the Sydenham Greenway.
answer_text: My Department recently held a public consultation in relation to Traffic Regulation Orders for the first phase of Sydenham Greenway. This will improve facilities for pedestrians and cyclists between Victoria Park and Inverary Avenue in the Sydenham area of Belfast. Officials are addressing a number of objections received to that consultation before construction on that phase can progress.Officials are also engaging with our Major Works Engineering Consultants to commence work on the design for Sydenham Greenway Phase 2 in the coming months. This Signature Active Travel project, aimed at extending active travel infrastructure to Holywood Exchange and into the town of Holywood, is likely to take 3 to 4 years for design before construction can commence.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of patients receiving methadone as part of addiction treatment in each of the last five years, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: Information on the absolute number of patients receiving methadone as part of addiction treatment is not available for two reasons: It is not possible to provide an accurate number of patients who are dispensed methadone products from BSO pharmacy payment systems. The majority of these products are prescribed through “substitute prescribing forms” which are different from the usual HS21 prescription forms and cannot be easily linked to patient details. Whilst there is a Northern Ireland Local Enhanced Service in place for Substitute Prescribing where GP practices are required to record the total number of patients treated, this data is only held at GP practice level and not held by SPPG.  Moreover, this data will include information on patients receiving other products for substitute prescribing and not just methadone.  Information has instead been provided for the number of methadone items dispensed by community pharmacies as this will give a complete picture of the level of prescribing.Table 1: Number of methadone items dispensed by Local Commissioning Group (LCG)1 and calendar year.LCG20202021202220232024Belfast 9,82110,5529,3408,0887,089Northern 5,2075,9606,6076,8096,266South Eastern 1,7981,8581,8871,9692,029Southern 3,0643,5113,1933,4223,487Western 647683591738629Total20,53722,56421,61821,02619,500Notes: Local Commissioning Groups (LCG) cover the same geographical areas as the Trusts and information is based on the location of the dispensing pharmacy. Items may be dispensed in pharmacies which are located in a different LCG to which the patient resides.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail how the space currently used by the Minor Injury Unit at the Ulster Hospital will be used after its closure on 18 June 2025.
answer_text: The new Urgent Care Centre at the Ulster Hospital which will have opened on 19 June 2025 follows the transfer of Minor Injury services from Bangor and Ards to the Ulster Hospital, Dundonald in September 2023.   It is part of the current programme of transforming services at the Emergency Department (ED) in the Ulster Hospital.  This programme commenced with emergency care moving to the new Acute Services Block while the previous ED facilities were used temporarily as a Minor Injuries Unit.  With the capital works on the Urgent Care Centre now complete the Acute Services Block will accommodate all Ulster Hospital emergency and urgent care in the one place.Part of the current Minor Injury Unit space will be repurposed to a dedicated Research and Development Hub, other parts will be retained as a dedicated decant space to facilitate future refurbishment projects (subject to capital funding).Persons with mobility issues may avail of the drop off area at the front of the existing ED where there will be direct lift access to the new Urgent Care Centre.  Individuals can also park within the multi-storey carpark and gain access to the UCC via the Acute Services Block.  Lifts are available both within the multi-storey carpark and within the Acute Services Block.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail any action his Department has taken in light of the Supreme Court judgment in the case For Women Scotland Ltd v The Scottish Ministers.
answer_text: The Equality Commission is currently considering any potential implications here due to the Supreme Court ruling and will issue guidance in due course.This issue must be approached with compassion, understanding and dignity for all.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail how those with mobility issues will access the Urgent Care Centre within the Ulster Hospital.
answer_text: The new Urgent Care Centre at the Ulster Hospital which will have opened on 19 June 2025 follows the transfer of Minor Injury services from Bangor and Ards to the Ulster Hospital, Dundonald in September 2023.   It is part of the current programme of transforming services at the Emergency Department (ED) in the Ulster Hospital.  This programme commenced with emergency care moving to the new Acute Services Block while the previous ED facilities were used temporarily as a Minor Injuries Unit.  With the capital works on the Urgent Care Centre now complete the Acute Services Block will accommodate all Ulster Hospital emergency and urgent care in the one place.Part of the current Minor Injury Unit space will be repurposed to a dedicated Research and Development Hub, other parts will be retained as a dedicated decant space to facilitate future refurbishment projects (subject to capital funding).Persons with mobility issues may avail of the drop off area at the front of the existing ED where there will be direct lift access to the new Urgent Care Centre.  Individuals can also park within the multi-storey carpark and gain access to the UCC via the Acute Services Block.  Lifts are available both within the multi-storey carpark and within the Acute Services Block.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail what consideration her Department has given to allowing volunteers' Access NI certificates to be used for multiple organisations.
answer_text: The current position is that AccessNI disclosure certificates are accurate on the day they are issued.  They are not an indicator of future behaviour, therefore potential safeguarding risks could arise where employers or voluntary organisations accept aged certificates on the presumption that nothing has changed; the older the certificate, the greater this risk. The recently enhanced Police National Database (PND) presents opportunities to introduce continuous monitoring of police information which could facilitate the portability of AccessNI disclosure certificates.Discussions are at an early stage and work in this regard will need to include a full assessment of associated costs, not least as applicants are required to cover these through the fees they pay.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any action his Department has taken in light of the Supreme Court judgment in the case For Women Scotland Ltd v The Scottish Ministers on 16 April 2025.
To ask the Minister of Education to outline any action his Department will take following the completion of the independent Strategic Review of the Northern Ireland Curriculum.
answer_text: The Strategic Review of the Northern Ireland Curriculum was published on 3 June 2025.  The report makes 21 recommendations and sets out the need for a new curriculum framework that is purpose-led; knowledge-rich; continuous and coherent; specific and focussed; and flexible and inclusive.  I am currently considering the findings of the Review and will provide a formal response to each of the recommendations in the coming weeks.  However, I am convinced that the report sets out a compelling case for reform and work must commence at pace.  To that end, I have signalled my intention to establish a Curriculum Taskforce that will lead the design and development of the new curriculum.  I have appointed renowned educationalist Christine Counsell to chair the Taskforce.  Further information on the Taskforce will be provided in the coming weeks in advance of it being fully operational in Autumn 2025.  In line with commitments within TransformED, I wish to have a new statutory curriculum framework in place by autumn 2026.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail what action her Department is taking to improve (i) productivity; and (ii) export intensity, in light of the findings of the Ulster University Economic Policy Centre report, Delivering balanced regional growth in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: This research provides further evidence around the importance of tackling regional imbalance in economic opportunity. Work to address this will take place at two levels. Invest NI has a stronger regional focus and greater support including on productivity and exports available through its regional office network. At a local level, Local Economic Partnerships will be empowered through the Regional Balance Fund to identify projects that would address local needs including on productivity and export intensity.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the percentage of the prison population, made up of prisoners being held on remand awaiting trial, in each of the last 12 months.
answer_text: The following table sets out the percentage of the prison population being held on remand and awaiting trial on the first day of each of the last 12 months.MonthProportion of Prison Population on Remand/Awaiting Trial1 July 202434.8%1 August 202436.3%1 September 202437.4%1 October 202435.9%1 November 202436.8%1 December 202436.9%1 January 202536.6%1 February 202537.6%1 March 202538.8%1 April 202536.7%1 May 202537.3%1 June 202538.6%Source: PRISM – Management InformationNote: Information is snapshot data, correct as at midnight on 1st day of each month.Individuals are classified as ‘Remand’ or ‘Awaiting Trial’ depending on what Court Tier (Magistrates or Crown) the holding charges are being heard in. When the Northern Ireland Prison Service refers to ‘Remand’ prisoners this usually combines those who are on ‘Remand’ and ‘Awaiting Trial’.An individual serving a custodial Sentence, may also have had separate holding charges active in either a Magistrates and/or Crown Court. They are not classified on PRISM as ‘Awaiting Trail’ or ‘Remand’ due to serving a custodial sentence.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether her Department has received an Enhanced Drainage Area Plan for Bangor from NI Water, as outlined in the Northern Ireland Flood Risk Management Plan 2021-27.
answer_text: NI Water has advised that it is currently developing an Enhanced Drainage Area Plan (DAP) of the Bangor catchment and anticipate the outputs of the Enhanced DAP will be available in Spring 2026 and will be shared with DfI at this point.For any future queries relating to operational matters, you should contact NI Water directly, through the Elected Representatives Hotline on 0345 300 6461 or through waterline@niwater.com . Making direct contact with NI Water will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of limited public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for an update on the introduction of the Dilapidation Bill.
answer_text: I am pleased to advise that the introduction of the Dilapidation Bill was approved by the Executive on 5 June. The Bill will be introduced as soon as possible, subject to the approval of the Speaker.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the review of Trust Ports.
answer_text: The Review of Trust Port consultation closed on 10th March 2025 and responses are being considered.  Following this consideration of the responses, my officials will provide me with a policy paper outlining the potential options available to me to address the issues identified.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail what consideration her Department has given to the creation of a TrafficWatchNI app.
answer_text: My Department has previously considered the implementation of a TrafficWatchNI App, however, the development and maintenance of a dedicated mobile application does not offer sufficient advantages over the present web-based solution, to represent good value for money.  It is worth noting that many features of a mobile application are achievable with the present website, namely:     The website displays all its content successfully on a mobile device, via the device’s web browser.  The web pages have been designed to respond to the screen’s size and offer an effective user experience.       Users may subscribe to email notification and receive mobile friendly alerts about significant traffic events and abnormal traffic conditions.  This facility is currently being upgraded.The TrafficWatchNI website is very popular with over 10m views in the last 12 months.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for an update on the action her Department is taking forward, following the conclusion of the joint consultation on Anti-Social Behaviour.
answer_text: As set out in the recent report following the joint consultation, there is strong support for updating the legislative framework to tackle drinking in public. As this is likely to be a cross-cutting issue it has been necessary to work with partners in the Department for Communities in developing proposals in this area. My Department has recently written to the Chief Constable to seek his views on the proposal with a view to bringing forward legislation in the next assembly mandate. This will be subject to Executive and Assembly approval.As also detailed in the consultation report further work has been, and will be, required with respect to any potential amendment to Anti-Social Behaviour Orders (ASBOs). Initially this was taken forward by a working group comprising of statutory partners together with representatives from the Community and Voluntary sector. My officials will now consider all the issues raised throughout this exercise to inform the final direction of the ASBO, again with a view to taking forward any potential amendment in the next Assembly mandate, subject to Executive and Assembly approval.
