question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for a breakdown of which pre-schools in the North Down constituency will be considered for the next round of standardisation of the Pre-School Education Programme, September 2026.
answer_text: I refer the member to the answer to AQW 27241/22-27 which is published in the answers booklet for the week ending 23 May 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the number of new Specialist Provision in Mainstream Schools to be set up for the 2025-26 academic year, in North Down.
answer_text: The Education Authority’s Children and Young People’s Service continues to engage with schools across Northern Ireland to establish new specialist provision in mainstream classes for September 2025.  At the current time, no new provisions have been confirmed for North Down.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any areas within North Down being considered to be part of the Urban Drainage pilot project, using nature-based drainage solutions.
answer_text: The locations for interventions as part of the Urban Drainage pilot project have not yet been finalised.  My officials are currently exploring opportunities to deliver the pilot project within the Living With Water in Belfast Plan area where a specific catchment may be selected as the primary focus of this work.This does not preclude the inclusion of other areas where suitable nature-based interventions are identified, particularly those that may effectively demonstrate the benefits of nature-based drainage solutions.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the opening of an Urgent Care Centre at the Ulster Hospital.
To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to outline their engagement with the Prime Minister in relation to funding for public services during their meeting on 23 May 2025.
To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail what action her Department is taking to address allegations of driving licenses being issued fraudulently.
answer_text: As this matter is subject to a PSNI investigation I cannot comment at this time.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 26623 22-27, to detail the total anticipated cost of the Translink Rail Halt Accessibility Assessment project.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for Translink.  I have sought the requested information and have been advised as follows:The City Airport Rail Connectivity Feasibility project also considers access to City Airport from this rail halt. This study is due to complete in the coming months and costings will be known at that stage. The anticipated cost of the Rail Halt Accessibility Assessment Project is £1.8m. For any future queries relating to operational matters, you should contact Translink directly at aqw.request@translink.co.uk.  Making direct contact with Translink will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of limited public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail (i) the number of letters issued by her Department to sub-postmasters and sub-postmistresses in relation to the quashing of convictions; and (ii) the number of letters still to be issued.
answer_text: To date, my Department has issued letters to 21 individuals whose convictions have been quashed by the Post Office (Horizon System) Offences Act 2024 (‘the Act’).A caution received by an individual is currently being assessed.  I would also encourage anyone who believes that their convictions may be within scope of the Act to contact my department, so that I can be assured that all relevant convictions have been identified.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance for an update on his Department's consideration of the business case to increase PSNI officer numbers to 7,000.
answer_text: ANSWERMy Department has completed its review of the PSNI Workforce Recovery Outline Business Case and is satisfied with the case made, subject to affordability. I will be meeting with the Justice Minister in the coming weeks to discuss this matter. However, given the wider budgetary implications of the proposed increase in police officer numbers at an estimated cost of £206 million over five years, this will  be a matter that the Executive will need to consider.  In my view, this is best done in the context of discussions around the upcoming multi-year Budget, which will follow the Spending Review in June.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any action his Department has taken in light of the Supreme Court judgment in the case For Women Scotland Ltd v The Scottish Ministers.
answer_text: In light of the recent UK Supreme Court judgment regarding biological sex, I will be setting out the department’s position in the coming weeks.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail her Department's spending on legal aid for asylum cases in each of the last five years.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for an update on the work of the Right Care Right Person Strategic Oversight Group.
answer_text: As I have previously indicated the Right Care Right Person initiative is an operational matter for the PSNI.  My Department alongside the DoH and other stakeholders, is seeking to assist the PSNI in the development of RCRP by providing a joint multi-agency co-ordination role alongside the Silver Group (the key decision making group) in the development, implementation and monitoring of the RCRP approach in Northern Ireland;  and ensuring effective future partnership working between all relevant statutory partners in the delivery of mental health care. I should clarify that, the RCRP Strategic Group is not an oversight group. It most recently met on the 15th April and considered agenda items such as the remit of the group and how it could best assist the Silver Group – which is the decision making level and is jointly chaired by the PSNI and DoH It is due to meet again in July.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail what action his Department is taking to address concerns around toxic seaweed at Ballyholme Beach and the risks it poses to domestic pets.
answer_text: On 13 and 14 May 2025, DAERA, including NIEA, officials investigated a dog fatality alleged to have occurred after a visit to Ballyholme Bay, Bangor.  No visible signs of pollution were present and microbiological samples showed low levels of bacteria in the bathing water, consistent with excellent water quality.  Samples were also collected from the Ballyholme River and these had higher bacterial counts, but were typical of this type of urban waterway.  A survey of the site found the plant Poison Parsnip (Water Hemlock) growing at the Banks Lane end of the beach, and at the start of the pathway to Ballymacormick Point.  This plant is known to be highly toxic to animals.  Although it is not possible to positively confirm that this was the cause of the dog’s death, the Department urges pet owners to be particularly vigilant for this plant.  DAERA has informed the Council of the presence of Water Hemlock and the Council has posted warning messages on socal media.No other reports of health issues with domestic or wild animals have been received by the Department. NIEA continues to monitor the situation.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail what action his Department is taking to make farmers aware of the public consultation on the proposed Nutrients Action Programme for 2026 - 2029.
answer_text: A formal press conference was held and a press release issued on 1 May 2025 when the consultation on proposals for the Nutrients Action Programme for 2026 – 2029 was launched.  A press release was also issued to announce the extension of the consultation period, and both press releases were covered in the main agripress. My Officials have also been engaged with key Agri-food sector organisations representing the farming community through a series of in person meetings.An in-person information event was held on 20 May with another one planned for 29 May; two online sessions are also planned for 28 May.  These will focus on the new measures being proposed and the science which supports them.  The online sessions will be recorded and made available on the Department’s website.Details of these events have been published on the Department’s website.  There continues to be a series of social media posts highlighting the consultation and information events being held. Anyone with an interest is encouraged to attend the events and engage with the consultation process.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail any engagement they have had with the UK Government in relation to the reasons for the increase in anticipated costs of accommodation for Asylum Seekers, in light of the National Audit Office report on the Home Office's asylum accommodation contracts.
answer_text: The provision of asylum accommodation is not devolved. It is for Home Office and their asylum accommodation contract providers to forecast and manage costs in relation to the asylum estate.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the number of jobs created by foreign direct investment during 2024-25, broken down by local council area.
answer_text: Invest NI reports its performance on a financial year basis with 2023-24, ending 31st March 2024, being the most recent year available.  Information relating to performance on a District Council Area basis in 2024-25 is provisionally planned for release this summer. For the Member’s future reference, Invest NI proactively publishes a full breakdown of financial support offered to every business over the past 5 years on the NI Open Data online portal which can be accessed from the following link: Invest NI Open Data.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for an update on the development of the Ards and North Down digital hub.
answer_text: The Outline Business Case for the Creative Innovation Hub (known as ‘The Shed’) has been approved by my Department and the Contract for Funding to support the project is currently being drafted. The Project Team has engaged early with Construction & Procurement Delivery (CPD) to assist with procurement and give assurance the project is compliant with public procurement law and policy. Negotiations to secure the purchase of a suitable site for the construction of the hub in Holywood is ongoing and once this is concluded, an Operator and Design Team will be procured.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any engagement her Department has had with Artemis Technologies in relation to (i) the promotion of the proposed Belfast to Bangor ferry route; and (ii) a timescale for its introduction.
answer_text: Officials in my Department and in Invest NI have met with Artemis Technologies to discuss these matters.  Artemis has stated publicly that the vessel is scheduled to launch before the end of 2025.  Following this, the company will test and demonstrate the technology to the classification authorities before welcoming the first passengers on board.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail any support his Department is considering for the social care sector in light of the findings of the Cost of Doing Business Sectoral Study.
answer_text: ANSWERI have shared a copy of the Cost of Doing Business report with the Chief Secretary to the Treasury, and impressed upon him when we met on 24th April, the need to take urgent action in the June Spending Review to address the impacts of the National Insurance Contributions’ changes.I have also shared a copy of the research with my Executive colleagues so they can consider the implications of this work for their departments and the areas they are responsible for.The Department of Health received £8.4 billion in the final Budget for 2025-26.In addition, as part of the agreement around the final Budget, the Executive also committed to providing the Department of Health with a further £87.3 million in the upcoming June Monitoring round. This includes £50 million earmarked for reducing waiting lists with a further earmarked amount of £3.5 million for Family Health Service practitioners (GPs, dentists, and so on) to help with the increased costs relating to the rise in employers’ National Insurance Contributions.The remaining £33.8 million of the planned June Monitoring allocation can be used by the Health Minister to help meet wider budgetary pressures. Now that the Executive Budget has been agreed, it is for each Minister to determine how to best use this funding in line with their priorities for the department.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure how her Department will prioritise road safety as part of its No Mow May approach to grass cutting.
answer_text: My Department's policy for verge management on the road network can be found at: https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/sites/default/files/publications/infrastructure/verge-management-%28grass-cutting%29-dem-193-22.pdfWith over 45,000km of grass verges to maintain, my Department aims to ensure the verges are cut before the grass gets too long as this would create a risk to road safety.   There is little benefit in cutting during May from a road safety perspective as the grass is generally shorter earlier in the year, and it is later in May or early June before longer grass has the potential to create road safety issues. That said, some grass cutting will take place for both safety and operational reasons in May if required.My Department is committed to protecting wildlife and promoting biodiversity and our approach to verge management aims to maintain the right balance between road safety and the control of grass and weeds while achieving a greater focus on environmental protection.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the work of the Independent Review of Teacher Workload Panel.
answer_text: On 1 May 2025 I was pleased to announce the appointment of the independent panel who will undertake the Review of Teacher Workload. The panel will examine all aspects of teacher workload and will monitor the implementation of a series of workload commitments issued alongside the 2024/25 pay offer. They have been asked to report to me by the end of November 2025.The appointments to the panel are Paul Sweeney (Chairperson), Larry Flanagan (the Northern Ireland Teaching Council nomination) and Liam Perry (the Management Side nomination).  The panel held its first meeting on 12 May 2025 and are seeking early engagement with the key stakeholders in the education sector.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy (i) for her assessment of the Treasury Committee's report, Acceptance of cash; and (ii) to detail any action her Department is taking to promote cash acceptance in light of the findings of this report.
answer_text: My Department does not have direct responsibility for financial services, as regulation of financial services is a reserved matter. The Department of Finance (DoF) however acknowledges that the financial services landscape has been transforming rapidly, marked by a steep decline in cash usage, widespread adoption of digital payments, and ongoing bank closures. Cash however remains a fundamental part of our economy, and the North is more "cash-heavy" than any region in Britain. The evolving nature of banking services can present challenges, particularly for vulnerable groups that face a number of barriers to accessing banking services; and rural communities that often lack digital infrastructure. Following the banking roundtable in November 2024, the DoF published a report summarising local access to cash issues, which the current Minister of Finance has formally presented to the Treasury and British regulatory authorities for consideration, given their legislative authority. The Finance Minister plans to continue to engage with stakeholders and to host an annual banking roundtable, to ensure local issues are brought to the attention of relevant authorities in Britain. The DoF will also be observing developments in the South, where the Dáil recently passed a bill aimed at protecting the role of cash in the Irish financial system. The Consumer Council performs certain functions related to financial services. It is funded by the Treasury for consumer research, advocacy, education and outreach. The Consumer Council responded to a call for evidence launched by the Parliamentary Treasury Committee last November, examining whether rules may be required to govern the acceptance of physical cash, and highlighted consumer reliance on cash and the importance of consumers having a choice of  how they pay for goods and services. The Treasury Committee report sets out how the acceptance of physical currency for goods and services is not currently specified in legislation. It highlighted that, having heard evidence on the impact of declining cash acceptance on vulnerable communities, the Treasury Committee believes that in the future it may be necessary for the Treasury to mandate cash acceptance to ensure that those who rely on cash are adequately supported. I would encourage all businesses to offer consumers a choice of ways to pay, including cash.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the number of Penalty Notices for Disorder that have been issued for the possession of cannabis in each of the last five years.
answer_text: There is no specific offence for possession of cannabis in Northern Ireland.  Possession of cannabis may be prosecuted under the offence description of Possession of a Class B controlled drug.  However, Departmental prosecutions and convictions datasets do not hold information on the specific type of drug involved, so it is not possible, from information held, to directly answer the question.  In the period 2020 – 2024, there were no cases where a Penalty Notice for Disorder was issued for the offence of Possession of a Class B controlled drug.  Where such offences were dealt with outside of court, they were dealt with by way of out of court diversionary disposals such as cautions, informed warnings or diversionary youth conferences.  Figures for the numbers of out of court diversionary disposals issued for the offence of Possession of a Class B controlled drug have been provided in the following table.Out of Court Diversionary Disposals for Possession of a Class B controlled drug offences, 2020 – 2024YearDiversions20207072021758202272420237692024532Note:Figures relate to cases where there was at least one out of court diversionary disposal for the offence of Possession of a Class B controlled drug.Figures for 2024 are provisional.In addition to the diversionary disposals mentioned, police may deal with possession of cannabis type offences by way of Community Resolution Notice (CRN).  CRNs are non-criminal disposals which the PSNI have the discretion to issue to anyone over the age of 10 years for a range of low-level offences. Information on the number of CRNs issued by police is a matter for the Chief Constable, who is accountable to the Northern Ireland Policing Board.  I am committed to respecting the operational independence of the Chief Constable and the role of the Northern Ireland Policing Board.You may, therefore, wish to direct any query relating to the number of CRNs issued in relation to possession of cannabis type offences, to the PSNI.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail when her Department will publish the (i) Road Safety Strategy Action Plan 2025-26; and (ii) Road Safety Strategy Action Plan 2024-25 progress report.
answer_text: The Road Safety Strategy Action Plan 2025-26 is currently under development and the Road Safety Action Plan 2024-25 is currently being reviewed, I expect both to be published summer 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any locations in North Down that are being considered for destination Stewardship Plans as part of her Department's Tourism Vision and Action Plan 10 Year Plan.
answer_text: The Destination Stewardship Plan will include a Stewardship framework and principles to be used as a blueprint to sustainably develop tourism destinations. Tourism NI has procured an experienced contractor to co-create the Destination Stewardship Framework. The specialist contractor is collaborating with Tourism NI, all councils and other stakeholders to develop a framework to inform the decision on destinations selected for the pilot Plan. Upon completion of this process my Department will be in a position to advise on the identified destination selected.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for an update on the Ards and North Down Local Economic Partnership.
answer_text: The Department and Invest NI have been working with Ards and North Down council over the past months to help establish their Local Economic Partnerships (LEP). Overall good progress has been made, the Department have approved draft Terms of Reference and the Council are seeking to arrange the first LEP meeting for May or June.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to outline her engagement with the Minister of Finance in relation to the findings of the sectoral study on the Cost of Doing Business in Northern Ireland.

To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to outline how their Department will work with the newly appointed Commissioner for Older People for Northern Ireland to encourage best practice in the treatment of older people.
answer_text: Siobhan Casey was recently appointed as Commissioner for Older People, and we met with Siobhan on her first day in post on 7 April 2025. We recognise the role of Commissioner as being vital in safeguarding and promoting the interests of older people here and ensuring that the welfare and rights of this sector are respected and protected, and that their voice is heard. In relation to Age Goods, Facilities and Services, our officials will continue to liaise with stakeholders. We look forward to engaging with the new Commissioner, once she sets her priorities for the year ahead.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 23121/22-27, (i) whether the findings of the internal audit into missed appointments within the South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust will be published; and (ii) to detail any action being taken as a result of the findings of this audit.
answer_text: The Did Not Attend (DNA) audit reviewed a small sample of patients (circa 200) and was undertaken to provide a high-level understanding of the DNA rates within that specific Trust. While the findings are not being published, the outcome and associated recommendations are being shared with the Trust. The recommendations include actions to enhance appointment reminders, such as increasing the use of telephone reminders to complement the existing SMS reminders in those specialties where there are high levels of DNAs. Additional recommendations focus on improving communication with patients by increasing the visibility of booking office contact details, to support patients who need to cancel their appointments. The audit also recommended increased public awareness of the impact of missed appointments by launching public awareness campaigns utilising social media platforms.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail when he will publish the findings of the Cost of Doing Business research.
answer_text: ANSWERThe Ulster University Economic Policy Centre report on the Cost of Doing Business research was published on my department’s website on 6 May 2025. A copy of the report is available at: UUEPC - Cost of Doing Business Impact Report.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice, pursuant to AQW 25734/22-27, for her assessment of the risk to the public caused by the lack of a statutory duty on specified authorities to implement PREVENT in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: With reference to the answer to AQW 25734/22-27, PREVENT forms part of the UK Government’s CONTEST counter-terrorism strategy.  As noted therein, the statutory basis for PREVENT does not extend to Northern Ireland, and decisions regarding counter-terrorism strategy are reserved to the UK Government.As such, no formal assessment has been undertaken by the Department of Justice in relation to PREVENT in Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 25644/22-27, for his assessment of the reasons behind the increase in spending between 2022-23 and 2023-24.
answer_text: As reported by SEHSCT, staff across South Eastern Trust order their uniforms in line with the Trust Uniform Policy.  Costs vary from year to year as staff join the Trust, change roles or leave.  Others will regularly need to replace their uniforms.  In recent years following the pandemic, staff were encouraged to change from ‘scrubs’ to uniforms. This may have resulted in more orders being placed.

To ask the Minister of Health to detail the staff wellbeing services available to staff within the South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for an update on the future of the School Milk Subsidy Scheme following the closure of his Department's survey in January 2025.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail her Department's anticipated spending on road safety advertising in 2025-26.
answer_text: I have not taken any decisions on my Department’s 2025-26 budget, as this is subject to consideration of the responses to my Department’s Draft 2025-26 Budget Equality Impact consultation.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the total cost for the development and implementation of the mLink ticketing app.
answer_text: Translink has spent a total of £459,000 on mLink's implementation and development since its launch in 2014

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for an update on the creation of destination stewardship plans as part of her Department's Tourism Vision and Action Plan 10 Year Plan.
answer_text: Destination Stewardship Plans will be developed to align with a Destination Stewardship Framework and a set of principles that will be used as a blueprint to develop tourism destinations sustainably in the future.Tourism NI has procured a qualified and experienced contractor to co-create the Destination Stewardship Framework. An inception meeting took place on Friday 4th April to discuss and agree project goals and milestones. Engagement is now being planned with Tourism NI, Tourism Ireland and all councils to gather research, data and evidence. Following stakeholder engagement and a review of the current tourism landscape, initial reports and recommendations on next steps are anticipated to be delivered in June.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether her Department has given any consideration to introducing prevent duty guidance, similar to those in place in other jurisdictions.
answer_text: The Prevent Duty forms part of the UK Government’s counter-terrorism strategy, CONTEST. CONTEST addresses all forms of terrorism that affect the UK and the UK’s interests overseas. Decisions regarding counter-terrorism are a reserved matter and rest with the UK Government in accordance with Schedule 3 to the Northern Ireland Act 1998. PREVENT has a statutory basis in Great Britain, pursuant to Part 5 of the Counter-Terrorism and Security Act 2015. Part 5 of this Act does not extend to Northern Ireland, which means there is currently no statutory duty on specified authorities to implement PREVENT in Northern Ireland.There are currently no plans to introduce similar guidance to Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice, in light of the recent His Majesty’s Inspectorate of Constabulary and Fire and Rescue Services’ report on the effectiveness and efficiency of the PSNI, for an update on her efforts to ensure the PSNI is adequately funded in order to meet its responsibilities.
answer_text: The PSNI Workforce Recovery business case remains under consideration by the Department of Finance. However, affordability remains a primary concern.  I have continuously engaged with the Finance Minister and my Executive colleagues to advocate for a better financial settlement for my Department, to ensure the PSNI receives sufficient funding to stabilise and recover staffing numbers back to a sustainable level.  I will continue to engage and advocate as necessary.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any action his Department is taking to ensure air quality remains at safe levels within the Royal Victoria Hospital during the construction of the Northern Ireland's Children Hospital.
answer_text: I can confirm the following measures are taken to ensure air quality remains at safe levels within the Royal Victoria Hospital during the construction of Northern Ireland's New Children’s Hospital:Belfast Health and Social Care Trust has advised that the risk to air quality associated with construction of the New Children’s Hospital has been assessed and a range of mitigation measures have been included in the construction contract to ensure, so far as is reasonably practicable, that generation of dust, fumes and fungal hazards from the development site is kept to a minimum.  All Departments, Service Areas and Clinical Areas at the Royal Hospitals site have reviewed and updated Aspergillus General Risk Assessments, where required, to account for new and ongoing construction projects including the New Children’s Hospital.Ongoing monitoring arrangements will be in place for the duration of the construction contract to ensure the robust implementation of any identified risk mitigation measures.The construction contract includes an Air Quality and Dust Management Plan to manage the air quality during construction.  It sets out what the contractor is required to do during construction and details dust emission and control measures for the initial site planning and set up.  It has been drafted in line with the Institute of Air Quality Management’s (IAQM) Guidance on the assessment of dust from demolition and construction.  It sets out mitigation measures including Site Management, Preparation, Vehicles on Site, Waste Management.  In addition, it specifies the site monitoring, logging and reporting requirements and details the concentration threshold for alarm communications.The construction works for the project commenced on 3 March 2025 and are being delivered by John Graham Construction Limited.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether the Cost of Doing Business Research has been completed.
answer_text: I received a copy of the report on the Cost of Doing Business from the Ulster University Economic Policy Centre, on Monday 7th April, which I am now considering.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to outline what ongoing support will be provided to victims and survivors of Historical Institutional Abuse following the closure of the Redress Board to new applications.
answer_text: The support service for victims and survivors of institutional childhood abuse was launched in December 2020 and is provided by the Victims and Survivors Service in collaboration with WAVE and AdviceNI. A range of services are available including therapies, social support activities, personal and professional development and welfare advice. They will continue to be provided for a further five years, until 2030 and as recommended in the Hart report.A review of the provision of services is due to start shortly, to help ensure they continue to meet the needs of victims and survivors.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on his Department's review of the consultation on the Public Health Bill.
answer_text: The Department received approximately 8,200 submissions in response to the public consultation on policy proposals to inform the development of a new Public Health Bill for Northern Ireland. These responses are currently being analysed and will inform a consultation summary report which will be published on the Department’s website in due course. A further update will be provided when this process has been completed.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any consultation her Department carried out in relation to the inclusion of Irish language on physical signage and ticket machines at Belfast Grand Central Station.
answer_text: I am committed to equality, inclusivity and respect and in that context to increased visibility of the Irish Language across public services and in our society. My recent announcement is to provide physical signs in Irish which will be in addition to the existing signage at Belfast Grand Central Station. The precise details of the signs will be determined during the design process. Consideration was given as to whether this would benefit from conducting a full Equality Impact Assessment (EQIA). However, based on the anticipated positive impact, the Department has made the decision that a full EQIA is not required.NITHC, trading as Translink, is referred to as a Public Non-Financial Corporation. This effectively means they are a market body with a degree of commercial independence that is governed in policy terms by my Department. I am also answerable to the Assembly for the overall performance and delivery of the Translink Group.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the anticipated cost of including Irish language on physical signage and ticket machines at Belfast Grand Central Station.
answer_text: The cost of the new physical signage at the station is estimated to be £120,000 and for updating the ticket vending machines, the cost is estimated to be £28,000.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for an update on the submission of a business case to the Department of Finance in relation to increasing the number of PSNI officers to 7,000.
answer_text: This business case remains under consideration by the Department of Finance. However, affordability remains a primary concern.  I have continuously engaged with the Finance Minister and my Executive colleagues to advocate for a better financial settlement for my Department, to ensure the PSNI receives sufficient funding to stabilise and recover staffing numbers back to a sustainable level, and will continue to do so.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether existing signage at Belfast Grand Central Station will be removed to facilitate the addition of Irish language on physical signage within the station.
answer_text: I remain committed to the visibility and promotion of Irish Language across public services including the public transport network. The Irish language is a living and thriving language used daily by many people across the north.   As per my previous response, given the on-going legal proceedings in relation to my decision, it would not be appropriate to comment further.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail the number of applicants from the North Down constituency that have been successful in securing the Troubles Permanent Disablement Payment.
answer_text: The Victims’ Payments Board, which makes decisions on any entitlement to payment through the Scheme, has advised that its system does not currently allow for a breakdown of successful applications by constituency. Inclusion of post code information in applications is not mandatory and therefore a manual review of more than 10,000 applications would be required to retrieve this information. As of week commencing 24 March 2025, there have been 1,993 successful determinations resulting in over £83 million paid to eligible applicants since the Scheme opened for applications.  1,031 determinations have been unsuccessful.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the expected timescale for her Department's launch of a consultation into changes to speed limits within town and city centres.
answer_text: Road safety is a high priority for my Department and I am committed to working proactively to make our roads safer and address the needs of all road users. I would like to see what can be done to improve attitudes to speeding, not only for road safety purposes, but to make places cleaner and greener.  I have therefore asked my officials to prepare a paper on the use of permanent 20mph speed limits particularly near schools, in predominately residential areas and places such as town centres where there is a higher number of those walking, wheeling and cycling. I have also asked that consideration be also given to the national speed limits that apply on rural roads and HGV speed limits. I plan to hold a public consultation exercise later this year to ensure that other’s views are considered in the setting of speed limits.

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission for an update on the completion of works, including the removal of scaffolding, to the back of Parliament Buildings.

To ask the Minister of Health to detail any engagement his Department has had with the Infected Blood Compensation Authority in relation to assisting residents who have been impacted by the infected blood scandal to receive adequate compensation.
answer_text: Since IBCA was established by the Victims and Prisoners Act last year, officials in my department have attended regular four nations working group meetings with IBCA, to discuss the roll out of the UK-wide compensation scheme and to agree our approach to operational matters regarding the impact on the devolved Infected Blood Support Schemes (IBSS). I have agreed to meet Sir Robert Francis, Interim Chair of IBCA, in April to hear about IBCA’s plans for further engagement with the infected blood community in Northern Ireland, as well as to discuss priorities and objectives as the compensation scheme is scaled up in the coming months.As we approach the ‘transition phase’ during the financial year 2025/26, my officials will continue to work on a four nations basis with IBCA to identify and address any issues arising, to ensure a smooth and seamless transfer of our NI Scheme beneficiaries into IBCA, by 1st February 2026. Departmental officials also engage regularly with the infected blood community in NI and any concerns relating to the compensation process are followed up with IBCA through the four nations working group.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for an update on her Department's launch of a consultation on increasing the maximum sentences available for the offences of causing or allowing the death of a child or to suffer serious physical harm.
answer_text: My officials continue to work towards finalising and publishing a paper to consult views on proposals to increase the maximum sentences available for the offences of causing or allowing the death of a child or to suffer serious physical harm.  I anticipate publication within the coming weeks.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any consideration his Department has given to making relugolix combination therapy available.
answer_text: My Department has a formal link with the National Institute for Health and Care Excellence (NICE) under which NICE Technology Appraisals are reviewed locally for their legal and policy applicability here, and where found to be applicable, they are endorsed for implementation within Health and Social Care (HSC) organisations. In practice, this means that treatments that have been recommended by NICE for routine use in the NHS in England are also available in Northern Ireland.On 13 March 2025, NICE published its final draft guidance recommending the use of Relugolix–estradiol–norethisterone (relugolix combination therapy [CT]), as an option for treating symptoms of endometriosis in adults of reproductive age who have had medical or surgical treatment for endometriosis. Final guidance is expected on 16 April 2025.When the final guidance for this treatment is issued by NICE, my Department will consider it under the usual process for NICE Technology Appraisals as per the guidance set out in circular HSC (SQSD) 12/22 which is available at:https://www.health-ni.gov.uk/sites/default/files/publications/health/doh-hsc-sqsd-12-22.pdf

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) any action taken by his Department following the recommendations made within the Infected Blood Inquiry Report; and (ii) any recommendations yet to be implemented.
answer_text: (i) The UK-wide Infected Blood Public Inquiry (IBI) published their final report on 20 May 2024(https://www.infectedbloodinquiry.org.uk/reports/inquiry-report).In this report, the Inquiry made 12 overarching recommendations, which included a number of sub-recommendations (58 in total).These recommendations are wide-ranging and cut across a range of policy areas in my Department, including Quality, Safety and Improvement and Workforce Policy, as well as the culture and working practices of the wider Executive, Civil Service and the Health and Social Care system.Recommendation 1 covers the setup of a compensation scheme. This work had already been initiated since the publication of the second interim report in April 2023, and a UK-wide Infected Blood Compensation Scheme is now being fully established.In relation to the non-compensation recommendations, my Department has established a new Directorate with responsibility for coordinating the Northern Ireland response. The Healthcare Inquiries Oversight (HIO) Directorate will focus on ensuring delivery and alignment with recommendations from other Public Inquiries under the governance of the Inquiry Implementation Programme Management Board (IIPMB), chaired by the Permanent Secretary.An IBI Regional Group that includes representation from the Department, HSC Trusts, the NI Blood Transfusion Service and NI Transfusion Committee among others, has been convened to provide expert advice on the policy and operational aspects of each recommendation and to agree a collective course of action for each of the non-compensation IBI recommendations. A stakeholder group representing infected and affected communities has been convened and updated on my Department’s approach and actions on these IBI recommendations, and is now providing a liaison function, which will evolve as the implementation work progresses.As the IBI is a UK-wide Inquiry, some recommendations are being taken forward at UK level under the coordination of the Cabinet Office. The IBI implementation team within the HIO Directorate have been representing Northern Ireland in various groups, including the Cabinet Office Comprehensive Response Working Group. This Group coordinated the production of an update statement which I co-signed along the Minister for the Cabinet Office and my Devolved Nations counterparts, and which was published on 17 December 2024(https://www.gov.uk/government/publications/government-response-to-the-infected-blood-inquiry). This statement provided a progress update of the Government’s response as requested by the Inquiry under Recommendation 11, and also conveyed the Northern Ireland position.(ii) The recommendations formulated by the IBI are wide-ranging and complex, and whilst several aspects have already been embedded as current good practice across the system, my officials will endeavour to build the necessary assurance to submit to the IIPMB for sign off. Where this assurance is not readily available, a plan will be agre

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 23782/22-27, whether her Department will address regional imbalances in road maintenance budget allocations to local council districts as part of her Department's new road maintenance strategy.
answer_text: In distributing the resources available for road maintenance, allocations relating to the areas covered by my Department’s four Roads Divisions are already made based on need, using a range of weighted indicators tailored to each maintenance activity.  These take into consideration criteria such as road length, structural condition, traffic and population (for footway resurfacing). Divisions broadly use these indictors when apportioning across council areas to ensure, as far as possible, an equitable distribution of funds.It is not my intention to change the principle that allocations are made on the basis of need rather, I am acknowledging that the well-discussed funding shortfall, and the years of Limited Service, have seen the condition of our roads deteriorate and its time we tried something different. That’s why a new approach to road maintenance is one of my Foundations.We need a new approach to how the road network is maintained, one that focuses our limited resources in a way that balances safety and value for money. It should be data driven and supported by sound engineering judgement and address the problem in a meaningful and sustainable manner.My officials are working on a new Road Maintenance Strategy that I will be considering in detail in the coming weeks. We will focus on delivering higher-quality repairs instead of spreading resources too thinly, ensuring a more reliable and safer road network where we intervene.We will use modern technology to direct our investments where they are needed most, ensuring smarter maintenance decisions and will future proof our roads to meet the changing needs of our society including active travel, reducing the carbon footprint of our operations, and ensuring that the network is resilient after adverse weather events.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to outline what action his Department is taking to restore woodlands damaged by Storm Éowyn.
answer_text: My Department’s forests suffered significant wind damage as result of Storm Éowyn. Immediately after the storm, Forest Service staff and their contractors assisted both DfI Roads and NIE in clearing trees which were impacting the public road network and the electricity network as well as restoring public access to woodlands, residences and businesses that were impacted.  Forest Service is in the process of clearing storm damaged trees throughout the estate, expediting tree clearance works in areas most affected by reduced recreational provision and where local businesses are impacted. Harvesting plans are being revised across the Forest Service estate, prioritising tree clearance interventions in the wind damaged areas.  Harvesting operations have already commenced, and it is expected that this work will continue for a number of months. Following the clearance of the wind damaged areas, re-establishment will involve planting replacement trees or by allowing trees to regenerate naturally in some areas. This will be in line with longer term objectives, providing opportunities to accelerate plans to increase the diversity of species and forest resilience across the Department’s Forest estate.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the current ambulance handover facilities within the Ulster Hospital.
answer_text: My Department recognises the risk of harm to patients who are delayed on arrival by ambulance outside Emergency Departments and the impact this has on the Northern Ireland Ambulance Service’s (NIAS) capacity to respond to the next emergency call in the community. Whilst pressures manifest at the Emergency Department, flow through the system and getting those people medically fit for discharge back into the community remain significant issues, meaning that a whole system approach is required. The South Eastern HSC Trust understands that ambulance handover delays are a symptom of patient flow through a complex care system and has enhanced its ambulance triage process to assess the most appropriate care options for individuals arriving by ambulance.  The Trust has been working with regional partners, including NIAS, to implement improvements in the patient journey across hospital and community through the establishment of a Hospital & Community Flow Oversight Group.  This focuses on admission prevention, same day emergency care, improving patient flow through hospital and improving discharge processes.The Trust has also expanded its Hospital at Home team and implemented a Frailty Intervention Team to provide Consultant-led care for people in their own home and allow older people to return home more quickly.  The Trust’s Early Review Team also provides rapid reassessment of patient needs to reduce delays for patients waiting for packages of home care.The Ulster Hospital Emergency Department has five spaces within its ambulance handover triage area, with additional cubicles held for initial patient assessments.My Department is determined to find better ways to work as a system to tackle these complex issues.  The first of a series of four winter planning workshops to identify solutions to the system flow issues for 2025/26 was held on 4 March 2025.  I am encouraged by the engagement and enthusiasm from the HSC leaders from across the system who attended. My Department is also looking at initiatives used elsewhere and is engaging with the London Ambulance Service to understand the model implemented with NHS Trusts in the London area which has resulted in improved ambulance handover times.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any engagement her Department has planned with the motor insurance taskforce established by the Transport and Economic Secretary to the Treasury.
answer_text: The date for the next Motor Insurance Taskforce meeting remains to be finalised. In the interim, my Department’s officials continue to liaise regularly with the Department for Transport, which jointly convenes the Taskforce.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for an update on the work of the bTB Partnership Steering Group.
answer_text: The TB Partnership Steering Group (TBPSG) contains representatives from groups and organisations from the farming industry, wildlife and animal welfare, veterinary and public health sectors.  Only by working together in partnership with all key stakeholders and those with an interest in, or who are affected by bovine Tuberculosis (bTB), will we have any hope of making tangible progress in our fight against its maintenance and spread.The Group has met on a number of occasions since its establishment in January.  They are currently considering the CVO’s proposals in a spirit of co-design, and are working with the department to develop a new delivery plan.  I expect advice to be provided to me on the proposed delivery plan by the end of March 2025. It is my intention that this delivery plan will set out our priorities where action on this costly disease can be taken in the short term alongside setting out the steps required to enable other key actions to be implemented.  In some instances, this will require further public consultation or new legislation.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when his Department will formally publish its review of Emergency Medicine in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: I welcome the Getting It Right First Time (GIRFT) review of Emergency Medicine which my predecessor, Robin Swann commissioned and which was a recommendation in the Review of Urgent and Emergency Care. My officials and I are carefully considering the report’s recommendations.The review drew out three main issues for Urgent and Emergency Care in Northern Ireland:Patient handover delays from ambulance staff to ED staff and consequent poor ambulance response times.ED exit block for patients requiring hospital admission.Poor patient flow within hospitals and hospital exit block for patients requiring social and community care.Whilst the report has not yet been published, the recommendations from the review have formed the basis of the planning at a regional and local Trust level for unscheduled care preparedness for 2024/25.Since the completion of the review, the GIRFT Team have continued to work with my officials and with the Trusts on their improvement plans, and played a key role in the development of Unscheduled and Winter Plans for 2024/25.The GIRFT Team have also been involved as external advisors on work to improve and standardise Same Day Emergency Care (SDEC) services which were commissioned and expanded through the No More Silos programme.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance for an update on the completion of the Sectoral Study on the Cost of Doing Business in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: The Cost of Doing Business research being undertaken by the University Ulster Economic Policy Centre is on track to be delivered by the end of the month. The study was commissioned following concerns raised in the aftermath of the Chancellor’s Autumn Budget. Employers, understandably, have significant concerns regarding the impact of the changes in employers' National Insurance contributions coming into effect next month. That is in addition to other cost pressures that they already face, such as insurance and energy. To assess that, the research team has been undertaking its analysis and engaging widely, with around 25 sectoral and business bodies to date, in order to hear the voices of those potentially impacted. The report, when finalised, will put me in a position to provide as strong a case as possible to the Treasury ahead of the June Spending Review.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the initial budget for the creation of a new Urgent Care Centre within the Ulster Hospital; and (ii) the expected final cost.
answer_text: The initial budget for the creation of the new Urgent Care Centre at the Ulster Hospital was £5.042m.  The Urgent Care Centre project is projected to come in under budget at £4.910m.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for an update on her Department's engagement with NIE Networks in relation to goodwill payments for those who suffered from prolonged periods of electricity loss as a result of Storm Éowyn.
answer_text: Following disruption and damage caused by Storm Éowyn on 24th of January, a working group involving the Department for the Economy, NIE Networks, and the Utility Regulator, was established to consider compensation payments for electricity customers most affected by power outages. The working group was established at the request of the Executive to consider options where compensation payments would be recovered through customer electricity bills. The working group has now updated the Executive of its findings that there is currently no existing route to make payments to customers. NIE Networks has applied a severe weather exemption provided for in legislation under which customers can apply for compensation if they have been off electricity supply. The working group concluded that any other options involving recovery of payments from network charges would require legal or regulatory modifications which cannot be applied retrospectively for those customers impacted by Storm Éowyn.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for their assessment of the findings of the Individual Wellbeing in Northern Ireland Report 2023-24.
To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the Bangor Bus and Rail Station refurbishment programme.
answer_text: A proposal to commence a project for Bangor Station Refurbishment, has been submitted to my Department and is under consideration by officials.  Translink also plan, subject to budget availability, to progress with proposals for a secure cycle parking facility at Bangor Station in 2025/26.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the road maintenance budget allocation in each of the last five years, broken down by local council area.
answer_text: Details of total resource and capital Structural Maintenance expenditure on the road infrastructure by District Council for each of the last five financial years is set out in the Table below. question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the current number of full-time staff employed within the private streets staffing structure.
answer_text: The private streets staffing structure within my Department equates to 36 posts of which 29.5 are filled.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the current average waiting time for an Access NI check.
answer_text: The current average turnaround times for AccessNI checks are as follows:-Level of checkCurrent turnaround timeBasic Checks (through a Responsible Body)1 dayBasic Checks (direct from citizen)1 dayStandard Checks 1 dayEnhanced Checks 4 days** 	70% of Enhanced checks are completed in 1 day of receipt – the remaining 30% are referred to police for consideration.On a weekly basis, AccessNI publishes average turnaround times for all levels of check on the  nidirect website - details can be viewed at Costs and turnaround times | nidirect.

answer_text: Information for convictions at courts and out of court diversionary disposals for cases involving ‘possession of a class B drug with intention to supply’ offences that were dealt with in the period 2019 to 2023, the most recent five years for which information is available, as well as for the average length of any custodial period imposed, has been provided in the following tables:Table 1: Convictions at courts, and out-of-court diversionary disposals, for ‘possession of a Class B drug with intention to supply’ offences, 2019 to 2023YearConvictionsDiversions2019144*2020133620211896    2022171820231846Note:1. Figures for convictions relate to initial disposal at court only.  Results of appeals are not included. 2.
