question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any engagement his Department had with VisitBritain during the development of the Tourism Vision and Action Plan.
answer_text: The remit of VisitBritain supports the whole of Britain internationally in partnership with the national tourist boards for Scotland, Wales and London & Partners as well as with global industry and non-industry partners.  The north of Ireland is excluded from this remit.The membership of the Tourism Partnership Board was agreed in consultation with the Chair, Stephen McNally, CEO/Founder SERLS Hospitality Ltd.  It comprises representatives from the tourism and hospitality sectors, Executive departments and tourism agencies, including Tourism Ireland, which has responsibility for marketing the whole island of Ireland as a tourism destination in Britain and in overseas markets.For many years, Tourism NI has agreed an annual Memorandum of Understanding with Visit Britain incorporating collaborative corporate engagement and both consumer and business tourism promotional activities.  The Memorandum of Understanding includes an agreement for Tourism NI representation at senior Visit Britain and UK National Tourism Organisation levels, presenting valuable opportunities for engagement and consultation with these organisations. Such senior representation forums include Visit Britain Board meetings, Chief Executive meetings and Strategic Partner Board meetings. These forums are useful for engagement and discussions on strategic issues such as the development of the Tourism Action Plan.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy why there are no representatives from VisitBritain on the Tourism Partnership Board.
answer_text: The remit of VisitBritain supports the whole of Britain internationally in partnership with the national tourist boards for Scotland, Wales and London & Partners as well as with global industry and non-industry partners.  The north of Ireland is excluded from this remit.The membership of the Tourism Partnership Board was agreed in consultation with the Chair, Stephen McNally, CEO/Founder SERLS Hospitality Ltd.  It comprises representatives from the tourism and hospitality sectors, Executive departments and tourism agencies, including Tourism Ireland, which has responsibility for marketing the whole island of Ireland as a tourism destination in Britain and in overseas markets.For many years, Tourism NI has agreed an annual Memorandum of Understanding with Visit Britain incorporating collaborative corporate engagement and both consumer and business tourism promotional activities.  The Memorandum of Understanding includes an agreement for Tourism NI representation at senior Visit Britain and UK National Tourism Organisation levels, presenting valuable opportunities for engagement and consultation with these organisations. Such senior representation forums include Visit Britain Board meetings, Chief Executive meetings and Strategic Partner Board meetings. These forums are useful for engagement and discussions on strategic issues such as the development of the Tourism Action Plan.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to outline what action her Department is taking to mitigate the impact of the increase to employers' National Insurance contributions on hospitality businesses.
To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department has any plans to introduce bowel cancer screening for people over 50 years of age.
answer_text: The three-year strategic plan for health and social care was published on 10 December 2024 and includes an outcome to expand the bowel screening programme, subject to additional funding, by April 2027. Currently, in Northern Ireland, men and women aged 60-74 years old, who are registered with a GP, are eligible for bowel screening and automatically receive a home testing kit every two years. It is important to note that screening is a pathway, not just a ‘test.’ As such, where a Screening Programme makes changes to its eligibility, it is important that the associated infrastructure, to include diagnostic capacity and any treatments that may be required, are aligned.  Work to meet the commitments to lower the age range is ongoing but must be viewed within the context of wider financial and capacity challenges within the supporting services.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the number of road defects identified between 1 January 2024 and 31 December 2024, broken down by local council area.
answer_text: Road surface defect data dating back to 2008/2009 to date is regularly updated and publicly available on the OpenDataNI portal at https://www.opendatani.gov.uk/dataset/surface-defects.  The data is updated each evening and can be downloaded in various formats.  The information includes: spatial location details, road section name, local district council, surface defect type (e.g. potholes, cracking, depressions, rutting), date recorded, defect status, target response time, and actual completion date.   It is important to note that there can be a time lag between when each surface defect record is repaired and when this is updated on the maintenance management system.  While many roads would undoubtedly benefit from investment, due to a limited budget, resurfacing work must be taken forward through the prioritisation of those deemed most in need of intervention. In the meantime, officials in my Department will continue to undertake regular inspections and repair defects in accordance with the Department’s policy.

answer_text: The three-year strategic plan for health and social care was published on 10 December 2024 and includes an outcome to expand the bowel screening programme, subject to additional funding, by April 2027. question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for his assessment of the findings of the Economic and Social Research Institute and the National Institute of Economic and Social Research report on Northern Ireland’s predicted economic growth.
answer_text: I welcome this report and believe it will be helpful for understanding the economic linkages within the all-island economy and how they will affect our economic growth over the medium term. The ESRI forecast is in line with expected growth trends produced by other economic institutes such as the Ulster University Economic Policy Centre, specifically that growth is expected to be strong in the short term before levelling out towards the end of the decade.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) for an update on his Department’s road safety review of the A2 Bangor to Belfast dual carriageway; and (ii) to detail any actions implemented as a result of this review.
answer_text: Road safety remains an extremely high priority for my Department and we are committed to working proactively to make our roads safer and address the needs of all road users. My officials are in the process of reviewing the latest Road Traffic Collision data from the PSNI for accident cluster sites, including the A2 Bangor to Belfast dual carriageway, to identify if there are any common causation factors which would benefit from the introduction of effective road safety measures. Any engineering measures which are proposed will be considered for inclusion in a future work programme, subject to available funding.In the meantime, I am pleased to advise that my officials are taking forward the cleaning of safety signage along the A2, as well as cutting back any overgrown vegetation. Action is also being taken to resolve reliability issues with the signalised junctions at Ballysallagh and Seahill. One of the best ways to ensure the safety of our roads and fulfil our long-term goal of eliminating death and serious injury on roads by 2050 is by changing driver behaviour.  As road users we all have a personal responsibility to behave in a way that keeps ourselves and others safe.  The sad reality is that if we do not change our own attitudes when we are using the roads, our death toll will continue to increase.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail (i) the current average time taken for NIEA to respond to a planning application; and (ii) the current longest period a planning application has been awaiting a response from NIEA.
answer_text: From 1 April 2024 to 16 January 2025 the average time taken for NIEA to respond to a planning application is 20 days.  The longest period a planning application has been awaiting a response from NIEA is 469 days (as of 16 January 2025).  It was received on 05 October 2023.  This case is an anomaly given the complexities involved in this development. Most delayed casework dates from 09 April 2024, which is the date on which the second longest application awaiting a response was received.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the current average waiting time for a domiciliary care package, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: My department does not hold this information for either of your requests and the HSC Trusts also do not hold this information.  What I can inform you is that as soon as an individual is assessed as medically fit for discharge from hospital, the HSC Trusts prioritise these patients for discharge. The Trusts will fully utilise both their internal homecare service as well as the independent sector homecare provision and this will include optimisation of the rapid response homecare service provision which is specifically designed to support with hospital discharges.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any engagement his Department has had with the UK Government in relation to ensuring Northern Ireland benefits from the AI Opportunities Action Plan.
answer_text: I met and discussed the AI Opportunities Action Plan with Peter Kyle MP, the Secretary of State for Science, Innovation & Technology on 8 January 2025. Our officials will work together as we seek to maximise the opportunities outlined in the Action Plan for the North.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the timescale for the opening of a new urgent care centre in the Ulster Hospital.
answer_text: The South Eastern Trust has advised the Department that the new Urgent Care Centre will open in June following a short period of commissioning of relevant and necessary clinical equipment.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the average waiting time for a domiciliary care package for a patient being discharged from the Ulster Hospital, in each of the last 12 months.
answer_text: My department does not hold this information for either of your requests and the HSC Trusts also do not hold this information.  What I can inform you is that as soon as an individual is assessed as medically fit for discharge from hospital, the HSC Trusts prioritise these patients for discharge. The Trusts will fully utilise both their internal homecare service as well as the independent sector homecare provision and this will include optimisation of the rapid response homecare service provision which is specifically designed to support with hospital discharges.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) any strategic interventions contained in his Department’s Road Safety Action Plan 2024-25 that are yet to be progressed; and (ii) any strategic interventions his Department does not expect to progress during 2025.
answer_text: All Strategic Interventions in the 2024-25 action plan are progressing.  A report summarising the progress made throughout 2024-25 will be available Summer 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the introduction of a School Uniforms Bill.
To ask the Minister of Justice for her assessment of the current gaps in legislation in relation to the misuse of deepfake technology.
answer_text: The fast pace of technological advances has led to the increasing prevalence of online abuse, mainly against women and girls. This is particularly evident in the making of sexually explicit deepfake images, behaviour which has a devastating impact on its victims. Currently, such behaviour is not a criminal offence. The UK Government has announced that it plans to bring forward, in a Crime and Policing Bill, proposals to make it an offence to create these images. I have already been approached by the Minister responsible, the Minister of State for Policing, Fire and Crime Prevention, on a possible extension of the offence to Northern Ireland. Subject to the agreement of the Executive, the Justice Committee, and the Assembly, I intend to take this opportunity to ensure there is similar provision here by extending the offence to Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for an update on the appointment of a new Commissioner for Older People for Northern Ireland.
answer_text: We recognise that the role of Commissioner is vital in safeguarding and promoting the interests of older people here. We are pleased to report that the competition to appoint a new Commissioner for Older People is in the final stages.In accordance with the Commissioner for Public Appointments for Northern Ireland (CPANI) Code of Practice, we chose to meet with appointable candidates prior to a final decision. These meetings took place earlier this month.An announcement regarding the successful candidate will be made shortly, following the completion of the usual pre-appointment formalities.We want to thank the previous Commissioner, Eddie Lynch for his commitment to this post and for everything he has done to promote the rights of older people.

answer_text: Vehicle Excise Duty (Motor Tax) is an excepted matter that is administered by the Driver and Vehicle Licensing Agency (DVLA) in Swansea on behalf of HM Treasury.If you wish to request this information from the DVLA you can contact them using the following dedicated email address for elected representatives at DVLAMinisterials@dvla.gov.uk.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) his Department’s policy in relation to the gritting of footpaths; and (ii) whether his Department is considering any changes to this policy.
answer_text: The current winter service policy and procedures operated by the Department utilises the widely accepted practice of targeting limited resources for this service on the higher trafficked main through routes. The application of this policy means that 28% of the total road network, which carries around 80% of traffic, is salted. It must, however, be emphasised that the service provided cannot eliminate the risks and even on roads that are salted, ice free conditions cannot be guaranteed. My Department has no statutory duty to salt footways, however where resources permit, will work with Councils during prolonged periods of snow and ice to clear footways in busy town centres.At this time, there are no plans to make changes to this policy.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the number of late licensing penalties issued for untaxed vehicles in each of the last five years, broken down by local council area.
answer_text: Vehicle Excise Duty (Motor Tax) is an excepted matter that is administered by the Driver and Vehicle Licensing Agency (DVLA) in Swansea on behalf of HM Treasury. If you wish to request this information from the DVLA you can contact them using the following dedicated email address for elected representatives at DVLAMinisterials@dvla.gov.uk.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail his Department’s capital spending on tourism in North Down in the (i) 2023-24; and (ii) 2024-25 financial year.
answer_text: Tourism NI works closely with the tourism industry in North Down and across the North to support the development of engaging and immersive tourism experiences.Tourism NI’s Marketing Division provide significant platforms to the North Down tourism industry, featuring products, experiences and events on discovernorthernireland.com. Tourism NI’s advertising campaigns, PR, social and digital activity drive traffic to the website. The team also support in terms of providing industry platforms such as Meet The Buyer bringing industry and operators together to do business.No open capital funding schemes for the tourism industry were run across the North within the 2023-24 and 2024-25 financial periods.However, between 2020 and 2023, Tourism NI provided a combined total of approx. £285k of capital funding to eight tourism businesses in the North Down area as part of their Experience and Website Development funding programmes.  In addition, in 2021-22 and 2022-23 periods, Tourism NI provided a total of approx. £170k revenue funding to Ards & North Down Borough Council as part of a Market Led Product Development programme, and which benefitted several tourism businesses in the North Down area. Tourism NI’s Business Solutions team do not provide direct funding to tourism operators; however, they do provide dedicated sales and marketing opportunities and support and work with range of stakeholders to deliver an annual programme of familiarisation visits to showcase the North to a range of international buyers.  During the period of 1st April 2023 – 31st March 2024 North Down were featured on 4 Familiarisation trips and from 1st April 2024 – 31st March 2025 North Down were featured on 2 Familiarisation trips, together approx. 70 operators visited the region during these periods.Tourism NI also deliver and recruit for a series of events throughout the year, which take place in the North and in market providing our industry with platforms to engage with buyers directly including the flagship Meet the Buyer event and the ITOA Workshop. During the period of 1st April 2023 – 31st March 2024 Ards and North Down Council have taken part in 5 platforms including ITOA, Meet the Buyer, World Travel Market, France virtual workshop and Tourism NI evening in Dublin and from 1st April 2024 – 31st March 2025 have taken part/plan to in 3 platforms including ITOA, World Travel Market and Meet the Buyer.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for an update on the proposed Dilapidation Bill.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the potential additional roll-out of dementia safeguarding scheme wristbands in the Ards and North Down Borough Council area.
answer_text: My officials maintain contact with the Locality Planning Department within Ards and North Down Council regarding roll out of this scheme in the area.The Council have continued to work on this and have established a working group (with key partners including PSNI, HSC staff and a representative from the Police and Community Safety Partnership). The group are exploring funding options and are striving to progress this scheme in the North Down and Ards area.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail any plans his Department has to introduce legislation on age discrimination in relation to goods, facilities and services.
answer_text: No one in our society should be discriminated against because of their age. Advice on this issue is currently under review.Junior Ministers met with Claire Sugden MLA to discuss her proposed Age Goods, Facilities and Services Bill and the challenges in bringing forward legislation on this issue.  Development of any age discrimination laws will require careful consideration and work to inform their scope, as well as required exemptions.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail his Department’s planned spending on road safety advertising during the 2024-25 financial year.
answer_text: The Department’s total planned expenditure for Road Safety Advertising in 2024-25 is £650k.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of incidents of (i) drug dealing; and (ii) illegal drug use recorded at hospital buildings in each of the last five years, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: Information on the number of reports of illegal drug dealing and illegal drug use on hospital premises is not routinely kept by HSC Trusts.  Three Trusts have, however, been able to give an indication as to the prevalence of illegal drug dealing activity on HSC premises.South Eastern HSC Trust, Western HSC Trust and Northern HSC Trust information is shown in the table below:Illegal drug dealing recorded at hospital buildingsTrustYearNumber of incidents of intent to supply a controlled drug on hospital premisesSouth Eastern Trust2019/20Two separate alleged incidents of intent to supply a controlled drug to a patient. PSNI informed2020/21Zero incidents.   2021/22Two separate incidents of intent to supply a controlled drug to a patient. PSNI informed2022/23Zero incidents.    2023/24One incident of alleged criminality on hospital premises related to the supply of controlled drugs. Under investigation by PSNINorthern Trust2019/20Two reports2020/21One report2021/22Three reports2022/23Three reports2023/24Four reports Western Trust2019/20None2020/21None2021/22None2022/23None2023/24NoneIllegal drug use recorded at hospital buildingsAll HSC Trusts noted that information pertaining to illegal drug use on hospital grounds is not routinely captured and cannot be provided. Any potential criminal activity on Trust premises is reported to the PSNI who should be approached for further information on this matter.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice (i) for her assessment of the impact the current Barrister strike will have on access to justice; and (ii) to detail any action her Department is taking to prevent further industrial action.
answer_text: It is difficult to immediatiately assess the impact of the prolonged withdrawal of services by the Criminal Bar Association on access to justice but there is no doubt the action will exacerbate the existing delays we have been working hard with justice system partners to address and, more significantly, will impact on the victims and witnesses who are waiting to give evidence and receive justice. The uncertainty caused by the withdrawal will only add to the stress and anxiety they may already be experiencing.The withdrawal is particularly disappointing because my Department has been engaging with professions on an ongoing basis since the outset of the access to justice reform programme. I also met the representatives of the professional bodies in advance of my statement to the Assembly in December which highlighted my intention to implement an uplift in fees as soon as possible. Officials met again with representatives of the Bar and CBA when the latest withdrawal of services was announced in the press at the end of December and I am, of course, continuing engagement in an effort to address the concerns of the CBA insofar as is possible and in the interests of ending the withdrawal of services and averting further action and the consequent disproportionate impact on victims and witnesses.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any action his Department is taking to improve access to face-to-face GP appointments as part of the Health and Social Care Three Year Plan.
answer_text: I recognise the importance of access to GP services for patients.  In response to increasing patient demand GPs are utilising a mixed consultation model with telephone, video and face to face consultations as a means of managing the rising demand on services within the existing workforce while retaining responsiveness to those patients who need the most urgent attention and care.There are approximately 200k consultations across general practice in Northern Ireland each week, with over 50% of those delivered face to face.As independent contractors, and clinical professionals, GPs are responsible for making appropriate decisions about the treatment, care and management of individual patients.  This also means that decisions about the day to day running of the surgery, including the use of technology, telephony and the appointment system, are determined locally by the GP practice within the constraints of their available workforce.As stated in the Health and Social Care Three Year Plan, long-term improvement and increasing capacity to better meet the needs of our citizens and closing the gap between demand and capacity will require reform and transformation, as we move health care closer to the individual and support early intervention across primary care. One of the key strategies related to this is that by April 2027 we will have completed a review of the funding model for General Medical Services, to ensure that the service is sustainable for the future and improve access for patients, as part of a wider agenda to increase the level of investment in Primary Care.Another is that by April 2025 I will publish a plan for the completion of the Primary Care Multi-Disciplinary Team model across all areas of Northern Ireland and, subject to additional funding being secured, by April 2027 approximately 50% of the population will have access to MDTs with the rest to follow as a priority.A range of additional measures have already been put in place to support primary care to adapt flexibly to increasing demand.  These include, reinforcing the GP workforce, increasing the range of healthcare professionals working in primary care, investment in general practice and the implementation of new and innovative ways of working, including making greater use of technology and telephony.  As part of supporting work in this area, my Department has also established a GP Access Working Group to explore issues relating to access to services and develop a programme of work to help address these and improve the experience for patients. As part of this, work has commenced on developing guidance for practices in management of patient demand and optimising workflow within practices to maximise efficiency.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether his Department is considering additional actions to reduce the impact of traffic congestion when works to the Sydenham bypass recommence.
answer_text: The Department recognises the inconvenience caused by traffic congestion during the essential works on the Sydenham Bypass. These works are a vital investment in improving the long-term safety and resilience of this key transport corridor, and careful planning has been undertaken to manage the impact on road users as far as possible.Works are currently suspended during the Christmas embargo period, with the road reopened from 25 November 2024 to minimise disruption during the busy holiday season.Works are planned to recommence on 6 January 2025 and will be completed primarily with overnight weekday closures and a number of weekend closures. Introducing additional restrictions on the contractor, such as limiting work to specific times or excluding daytime hours on weekends, would significantly increase the cost of the project and prolong its duration. The current arrangements represent a balance between minimising disruption to the public and delivering the scheme in a cost-effective and efficient manner.The Department remains committed to monitoring the progress of these works and ensuring that disruption to road users is minimised within the constraints of the project. On completion, the benefits of this scheme will significantly outweigh the temporary inconvenience caused by the works.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the introduction of an Adult Protection Bill.
answer_text: The draft Bill is largely complete, though discussions continue around one outstanding policy issue.  However, it is not thought that the outstanding issue would impact introduction of the draft Bill as amendments could be made later, if necessary.My officials are currently engaging with Departmental Finance and Economists around the approval of the business case to support the draft Bill.  Following approval of the business case, the draft Bill will be submitted to the Executive for their consideration in advance of introduction.It is intended that the draft Bill will be introduced to the Northern Ireland Assembly as soon as possible, following the completion of this process.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail the expected timescale for the appointment of a new Commissioner for Older People.
answer_text: A public appointment competition for the role of Commissioner for Older People for Northern Ireland is well progressed, and an appointment decision will be announced in due course.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any action his Department is taking to support the poultry sector following the confirmed case of bird flu in the Republic of Ireland.
answer_text: I am aware of the confirmed case of Highly Pathogenic Avian Influenza (HPAI) which was recently detected in a wild bird in the Republic of Ireland. Further, I can advise that my Department is currently implementing a number of measures to ensure the poultry sector in Northern Ireland is prepared and protected against the potential of any HPAI incursion here.Officials have been undertaking a comprehensive communication campaign across DAERA social media channels and other external media to highlight the three key messages of reporting dead wild birds, registering kept birds and the utmost importance of biosecurity. The key messages have been disseminated to stakeholders, local councils and other public bodies including NICS departments. Furthermore, detailed biosecurity guidance and a checklist has been provided on the DAERA website.  There has also been regular engagement with stakeholders, the most recent meeting having taken place on Thursday 19 December 2024.Officials will continue to engage with colleagues across the UK and ROI to monitor the situation to determine next steps as appropriate.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for an update on the creation of Local Economic Partnerships.
answer_text: My officials have shared guidance and held subsequent meetings with all 11 councils, who are on track to have their Local Economic Partnerships operational by 1st April 2025. It will be for each Partnership to identify and agree on actions that support delivery of their economic priorities within their areas. My department and Invest NI will support the LEPs in identifying projects over the coming months.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether improved measures to recover unpaid domestic rates are being considered as part of her review of domestic rates.
answer_text: Although not specifically part of the review I announced, my Department continuously seeks opportunities to make improvements in the administration of the rates system, including those that drive efficiencies. Alongside well established and robust billing, collection and recovery procedures, Land & Property Services carefully manage the recovery of unpaid rates to maximise revenue collection and reduce revenue loss.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the five new GP Federation areas to benefit from further investment in implementing the multidisciplinary team model, as outlined in his statement to the Assembly on 10 December 2024.
answer_text: A road map for the full implementation of the MDT model across all 17 Federation areas was developed and approved by Minister Swann in March 2022. The roadmap ensures that the benefits of implementation of the model are as equally distributed as possible, while being mindful of current challenging workforce and financial constraints.The proposed order of the next five GP Federation areas for roll out of the multidisciplinary team model are South West, North Belfast, East Antrim, Armagh & Dungannon and Craigavon.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the total amount of funding allocated to the creation of Local Economic Partnerships, broken down by local council area.
answer_text: Local Economic Partnerships will be supported by dedicated funding of £45m. I will announce the funding allocation for each area in January 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health how the £17 million allocated to mental health services, as outlined in his statement to the Assembly on 10 December 2024, will be allocated among the Health and Social Care Trusts.
answer_text: Mental health remains a priority for me but to date we have not been able to secure the additional funding required to deliver the ambition set out in the Mental Health Strategy 2021-2031. My three-year plan commits to an additional £17million to support priority areas under the Strategy which will make the greatest difference.  This includes greater integration of the voluntary and community sector in service delivery and improving services, including crisis interventions and the new Regional Mental Health Service.  However, I must reiterate that this commitment is subject to the Department receiving additional funding.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance why she has not progressed the NI Water staff pay award for 2023-24.
answer_text: You will be aware that this issue has been satisfactorily resolved and industrial action has been averted.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any action his Department is taking to mitigate the impact of NI Water staff industrial action.
answer_text: Mitigating against the impacts of industrial action is an operational matter for NI Water, however in terms of my Department’s role, I can advise that all necessary approvals are now in place for NI Water’s pay award for 2023-24 and that the Unions have now suspended their action and will ballot their members on the pay offer. This has reduced the risk of any imminent industrial action, however my Department will continue to monitor the position and take action as necessary to protect essential public services.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for his assessment of the potential benefits of the RAISE Programme in (i) Donaghadee; and (ii) Millisle.
answer_text: For disadvantaged communities in Millisle and Donaghadee the RAISE Programme presents an exciting opportunity to tackle educational disadvantage and embed innovative ways of collaborative working between schools, communities, families and wider partners.The Programme will enable the development and delivery of a bespoke Strategic Area Plan.  This will be co-designed by schools and other settings, families/carers and others with a valuable role to play in supporting children and young people’s learning in the locality.Engagement is currently underway with key stakeholders serving children and young people in the area.  This will inform the Strategic Area Plan by identifying the specific problems they are facing locally.  The Plan will set out a range of actions to achieve the strategic objectives of raising achievement and addressing educational disadvantage, and also of raising aspiration and increasing the value of a whole community approach to education.Ultimately this will help to deliver in Millisle and Donaghadee on the mission set by the report ‘A Fair Start’ to ensure all children and young people regardless of background are given the best start in life.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether she is considering any additional rates support for businesses in light of increases to employer National Insurance contributions.
answer_text: On 9 December I announced my plans to commission research into the cost of doing business. That work, prompted by the issues raised since the Chancellor’s Autumn Budget, is required to establish an objective evidence base for policy work. I have also announced a strategic review of the Small Business Rate Relief scheme in 2025/26. The review will take place as part of an overarching Strategic Review Cycle across the full suite of rates supports. That broader process is required to ensure that rate support is fully aligned to Executive priorities.Rates remain our only substantive income-generating lever for public services. The more revenue spent on rate support, the less there is for vital public services.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail (i) any support her Department is providing to the children’s daycare sector; and (ii) whether she intends to review support of the children’s daycare sector in light of increases to employer National Insurance contributions.
answer_text: For the answer to both parts (i) and (ii) of this question, I would refer the member to my answer to AQW 17520/22-27.In relation to part (ii), on 9 December I also announced my plan to commission research into the cost of doing business here. That work will have a particular focus on the impact of planned additional employer National Insurance contributions. The sectors most impacted on will be engaged in that work, and the findings will help inform wider Executive decision-making.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the number of schools in North Down that will receive (i) new desktop computers; and (ii) funding for outdoor play equipment.
answer_text: All post primary schools in North Down will receive an allocation of desktop computers to replace those which have reached their end of life to ensure that students have access to secure, modern technology.  (ii) Every grant-aided pre-school, nursery and primary school in North Down and across Northern Ireland will receive funding for outdoor play equipment.  The level of funding received will be dependent on pupil numbers and details of the scheme will be provided to schools and settings over coming weeks.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the longest time anyone has waited for a (i) hip replacement; and (ii) knee replacement, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.
To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the anticipated cost to her Department associated with implementing an uplift across criminal, civil and family cases.
answer_text: Work is ongoing on the business case for the Enabling Access to Justice Programme.  Proposals will also be subject to detailed consultation and so the final shape of the Programme and full costs will crystalise over time but at this juncture it is anticipated the uplift in fees will cost approximately £9.5million. The Programme aim is, however, to better ensure sustainability and my expectation is that any increases in costs will be offset by savings realised through other aspects of reform as the Programme progresses.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the expected timescale for the application process to provide outdoor covered sports spaces across 20 primary schools.
answer_text: The application process for the pilot project to provide outdoor covered sports spaces for 20 primary schools will be launched by my department in the new year.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, in light of the levels of untreated sewage in Belfast Lough, to detail any action his Department is taking to address the harmful levels of bacteria in the lough.
answer_text: The water quality issues in Inner Belfast Lough have been known for some time and were a catalyst for The Living With Water in Belfast Plan, which was published by my Department in November 2021, following Executive endorsement. The Plan aims to help address the increasing demands on the city’s wastewater and drainage network caused by population growth and increase in commerce, as well as the more frequent extreme weather events resulting from climate change.A review of the Belfast Plan was commissioned due to a rise in the estimated costs to deliver it, from £1.4bn to £2.1bn. The review has concluded that the Belfast Plan is still needed, however, the original 12-year delivery timeframe is no longer viable. Projects in the Plan, which include proposals to improve the water quality in Belfast Lough, will now be taken forward by my Department and NI Water as normal business, at a scale and pace achievable within available budgets rather than through a structured programme. It was always accepted that additional funding would be required to deliver the Plan.  In the New Decade New Approach agreement, Living With Water was identified as a beneficiary of increased funding, however, this was not received.I remain committed to the Belfast Plan objectives to help protect against flooding, to enhance the environment and to grow the economy and will continue to make the case for additional funding to expedite its delivery.  In this financial year, I have provided NI Water with c£0.5billion funding.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any consideration his Department has given to an eradication programme that addresses all forms of bovine TB infection.
answer_text: Bovine Tuberculosis (bTB) is one of the most challenging issues faced by cattle farmers in Northern Ireland.  With herd incidence currently at 10.41%, as of end of October 2024, it is essential that we reassess our approach to get disease levels lower and set us on a path towards eradicating the disease. At my request, the Chief Veterinary Officer (CVO) has undertaken a comprehensive review of bTB in Northern Ireland which considers fully all of the factors which are contributing to our current high rate of disease. The CVO has put forward almost 40 proposals across three key pillars – ensuring that all those with an interest in, or who are affected by bTB, work in partnership to reduce disease levels; measures to limit and prevent the spread of TB from cattle to cattle; and proposals to address the transmission of bTB from wildlife to cattle.  New policy proposals within the CVO’s review will be subject to further policy development and public consultation where appropriate.This review has been published and can be found using the link below:https://www.daera-ni.gov.uk/publications/chief-veterinary-officer-review-bovine-tuberculosis-northern-ireland-november-2024.However, I have already accepted his recommendation that a new bovine TB Partnership Steering Group be established.  I have asked my officials to have this in place by January 2025 to consider carefully the CVO’s proposals and assist the department, in a spirit of co-design, to develop a delivery plan for my consideration by March 2025.  This delivery plan should clearly set out our approach and the actions to be taken, across both government and industry, to tackle bTB in the time ahead.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (i) for his assessment of media reports on the presence of harmful bacteria above the legal limits for bathing water within Belfast Lough; and (ii) to detail any action his Department is taking to address these reports.
answer_text: I have not seen the data behind the media reports at this stage however I am on the record in expressing my serious concern about the level of sewage pollution in Belfast LoughMy Department conducts a bathing water monitoring during the season, which runs from 1 June to 15 September each year.  The programme covers a total of 26 identified sites and a further 7 candidate sites.  Five of those sites lie within Belfast Lough, at Helen’s Bay, Crawfordsburn, Ballyholme and the new candidate site at Brompton. Results can be found at https://www.daera-ni.gov.uk/articles/about-bathing-water-quality  I announced the 2024 results on 29 October.   Helen’s Bay, Crawfordsburn and Groomsport met the highest ‘Excellent’ standards and Ballyholme and Brompton met the ‘Sufficient’ standards.  Classifications are typically based on 80 samples taken over 4 years.  At this point, there are only 40 samples from the candidate site at Brompton, where there are two years of sampling to date.  Within the sampling programme, when a higher bacterial count is detected, my officials work with the Bathing Water Operator to post ‘advice against bathing’ at the site.  This is to allow for resampling and investigation into the source of the pollution.  The advice is updated within 24 hours and specific studies are carried out at the more vulnerable bathing waters like Ballyholme.  My Department also gives general advice about not bathing for 48 hours after heavy rainfall.  All data is posted on both the DAERA and NI Direct websites to enable bathers to make informed choices about the sites that are more vulnerable to pollution in wet weather.The Northern Ireland Environment Agency (NIEA) regulates sewage discharges to the water environment, including Belfast Lough, through the issue of Water Order Discharge Consents under the Water Order (as amended) 1999. The NIEA is engaged with NI Water on its programme to install storm overflow monitors, known as Event Duration Monitors (EDMs) which will improve monitoring at Combined Sewer Overflows and Emergency Overflows. DAERA also has a statutory obligation under the Urban Waste Water Treatment Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2007 to designate sensitive areas.  These are areas in need of protection through the provision of additional treatment at sewage treatment plants whose discharges impact the relevant waters. I have already indicated that, subject to the required Executive approval, I plan to designate the Inner Belfast Lough Shellfish Water Protected Area as a sensitive area under the 2007 Regulations.  This will assist in improving water quality in Inner Belfast Lough.  I have asked officials to progress this work as quickly as possible.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the number of preschools moving from part-time to full-time provision in this budgetary year, broken down by constituency.
answer_text: As a result of work being undertaken in the current financial year, I have approved 77 pre-school settings to transition to deliver the standardised pre-school education session length from September 2025.  Details of all settings approved thus far to transition have been published on the Department of Education website at Minister’s Decisions on Pre-School Settings Requesting Transition from September 2025 | Department of EducationIn addition, a further list of settings that had requested to transition from September 2025 are published on the website at Tranche three - Pre-School Settings Requesting Transition From September 2025 (V2).pdf.  Consultation on this tranche of settings will close on 13th December and I expect to make decisions on these settings shortly thereafter.It will take a number of years for all pre-schools to transition to the standardised session length. The speed of transition will largely be dependent on the resources available from the Executive at each stage, as well as the circumstances of individual pre-school settings.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance for her assessment of the efficiency of current procurement policy.
answer_text: ANSWER	The NI Audit Office published a report on Public Procurement in April 2023 with the corresponding Public Accounts Committee  report published in November 2024.  	I am considering the Committee’s recommendations fully and plan to bring a detailed Memorandum of Reply to the Executive for approval on these cross cutting matters.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail his Department’s industrial workforce vacancy rate in each of the last five years, broken down by division.
answer_text: My Department has been operating in a difficult financial environment for a number of years due to underfunding and austerity by the British Government. This has had a significant impact on my department’s ability to deliver essential services, and to fill vacant posts. NICSHR has responsibility for recruitment for the NICS. When a department identifies a vacant post and where the funding for the post has been approved, the request is referred to the Department of Finance, NICSHR to initiate the recruitment process.NICSHR does not hold the data regarding  the industrial workforce vacancy rate in each of the last five years, broken down by division, but have provided the number of staff vacancies at Industrial Grades in the Department of Infrastructure held at the beginning of each of the last five financial years, broken down by Division (Group).  Number of vacancies by Division(Group)Financial YearRoads and RiversTRAMRiversNumber of vacancies2020/2149492021/2286862022/2376762023/2498982024/25342458Due to Departmental restructuring, please note that until March 2024 Roads and Rivers were centrally recorded as one Division.  The system was amended in April 2024 to record both Divisions separately as DfI Transport and Roads Asset Management (TRAM) and DfI Rivers.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether he will commit to ensuring that any additional roll-out of the part-time 20 mph speed limit zones at schools scheme will be regionally balanced in terms of schools selected.
answer_text: In recent years there have been two tranches of part-time 20mph speed limits which introduced these measures at 216 schools and represented an investment of about £4m which was distributed equally between the four Divisions.  The review of this initiative recommends that the Department should provide a third tranche of part-time 20 mph speed limits at schools however this will be more modest in size and is dependent on the availability of funding.   The review report also concludes that a more targeted approach is needed to ensure that the correct safety intervention is selected.  While I would be keen to have a regional balance, I think it is more important that those schools in greatest need of safety measures are treated as this will achieve the best outcomes in terms of road safety.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of people receiving treatment for gambling addiction in each of the last five years, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: Currently, there are no commissioned treatment services for gambling addiction within Health and Social Care Trusts. Therefore, the Trusts do not capture data in relation to those who report with problem gambling or gambling addiction.However, individuals who present with a primary diagnosis of mental health and/or drug/alcohol addiction may also receive support for a coexisting gambling addiction within current Trust services or can be signposted to support services offered through the community and voluntary sector.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on his Department’s roll-out of NHS-funded community audiology services.
To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to provide a timescale on his Department’s decision to progress an additional tranche of the part-time 20 mph speed limit zones at schools.
answer_text: Potential funding requirements for progressing an additional tranche of part -time 20 mph speed limits at schools will be included within budget planning considerations for the next financial year.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of people waiting for hearing care appointments in each of the last five years, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.
To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for a timescale on when the Reform and Transformation Unit will become operational.

To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of community and voluntary sector organisations that have received funding through his Department’s Core Grant Funding Scheme in each of the last five years, broken down by constituency.
answer_text: Details of the number of organisations that have received funding through the core Grant Funding Scheme since 2020/2021 are provided in the table below. The Department does not routinely collect information at a constituency level and therefore details of funding provided to organisations is only available at a regional level.YearNumber of Organisations2020/2021642021/2022642022/2023642023/2024622024/202561

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any consideration his Department has given to the creation of additional women’s health hubs.
answer_text: While there are a number of different approaches to ‘women’s health hubs’ across the UK, the concept aims to provide a range of services with rapid, local access within a single visit, provide assessment and treatment where possible and reduce referrals into elective waiting lists. In Northern Ireland, Health and Social Care Trusts have established ambulatory care models with a focus on gynaecology and early pregnancy services, which can reduce presentations at Emergency Departments and referrals to gynae waiting lists. Through the above regional planning structures, it is envisaged that further work to develop appropriate service models and enhance existing services across NI Trusts will be progressed through 2025, subject to available staff capacity and resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for an update on the creation of a Creative Innovation Hub in Holywood.
answer_text: The Outline Business Case presented by Ards & North Down Borough Council for the development of a Creative Innovation Hub (known as ‘The Shed’) to be based in Holywood, has been approved by my Department. A Departmental Approval letter has recently issued to officials in the Belfast Region City Deal and the Council.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the total spend on temporary staffing within the South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust in each of the last five years.
answer_text: Trust expenditure on temporary staff has been incurred to ensure that safe and effective services are sustained and contributes importantly to maintaining service provision to patients and clients. HSC Trusts employ temporary staff for several reasons, for example, cover for sickness and maternity/paternity leave; cover for existing vacancies; and when demand increases over the winter months.Table 1 illustrates the total expenditure on temporary staffing by South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust for the financial years between 2019/20 and 2023/24.Table 1 - Total Expenditure by Trust for Temporary Staff, 2019/20-2023/24 2019/202020/212021/222022/232023/24 £££££SEHSCT31,220,60347,138,525  55,442,38362,334,07172,395,454

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure how the £1 million allocated to gully clearing as part of the October Monitoring Round will be allocated among divisions.
answer_text: The additional funding allocated to my Department through the October Monitoring Round has enabled me to fund a £1m targeted intervention for gully cleaning and drainage maintenance by means of cleaning and desilting of lines and manholes.Work has started on the planning and delivery of this work for delivery this financial year. Initially, £200k has been allocated to each Division however, the development of the programme is on-going and so the remaining £200k is being held to fund high priority sites as they are identified.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any consideration his Department has given to commissioning an independent expert-led review of how NI Water is funded.
answer_text: My Department has previously carried out considerable work on the funding and governance options for NI Water, and officials continue to engage with the company within my policy framework.  The working group established to oversee the previous work comprised corporate finance and public expenditure experts from the Strategic Investment Board, Department of Finance and Department for Infrastructure.  The group was also attended and advised by water sector industry experts from the Utility Regulator and NI Water.   Alongside this work, I am progressing a three-pronged approach in (i) continuing to work with Executive colleagues to try and increase wastewater investment; (ii) reviewing the policy and legislation around developer contributions; and (iii) in securing Executive agreement for the Water, Flooding and Sustainable Drainage Bill to be introduced to the Assembly

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the number of Article 161 Agreements in North Down in each of the last five years.
answer_text: The management of public wastewater infrastructure is an operational matter for NI Water. However, I have sought the requested information from NI Water and been advised that there have been 39 Article 161 Agreements in the North Down constituency over the last five years, with the number for each year shown below. Year Number of Article 161 Agreements Authorised in North Down constituency2020 14 2021 10 2022 10 2023 4 2024 1 For any future queries relating to operational matters, you should contact NI Water directly, through the Elected Representatives Hotline on 0345 300 6461 or through waterline@niwater.com.  Making direct contact with NI Water will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of limited public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any additional road maintenance projects to be carried out as a result of October Monitoring Round funding, broken down by constituency.
answer_text: The additional funding allocated to my Department through the October Monitoring Round includes almost £19m towards essential and structural maintenance of our roads and street lighting work to progress existing and new schemes.Work has started on the planning and delivery of new schemes for delivery this financial year. However, the development of the programmes is on-going so full breakdown of these is not yet available.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on his Department’s consultation on the School Uniform Policy.
answer_text: I launched a public consultation in June to gather views on a range of proposals to ensure school uniforms are affordable. The consultation closed on 27 September, with just under 7,500 responses received. Analysis of the responses is being finalised, with a report on the outcome of the consultation to be published shortly.It is clear that there is strong support for school uniform policy guidance to be made statutory. I have written to Executive colleagues to seek agreement to introduce a School Uniforms Bill to this effect, as soon as practicably possible in the coming weeks.It will then follow due process and scrutiny through the Committee and Assembly stages, and I look forward to working with Assembly colleagues to progress this legislation and make a difference for families most in need of our assistance.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for his assessment of the level of funding required to move away from a limited service of roads maintenance.
answer_text: My Department’s resource budget, which is used to deliver day-to-day maintenance activities such as patching and grass cutting, has been under pressure for almost a decade as a result of Tory cuts and austerity. In response, my Department has had to reduce its routine road maintenance activities to a limited service, which prioritises delivery of essential services for maintaining public safety and protecting the transport network. Consequently, only the highest priority defects across the network are currently being repaired.The level of repairs that can be carried out in any financial year depends on the funding allocated and until such times as sufficient funding is made available, it will not be possible to change the current level of service. It is estimated that c.£64m would be required for my Department to deliver normal Essential Maintenance service levels.  The value of our infrastructure cannot be overstated, and I will continue to work with Executive colleagues to ensure appropriate investment is made.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of mental health nurses responding to 999 calls, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: There are currently 16 Mental Health nurses from the South Eastern Trust (SEHSCT) area on the Northern Ireland Ambulance Service (NIAS) rota to provide Hear & Treat Mental Health services within the Integrated Clinical Hub as part of NIAS’ 999 response. The service is currently operational during peak times, with mental health professionals available in ambulance control from 6pm to 2am on Friday to Sunday evenings.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the benefits of having mental health nurses dealing with mental health-related 999 calls.
To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the delivery of the Belfast Living With Water Programme.
answer_text: Following an increase in the estimated costs to deliver the Living With Water in Belfast Plan from £1.4bn to £2.1bn, my Department carried out an affordability review of the Plan. As reported to Executive colleagues and the Committee for Infrastructure in October 2024, the Review has concluded that the Belfast Plan still needs to be delivered but that the original 12 year delivery timeframe is no longer viable. Projects in the Plan will now be taken forward by my Department and NI Water as normal business at a scale and pace achievable within available budgets rather than through a structured programme. It was always accepted that additional funding would be required to deliver the Plan.  In the New Decade New Approach agreement, Living With Water was identified as a beneficiary of increased funding from the British Government’s infrastructure revolution, under the title of “Turbo Charging Infrastructure”, however, this was not received.I remain committed to the Belfast Plan objectives to help protect against flooding, to enhance the environment and to grow the economy and will continue to make the case for additional funding to expedite its delivery.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail his Department’s engagement with Post Office UK in relation to potential branch closures and job losses.
answer_text: Under the Northern Ireland Act, postal services are a reserved matter and come under the responsibility of the Department for Business and Trade. The Post Office do not consult with my Department on future planning.	There are statutory consultation processes which are engaged in cases of larger scale redundancies. It is my expectation that all employers would comply with their responsibilities.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail his Department’s engagement with NI Water in relation to the impact of potential industrial action by NI Water staff.
answer_text: Planning for the impact of industrial action is an operational matter for NI Water to manage however my Department engages continuously with NI Water as part of normal governance and monitoring arrangements, and it will continue to do so, to provide assistance in the event of mitigating the potential impacts of industrial action.In terms of mitigation, NI Water has advised that, as of 25 September 2024, it invoked its’ Industrial Action Contingency Planning Team, consisting of senior representatives from a range of business areas.  Impact assessments have also been undertaken, to identify risks and potential mitigating actions, and reviews of contingency planning procedures and processes have been carried out to ensure clarity around any potential incident management and escalation.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the planned upgrades to the Kinnegar Wastewater Treatment Works.
answer_text: The planned upgrade of Kinnegar Wastewater Treatment Works (WwTW) is included in the Living With Water in Belfast Plan which, having been endorsed by the NI Executive, was published by my Department in November 2021. I am advised that NI Water has appointed an integrated design and delivery team to the project, and the planning approval process commenced in 2024. I am also informed that the outline design of the upgrade is now substantially complete. In 2023, NI Water advised that the updated estimate for its elements of the Belfast Plan had risen by £700 million, pushing the overall costs from £1.4 to £2.1 billion. In response to this increase my Department conducted an affordability review which concluded that the need for the Belfast Plan continues to exist, however, the original timescale of 12 years is no longer achievable. Delivery of the projects in the Belfast Plan, including Kinnegar WwTW, will now be taken forward by my Department and NI Water as normal business at a scale and pace achievable within available budgets.  NI Water has been exploring options for a phased approach to the upgrade of Kinnegar WwTW, so that if additional capital becomes available, it will be in a position to proceed with delivery of the phases that can be afforded, subject to its prioritisation with other projects.In this financial year, I have provided NI Water with c£0.5billion funding, however, I will continue to make the case for additional funding for water and wastewater services.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the number of jobs created by foreign direct investment in each of the last five years, broken down by local council area.
answer_text: Invest NI reports its performance on a financial year basis with the year ending 31st March 2024 being the most recent available.  During the period 2019-20 to 2023-24, Invest NI offered support to inward investment businesses which will lead to the creation of 10,120 new jobs, the details of which are set out in the table below by District Council Area and financial year.Invest NI Inward Investment New Jobs by District Council Area (2019-20 to 2023-24)DCA19-2020-2121-2222-2323-24TotalAntrim and Newtownabbey56240101262Ards and North Down11000011Armagh City, Banbridge and Craigavon2081245139Belfast1,8138191,4849461,9667,028Causeway Coast and Glens201001031Derry City and Strabane104110354207622Fermanagh and Omagh52502261Lisburn and Castlereagh1941430050387Mid and East Antrim2562002273Mid Ulster283100142Newry, Mourne and Down1411211582143NI Wide / Not Yet Determined3001866051201101,321Total2,2991,0912,4121,8722,44610,120Notes:Table totals may not add due to rounding.NI Wide / Not Yet Determined refers to projects that cannot be allocated at this level or those that have the potential to have an impact on multiple locations throughout Northern Ireland.	In addition to financial assistance, Invest NI also provides wider support to all businesses through advice, assistance or guidance from our staff and through nibusinessinfo.co.uk, our online business advice portal. 	For the Member’s future reference, Invest NI proactively publishes a full breakdown of financial support offered to every business over the past 5 years on the NI Open Data online portal which can be accessed from the following link: Invest NI Open Data.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance for an update on her Department’s introduction of a baby loss certificate scheme.
answer_text: ANSWERI am keen to see this scheme taken forward as quickly as possible to give bereaved families the opportunity to have formal recognition of their loss. I want all parents and families who have suffered the devastating loss of a baby to feel that their grief is recognised.My Department will introduce new legislation to enable a Baby Loss Certificate Scheme in the coming months. This will be the start of the process to establish a scheme here. We will continue to work with the Department of Health to specify the detail of this important initiative, to make sure that it meets the needs and expectations of the bereaved families, and to scope delivery options. My officials continue to progress this work at pace. A more detailed schedule will be made available when this legislation has been considered by the Assembly.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for an update on letters issued by her Department to sub-postmasters and sub-postmistresses in relation to the quashing of convictions.
answer_text: On 19 September 2024, my Department issued letters to twelve individuals whose convictions have been quashed by the Post Office (Horizon System) Offences Act 2024 (‘the Act’).My Department continues to work with criminal justice organisations and the Post Office Limited in order to ensure all individuals whose convictions may have been quashed by the Act are identified.  Assessments of a number of further convictions are ongoing, and I am hopeful that more letters can be issued shortly.I would also encourage anyone who believes that their convictions may be within scope of the Act to contact my Department, so that I can be assured that all relevant convictions have been identified.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance how she intends to use Barnett consequentials from the rates relief for independent retailers included in the Autumn Budget 2024.
answer_text: Barnett consequentials are unhypothecated and it will be for Executive to decide on how to allocate these in line with local priorities. However, it is worth noting that the consequentials received for 2025-26 included a negative consequential, or reduction, of -£58.2 million in respect of business rates.The Barnett consequentials announced in the Autumn Budget for 2025-26 will be considered as part of Budget 2025-26.I am working at pace to develop proposals for a draft Budget 2025-26, which I intend subject to Executive agreement to publish in December 2024.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any action his Department is taking to increase the availability of defibrillators in schools.
answer_text: On 25 November I announced a significant new initiative to invest more than £700,000 of funding to purchase and install defibrillators to all schools in Northern Ireland.It is anticipated that the provision will be rolled out to all schools by the end of 2025, which will boost availability of defibrillators for all communities across Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on a further roll-out of the 20mph speed limit scheme for schools.
answer_text: A review of the initiative introducing part-time 20mph speed limits at schools has been completed and the report has been shared with the Infrastructure Committee, and I am pleased to say it is available on the Department’s website:https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/publications/review-part-time-20mph-speed-limits-schoolsOne of the best ways to ensure safety on our roads and fulfil our long-term goal of eliminating death and serious injury by 2050, is by changing road user behaviour. As road users we all have a personal responsibility to behave in a way that keeps ourselves and others safe.  The sad reality is that if we do not change our own attitudes when we are using the roads, our death toll will continue to increase.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure how his Department intends to utilise the additional (i) £22.8 million in Resource Department Expenditure Limit allocations; and (ii) £39.6 million in Capital allocations, as outlined in the Minister of Finance statement on 11 November 2024.
answer_text: The additional resource and capital allocations provided to my Department, as outlined in the Minister of Finance statement on 11 November 2024 will be used as towards fully funding my department’s Winter Service, and an additional £1m targeted intervention for gully emptying.In addition, almost £19m from the allocations is being used towards essential and structural road maintenance and street lighting work.NI Water is also receiving an extra £31m in total, £19.5m of which is capital funding for targeted action to release wastewater constraints, while Translink will also receive a combined resource and capital allocation of almost £12m.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the number of Penalty Charge Notices that made up the £1,306,562 written-off in 2023-24 as claims waived or abandoned, broken down by constituency.
answer_text: Penalty Charge Notices (PCNs) are recorded by Council areas, and so it is not possible to provide details by constituency. However, the table below provides details of the PCNs that made up the £1.3m write-off in 2023/24, by council area. CouncilsPCNsTotal DebtAntrim & Newtownabbey106 £    12,894.00 Ards & North Down319 £    39,491.70 Armagh, Banbridge & Craigavon581 £    72,300.60 Belfast5673 £  622,510.02 Causeway Coast & Glens465 £    56,524.40 Derry & Strabane1617 £  203,319.88 Fermanagh & Omagh445 £    55,269.41 Lisburn & Castlereagh344 £    42,758.70 Mid & East Antrim387 £    47,367.20 Mid Ulster323 £    39,043.20 Newry Mourne & Down923 £  115,083.02 The Department has robust recovery procedures in place and will only consider a write-off debt when all of these procedures have been exhausted. The PCN recovery process involves the issuing of three statutory pieces of correspondence followed up with a clamping operation or collection of the debt using the Enforcement of Judgements Office (EJO).

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any action his Department is taking to encourage overseas tourism by promoting Ulster-Scots links with the United States of America.
answer_text: Tourism Ireland’s “US Market - A Strategy for Growth 2018-2025” sets out the ambition for the US market together with a strategy and action plan to drive growth, owned collectively by Tourism Ireland, Fáilte Ireland, Tourism NI and industry. The strategy sets out an ambitious agenda to grow visitor numbers and revenue in Ireland and one of its objectives is to leverage products and experiences including the Irish Diaspora, and Scots Irish / Ulster Scots.   A Working Group has been established that includes Tourism NI, Tourism Ireland, the Ulster-Scots Agency and other stakeholders to reflect on the shared cultural heritage.  A programme of events is being planned to promote and celebrate the Ulster-Scots contribution to the creation and development of the US in the run up to the 250th celebrations. In Tourism Ireland’s ongoing activity to promote ancestral connections, Ulster Scots heritage features in the organisation’s international travel trade and consumer channels.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice how her Department intends to utilise the additional (i) Resource Department Expenditure Limit allocations; and (ii) Capital allocations, provided to her Department as outlined in the Minister of Finance statement on 11 November 2024.
answer_text: (i) My Department received a total allocation of £39.3m non-ringfenced Resource DEL in October Monitoring as follows:£36.1m - general allocation for non-ringfenced Resource Departmental Expenditure Limit (DEL) towards stabilisation pressures;£2.8m - earmarked allocation for Police Service of Northern Ireland in respect of costs for policing the summer disorder; and£0.4m - Judicial salaries funded by the NI Consolidated Fund for the NI Courts and Tribunals Service.Easements have also been identified as a result of pro-active action by my Department to reduce expenditure to live within budget. This includes taking difficult decisions to reduce spend by holding vacancies as they arise across the Department. Coupled with some easements arising from demands being less than planned, my Department has been able to reprioritise a total of £7.4m funding to help address pressures across the Department.My Department therefore has a total of £46.7m available for allocation consisting of:October Monitoring allocation from the Executive - £39.3m; andDoJ reprioritisation - £7.4m.Details of the allocations provided to individual business areas are contained in the table below.Business AreaTotal funding allocated£kLegal Services Agency NI5,185NI Courts and Tribunals Service600Agencies Total5,785Criminal Justice Inspection NI70Office of the Police Ombudsman NI329Probation Board NI110Police Service of NI35,899NDPBs Total36,408Legacy Inquests - PSNI834Legacy Inquests - other areas3,221NI Consolidated Fund425Total46,673(ii) My Department received the following Capital allocations in the October Monitoring round:£0.03m reclassification from Resource DEL to Capital DEL for PSNI in respect of the NI Road Safety Partnership.The reclassification request was submitted by the Department of Justice to the Department of Finance as part of October Monitoring, and was approved by the Executive.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any consideration his Department has given to lowering the age limit for prostate cancer testing.
answer_text: Screening for prostate cancer is currently not recommended by the UK National Screening Committee (UK NSC). They last considered the evidence for screening for prostate cancer in 2020 and concluded that screening was not recommended.  This is because of the poor ability of the prostate specific antigen (PSA) test to predict cancer, which could lead to unnecessary tests that carry risks of potentially life-changing harm. Additionally, some prostate cancers may not produce elevated PSA levels, leading to false-negative results that provide false reassurance. Prostate cancer is rare in men under 50 but the risk increases with age. The UK NSC has commissioned a prostate cancer screening evidence review and cost effectiveness model, and it is expected to review this work during 2025. The UK NSC process will include a public consultation. The UK Health Departments, through the National Institute of Health and Care Research (NIHR), have invested in a significant programme of research to address the evidence gaps in this important area. A clinical trial (called the TRANSFORM trial), which will compare the most promising tests and provide evidence about the best way to screen for prostate cancer, is due to start in early 2025.My department will continue to be guided by the UK NSC’s recommendations on proposals for screening programmes. Anyone with symptoms which they believe may be related to the prostate gland should contact their GP.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any plans his Department has to improve education on the dangers of illegal drugs within schools.
answer_text: Education on the dangers of illegal drugs is a statutory requirement within the Northern Ireland curriculum. At primary level, within the Personal Development and Mutual Understanding area of the curriculum, pupils are taught about the importance of making good choices and being aware of the effects of drugs on their own and others health. At post-primary level, in the Learning for Life and Work area of the curriculum, pupils explore the concept of health as the development of a whole person and investigate the effects on the body of illegal substances and the risks and consequences of their misuse.While the specifics of what is taught, and the resources or programmes used to deliver education on drugs is a matter for individual schools and teachers, the Council for Curriculum, Examinations and Assessment has already developed and issued guidance documents and teaching support materials which can be used to improve delivery of education relating to illegal drugs.
