Additionally, schools have the flexibility to work directly with the Public Health Agency, Police Service of Northern Ireland and other organisations to provide learning opportunities in support of delivery of drugs education within the curriculum.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of his Department’s ability to deal with additional pressures from the Right Care, Right Person policy.
answer_text: It is widely recognised that there are already significant pressures across health and social care, with very significant staffing and budgetary pressures across the system.  The safety of both patients and staff will remain a priority for my Department when any changes are introduced as a result of the Right Care Right Person model.Therefore, a robust joined up approach to the rollout of Right Care Right Person (RCRP) is essential in order to manage and minimise any impact of implementation on already stretched services and on health and social care staff, while ensuring that the most vulnerable in society are protected.  In order to ensure that the approach to RCRP implementation is planned and developed through cross-agency partnerships a RCRP Strategic Oversight Group has been established, chaired jointly by DoH and DoJ.   The focus of the Oversight Group is to identify and address the required operational change, while recognising and respecting the operational independence of PSNI, and ensuring effective future partnership working between all relevant statutory partners in the delivery of health and social care, including mental health care.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any action his Department is taking to ensure staff safety is maintained as a result of the Right Care, Right Person policy.
answer_text: It is widely recognised that there are already significant pressures across health and social care, with very significant staffing and budgetary pressures across the system.  The safety of both patients and staff will remain a priority for my Department when any changes are introduced as a result of the Right Care Right Person model.Therefore, a robust joined up approach to the rollout of Right Care Right Person (RCRP) is essential in order to manage and minimise any impact of implementation on already stretched services and on health and social care staff, while ensuring that the most vulnerable in society are protected.  In order to ensure that the approach to RCRP implementation is planned and developed through cross-agency partnerships a RCRP Strategic Oversight Group has been established, chaired jointly by DoH and DoJ.   The focus of the Oversight Group is to identify and address the required operational change, while recognising and respecting the operational independence of PSNI, and ensuring effective future partnership working between all relevant statutory partners in the delivery of health and social care, including mental health care.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the creation of a new health and wellbeing centre in Bangor.
answer_text: Plans to replace Bangor Health Centre and Bangor Hospital with a new Primary and Community Care Centre are included in the regional Primary Care Infrastructure Development Strategic Implementation Plan.  This project has also been identified as a key priority in the South Eastern Trust’s Capital Plan which has been submitted to the Department of Health.The South Eastern Trust awaits the outcome of the Department of Health’s Regional 10-year Capital Plan, which will identify when funding will be available to enable this project to commence.  In advance of this, an Outline Business Case is being developed by the Trust, which will include an assessment of potential locations for any development.The Department of Health remains committed to working with the Strategic Planning and Performance Group and the South Eastern Trust to progress this project as soon as possible to deliver new primary and community care infrastructure for the Ards and North Down population.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether his Department is considering spending on improvements to (i) Belfast International Airport; and (ii) George Best Belfast City Airport.
answer_text: As Minister for Infrastructure, my powers relating to our three main airports are limited to the Airports (NI) Order 1994.  These are powers to control noise, control land in the interests of the safe and efficient use of airports, make byelaws, provide for airport constabularies and airport consultative committees, and to give grants to assist capital expenditure.My Department plays an important role in supporting transport infrastructure, including access to both of Belfast's airports, by investing in roads and other relevant public infrastructure. However, Belfast International Airport and George Best Belfast City Airport are privately owned and are expected to fund their own operational and facility improvements independently. As such, my Department is not considering direct investment in improvements at either airport, as this remains the responsibility of their private sector owners. My Department’s focus will continue to be on facilitating broader infrastructure enhancements that support the efficiency and accessibility of our overall transport network including links to our airports.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether his Department is considering changes to financial arrangements for the funding of higher education and the associated student support costs.
answer_text: I am committed to a review of Higher Education Funding, including financial support provided to students.  I have instructed my officials to bring forward detailed proposals for this review.Separately, the student support package for 2025/26 is currently under consideration.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the number of jobs created by foreign direct investment in the Ards and North Down Borough Council area in each of the last five years.
answer_text: Invest NI reports its performance on a financial year basis with the year ending 31st March 2024 being the most recent available.  Over the period 1st April 2019 to the 31st March 2024, Invest NI provided support to externally owned businesses, within the Ards and North Down Borough Council area that will lead to the creation of 11 new jobs.Invest NI Inward Investment New Jobs in Ards and North Down Borough Council (2019-20 to 2023-24)YearNew Jobs2019-20112020-2102021-2202022-2302023-240Total11	In addition to financial assistance, Invest NI also provides wider support to all businesses through advice, assistance or guidance from our staff and through nibusinessinfo.co.uk, our online business advice portal. 		For the Member’s future reference, Invest NI proactively publishes a full breakdown of financial support offered to every business over the past 5 years on the NI Open Data online portal which can be accessed from the following link: Invest NI Open Data.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any feasibility studies being undertaken by his Department following the All-Ireland Strategic Rail Review.
answer_text: The Department and Translink were successful in receiving funding through the British Government’s Union Connectivity Development Fund to take forward four rail feasibility studies which are due to report by Spring/Summer 2025:Antrim to Lisburn - Examining the reopening of the line between Antrim and Lisburn with a link to Belfast International AirportPortadown to Armagh - Examining reinstating the railway from Portadown to Armagh. Portadown to Derry - Examining the extension of the railway into Tyrone, Derry, and Donegal to support direct services between Dublin, Belfast, Derry, and Letterkenny.Electrification of the railway between Belfast and the Border – Examining options to electrify the section from Belfast to the Border on the Belfast-Dublin corridor. Translink is also progressing three further feasibility studies across the rail network:Belfast to Derry Existing Line – Considering improving capacity and line speed on the existing Belfast-Derry line, including the feasibility of new stations in the Derry city area. Due to report Spring/Summer 2025.George Best Belfast City Airport – Considering improving pedestrian access at George Best Belfast City Airport. Due to report Spring/Summer 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail his engagement with the Minister for Infrastructure in relation to accessibility for emergency service vehicles within residential areas.
answer_text: It is imperative that all emergency services have access to residential properties during call outs. I raised this issue at the Executive meeting held on 7 November 2024. Ambulance crews can experience a range of issues which can affect response times, including traffic congestion, road closures, poor weather conditions, pedestrian activity, lack of house numbers, etc. It is important that the Executive continues to work together to ensure clear passage for emergency services.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on his Department’s pay awards in this budgetary year.
answer_text: Ahead of the NI Executive monitoring round, my Department was faced with a budget deficit in terms of available funding to meet the cost of maintaining core health and social care services at current levels.Despite an unprecedented level of savings of £200m implemented across HSC, the allocation of £350m in October monitoring, while welcome, has still left a funding gap of £100m to meet service pressures and pay uplifts.Last week I met with Trade Unions representing Agenda for Change health staff and firm proposals on a pay deal have been put to them, which they will now consider. It is important to recognise that further work still needs to be undertaken in order to reach a resolution to the issue of health service pay. It is also important to recognise that the challenges facing public sector pay in general will only be resolved through an all-executive approach.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the value of rates her Department expects to receive as a result of the proposed changes to the domestic rates cap.
answer_text: I have put forward proposals that I consider to be both proportionate and reasonable. The precise value in terms of revenue raised will be dependent upon the Executive’s final decisions in this area. Any policy change in this area is a matter for the Executive and it has not yet been agreed to.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail her engagement with the Minister of Finance in relation to the UK Government’s decision to not allow a reserve claim for costs relating to the PSNI data breach.
answer_text: The estimated costs relating to the PSNI data breach are not affordable from my Department’s current budget in the absence of additional funding being provided by the Executive or a Reserve Claim.I have therefore engaged regularly with the Minister of Finance to explore options to secure funding for these costs. This includes discussions at bi-lateral meetings, in my responses to Executive papers relating to the budget and with colleagues at Executive meetings.My officials have also engaged in detail with officials in the Department of Finance on this matter.I will continue to work closely with the Minister of Finance to explore all options for funding and will support the Minister in future efforts to negotiate for a Reserve Claim.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for his assessment of the UK Government’s decision to no longer ring-fence farming and fishery subsidies.
answer_text: The existing farming and fisheries funding continues to be earmarked by HM Treasury until 31 March 2025 and it is important to provide certainty on this funding after that in light of the change announced by the Chancellor on 30 October 2024.	I shall be continuing my engagement with the Minister of Finance and Executive colleagues as part of the ongoing Budget 2025-26 exercise to seek that this funding for agriculture, agri-environment, fisheries and rural development is earmarked by the Executive following the end of earmarking by HM Treasury.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the value of unpaid rates in each of the last five years.
answer_text: I would refer the Member to the response provided for AQW 17211/22-27.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for his assessment of the benefits of increasing the number of social workers employed within primary schools.
answer_text: I am aware that the schools which utilise social workers report that they are having a very positive impact.I also welcome the recent “School Social Work Guidance - for schools employing social workers” published by the Northern Ireland Social Care Council (the Social Care Council). The purpose of this guidance is to help schools and / or other educational institutions who are considering engaging a social worker.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail (i) any engagement his Department has had with Just Eat in relation to money lost by local businesses to suspected fraud; and (ii) any action his Department is taking to support food businesses affected by suspected fraudulent activity through the Just Eat platform.
answer_text: My Department has advised impacted businesses to consider reporting any alleged fraud to Action Fraud   - https://www.psni.police.uk/safety-and-support/keeping-safe/protecting-yourself/scams-and-fraud/reporting-fraud.  My Department’s Trading Standards Service (TSS) also provides support and assistance to businesses affected by scams. Traders seeking further advice should contact Consumerline on Freephone 0300 123 6262 or by e mail to Consumerline@economy-ni.gov.uk. The TSS has not to date had direct engagement with Just Eat on this issue or with any businesses affected by this specific issue.Just Eat appear to be aware of attempts by scammers to defraud businesses by gaining access to customer accounts and have provided the following advice to their partners :https://partner.just-eat.co.uk/knowledge-centre/update-on-scammers/TSS may be able to assist businesses in getting their money refunded. New rules introduced by the Payment Systems Regulator on 7th October 2024 offer greater financial protection to victims of fraud, with a reimbursement requirement applying to consumers, micro-enterprises, and charities.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail what action his Department is taking to improve water quality to beyond the sufficient standard rating at Ballyholme Beach.
answer_text: My Department is currently working with NI Water and the Agri-Food and Biosciences Institute on a study to determine the sources of pollution that are adversely impacting water quality in order to enable the future targeting of remediation actions. Results from this study are expected in mid-2025.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail what action his Department is taking to support family farms who may be impacted by the recent announcement on changes to inheritance tax.
answer_text: I recognise that the recent move on inheritance tax has significant ramifications for famers and is the subject of much concern in Northern Ireland's farming community. I met with the NI Secretary of State on 4th November 2024 and expressed my concerns and urged that the matter be reconsidered and will continue to do the same in meetings with other UK Government Ministers. I would encourage farmers to seek advice from professional tax advisors regarding the impacts to their farms. As taxation policy is not devolved, the actions that my Department can undertake are limited but I will do whatever I can to encourage the UK Government to take into account the concerns of the farming community and to look again at the potential impact and approach taken.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any action his Department is taking to increase the number of Physicians Associate positions.
answer_text: My Department’s officials have been working proactively with employers in the HSC Trusts to try to secure posts for the recent graduates from the MSc in Physician Associates programme at Ulster University. Unfortunately, these endeavours have been frustrated by the current very challenging financial situation faced by my Department.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, in light of his Written Ministerial Statement on 23 October 2024, to detail the additional short break provisions for disabled children that will be available in the South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: On 23rd October 2024, I was pleased to be able to approve additional regional funding for the Children with Disability service across all HSC Trusts of up to £13m a year in multi-year funding, including up to £2m for the rest of the current financial year.It is intended that this additional funding will assist with both increasing short breaks capacity and expanding family, therapeutic and behavioural supports in the community across all HSC Trusts.With regard to the allocation of the funding – each HSC Trust (inclusive of SEHSCT) is currently working intensively with my Department to prioritise their short-term action proposals in respect of assisting with solutions to improve family support, short breaks, residential care and behavioural support for those families who have Children with Disability. This work will help to confirm financial allocations and delivery plans for each HSC Trust.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health where the £13 million of spending outlined in his Written Ministerial Statement of 23 October 2024 has been sourced from within his Department’s budget.
answer_text: £2m funding for short breaks for children with disabilities has been confirmed for 2024-25 from several easements from across the HSC system that have recently been reported.The impact of this for future years is estimated at £13m.  The Budget settlement for future years has not yet been agreed and so it is not possible to definitively answer your question.  However, whilst the future financial position will remain challenging for Health it needs to be offset again the severe service delivery risk in this area.  There is also a risk that costs will increase if the need is not met in an appropriate manner.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail his engagement with the Minister of Education in relation to increasing the number of social workers in primary schools.
answer_text: I have had no direct engagement with the Minister of Education on this issue. I am aware that a small number of secondary schools do now employ social workers and that the Chief Social Worker has recently launched Northern Ireland Social Care Council (NISCC) Schools Social Work Guidance.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for an update on financial contributions from institutions involved in the Historical Abuse Redress scheme.
To ask the Minister of Education, following the closure of the consultation on the School Uniform Policy, to provide an update on the policy.
answer_text: Boards of Governors and school leaders are responsible for setting the uniform policy in their school. To support schools, my Department has comprehensive school uniform policy guidance in place which highlights measures to keep school uniforms affordable, for example keeping the number of branded items to a minimum and avoiding single supplier arrangements. Schools are reminded of the guidance regularly, however, currently they do not have to follow it. I launched a public consultation on school uniform policy in June, which asked for views on making the current guidance statutory.The consultation closed on 27 September. There were just under 7,500 responses overall, and approximately 4,000 of those were from children and young people.Officials are currently analysing the responses from the consultation and will provide me with a report on the outcome of the consultation in the coming weeks.Whilst analysis of the responses is on-going, it is clear that there is strong support for school uniform policy guidance to be made statutory and I intend to bring a paper to the Executive in the coming weeks seeking support to draft a Bill to this effect.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for his assessment of (i) the Royal Society for the Protection of Bird’s Annual Bird Crime Report 2023; and (ii) the lack of prosecutions for illegally killing birds of prey.
answer_text: (i)The killing of birds of prey, whether deliberate or through improper use of rodenticides and other poisons, is rightly considered a crime and adversely impacts our rich biodiversity, as well as the future survival of already vulnerable species. The RSPB report covers the UK and makes linkages between shooting estates and bird of prey persecution. My officials advise that there are comparatively few shooting estates in Northern Ireland, and local data points more to the unlawful use of poisons in the wider countryside.NIEA has partnered with the Police Service of NI (PSNI) and over twenty other statutory and non-governmental organisations to form the Partnership for Action against Wildlife Crime Northern Ireland (PAWNI). PAWNI works to raise awareness of wildlife crime and promotes effective deterrence and enforcement. Birds of prey are a particular focus and PAWNI published the Northern Ireland-specific bird of prey crime report which is the source of the RSPB’s data for Northern Ireland.  Satellite tracking and remote cameras have been deployed to protect particularly vulnerable species such as hen harriers and peregrines and their nests.(ii) These are often crimes that occur in remote areas and incidents may remain undetected and/or unreported or have insufficient evidence to identify a perpetrator. Up-to-date evidence to inform action against wildlife crime is very important. I would encourage anyone who comes across a suspected wildlife crime or has information on those involved in perpetrating these offences, to report it to the PSNI (tel 101) as soon as possible providing good quality locational data.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) for his assessment of the findings in the Story of Belfast Lough document; and (ii) to detail any action being taken by his Department as a result of the findings.
answer_text: The ‘Story of Belfast Lough’ was produced by NI Water and published on its website.   The key water quality issues in Belfast Lough, referenced in the document, have been known for some time and are identified in my Department’s Living With Water in Belfast Plan, which was published in November 2021 following public consultation and Executive endorsement.The NI Water ‘Story of Belfast Lough’ document outlines some of the further work carried out through the Living With Water Programme since 2021. This includes integrated environmental modelling, which was funded by my Department and has been beneficial in identifying the sources of the pollution in Belfast Lough and informing the future design standards for the upgrades needed to the wastewater treatment works that discharge into Belfast Lough. It has been accepted that delivery of my Department’s Living With Water in Belfast Plan is the optimum solution to addressing the city’s drainage and wastewater infrastructure issues, which will help protect against flooding, enhance the water environment and grow the economy. It was always acknowledged that additional funding would be required to deliver all aspects of the Belfast Plan within the original 12-year timeframe.  In the New Decade New Approach agreement, published in January 2020, Living With Water was identified as a beneficiary of increased funding, however, this was not received. In 2023, NI Water also increased the cost estimate of its elements of the Belfast Plan by £700 million, pushing the overall costs from £1.4 to £2.1 billion.  An affordability review conducted by my Department in response to this increase has concluded that the need for the Belfast Plan continues to exist, however, without the necessary budget, its original timescale is no longer achievable. Delivery of the projects in the Belfast Plan will therefore be taken forward by my Department and NI Water as normal business at a scale and pace achievable within available budgets.  In this financial year, I have provided NI Water with c£0.5billion funding, however, I will continue to make the case for additional funding for water and wastewater services.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any consideration his Department has given to introducing a legal requirement for local councils to report waste crime data.
answer_text: Councils are asked to complete a quarterly return on flytipping to my Department though the NIEA. In addition, nine of the eleven Councils are signed up to a Flytipping Protocol with NIEA. Under this Protocol local councils and NIEA recognise the importance of reporting Flytipping and commit to working collaboratively to develop and improve the collection and analysis of relevant data to tackle waste crime.  At this time, no consideration has been given by my Department to introducing a legal requirement for local councils to report waste crime data.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether his Department intends to increase Invest NI’s total spending in the Ards and North Down Borough Council area as part of his Sub-Regional Economic Plan.
answer_text: The focus of my economic vision is about growing the economy across the North. This will require increased investment by Invest NI in all areas.My Department and Invest NI recognise the need to do more in Ards & North Down to improve the business base, to grow productivity performance and to encourage higher productivity businesses in the area. This imperative is reflected in the Sub-Regional Economic Plan and Invest NI’s draft 2024/25 – 26/27 Business Strategy.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail any engagement she has had with the Minister of Justice in relation to the expected costs as a result of the PSNI data breach.
answer_text: As part of the 2024-25 Urgent In-Year Exercise, I met with Minister Long on 17 September 2024, and we discussed the various financial pressures faced by the Department of Justice (DoJ). Among the pressures discussed was that relating to the PSNI Data Breach. The Minister also highlighted the matter in previous discussions in relation to the 2024-25 Budget, as well as in relation to June Monitoring.Further to this, my officials have been actively engaging with counterparts in DoJ, to ensure that my department remains aware of the potential costs that may be incurred. A financial exercise is currently underway for future years, in which I have commissioned returns from Ministerial colleagues to inform and enable the Executive to develop Budget plans for 2025 and beyond. This provides a further opportunity for Minister Long to highlight DoJ’s financial pressures including those relating to the Data Breach incident.  I have also raised the matter both in discussions and in writing with the Chief Secretary.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, in light of the Criminal Bar Association's intention to take industrial action, for an update on her Department’s fundamental review of the criminal legal aid framework.
answer_text: I was surprised and disappointed that the Criminal Bar Association have decided to withdraw their defence services for some cases before the courts from the start of November. I believe that such action is premature, given ongoing work by the Department, and is also counterproductive. This and the day of withdrawal of service across all criminal cases on the 4 November will only apply more pressure to a system which has been consistently underfunded for years. I and my officials have and will continue to make the case for the funding necessary to deliver the Justice system that people need at the time they are most vulnerable.The Department is nearing the end of the work to review the recommendations made within the report on the Fundamental Review of Criminal Legal Aid by Judge Burgess.  This report is informing part of the Enabling Access to Justice reform programme and it is critical that proper time is taken to analyse and assess the implications of recommendations in the context of the programme and wider Departmental pressures. I expect to be in a position to share my proposed course of action with Assembly colleagues in coming weeks.

answer_text: The Land & Property Services rating system does not hold information at constituency level. (i) and (ii) The table below provides a breakdown of the number of businesses that have applied for and the number of businesses that have received Back in Business rate support by District Council. This is since the scheme was relaunched in May 2024.District CouncilNo. of Businesses Applied for Back in BusinessNo. of Businesses Awarded Back in BusinessAntrim and Newtownabbey32Ards and North Down31Armagh City, Banbridge and Craigavon30Belfast104Causeway Coast and Glens42Derry City and Strabane District Council20Fermanagh and Omagh21Lisburn and Castlereagh20Mid and East Antrim54Mid Ulster85Newry, Mourne and Down11Total43*20* Figures provided as at 25th October 2024. question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any action his Department is taking to avoid job losses in the Minerals Products Association Northern Ireland.
answer_text: Membership of the Minerals Products Association Northern Ireland is made up of private businesses many of which carryout work directly or indirectly for my Department. They will also have other contracts not connected to my DepartmentMy Department is seeking additional funding through the October monitoring round to ensure a continued improvement of  road and active travel infrastructure, if successful, the additional funding may contribute to securing jobs in the construction industry.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether his Department intends to allocate additional funding for roads maintenance contractors as part of the October Monitoring Round.
answer_text: The outcome of October Monitoring has not yet been agreed. Once this has been agreed, and if my Department receives additional funding, I will consider in line with my priorities and competing pressures.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any action his Department is taking to create infrastructure for emerging waste streams such as batteries and solar panels.
answer_text: My department supports the creation of waste infrastructure through engagement with industry and stakeholders, but it should be noted that final decisions on planning, procurements and development of waste infrastructure does not rest with my department. However, my department provides input as a statutory consultee to the planning process where appropriate and the Northern Ireland Environment Agency assesses applications, and where appropriate, grants Waste Management Licences or Pollution Prevention and Control Permits. Officials in my department continue to engage with counterparts across the UK on policy development for batteries.  My recent commitment to introduce legislation to ban single use vapes is another positive example of this collaboration, and action here will reduce the volume of batteries ending up in waste collection processes. I understand also that the Economy Minister’s department is engaging with the private sector and research bodies in respect of permanent magnet recycling to recover magnet rare earths and the recovery for reuse of other critical minerals.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for his assessment of his Department’s ability to maintain road safety standards if additional funding is not provided to roads maintenance contractors.
answer_text: My Department places high importance on the need to adequately maintain the road network through day-to-day Essential Maintenance activities such as patching and grass cutting, and also through a properly resourced programme of Structural Maintenance to maintain the structural integrity of the road network. However, allocations of funding to maintain our roads have been less than required for many years.Routine Maintenance includes the key functions of defect repair, grass cutting, gully emptying street lighting maintenance and traffic signal repair. These are important aspects of road maintenance in ensuring public safety and all have been affected by inadequate budgets over many years.In response, my Department has had to reduce its routine road maintenance activities to a limited service, which prioritises delivery of essential services for maintaining public safety and protecting the transport network. Consequently, only the highest priority defects across the network are currently being repaired.One of the best ways to ensure safety on our roads and fulfil our long-term goal of eliminating death and serious injury by 2050, is by changing road user behaviour. As road users we all have a personal responsibility to behave in a way that keeps ourselves and others safe. The sad reality is that if we do not change our own attitudes when we are using the roads, our death toll will continue to increase. My department will continue to promote the road safety public information campaigns and other road safety advice through www.sharetheroadtozero.com I would encourage everyone to share road safety messaging and to take the road safety pledge.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the timescale for her Department’s scoping work on a baby loss certificate scheme.
answer_text: I am keen to see this scheme taken forward as quickly as possible to give bereaved families the opportunity to have formal recognition of their loss. I want all parents and families who have suffered the devastating loss of a baby to feel that their grief is recognised.I met with the Health Minister in September to receive an update on progress from General Register Office officials in my Department who are working closely with the Department of Health officials on how the baby loss scheme work may be developed. I recently met with the Minister for Health again to mark Baby Loss Awareness Week. My officials and those from the Department of Health have met with managers from the Department of Health and Social Care in England and with the National Records of Scotland officials to learn more about how their schemes operate. This ongoing engagement will provide important lessons for the development of our scheme and work is ongoing to scope and assess options for the introduction of baby loss certificates for parents here who have lost a baby during pregnancy up to 24 weeks. As you will have seen from my recent announcement, my Department will introduce new legislation to enable a Baby Loss Certificate Scheme in the coming months. This will be the start of the process to establish a scheme here. We will continue to work with the Department of Health to specify the detail of this important initiative, to make sure that it meets the needs and expectations of the bereaved families, and to scope delivery options. My officials continue to progress this work at pace: A more detailed schedule will be made available when this legislation has been considered by the Assembly.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any action his Department is taking to improve its efficiency in responding to correspondence.
answer_text: The principal issue with the response times to correspondence and inquiries within my Department relates to a combination of high workloads and staff resource pressures, rather than inefficient systems.  My Department has been operating in a difficult financial environment for a number of years due to underfunding and austerity by the British Government. That has had a significant impact on my Department’s ability to deliver essential maintenance functions, including Limited Service.  Recruitment and retention of staff is also challenging, meaning that the part of the Department responsible for managing the road network currently has a higher than acceptable vacancy rate.My Department is taking forward a number of initiatives to improve the recruitment and retention of staff and I will continue to raise staffing and financial concerns with my Executive colleagues.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the average waiting time for paediatric dental surgery in each of the last five years, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: All five Health and Social Care (HSC) Trusts offer dental treatment under General Anaesthetic (GA) to children. The Strategic Planning and Performance Group (SPPG) regularly collate performance information from these services. However, it is not collected in a manner that definitively answers the question posed. In order to provide an answer, SPPG contacted all Trusts requesting current information regarding waiting lists for GA dental services. Each HSC Trust has provided responses for the current average waiting time from initial referral, indicating the clinical need to be urgent, for a GA treatment appointment (i.e. referral to treat time).Southern Health and Social Care Trust: < 1 month.South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust: 1-2 months.Belfast Health and Social Care Trust: > 1 year.Northern Health and Social Care Trust: 6-12 months.Western Health and Social Care Trust: 1-2 months.It should be noted that the average times for acute patients would be less than those above as they are given a higher priority, and those with a routine priority grading would be longer. In addition, complete and comparable data for waiting times over the past 5 years are not available.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any support available for Nursery provision at Killard House School, Donaghadee.
answer_text: Killard House do not currently have a nursery class due to an increased need for Primary 1 placements since 2022.Over the last two years, Specialist Provision – Early Years/Foundation (EYF) have been established to address capacity issues at Killard as set out below.2023/24Victoria Primary School (PS), Ballyhalbert - one class EYF to support capacity at Killard House.      2024/25Kirkistown PS – one class EYF to support capacity at Killard House.Newtownards Nursery School (NS) - one class Early Years (providing capacity for both Clifton and Killard House).

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any support his Department is providing to schools with pupils who have additional medical needs.
answer_text: My Department works together with the Department of Education to support children with additional needs who attend schools in Northern Ireland and require additional support to allow them to access the curriculum.  Each child will have specific, individual needs and therefore the support for each child is assessed through their personal Special Educational Needs (SEN) assessment.  This support is normally delivered through the employment of Classroom Assistants but can also be provided by allied health professionals (AHPs). Under the terms of the Northern Ireland Practice and Education Council delegation framework, Classroom Assistants, having received the required training from Community Children’s Nurses, provide child specific, timely care, and effectively lead in the delivery of individual support for each child, in line with the child’s individual care plan.AHPs, such as speech and language therapists or occupational therapists, can deliver universal support beneficial to all children with additional medical needs, or targeted and specialist support where it is deemed necessary.School nurses deliver the universal 5–19 year school-aged child health promotion programme, “Healthy Child Health Future”, to all school-aged children in NI.  This programme sets out two key contacts every child can expect to receive within school.  School-aged children and their families requiring targeted support will be offered additional support, proportionate to their needs.  They also play a key public health role in the promotion and delivery of the annual school based influenza (flu) immunisation programme to children from Primary 1 to Year 12.  Vaccinating children helps provide protection against infection with the flu virus and reduces further spread to other vulnerable children and adults.  School nurses are Specialist Community Public Health Nurses (SCPHNs), who have undertaken additional training.  Each school within NI has a named School Nurse, who is the key point of contact between education and health.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail his Department’s plans to further utilise AI technologies to address waiting times.
answer_text: My Department’s Chief Digital Information Officer is working to ascertain if AI technologies can be utilised to help reduce current waiting times. Progress has been made in this area, including a trial which is underway in Antrim Area Hospital with an AI application to improve the detection of fractures in the Emergency Department.  Following formal evaluation of this trial period, a decision will be made as to its full implementation into other areas.  Other areas being explored include the increased use of AI tools on scanners to reduce sequence time, hence reducing scanning time and increasing throughput. There are future plans to consider testing AI algorithms to assist with referral/ decision making, scheduling and acquisition, reporting and triage of reporting, and results acknowledgement/alerts.My Department is also working with Trusts and the Digital Health and Care Northern Ireland (DHCNI) team to address patient waiting list duplications through the development of a new electronic application which helps identify duplicate referrals within the same Trust and across Trusts. All these initiatives have reduced the administrative input and have helped improve productivity and efficiency and ultimately helps reduce waiting times.Another area where my Department utilises smart technology is in its use for waiting list validation. The roll-out of text reminders and the use of QR codes, which are placed on patient letters, are scanned and patients are directed to a survey which they are prompted to complete. The results of the survey help the relevant clinician determine whether the patient needs to remain on the waiting list.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail his Department’s spending on the bilingual traffic sign pilot in West Belfast.
answer_text: On 10 October 2024, I announced bi-lingual traffic sign pilot for the Gaeltacht Quarter in West Belfast, which will install bi-lingual traffic signs and road markings on the Falls Road between the Springfield Road and Whiterock Road.  The current estimate for this work is £50,000.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail his Department’s expected timescale for the review of findings from the public consultation on policy proposals to inform the development of a new Public Health Bill for Northern Ireland.
answer_text: My Department have received a high volume of responses to the public consultation on policy proposals to inform the development of a new Public Health Bill for Northern Ireland.  The consultation closed on 14 October 2024 and the process to consider the responses has commenced, the timeframe to complete this review, given the high volume of responses, is still under consideration at this early stage.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the accessibility of the public consultation on policy proposals to inform the development of a new Public Health Bill for Northern Ireland.
answer_text: The Department has engaged in a consultation process in line with best practice, as is found in, among other documents, The NICS Guide To Making Policy That Works – (Executiveoffice-Ni.Gov.Uk).As some misunderstandings developed around some aspects of the consultation, on the 4 October 2024 the Department published a ‘fact file’ to help address some of the key concerns around the introduction of this Bill.The fact file can be found at: Fact file – consultation on a Public Health Bill | Department of Health (health-ni.gov.uk)

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for an update on the public consultation on a Statutory Public Inquiry and Financial Redress Scheme on the Mother and Baby Institutions, Magdalene Laundries and Workhouses.
answer_text: Following good attendance at the 16 consultation events, including nine in-person, held in Belfast, Omagh, Newry and the North-West, the public consultation on the proposals for a public inquiry and redress scheme closed on 19 September 2024. We intend to publish an analysis of the 269 consultation responses received in the coming weeks.  We are pleased with the many areas of broad agreement and where positive amendments can be made.However, there remain many sensitive policy issues which will require discussion with Executive Colleagues before any final decision can be taken. It will be important for us to engage and listen with victims and survivors in this period.We aim to do the majority of this work before the end of 2024.Once the draft bill is agreed by the Executive, it will be introduced into the Assembly for it be considered fully. In parallel, work on the practical requirements to establish the Inquiry and redress service continues.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the amount of Invest NI funding awarded in the Ards and North Down Borough Council area in each of the last five years.
answer_text: Invest NI reports its performance on a financial year basis with the year ending 31st March 2024 being the most recent available.  Over the period 1st April 2019 to the 31st March 2024, Invest NI offered support to businesses within Ards and North Down Borough Council totalling £17.78m. In addition to financial assistance, Invest NI also provides wider support to all businesses through advice, assistance or guidance from our staff and through nibusinessinfo.co.uk, our online business advice portal. 		For the Member’s future reference, Invest NI proactively publishes a full breakdown of financial support offered to every business over the past 5 years on the NI Open Data online portal which can be accessed from the following link: Invest NI Open Data.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail the rationale for arranging a flight to Portugal for a civil servant to brief the First Minister in advance of the Assembly recall on 8 August 2024.
answer_text: Given the seriousness of the situation, the First Minister returned from her family holiday to deal with the ongoing threat and intimidation which was having a devastating impact on the victims and communities. The department put in place travel arrangements to ensure the First Minister was not travelling alone on departmental business. The accompanying Civil Servant assisted with the preparations for the First Minister to jointly chair an urgent meeting of the Executive. At this emergency meeting of the Executive, Ministers were briefed by the Chief Constable on public disorder and ongoing racist attacks.  The First Minister and deputy First Minister updated MLAs during an urgent recall of the Assembly.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the locations that will be included in Tourism NI’s pilot sub-regional Destination Stewardship Plan.
answer_text: Regional Balance is one of my key priorities and Tourism NI is in the early stages of developing a Destination Stewardship Framework.  This framework will include Stewardship principles and will be used as a blueprint to develop tourism destinations sustainably across the North in the future.Tourism NI is currently procuring a suitably qualified and experienced contractor to co-create the Destination Stewardship Framework, and this will also entail in-depth consultation with key stakeholders, including Ards & North Down Borough Council and tourism providers in the Strangford Lough area. A key proposed output will be the development of a pilot Destination Stewardship Plan, based on one of the tourism destinations identified as part of the Framework.  This pilot destination plan will be selected and informed by the specialist contractor in collaboration with Tourism NI, and using appropriate research, data and evidence.  It is also proposed that this pilot will help to inform the other destinations plans and their development across the North in the years to come.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister why the First Minister did not utilise video conferencing software to receive briefings in advance of the Assembly recall on 8 August 2024.
answer_text: Given the seriousness of the situation, the First Minister returned from her family holiday to deal with the ongoing threat and intimidation which was having a devastating impact on the victims and communities.The department put in place travel arrangements to ensure the First Minister was not travelling alone on departmental business. The accompanying Civil Servant assisted with the preparations for the First Minister to jointly chair an urgent meeting of the Executive.  At this emergency meeting of the Executive, Ministers were briefed by the Chief Constable on public disorder and ongoing racist attacks.  The First Minister and deputy First Minister updated MLAs during an urgent recall of the Assembly.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the action his Department is taking to improve early diagnosis of blood cancer.

To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any engagement he has had with the Minister for Infrastructure in relation to farms impacted by the proposed A5 Western Transport Corridor project.
answer_text: The Department for Infrastructure is the department responsible for the A5 Western Transport Corridor project and, as such has been leading on relevant engagement. I provided an update on my Department’s consideration of the project to the Minister of Infrastructure on 1 October 2024 including matters relating to ammonia and planning.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any action his Department is taking to address (i) low median wages; and (ii) low labour productivity in the Ards and North Down Borough Council area.
answer_text: My Department is working with the Living Wage Foundation and Advice NI to promote the Real Living Wage in the North. Invest NI has a key role to play in supporting businesses in Ards and North Down, and across the North. Invest NI’s Draft Business Strategy 2024-27 highlights how it will improve and enhance its delivery to help businesses improve their productivity.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail his Department’s rationale for the use of the Belfast Metropolitan Area when determining which areas to reduce investment from Invest NI as part of the Sub-Regional Economic Plan.
answer_text: The focus of my economic vision is about growing the economy across the North. This will require increased investment by Invest NI in all areas. The target for Invest NI to increase investments outside the Belfast Metropolitan Area is therefore not about reducing investment in that area rather about growing overall investment but with an increased focus on areas outside the greater Belfast area.  This also reflects the need for a geographical shift in focus to deliver the improvement in regional balance.Tackling regional balance is a priority in the draft Programme for Government. The target will contribute to this priority.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) for his assessment of the findings contained in the UK Blood Cancer Action Plan; and (ii) to detail the action his Department is taking as a result of the Action Plan.
answer_text: Whilst my Department acknowledges the helpful recommendations outlined within the UK Blood Cancer Action Plan, it should be stated that it was not consulted or involved in the development of this Action Plan. However, the recommendations around the themes of workforce, diagnosis and care, access to clinical trials and data, are all areas we acknowledge require work and we have been working to address these in Northern Ireland.Haematology services in NI have been under growing pressures for some time amidst serious workforce concerns for haematological services UK wide.  In order to address the severe regional pressures within haematology services, in 2023 the DoH NI Cancer Policy and Projects Team initiated a project for the redesign of Haematology Treatment Pathways across NI to include the three main haematological pathways: Myeloma, Lymphoma and Leukaemia. The Strategic Planning and Performance Group (SPPG) of my Department were asked to implement the recommended actions in April 2024 and have engaged with Trusts on the reports’ recommendations.  While some recommendations are cost neutral, others require investment, and resource to implement.  Some progress has been made on early stage actions including baseline assessment and identification of nursing workforce gaps, maximising virtual clinics, identifying appropriate time and process for holistic needs assessment, weekly multi-disciplinary Team (MDT) meetings, adoption of NICE guidance on bispecific antibodies in Multiple Myeloma and development of a haematology cancer nurse career pathway. Progression of the actions will be dependent on consultant capacity, and upon reform and greater skill mix within haematology services, including haematology specialist and advanced nursing roles. These roles are key to releasing consultant capacity and provide alterative options for managing complex patients. The scale and pace of progress will be dependent on investment in the nursing workforce (action 39 of the NI Cancer Strategy) and Advanced Nurse Practitioners (ANPs) to take forward a significant number of the recommended actions in the DoH Haematology Pathway Reports.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail any engagement she has had with the UK Government in relation to addressing the exploitation of the Common Travel Area by criminal groups.
answer_text: The Common Travel Area (CTA) is an excepted matter and I can confirm that whilst I have not had any direct engagement with my counterparts in the UK Government on this specific issue, we routinely discuss issues related to cross-border organised crime.My Department leads on a range of work in Northern Ireland in relation to organised crime, including tackling those Organised Crime Groups (OCGs) that seek to exploit the CTA.   This includes working closely with criminal justice partners, through the Organised Crime Task Force (OCTF), to ensure that collective efforts are focused on the pursuit of criminals.   The OCTF brings together law enforcement agencies, along with Government Departments, academia and the business community to combat organised crime.  The OCTF Strategy Group provides leadership and supports collaborative, joined-up responses to the threats posed by organised crime.  It includes senior representation from agencies operating in Northern Ireland, including Immigration Enforcement, a Directorate of the UK Government’s Home Office and UK Border Force.  Border Force is a law enforcement command within Home Office that secures the UK border and facilitates the legitimate movement of individuals and goods, whilst preventing those that would cause harm from entering the UK.  OCTF partners regularly engage with each other in relation to ongoing investigations and conduct operations against OCGs suspected of involvement in organised crime.  The OCTF publishes an annual report to highlight the efforts and successes of partners who work together to tackle organised crime in Northern Ireland.  Further information on all aspects of the OCTF’s work can be found at:OCTF Annual Report and Threat Assessment 2022-2023 | Organised Crime Task Force

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice (i) for her assessment of the findings included in the Commissioner for Victims of Crime 2024 Victim Survey; and (ii) to detail any action her Department is taking in light of these findings.
answer_text: I am grateful to the Commissioner Designate for engaging with victims of crime and seeking feedback on their experiences of the criminal justice system. The results from this survey reflect the challenges we face to improve confidence in the justice system. This is a multifaceted issue which is affected by a range of factors. It is acknowledged that not all victims will have a positive experience of the justice system, and feedback can help inform future change. My Department continues to develop new initiatives and strategies to help support victims, and there are many examples of positive feedback including from those who can give their evidence from a Remote Evidence Centre or who have had access to free legal advice from a Sexual Offences Legal Adviser.   Extending best practice across a diverse system is a complex process; reform takes time to embed and for the impact to be felt by all, it also requires resources.  I am acutely aware of the funding pressures on each part of the system and the subsequent impact on victims going through the system. My Department is developing a new Victim and Witness Strategy which aims to place victims at the centre of its focus. The Victim Charter sets out the entitlements and services that victims of crime can expect to receive from the criminal justice system and provides information about what to do if not satisfied with the support received. Victim Champions, who are leaders in each of the criminal justice organisations, will soon hold a Summit to promote and reinforce that a trauma informed approach to supporting victims must be embedded across all parts of the system. I also plan to introduce a Sentencing Bill which will aim to improve confidence by making clear the purposes and principles of sentencing; rationalising and simplifying the arrangements for unduly lenient sentence referrals; creating a new offence of assaulting a person who is providing a public service or performing a public duty; and increasing the maximum penalties for offences of causing death by driving. The Probation Board for Northern Ireland’s Victim Information Unit also works to inform victims about their rights, about Victim Information Schemes and about the key stages of the sentence of the person who has offended in a manner which is accessible, understandable and supportive.My Department will continue to engage with the Commissioner Designate for Victims of Crime and all criminal justice partners on the detail of the recent survey results to establish whether any specific gaps in services can be identified and addressed. I am determined to improve confidence in the criminal justice system and will continue to do all I can to secure additional resource for the system, which will be invested in ways that improve victim experience.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any consideration his Department has given to increase local council powers of enforcement in relation to the misuse of household bins.
answer_text: The Department has received correspondence from one local council in June of this year in respect of the above concern and acknowledges this further query. The Department responded to the previous query on this matter, requesting evidence and support from across the district councils that such powers are necessary and all other potential options have been given due consideration. This would be seen as a prerequisite before committing significant resource and instigating the creation of any additional powers requested.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any action his Department has taken to introduce staff-worn body cameras in the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: The Belfast Health and Social Care Trust is scoping the introduction of a pilot scheme for staff to wear body cameras in two service areas.  The Trust will be working with staff, patients and their families, as well as consulting with Trade Union colleagues to involve them in the development of the pilot.It is envisaged that the pilot will commence in April 2025 following the consultation approval process.I am assured that all relevant governance processes are being followed. Following delivery, the pilot will be evaluated for learning and improvement ahead of any formal decision making on implementation of staff worn body cameras within Belfast Health and Social Care Trust.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department has any knowledge of inaccuracies in patient addresses as a result of the roll-out of the encompass system.
answer_text: Data inaccuracies in patient addresses are a known historical challenge across HSC systems and are not necessarily a consequence of the encompass implementation. Within encompass, core demographics (including permanent address) are sourced from the Northern Ireland Digital Identity Service (NIDIS) introduced in 2023 to consolidate, simplify, and improve how patient identity is managed and shared throughout Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether his Department has any plans to introduce legal protections for hospitality workers’ tips, similar to those available in other jurisdictions.
answer_text: My ‘Good Jobs’ Employment Rights Bill consultation invited views on a range of proposals to strengthen our employment rights framework to help deliver the Good Jobs agenda. This included proposals to introduce new legislation to ensure that tips left to workers are passed on to them in full.  The consultation closed on 30 September and my Department will now analyse the responses to inform next steps.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for a timescale on the launch of her Department’s consultation on increasing the sentence involving the death or serious harm of a child.
answer_text: The initial three year action plan for the “Domestic and Sexual Abuse Strategy 2024-2031” includes a commitment for my Department to consult on increasing the maximum sentences available for the offences of causing or allowing the death of a child or to suffer serious physical harm within this financial year. I plan to publish the consultation at the start of 2025.  As the offence also relates to “vulnerable adults”, the consultation will seek views on proposals to increase the maximum sentences for both children and vulnerable adults.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the action his Department is taking to minimise traffic disruption caused by roadworks on the Sydenham bypass.
answer_text: The resurfacing of the A2 Sydenham Bypass is a substantial scheme and the Department is aware that projects of this scale will inevitably cause some disruption and delays, however all work has been carefully planned in order to minimise inconvenience to the public and complete the scheme in as short a period as possible.  These essential works are part of our commitment to enhancing road safety, improving the driving experience and reducing long-term maintenance costs.  Once completed, the resurfaced road will offer a smoother and more durable surface, benefiting thousands of daily commuters and supporting the long-term infrastructure of this key route.To minimise disruption closures and lane restrictions have been limited to overnight and at weekends.  The weekend closures will continue over the next seven consecutive weekends, commencing at 10pm Friday until 6am on Monday, between the Tillysburn junction and the start of the M3 with weeknight overnight closures in operation for the duration of the schemeDuring periods of closure a diversionary route will be clearly signed via Holywood Road, Newtownards Road and Middlepath Street.  An alternative route will also be available via Holywood Exchange, Airport Road and Sydenham Road.  Alternative routes will also be signed for cyclists and pedestrians.The Department continues to monitor progress throughout and will take further mitigation measures to alleviate traffic congestion and delay where possible.The majority of the scheme is expected to be completed ahead of the Christmas period when the road will remain open, however more closures may be required in the new year.  In addition, information relating to associated road closures and lane restrictions can be found by on: www.trafficwatchni.com

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to outline what engagement he has had with the Minister for Infrastructure regarding the impact that pressures on the wastewater infrastructure is having on the local economy.
To ask the Minister of Health to detail the overnight respite facilities for children with disabilities currently available in the South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: Currently the regionally availability of short breaks is significantly limited. There has been an increase in the number of children and young people with disabilities and there has been an increasing demand for access to short breaks services. Some children and young people with a disability have also required longer-term residential placements, and this has led to occasions when overnight short breaks units have had children and young people placed there for longer than 90 days. This has meant that these units at times cannot maintain a short breaks service.The South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust (SEHSCT) have advised that they have two facilities operating at present providing short breaks Greenhill facility is providing short breaks for 14 young people, the Greenhill facility is a two bedded home operating 2/3 nights per week delivering 2 to 3 short breaks per week.Forest Lodge is a nurse lead facility that is shared with BHSCT. Forest Lodge provides care to children with complex health needs. Forest Lodge  has three beds operational, one bed used by SEHSCT. Forest Lodge is operating 5 nights per week supporting 7 young people at present from the SEHSCT.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any consideration his Department has given to an independent review into the funding of NI Water.
answer_text: My Department had already carried out considerable work on this issue in 2019 and 2020 which I have asked my officials to update in collaboration with NI Water.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the engagement she has had with the Minister for Communities in relation to homelessness among prisoners released from (i) HM Prison Maghaberry; (ii) HM Prison Magilligan; and (iii) Hydebank Wood College and Women's Prison.
answer_text: The Department for Communities and the Northern Ireland Housing Executive (NIHE) have responsibility for assessing and meeting housing needs and working with communities to develop programmes to meet those needs. However, accommodation challenges for those leaving the criminal justice system requires a multi-agency approach. In October 2022 I met with the Ministers for Communities and Health to explore how to tackle chronic homelessness following a number of serious incidents which occurred as a result of homelessness following release from custody. In the absence of an Executive, officials from my Department took forward a range of cross-departmental collaborations to mitigate the risk of homelessness for those leaving prison, through the Department for Communities Interdepartmental Homelessness Action Plan. My Department has contributed to years one to four of this plan and has recently submitted proposals for the year 5 iteration in support of this work and the focused actions under priority area three; “Support for those leaving places of care” which names prisons. One of my Department’s actions in the Plan is to deliver early intervention support for those exiting custody. In April we introduced NI Housing Executive Advisors into the custodial setting at Maghaberry. This initiative provides the opportunity to address accommodation issues for prisoners in the weeks leading up to release.  Subject to an evaluation of the initiative, we hope to roll this intervention out to both Magilligan and Hydebank Wood.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) the number of sports clubs in North Down eligible to apply for funding for electric vehicle charge points; and (ii) the eligibility criteria for this funding.
answer_text: The administration and operation of the Shared Island Sports Club EV Charging Scheme is a matter solely for the Irish Government, as the Scheme founder.  Whilst neither I, nor the Department have any remit over the Scheme, my officials have sought the requested information and can advise as follows:The Irish Government launched ‘The Shared Island Sports Club EV Charging Scheme’ in January 2023, resourced through the Irish Government’s Shared Island Fund. The scheme was designed to support the roll-out of publicly accessible, fast-charging for electric vehicles, for sports clubs and communities across the island of Ireland. 48 Sports Clubs across the North representing a wide range of sporting bodies were successful in their applications and the scheme has now closed.All sports clubs in North Down who met the following criteria were eligible to apply: They are affiliated to a National Governing Body recognised by Sport Ireland or a Northern Ireland Sport Governing Body and which operates on an all-island basis; orThey are affiliated with a Governing Body for Sport, which has a partnership in place with its equivalent body in the other jurisdiction.The full eligibility and selection criteria for the scheme, can be found here:https://www.pobal.ie/programmes/zevisports/

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any consideration his Department has given to promoting road racing as part of its Tourism Action Plan.
answer_text: The Tourism Action Plan will contain high level strategic actions to grow the tourism sector over the next 10 years. As part of its development, the Tourism Partnership Board will consider issues around developing a long-term programme of events capable of contributing to the delivery of my economic vision. Since 2013, through Tourism NI’s events funding programmes, my Department has provided over £2m of financial support to road racing events here. This has included well known events such as the Armoy Road Races, Mid Antrim 150, North West 200, Ulster Grand Prix, Cookstown 100 and Tandragee 100. My Department and Tourism NI recognise the value that road racing events bring to the region.  They provide a visitor experience for spectators from all over the world, benefitting the local economy.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the action his Department is taking to improve road safety amongst novice drivers.
answer_text: I am very aware that, as a category, new drivers are disproportionately represented in fatal and serious road collisions.The Road Traffic (Amendment) Act 2016 provides the statutory framework for graduated driver licensing (GDL). The purpose of GDL is to provide a revised training, testing and post-test regime for new drivers. Further subordinate legislation, dedicated funding and significant operational planning activity are required in advance of the implementation of the new arrangements. I have asked my officials to seek legal advice which will help me determine the best way forward.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail (i) the number of letters issued by her Department to sub-postmasters and sub-postmistresses in relation to the quashing of convictions; and (ii) the number of letters still to be issued.
answer_text: On 19 September 2024, my Department issued letters to twelve individuals to advise that their convictions have been quashed by the Post Office (Horizon System) Offences Act 2024.The convictions of a further 14 individuals are still under active consideration by the Department.  In these cases, limited or no information is held by the criminal justice system in Northern Ireland, due to the passage of time.  Therefore, my Department has sought information from the Post Office Limited in respect of these individuals and it is anticipated that information will be shared with us in the coming weeks to enable us to make a determination on whether those convictions were quashed by the legislation.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail how her Department intends to allow the retention of biometric data that could later be utilised as part of a legacy case.
answer_text: The Northern Ireland Office has made provision in the Independent Commission for Reconciliation and Information Recovery (Biometric Material) Regulations 2024 for the preservation of biometric material for the purposes of legacy investigations by the Independent Commission for Reconciliation and Information Recovery.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the projected number of jobs to be created in North Down as a result of the Belfast Region City Deal.
answer_text: My officials are working collaboratively with colleagues in the Ards and North Down Borough Council to support the development and delivery of the £72.8m Bangor Waterfront Regeneration Programme funded by the NI Executive through the Belfast Region City Deal.The project will regenerate 2.2 miles of Bangor’s waterfront from Skippingstone Beach to Ballyholme and is projected to create an additional 489 jobs.Additionally, the Department for the Economy leads on the Ards and North Down Innovation Hub, known as “the Shed”, which will be a new Regional Innovation Hub project within the Belfast Region City Deal. It will be based in Holywood and is projected to create 124 jobs.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for a timescale of the review of the Northern Ireland Policing Board.
answer_text: I anticipate that the review will conclude by the end of December 2024.
