question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure when train services will commence from Belfast Grand Central Station.
answer_text: The Department is responsible for ensuring NI Railways’ regulatory compliance with the rail safety and interoperability regulations and grants authority for new infrastructure to enter into service. Officials are working through the process with the safety of the travelling passengers’ paramount.. Translink have now announced that rail services will commence from 13th October.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether she has plans to provide additional resources to the Planning Appeals Commission, following her meeting with the Minister for Infrastructure on 16th July 2024.
To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the draft report on the Part-time 20mph Speed Limits at Schools Review.
To ask the Minister of Health to detail any action his Department has taken to raise awareness of the public consultation on policy proposals to inform the development of a new public health bill.
answer_text: The Department publicised the launch of the public consultation on policy proposals to inform the development of a new public health bill by issuing a press release on its website. This press release was also disseminated to a wide variety of broadcast and print media, and published on the Department’s X and LinkedIn accounts. Additional posts on these channels have been made ahead of the consultation’s closing date which has been extended until 14 October 2024Since 5 July 2024, the consultation document itself, and several options for providing a response to it, have been publicly available on the NI Direct and Department of Health websites. In addition to its advertising and publication on the open platforms described above, the Department also directly communicated news of the consultation launch to over 500 individuals and organisations, who were encouraged to share it with interested parties in their networks. These recipients comprised a number of statutory consultees, as well as a broad range of recipients from the public, private and non-profit sectors who were identified as potentially having a particular interest in the proposed legislative reform.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any action his Department is taking to address the decline in butterfly numbers.
answer_text: This year has seen a significant decline in butterfly numbers, likely exacerbated by poor weather but also reflecting a long term and continuous decline, largely due to habitat loss and changing land management.My Department has been actively engaged with the development and implementation of actions in the 2021-2025 All-Ireland Pollinator Plan. NIEA officials work closely with partner organisations to develop pollinator actions into project work plans. Conservation action is also supported and funded through the Environment Fund and Environmental Farming Scheme to support habitat restoration and creation, including those that support butterfly species and other pollinators.  NIEA is engaged through the UK Pollinator Monitoring Scheme to gather data to identify population trends, impacts and threats for Northern Ireland. Additionally, Northern Ireland Priority Species lists, including butterflies, are currently being updated. The lists are used to guide local government and public bodies to meet their biodiversity duties through policies, strategies and decision making, and to support conservation actions by land-owners and land managers.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any plans his Department has to engage with VisitBritain during the development of the tourism action plan.
answer_text: For many years, Tourism NI has agreed an annual Memorandum of Understanding with Visit Britain incorporating collaborative corporate engagement and both consumer and business tourism promotional activities. The Memorandum of Understanding includes an agreement for Tourism NI representation at senior Visit Britain and British National Tourism Organisation levels, presenting valuable opportunities for engagement and consultation with these organisations. Such senior representation forums include Visit Britain Board meetings, Chief Executive meetings and Strategic Partner Board meetings. These forums are useful for engagement and discussions on strategic issues such as the development of the Tourism Action Plan.  In addition to these forums, Tourism NI participates in other Visit Britain led quarterly British National Tourism operational boards across many business areas, including marketing, business development and data analytics. These too provide valuable knowledge sharing, collaboration and discussion opportunities.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail his Department's plans to roll-out dementia safeguarding scheme wristbands in the Ards and North Down Borough Council area.
answer_text: My Department recognises that people living with Dementia, and those who care for them, across Northern Ireland want to be assured that they are safe and that they can be appropriately assisted, if needed, when in public spaces.   My Department welcomes any new and innovative ideas that improve the lives of individuals living with Dementia in Northern Ireland. My officials have contacted both the South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust and the Locality Planning Department within Ards and North Down Council.  Ards and North Down Borough Council’s Locality Planning team have also reached out to Age Concern Causeway to explore the roll out of the Dementia safeguarding scheme wristbands within the Ards and North Down area.  The South Eastern Trust advised that they have also contacted Age Concern in Causeway and, while the pilot is currently focused on the Coleraine area, Age Concern is open to engaging in discussions about potential roll outs of the scheme across Northern Ireland.  The Trust highlight that they continue to be supportive of any initiative that will improve the lives of those living with a dementia and/or their carers.   They have also advised that they will be raising awareness of this initiative through the Dementia Service Improvement Leads, the Dementia Navigator service, and other Dementia projects across the Trust area.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the potential benefits to health outcomes following the introduction of minimum unit pricing for alcohol.
answer_text: The latest official figures show that in 2022, 356 people died from alcohol-specific causes, representing the highest on record. Alcohol misuse contributes to some of the biggest inequality gaps, with alcohol-related deaths up to 5 times higher in the most deprived areas compared to the least deprived areas. In addition, alcohol misuse costs Northern Ireland up to £900m every year. Therefore, one of the key priorities of my Department, and for the Executive, is to tackle the harm caused by the use of alcohol and other drugs. Our substance use strategy Preventing Harm Empowering Recovery is underscored by five population-level outcomes. The vision of this strategy is that people are supported in the prevention and reduction of the harm related to the use of alcohol and other drugs, and will be empowered to maintain recovery.  In respect of alcohol, as the Strategy sets out, the wider legislative environment has an impact on the availability, accessibility, and the behavioural norms that exist in relation to the use of alcohol. The Strategy therefore contained a commitment to consult on the introduction of a range of potential options to seek to reduce alcohol harms through a pricing mechanism, including Minimum Unit Pricing – this consultation was completed in 2022. I believe the modelling carried out in association with the consultation indicates that MUP could have significant positive benefits for reducing the harm alcohol can cause, reducing the impact on our health service with fewer alcohol-related hospital admissions, and reducing the number of alcohol-related deaths. MUP can have further positive impacts on wider society, with the model suggesting Northern Ireland could also experience fewer alcohol-attributable crimes each year. This is supported by the evidence emerging from the evaluation of this policy where it has been introduced in other jurisdictions like Scotland, Wales and Ireland. Having considered the options and the evidence, I believe that MUP has the potential to have a real and lasting positive impact on the level of alcohol misuse in Northern Ireland. This is a cross-cutting issue and I am keen to further discuss the potential introduction of MUP in Northern Ireland with my Executive colleagues and hope to be able to say more on this issue in the near future.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the prison capacity in each of the last five years.
answer_text: The Northern Ireland Prison Service adjusts the accommodation it has available and operational in response to a range of factors, including the size and configuration of the population and the use of cell-sharing.  For each of the last five years the maximum daily population the prison service accommodated is shown in the table below:YearMax20191,54120201,64220211,60820221,75120231,943Source: PRISM - Management Information

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail any plans her Department has to increase the maximum sentences for death by dangerous driving in an upcoming sentencing bill.
answer_text: The Sentencing Bill I plan to bring forward will deliver on a number of the recommendations arising from my Department’s public consultation on a review of sentencing policy.  This will include increasing maximum penalties for offences causing death by driving.It is proposed that the maximum sentence for causing death by dangerous driving, careless driving while under the influence of drink or drugs or careless driving and refusing to provide a specimen should be increased from 14 years to 20 years. In addition, I propose to introduce a life sentence as the maximum sentence for  repeat offenders of these offences within specified time limits. I also propose to increase the minimum period of mandatory disqualification for these offences from 2 to 4 years, and for a repeat offender, from 3 to 6 years.  The Sentencing Bill is expected to be a large and complex Bill, given the variety of matters to be included. On present planning assumptions I anticipate this Bill being introduced later in 2025.  This will be dependent upon the progress of earlier Assembly business.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail any engagement she has had with the UK government in relation to the progression of the Belfast Region City Deal.
answer_text: I refer to the Written Ministerial Statement of 18 September.https://www.finance-ni.gov.uk/publications/pausing-city-and-growth-deals-funding-commitment

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when he will answer AQW 13225/22-27.
To ask the Minister of Health when he will answer AQW 12931/22-27.
To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department is considering additional support for prescription deliveries.
answer_text: Community pharmacy services are commissioned in accordance with a 3-year commissioning plan for 2022-2025, developed by the Department and agreed with Community Pharmacy Northern Ireland (CPNI).Medicines delivery services are not a commissioned service in the current commissioning plan.Decisions as to whether to offer, withdraw or charge for such non-commissioned services are a commercial matter for individual pharmacies.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to outline the provisions in the draft Programme for Government 2024-2027 that will support victims and survivors.
answer_text: The draft Programme for Government sets out our proposed collective priorities of The Executive. The Programme for Government, however, does not include everything that every department is bringing forward separately, including work to support Victims and Survivors that we will progress through The Executive Office.We will also continue to support those affected through the Victims and Survivors Service.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what action his Department is taking to address growing waiting lists for a first consultant-led outpatient appointment within the South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust.
To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for a timescale on the creation of the Reform and Transformation unit.
answer_text: In the draft Programme for Government, we recognise the need for urgent Reform and Transformation to support and sustain the delivery of many of our public services. Reform and transformation cannot just be about money and how it is allocated. Instead, the focus needs to be on what we do, why we do it, how we do it, and being held to account for the results we achieve.To ensure we lay the foundations for this scale of change, we have committed to establishing a new Reform and Transformation Unit at the heart of government. Work is already underway to scope out how such an office would operate across Government and we will make further announcements in due course as this develops.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the support his Department is providing to farmers without access to the internet, following his Department’s move to online-only communication.
answer_text: My Department’s Area-based Schemes Division has been moving gradually towards a fully digital service for all schemes and services, including communications. A Customer Support Model has been developed to support farmers through this transition. Telephone support is offered to farmers to access their online services and communications and is available initially through the Area-based Schemes Advisory Service. Where further support is required, Digital Assistance is offered through local DAERA offices. Digital Assistance for accessing communications will be provided by telephone, and face-to-face appointments. By default, Digital Assistance will be provided by telephone first, with face-to-face only offered by exception. DAERA will triage calls and determine the type of assistance required. The assistance offered will be tailored to the individual’s needs whether this is due to the lack of IT skills or internet access. To further assist all our customers to transition to this new service, we may also issue text messages to prompt individuals when a new DAERA Message is available to view.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the action his Department is taking to improve pathways to appropriate opportunities for young people with special educational needs when they leave school.
answer_text: I am committed to ensuring that young people with statements of special educational needs (SEN) are supported in their transition from school into adulthood.My officials are working closely with colleagues across the Departments of Economy (DoE), Health (DoH) and Communities (DfC) as part of the End to End (E2E) Review of SEN, to develop and enhance clear and appropriate pathways for young people transitioning from education into adulthood.  Transitions is one of 12 workstreams within the E2E Review.   To further this work, my department and DoH jointly chair a Transitions Working Group which specifically aims to understand the experiences and perceptions of the transitions planning process.  The Transitions Working Group includes representation from the Education Authority (EA), the Public Health Agency (PHA) and officials from DfC and DoE.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department will bid for additional funding in the October monitoring round to support community pharmacies.
answer_text: The Department continues to be committed to working with CPNI on commissioning arrangements within the existing Ministerial mandate and financial envelope. An opening envelope of £147m was confirmed to contractors for the 2024/25 year, representing an increase of £19m on last year’s opening envelope, and equates to an increase of 14.8% overall. The Department of Health’s budget allocation for 2024-25 represented a 2.3% reduction in funding compared to our expenditure in 2023-24. Both my predecessor and I repeatedly warned about the consequences of the Executive and Assembly passing a below need allocation for our health service. The Department continues to face a significant funding gap to maintain existing services and the HSC continues to face an extremely unpredictable and volatile position. There are significant difficulties in terms of financial planning across the HSC and officials are continuing to work closely to review the positionThe Department of Finance did not commission an October monitoring round this financial year; instead, it held an In Year Information Gathering exercise to inform Executive decisions to manage forecast departmental overcommitments. This did not provide an opportunity to submit any bids for additional funding.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the creation of a new Urgent Care Centre in the Ulster Hospital.
answer_text: As advised to you in April, the South Eastern Trust has confirmed that progress is being made on the development of an Urgent Care Centre (UCC) at the Ulster Hospital, Dundonald.  The design of the new facilities, adjacent to the Emergency Department, has been completed along with the tendering process and the contractor has now commenced work on site.				It is expected that building work will be completed, as planned, by late January 2025.  The new Urgent Care Centre will then open following a short period of commissioning of relevant and necessary clinical equipment.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail his Department’s planned spending on road safety advertising in the current budgetary year.
answer_text: My Department’s planned spending on road safety advertising in the current budgetary year is currently £200,000. In addition to campaign advertising, my Department uses social media platforms to promote road safety messaging, primarily through ShareTheRoadToZero.com, a road safety community programme, which was most recently backed by Liverpool and international defender Conor Bradley on behalf of the IFA.One of the best ways to ensure safety on our roads and fulfil our long-term goal of eliminating death and serious injury by 2050, is by changing road user behaviour. As road users we all have a personal responsibility to behave in a way that keeps ourselves and others safe. The sad reality is that if we do not change our own attitudes when we are using the roads, our death toll will continue to increase. I would encourage everyone to share road safety messaging and to take the road safety pledge.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the launch of the consultation on his Department's Active Travel Delivery Plan.
answer_text: My officials are currently finalising the draft Active Travel Delivery Plan and are putting in place arrangements for the launch of a public consultation.  It is my intention that this consultation would launch before the end of the year, and I hope to be in a position to announce the exact date in the near future.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the proposed new build at Priory Integrated College.
answer_text: The plans for the new school build for Priory Integrated College are well advanced, with the technical design and pre-tender estimate report (RIBA Stage 4) anticipated to be lodged with the Department for consideration in October 2024.Planning approval for the project was granted on 2 July 2024. However, given the many immediate pressures on my department’s capital budget, the project will not be permitted to move to construction until there is sufficient capital budget available to do so.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the development of the proposals included in the All-Ireland Strategic Rail Review.
answer_text: I was pleased to publish the All-Island Strategic Rail Review alongside Minister Ryan on 31 July. The Review, which provides a roadmap to transform the rail network across the island out to 2050, is an evidence-based assessment of the potential to enhance and improve our rail network and a coherent framework to deliver it. Now that the Review has been published, one of the key initial steps will be to prioritise the recommendations and develop an implementation plan and my officials are currently considering how best to take this forward for the interventions identified for the North. More detailed work will be needed to test the feasibility of recommendations and each recommendation will be subject to separate appraisal and decision making in line with normal processes.  Given the scale of the interventions and the magnitude of transformation involved, delivering the recommendations will undoubtably be challenging and we will need to explore all avenues of funding available given the importance of this work in terms of economic activity, connectivity and regional balance.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for her assessment of the benefits of allowing additional broadcasting in courtrooms.
answer_text: Allowing broadcasting in certain courts will support the principle of open justice and allow the public to better understand the operation of the legal system in Northern Ireland, and how judicial decisions are made. There is a need to take great care in preparing for such a move. My Department is preparing a policy consultation on courts broadcasting which will take account of the positive evaluation of the pilot that concluded in April.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any action his Department is taking to support the holiday parks industry.
answer_text: Whilst tourism legislation in the North does not cover caravan parks and campsites, Tourism NI recognises the role that holiday parks play in the wider tourism sector. Sites are listed on the Discover NI website and Tourism NI engages regularly with the industry through the Caravan & Camping Forum. Holiday park operators can avail of support from Tourism NI’s Tourism Enterprise Development programme which spans practical advice, webinars, masterclasses and toolkits. The Programme helps businesses to assess operational effectiveness, control costs, identify growth opportunities, and share knowledge and good practice. The online platform, MyTourismNI, provides free learning across topics such as sales and marketing, digital, sustainability and people. A range of supports are also available on www.tourismni.com

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the average waiting time for an endometriosis diagnosis in each of the last five years, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: Endometriosis is a complex condition that affects patients to differing degrees.  A diagnosis is determined based on the individual’s presenting symptoms and associated medical and surgical history, as well as the results obtained from a wide range of clinical tests and diagnostic procedures.Endometriosis diagnosis takes place as part of the Gynaecology pathway. The following information is the average waiting time for Gynaecology from the “my-waiting-time information”. Health and Social Care Trust (HSCT)Average waiting time for patients defined as UrgentAverage waiting time for patients defined as RoutineBelfast HSCT2137Northern HSCT956South Eastern HSCT54130Southern HSCT4486Western HSCT29116Source: My-waiting-time information, Trust Websites. Of note: Average waiting time is the average (mean) of all patients waiting within the specialty at each hospital Trust and can be impacted by waiting times for subspecialty appointments. Patients waiting within subspecialties may experience a shorter waiting time or a significantly longer waiting time depending on the demand for a particular subspecialty appointment type.Some patients will wait less time than the average and some patients will wait longer than the average waiting time indicated.The data presented in the website reflects the average waiting time for those patients who are actively waiting for their appointment, procedure or endoscopy.Patients are being managed based on clinical urgency and therefore waiting times can vary depending on each patient’s clinical condition.The waiting time information provided is updated on the first Thursday of each month.Following the recent publication of the Getting It Right First Time Report, which provided a review of gynaecology services in Northern Ireland, including those suffering endometriosis, and the 20 recommendations to improve gynaecology services, the Department of Health’s Strategic Planning and Performance Group have established a forum with Trust, GP and PHA representation to oversee ongoing service improvement in this speciality and ensure that this improvement is implemented at a local and regional level. Trusts have also been asked to establish their own action plan to take forward recommendations and improvements which are relevant to their Trust. This should help to address the long waiting lists in each Trust area

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether his Department is considering additional measures to address the MOT backlog.
answer_text: In each of the last two years the Driver and Vehicle Agency (DVA) has conducted over 1.1 million vehicle tests which are the highest numbers ever recorded.  This increase in capacity has been achieved through a range of measures including the recruitment of additional vehicle examiners, with the latest tranche of new examiners being appointed to their test centres week commencing 16 September. The DVA also use overtime to provide cover for leave and offer vehicle test appointments on Sundays and bank holidays when testing is not normally available. In 2023, there were only seven days in the year when they did not offer MOT appointments.  Disappointingly, in the region of 900 customers per week fail to attend a booked appointment, which places additional demand on this service.On 30 April 2024, I announced the introduction of Temporary Exemption Certificates (TECs) to help reduce vehicle testing waiting times. From 1 June 2024, private cars first registered between 1 June 2019 and 31 May 2020 or between 1 June 2017 and 31 May 2018, which have a valid MOT issued by the DVA will have a one-year TEC automatically applied from the date their current MOT certificate expires.  I am confident that the introduction of TECs will provide relief for many customers and will gradually improve waiting times over the course of the implementation period, with DVA targeting an improvement to around six weeks by 31 March 2025.To further increase vehicle testing capacity, the DVA is investing in new test centres. The construction of a new test centre at Hydebank, in Belfast, is now completed and the DVA is going through a rigorous process of testing the newly installed equipment and software to ensure it is safe to use and meets the requirements set out in the contract specification.  Hydebank has already opened for driver testing and, subject to the equipment and software meeting the required standards, it is anticipated to open for vehicle testing later this year. The construction of a second new test centre at Mallusk has also commenced and this new test centre is expected to open in 2025. Once fully operational, these two new centres will provide the capacity to test over 200,000 additional vehicles per annum, which will help the DVA meet the future demand for this service.I have also agreed to explore with my officials the potential for biennial vehicle testing in the North. You may wish to note that a move to biennial MOT testing would involve significant policy development, engagement with a range of stakeholders and be subject to public consultation and then potentially amendment to primary legislation. Vehicle testing is a devolved matter, and so any move to biennial MOT testing would entail an Act of the NI Assembly.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for his assessment of (i) the current wastewater infrastructure in Donaghadee; and (ii) the impact the current wastewater infrastructure is having on property development in Donaghadee.
answer_text: The management of public wastewater infrastructure is an operational matter for NI Water. However, I have sought the requested information from NI Water and have been advised as follows:Donaghadee is served by North Down wastewater treatment works, which currently has available wastewater treatment capacity. However, there are significant capacity constraints within the Donaghadee wastewater network, due to the presence of eleven storm overflows that are operating more frequently than designed and these are therefore classified as Unsatisfactory Intermittent Discharges; and(ii) 	The current wastewater capacity constraints in the Donaghadee catchment mean that NI Water is unable to accept new connections to the sewerage network as to do so would increase the risk of out of sewer flooding which may impact homes and business and may also increase environmental pollution. However, there are some areas within the catchment where a bespoke drainage solution such as storm water offsetting may be possible, which could provide capacity within the combined sewer to facilitate a new connection. Developers should always use the Pre-Development Enquiry (PDE) process and follow NI Water’s three stage pre-planning process. PDEs are valid for an 18-month period from the date of issue and any solutions identified through this process, would be developer led and funded.However, there is an area of Donaghadee upstream of Warren Road Shore combined sewer overflow, where a bespoke drainage solution is no longer a viable option, due to the severity of the Unsatisfactory Intermittent Discharge at this location. For any future queries relating to operational matters, you should contact NI Water directly, through the Elected Representatives Hotline on 0345 300 6461 or through waterline@niwater.comMaking direct contact with NI Water will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of limited public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any engagement his Department has had with NI Water in relation to an increase in the number of Event Duration Monitors within storm overflows.
answer_text: My Department’s role is to set policy and provide funding to NI Water in the form of a subsidy, in lieu of domestic water charges. In terms of funding for 2024-25, I have provided NI Water with a total budget allocation of almost £0.5 billion, representing just under 40% of the total non-ring-fenced budget for my Department. This reflects the central importance of water and sewerage services to our society.NI Water has advised that within Price Control (PC) 21 (2021-27), it intends to install 700 event duration monitors (EDMs). This has been planned in three two-year phases and commenced in April 2021, with the PC21 (Phase 1) focusing on bathing and shellfish water locations. As of July 2024, 292 storm overflows have had EDMs installed and sufficiently verified to support effective use for operational maintenance and monitoring. In PC21 (Phase 2), NI Water is progressing installation of EDMs in other priority areas as agreed with the Northern Ireland Environment Agency (NIEA). NI Water will also be seeking agreements with NIEA for Phase 3 (2026-27), subject to funding.The information provided by the EDMs will give NI Water an accurate assessment of how discharge points are performing and will help to inform future investment plans.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the number of letters of offer issued as part of the Rural Micro Capital Grants Scheme 2024-25, broken down by constituency.
answer_text: I am pleased to confirm that 915 community-led voluntary organisations have been issued with Letters of Offer awarding total grants of just over £1.5m to help them address locally identified poverty or social isolation issues. department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for a timescale on replacing outstanding street lighting with LED lights.
answer_text: My Department’s programme to replace its sodium street light with energy efficient LED units is very well advanced.  Since 2018, approximately 222,200 LED units have been installed, which represents approximately 76% of the total street lighting network.However, the member will be aware that my Department has been operating in a difficult financial environment for a number of years due to under funding and austerity by the British Government. As the capital budget for my Department in 2024/25 is less than required, available funding must be prioritised accordingly.  Unfortunately this means that there will be limited progress on this programme in the current financial yearI will continue to work with Executive colleagues to ensure that appropriate investment is made, however the timescales for replacing the outstanding street lights with LEDs will depend on future funding made available to my Department.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance for an update on the sale of (i) Clarence Court; and (ii) Netherleigh House.
answer_text: My Department is progressing an ambitious review of its Office Estate with the aim of reducing the in-scope footprint by 40% by March 2028.With regard to both (i) Netherleigh House and (ii) Clarence Court, the sales process is nearing completion and officials are confident that both sales will be completed in the coming weeks.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of people diagnosed with polycystic ovary syndrome in each of the last five years, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: I have been advised that polycystic ovary syndrome, is a condition that is diagnosed primarily in Primary Care and therefore this information would not be held by the Trusts.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail any engagement she has had with the Minister for Infrastructure in relation to the appropriate resourcing of the Planning Appeals Commission.
answer_text: I met with the Minister for Infrastructure on 16 July 2024 to discuss a range of justice related planning matters, which included resourcing of the Planning Appeals Commission. You may wish to note that The Northern Ireland Audit Office has commenced a strategic review of the PAC.  Further details are available at: Work in Progress - Strategic Review of the Planning Appeals Commission | Northern Ireland Audit Office (niauditoffice.gov.uk).

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the number of Skill Up courses funded by his Department in each of the last four years.
answer_text: The Skill Up programme commenced in September 2021, supported by my Department and the NIO, with funding ending on March 31st 2024.  As part of my commitment to ensuring individuals have the right skills to enable good jobs and grow the economy, I have extended the Skill Up programme. The number of courses funded in each financial year are as follows:September 2021 – March 2022: 295April 2022 – March 2023: 401April 2023 – March 2024: 293	September 2024 – March 2025: 339 approved to date.		The flexible nature of delivery means that additional courses may be offered across this financial year in response to emerging opportunities or growth areas.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance for an update on the provision of baby loss certificates.
answer_text: I would refer the Member to the response issued for AQW 14853 22-27.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the action his Department is taking to address increasing waiting lists for a first consultant-led outpatient appointment in the South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust.
To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the consultation that was carried out by his Department in advance of the decision to move to digital-only communication.
answer_text: My Department completed an Equality Impact Assessment, Rural Proofing and a full consultation exercise in 2016 on the proposal to use online as the primary channel for communicating and transacting with customers of DAERA’s Area-based Schemes only.  Direct face to face consultation with representatives from the Agricultural Consultants’ Association (NI), the Northern Ireland Agricultural Producers’s Association and the Ulster Farmers’ Union regarding the use of secure messaging for Area-based Schemes has been ongoing throughout its implementation. It was specifically discussed with ACA(NI) representatives on 8 February 2024 who supported the move and influenced its timing.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for an update on the publication of her Department’s foundational review of civil legal aid.
answer_text: My officials are currently finalising a report on the findings of the foundational review of civil legal services that I expect to receive shortly.As previously advised, I will then use that report, the review of criminal legal aid, and work to prepare for the reform of legal aid taxation to inform decisions about the future direction of legal aid reform, which I will share with the Assembly and with stakeholders at the appropriate time.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the number of Northern Ireland Civil Service administrative officer vacancies are based in North Down.
answer_text: Details of the total vacancies in each department is not held centrally by Department of Finance. Each civil service department is responsible for managing its resources – both financial and staff. Each department determines its individual workforce needs and is responsible for ensuring that any additional staffing resources are affordable. When a department identifies a vacancy it needs filled, the request is referred to NICSHR to initiate the process to fill the post. NICSHR liaises with all departments on an ongoing basis to seek to prioritise recruitment activity within allocated financial and staff resources. My Department records vacancies by town rather than constituency. As at the end of August 2024, NICSHR was managing a caseload of 1 Administrative Officer general service vacancy in Bangor, within the North Down constituency.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on an additional roll-out of the 20mph speed limit scheme outside schools in North Down.
answer_text: There have been two tranches of part time 20mph speed limits delivered, which introduced these measures at 215 schools, an investment of about £4m.   A review of the initiative has been undertaken to see if a further tranche of the 20mph schools programme is justified, and the draft review report has been recently submitted to my office for consideration.  When I have had time to consider it, I plan to make the report public.One of the best ways to ensure safety on our roads and fulfil our long-term goal of eliminating death and serious injury by 2050, is by changing road user behaviour. As road users we all have a personal responsibility to behave in a way that keeps ourselves and others safe.  The sad reality is that if we do not change our own attitudes when we are using the roads, our death toll will continue to increase.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for a timescale on the publication of final National Institute for Health and Care Excellence guidance in relation to recommending use of the drugs Kaftrio, Orkambi and Symkevi for cystic fibrosis.
answer_text: The National Institute for Health and Care Excellence (NICE) final guidance is expected to publish around 24th July 2024.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for a timescale on the completion of her Department’s review of civil legal aid.
answer_text: I have instructed my officials to prepare a report on the foundational review of civil legal aid in September 2024.I will determine the future direction of the legal aid reform agenda in light of the findings and evidence contained in that review, in the report of the fundamental review of criminal legal aid, and the Department’s current work to prepare for the reform of taxation of costs for the purpose of determining legal aid remuneration.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for her assessment of the increase in legal aid spending since 2019.
answer_text: For a number of years prior to Covid, the annual expenditure on legal aid was £82m +/- 3%.  This decreased in 2020-21 as a direct impact of Covid on Court activity.  Since then, spend on Legal Aid has been increasing due to post-Covid recovery, an increase in the number of cases being progressed, and a general increase in the cost of cases.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 13224/22-27, to detail when his Department expects average waiting times for an MOT appointment to reach around six weeks.
answer_text: It is anticipated that Temporary Exemption Certificates will gradually improve waiting times for an MOT appointment over the course of this financial year.The Driver and Vehicle Agency is targeting to achieve an average waiting time of six weeks or less by 31 March 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the action his Department is taking to reduce the time taken by the Northern Ireland Environment Agency to respond to planning applications as consultees.
answer_text: As a statutory consultee within the planning system DAERA performance is measured against a duty to respond to a statutory consultation within 21 calendar days or otherwise agreed timescale.Indicative figures for 2023/2024 indicate the Agency has responded to 72% of its statutory consultations within the 21-day or agreed target date. This is the highest-level annual performance rate by the Department in the past 5 years.Over the last few years, the Department has undertaken a range of measures to improve performance including:A review and update of guidance and internal processes and procedures;Analysis of consultations being receipted to inform targeted measures to reduce the level of re-consultation and improve the quality of information being submitted with a consultation;Engagement with a variety of stakeholders including specific sectoral engagements with agents to improve the quality of information submitted with a consultation; andTemporary redeployment of additional caseworkers.Additional processes have been introduced such as the cessation of updates in order to allow staff to focus on the actual processing of consultations.In addition, the Department is working in collaboration with the Department for Infrastructure cross-governmental Planning Statutory Consultee Forum and has contributed to a number of exercises aiming to drive forward continuous improvements in the planning system.Going forward the Department will be seeking to enhance the permanent staff complement in this work area, subject to budgetary constraints.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether his Department plans to introduce sign language as a GCSE subject.
answer_text: As I advised in my answer to AQW 9071/22-27, a process is in train to develop a GCSE in British Sign Language in England.  Once that qualification is available in England it can be accredited for use in Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any action his Department is taking to encourage businesses to facilitate industry placements for undergraduate students.
answer_text: Invest NI financially supports industry-led networks via the Collaborative Growth Programme, including Manufacturing and Engineering Growth and Advancement (MEGA) in Mid-Ulster.  The MEGA Degree Apprenticeship route aligns academic learning with industry needs, improving career attractiveness and access to labour in the engineering sector.Separately since 2015 Invest NI and NI Screen have partnered with local studios to deliver an animation graduate and undergraduate placement scheme.  Similarly since 2022 NI Screen has delivered a scheme for the interactive/gaming sector, supporting 12-month paid industry placements. As an employer my Department annually facilitates 12-week intern and 51-week sandwich course placements for university students.More generally, my Department funds our higher education institutions for teaching, learning and research purposes, with the institutions themselves determining course provision and the approach to student placements.Higher Level Apprenticeships additionally provide industry approved qualification at Levels 4-7 through paid employment and ‘off the job’ training.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any plans his Department has to introduce a railway halt for George Best Belfast City Airport.
answer_text: The existing railway halt at Sydenham Station provides rail access to the George Best Belfast City Airport. The All-Island Strategic Rail Review (AISRR), is considering how the rail network on the island of Ireland can improve sustainable connectivity between major cities, enhance regional accessibility, support balanced regional development and contribute to decarbonisation. One of the recommendations from the draft Review is to improve existing rail-airport connections at George Best Belfast City Airport.Following closure of the AISRR public consultation in September 2023, work is ongoing to finalise the Review and its recommendations. However, prior to implementing any recommendations, including those in relation to rail connectivity to George Best Belfast City Airport, further work will be required to undertake feasibility studies and develop business cases to support future investment decisions. In parallel with the completion of the AISRR, Translink has commenced a Feasibility Study which is considering options to improve the existing rail connections to the George Best Belfast City Airport. The study includes engagement with both Belfast City Airport and my Department’s Roads Service to develop potential options including the projected costs. The feasibility study is due to be completed by the end of 2024.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail the level of engagement that has taken place with institutions on contributions for redress payments for Mother and Baby Institutions, Magdalene Laundries and Workhouses.
answer_text: The Executive Office has had constructive engagement with Institutions via PRONI and the Truth Recovery Independent Panel, resulting in 3,000 private records being deposited to date and discussions are ongoing regarding a further 1,500 items. The recently launched public consultation provides another important opportunity to engage with stakeholders on proposals for an inquiry and redress scheme. The inquiry will focus on answering three key questions of ‘What happened?’ ‘Why did it happen?’ and ‘Who was responsible?’. On completion of the formal investigation, it will be important to seek and obtain financial contributions for the redress scheme from those deemed responsible. This will likely follow a process similar to other redress schemes, which focus on the key stages of negotiation, mediation and arbitration, as required. Meaningful contributions from those responsible is an important part of the healing process and we are committed to this principle.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the number of Higher Level Apprenticeships participated in by (i) males; and (ii) females in each of the last five years.
answer_text: The breakdown of Higher Level Apprenticeship (HLA) programme participants by gender for each of the last five years is as follows:Academic YearHLA Participants at FE Colleges & CAFREHLA Participants at HE InstitutionsOverallFemaleMaleFemaleMaleFemaleMale2022/234551,080--4551,0802021/2233577525040058511752020/213056851552504609352019/203056751201704258452018/192404654580285545Please note, to prevent the identification of individuals, figures are rounded to the nearest 5, with 0, 1, and 2 rounded to 0.Statistics relating to HLA participants at Higher Education Institutions in the 2022/23 academic year have not yet been published.

answer_text: Figures for the number of convictions at courts for offences related to using a mobile phone whilst driving for the years 2019 – 2023 have been provided. question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the Non-Teaching Pay and Grading review.
answer_text: I am pleased to advise that following the successful conclusion of negotiations, a proposed agreement has been developed with the support staff unions.  GMB, NIPSA, Unite the Union and UNISON have agreed to consult their membership on the terms of the proposed agreement in the coming weeks.Full details of the proposed agreement can be found at Pay related issues | Department of Education (education-ni.gov.uk)  The Joint Negotiating Committee (JNC) support staff unions are conducting individual consultations using their own processes however they have suggested we may receive a collective response within two to three weeks.  Subject to a positive collective response, and allocation of funding by the Executive from June monitoring, stage 1 of the proposed agreement should be paid by late autumn 2024.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any action his Department is taking to review the effectiveness of new pothole repair technology in use in other jurisdictions.
answer_text: My Department works closely with the construction industry to monitor new technology and innovations in respect of pothole repairs.The Department keeps informed about new approaches to road maintenance through its engagement with other road authorities, Professional Engineering Institutions, universities and the wider construction and infrastructure industry. We are very open to adopting new approaches and innovative materials that offer an improvement to our current approach to road defect repair and are actively exploring how emerging technology can be used to better maintain the road network.     My Department will continue to liaise with contractors and suppliers to identify any opportunities in the future that could improve the repair of road defects.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the action his Department is taking to address the reduction in NHS dentistry work being carried out by dental practices.
answer_text: In March 2024, a 6% pay uplift for General Dental Services for 2023/24, in line with recommendations contained in the 51st Doctors and Dentists Review Board report, was announced. This uplift comes with a 7.3% uplift to non-staff expenses for 2023/24, with a 3% recurrent award in future years.In March 2024 the Department also announced a £9.2m investment package for General Dental Services to include:£1m for newly registered child patients through the reestablishment of the Enhanced Child Examination Scheme. This scheme provides children aged 0-10 who have not been registered with a dentist with an examination, individualised oral health advice and age-specific fluoride application to teeth to assist with preventing dental decay;£4.3m to fund a 30% enhancement to fees paid to dentists for health service fillings, extractions and root canal treatment for 2024/25, to support public access to priority treatments; and£3.9m for the treatment of high priority unregistered patients through a Dental Access Scheme.Until the Dental Access Scheme is implemented, a pilot scheme will continue to run to provide appointments to unregistered patients who require emergency dental treatment during the working week. If a patient is not registered with a dentist, requires emergency dental treatment, and has been unable to access an appointment, they can contact their local Strategic Planning and Performance Group office for advice.Pregnant and post-natal women are one group who – along with children aged under 18, or those in full-time education – are entitled to fully subsidised (free) Health Service dentistry. These groups will benefit from the impact of the above measures.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the action his Department is taking to ensure pregnant women have access to NHS dental treatment.
answer_text: In March 2024, a 6% pay uplift for General Dental Services for 2023/24, in line with recommendations contained in the 51st Doctors and Dentists Review Board report, was announced. This uplift comes with a 7.3% uplift to non-staff expenses for 2023/24, with a 3% recurrent award in future years.In March 2024 the Department also announced a £9.2m investment package for General Dental Services to include:£1m for newly registered child patients through the reestablishment of the Enhanced Child Examination Scheme. This scheme provides children aged 0-10 who have not been registered with a dentist with an examination, individualised oral health advice and age-specific fluoride application to teeth to assist with preventing dental decay;£4.3m to fund a 30% enhancement to fees paid to dentists for health service fillings, extractions and root canal treatment for 2024/25, to support public access to priority treatments; and£3.9m for the treatment of high priority unregistered patients through a Dental Access Scheme.Until the Dental Access Scheme is implemented, a pilot scheme will continue to run to provide appointments to unregistered patients who require emergency dental treatment during the working week. If a patient is not registered with a dentist, requires emergency dental treatment, and has been unable to access an appointment, they can contact their local Strategic Planning and Performance Group office for advice.Pregnant and post-natal women are one group who – along with children aged under 18, or those in full-time education – are entitled to fully subsidised (free) Health Service dentistry. These groups will benefit from the impact of the above measures.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline what engagement he has had with the Minister for Infrastructure in relation to improving emergency service response times to road traffic collisions.
answer_text: In October 2022, a draft Northern Ireland Road Safety Strategy to 2030 was published. While supported by Departments at the time, it unfortunately could not be finalised due to the absence of an Executive.  Following the return of Ministers, the Strategy has been updated and was brought to the Executive on 23 May 2024.  I support this strategy and welcome the ambition to deliver an interim target of 50% fewer deaths and serious injuries between 2020 and 2030.  The post-crash response pillar aims to establish an effective and appropriate response to collisions to help ensure road victims receive appropriate medical care and rehabilitation to minimise the severity of long-term impact of their injuries. The strategy identifies several key factors required for success in delivering the outlined 2030 targets and strategic outcomes. I am pleased it has secured Executive commitment; however, this needs to be followed-up with appropriate funding, resources and collaborative partnership, for which I will play my part.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for a timescale on the publication of the Road Safety Strategy Action Plan for 2024-25.
answer_text: My officials are currently working with road safety partners to finalise the road safety action plan for 2024/25.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on his Department’s review of its Supporting Newcomer Pupils policy.
answer_text: In October 2017, the Department commenced a review of its Supporting Newcomer Pupils policy.  This was in response to the growing number and increasing diversity of newcomer pupils arriving in Northern Ireland.  The Supporting Newcomer Policy public consultation launched in 2019 however, work was temporarily suspended in March 2020 due to the outbreak of the COVID-19 pandemic.  In 2022, work recommenced with policy proposals prepared for consideration of an incoming Minister.		My Department is also working alongside The Executive Office and other relevant Departments on the preparation of a draft Refugee Integration Strategy for Northern Ireland, and I wish to ensure that any targets designed to improve the educational experience of children arriving here seeking asylum or refuge are reflected in a revised Newcomer Policy.		Officials are in the process of planning work that will revisit consultation findings and policy proposals whilst considering the current context, including the draft Refugee Integration Strategy.  Proposals will then be presented for my consideration.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for her assessment of the current penalties for (i) drink driving; and (ii) driving whilst using a mobile phone.
answer_text: I have made no assessment of the current penalties for drink driving or driving whilst using a mobile phone as road safety policy is a matter for the Department for Infrastructure, as are the related offences and penalties.Where new offences and penalties are under consideration, my Department’s role is to advise on whether the proposals are proportionate and consistent with existing law for Northern Ireland.  If any change is being contemplated my officials will be happy to assist in this way.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department intends to introduce more localised care for those with cystic fibrosis.
answer_text: Belfast Trust is the regional centre for CF patients with support provided by a dedicated multidisciplinary team including medical, nursing, AHP, pharmacy and social care input.  There are a range of treatments which help control symptoms and prevent or reduce complications.Over the last three years my Department has supported the introduction of new, specialised medicines for adults and children with CF. These medicines help to correct the genetic malformation which causes CF, and have delivered transformative improvements in the health of these patients reducing their reliance on acute hospital care services. As these improvements are sustained there is an opportunity to consider the new and emerging needs of this patient group and the potential to reconfigure the current service model in ways which best support these.My Department is currently working with Belfast Trust to scope these changes in clinical demand to inform the future service delivery model for this condition.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the engagement his Department has had with the Shared Rural Network in relation to improving mobile coverage in North Down.
answer_text: The Shared Rural Network (SRN) is expected to extend 4G coverage in the most cost-effective, efficient, timely and environmentally friendly way for consumers and businesses in remote rural communities.Locally we will benefit from the mobile communications industry funded partial not-spot (PNS) element of the SRN Programme which will see 4G coverage here increase to 85% from all four Mobile Network Operators (MNOs) and 98% coverage from at least one MNO. My officials participate in the NI SRN Working Group to provide a local perspective. They receive regular updates from the programme and individual MNOs on implementation progress. My officials have facilitated closer working relationships with local stakeholders to aid delivery, through the SRN working group and wider barrier busting taskforce which will benefit the telecommunication industry beyond SRN as they continue to improve coverage here.  Progress against targets are measured by Ofcom. Mobile coverage obligations - Ofcom provides information about licence obligations and methodology and the Connected Nations Spring 2024: Interactive report - Ofcom provides latest coverage information and SRN progress. Connected Nations 2023 - Northern Ireland report - Ofcom provides specific local information. More information about the Shared Rural Network can be found at SRN.Org.UK.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on his Department’s use of road safety advertising.
answer_text: It has been well documented, that due to financial pressures my department was unable to allocate a budget for road safety advertising for financial years 2022/23 and 2023/24. My department’s 2024/25 budget is subject to an Equality Impact Assessment (EQIA) Consultation which launched on 11th June 2024. Responses received within the first four weeks of the EQIA consultation, with be used to inform final 2024-25 budget decisions.I have commissioned a Digital Road Safety Campaign to coincide with the launch of the new Northern Ireland Road Safety Strategy 2030. I hope to launch this campaign in the coming weeks. Until final decisions are made, officials have been using social media to promote the department’s road safety public information campaigns on www.sharetheroadtozero.com, and associated social media pages: @ShareTheRoadToZero on Facebook and @roadtozero for X. One of the best ways to ensure safety on our roads and fulfil our long-term goal of eliminating death and serious injury by 2050, is by changing our behaviour. As road users we all have a personal responsibility to behave in a way that keeps ourselves and others safe. The sad reality is that if we do not change our own attitudes when we are using the roads, our death toll will continue to increase. My department will continue to promote the road safety public information campaigns and other road safety advice through www.sharetheroadtozero.com. I would encourage everyone to sign up to the Share the Road to Zero community engagement program and take the Road Safety Pledge.

answer_text: Figures for the number of convictions at courts for offences related to drink or drug driving for the years 2019 – 2023 have been provided.
