question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the number of organisations in North Down to have received Rural Micro Capital Grant Scheme funding in each of the last five years.
answer_text: Eleven community and voluntary organisations in the North Down area have received Rural Micro Grants totalling £11,138.50 since 2019/2020 as outlined below.SchemeProjects Grant-aided£ total 2019/20202£2,121.552020/20212£2,674.102021/20223£1,762.122022/20234£4,580.73The Rural Micro Capital Grants Scheme 2024/2025 is currently open for applications. Grants of between £500 and £2,000 are available to rural community and voluntary organisations and social economy enterprises for projects tackling local issues of poverty and / or social isolation, provided evidence from their application, constitution and accounts proves they meet all the eligibility criteria. The scheme, administered by County Down Rural Community Network (CDRCN) in the North Down area, closes for applications at midday on 8 July 2024. Contact details for CDRCN are Mary McKeown, Telephone: 028 4461 2311 and e-mail: mary@countydownrcn.comI have also included below the link for those organisations considering applying for a rural micro capital grant. I look forward to hearing about those eligible organisations in North Down who apply. https://www.daera-ni.gov.uk/services/rural-micro-capital-grants-scheme-rmcgs
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the resurfacing of the city-bound carriageway of the Sydenham bypass.
answer_text: Having decided on indicative budget allocations (subject to a consultation on the equality impacts) my officials can now consider the detailed programmes of work across the Department’s areas of responsibility, including plans for infrastructure projects such as the Sydenham Bypass.The programme for 2024-25 will be developed and published once future budgets have been agreed. In the meantime, officials in my Department will continue to undertake regular inspections and repair defects in accordance with the Department’s policy.The city-bound carriageway of the Sydenham Bypass ranks highly on my Department’s Structural Maintenance Programme, and it is anticipated that resurfacing will be completed this financial year, subject to the availability of the necessary resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what engagement he has had with the Minister of Health in relation to improving post-traffic collision response.
answer_text: My Department’s response regarding any action required to re-open the carriageway is co-ordinated by the PSNI. Subsequent accident investigations are undertaken by the PSNI with my Department providing input as requested.My Department undertakes annual reviews of accident clusters which may identify potential accident remedial schemes; however these are engineering assessments and do not involve the Department of Health.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether he plans to increase the number of Higher Level Apprenticeships available in North Down.
answer_text: Question 9 - AQO 648/22-27Higher Level Apprenticeships are demand led, with employers creating opportunities in line with business needs. HLAs are a success story, growing significantly since they started in 2017, exceeding original forecasts.  Year on year we have seen expansion in the range of HLAs being offered across our economy. This growth enables more employers to use apprenticeships to attract new talent, as well as providing existing employees a route to gain new skills where they are moving to a new job role.  Most recent statistics show that 65 higher level apprentices had a home address in North Down.  HLAs are delivered by all six Further Education Colleges, CAFRE and the three local universities, offering access right across the North.

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission for an update on the refurbishment work to the Senate Chamber.
answer_text: On 28 June 2022 the Assembly Commission approved a business case for the refurbishment of audio and video systems in Assembly committee rooms, including the Senate Chamber.The primary aim in refurbishing the Senate Chamber is to provide a modern and resilient broadcast capable room for use by Assembly committees, visiting committees from other parliaments, and to provide a reserve capability for plenary sittings. There was also a need to expand the number of televised rooms to accommodate increasing committee workloads. New power points, cabling, microphones and cameras have been installed. The central table top has been replaced with a new one in wood and leather, in keeping with heritage guidance, whilst at the same time incorporating positioning for small screens, microphones and power points at each seating position. The base of the central table is now more akin to its original 1930s look and has been enhanced and matched by expert polishing.It is anticipated that all work will be completed in the second week of July with commissioning testing being undertaken in late July and the first half of August.The Senate Chamber will be available to Assembly committees immediately after summer recess.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the proposed Bangor to Newtownards greenway.
answer_text: This greenway project is led by the Ards and North Down Borough Council. A letter of offer was previously provided by the Department to the Council. Departmental officials have now received a full business case from the Council which is currently being reviewed and will be subject to the public expenditure process. Engagement is ongoing between the Department and the Council, including on funding and profiling. The Council has advised that they have received tender submissions from interested contractors and these are currently being considered.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 11271/22-27, for an update on the number of new specialist provision classes in mainstream schools established for September 2024 in North Down.
answer_text: As of 18 June 2024 (and pursuant to AQW 11271/22-27) there are seven new specialist provision classes confirmed for September 2024 in the Ards and North Down Council area.  Engagement between the Education Authority (EA) and schools is ongoing to secure the number of additional classes needed for September 2024.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the average waiting time for an MOT appointment since the introduction of Temporary Exemption Certificates, broken down by test centre.
answer_text: From 1 June 2024, private cars first registered between 1 June 2019 and 31 May 2020 or between 1 June 2017 and 31 May 2018, which have a valid MOT issued by the Driver and Vehicle Agency (DVA) will have a one-year Temporary Exemption Certificate (TEC) automatically applied from the date their current MOT certificate expires.It is too early to assess the impact the introduction of TECs is having on the average waiting time for MOT appointments.  However, I am confident TECs will provide relief for many customers and will gradually improve waiting times over the course of the implementation period, with DVA targeting an improvement to around six weeks.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he intends to review the 18.4 per cent claw back on generic drugs for community pharmacists.
To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the number of businesses that benefited from the Back in Business rate support scheme during 2022-23 in North Down.
answer_text: The Control of Major Accident Hazards (COMAH) Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2015 are enforced by a Competent Authority consisting of the Health and Safety Executive Northern Ireland (HSENI) and the Northern Ireland Environment Agency (NIEA) acting jointly. HSENI advises on risks to people and the NIEA leads on risks to the environment. A battery is considered an ‘article’ and not a dangerous substance. As batteries are not classified as dangerous substances under the Classification, Labelling and Packaging of Chemicals (Amendment) Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2015 (CLP), batteries would not of their own accord bring a BESS facility within the scope of COMAH.The definition (loss of control) is not intended to bring into scope premises which do not manufacture, use or store dangerous substances, solely because of dangerous substances being generated in an accident.Furthermore, the scope of COMAH is considered for BESS facilities due to dangerous substances generated through loss of control if other dangerous substances are already present. Therefore, each BESS facility needs to be considered individually to ensure appropriate requirements are applied.The above approach is consistent with other enforcement authorities in Britain and the South of Ireland.Operators of BESS facilities are required to comply with the Health and Safety at Work (NI) Order 1978, and other relevant statutory provisions. The operator has a duty to implement measures to control risks and mitigate consequences, so far as is reasonably practicable.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the current average waiting time from referral to completion of works for occupational therapy services in the South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust.
To ask the Minister of Finance to detail any engagement her Department has had with the UK government in relation to reducing VAT rates for hospitality businesses.
answer_text: Over recent years, my Department has repeatedly raised with the British Treasury, the negative impact the higher level of VAT on tourism & hospitality is having locally, compared to the lower rate that applies in the South.  My officials continue to highlight the adverse impacts of the taxation system in their regular meetings with Treasury counterparts.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on any actions he is taking to address the backlog within the Planning Appeals Commission.
answer_text: I recognise the resourcing challenges of the Planning Appeals Commission and it is my intention to discuss the appropriate resourcing of the Commission with the Minister of Justice.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for an update on any actions she is taking to address the backlog within the Planning Appeals Commission.
answer_text: As advised in AQW 11976/22-27, the Planning Appeals Commission (PAC) is an independent tribunal body. Its operation is the responsibility of the Chief Commissioner. It would not be appropriate for me as Minister of Justice to comment on matters that encroach on that independence.PAC’s performance is included in its Annual Review prepared by the Chief Commissioner. The 1 April 2022 – 31 March 2023 Annual Review acknowledges that the timeliness of decision-making in appeal work fell short and includes details of outstanding caseloads. It is available at: https://www.pacni.gov.uk/publications/202223-annual-report. I understand that the 2023/2024 Annual Review will be published in Autumn 2024.Northern Ireland Courts and Tribunals Service (NICTS) is responsible for providing resources and services to PAC and is accountable for the use of these resources and associated expenditure, whilst at the same time respecting the operational independence of PAC.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on an additional roll-out of the 20mph speed limit scheme outside schools in North Down.
answer_text: There have been two tranches of part time 20mph speed limits delivered, which introduced these measures at 215 schools, an investment of about £4m.  A review is underway to determine if a further tranche of the 20mph schools programme is justified. The findings of this review will be made public later this year.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether he will restore the pothole intervention level depth from 50mm to 20mm.
To ask the Minister of Health to outline any additional funding that will be provided to community pharmacies to assist them in meeting demand for their services.
answer_text: The opening position in 2023/24 was £128m. There was additional funding invested in 2023/24 linked with service pressures and additional services. Therefore, by year-end, over £140m was invested in community pharmacy services in 2023/24. The additional recurrent funding invested in 2023/24 has brought the recurrent funding to over £134m at the start of 2024/25. Prior to the allocation of a budget for the 2024/25 year, my Department committed to providing additional funding to this opening position in recognition of the service pressures that this sector is facing and within what is affordable. The opening annual budget for the community pharmacy sector is £147m, a rise of £19m in 2024 compared to April 2023.		This includes committing an additional £2m per quarter in 2024/25 in respect of ongoing service pressures. Whether this can be made recurrent will be dependent upon the delivery of efficiencies and the budget allocation.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what steps he is taking to address recruitment challenges in his Department.
answer_text: NICS-wide recruitment plans are managed and delivered by NICSHR Recruitment team. Over the past few years, the progression of departmental recruitment plans has been adversely impacted by a number of factors. This includes NICS restricted financial allocations hindering our ability to afford to fill vacancies at a time when private employers are able to offer higher salaries for securing expertise aligned with Professional and Technical grades.  While NICSHR currently have supply at some of general service grades, there is a significant gap in supply for NICS to avail of for many of the P&T Specialist grades.  My Department is working very closely with NICSHR and there are several competitions being taken forward by their Recruitment Team to generate supply to meet our Departmental requirements. These competitions are at various stages of development and progression and include recruiting a range of grades for General Service, P&T and Apprenticeship Programmes.  DfI are currently working on developing a 2024/2025 Workforce plan which will align with budget allocations.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance for her assessment of the impact that the Back in Business rate support scheme will have on empty retail units.

To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the number of block de-registrations carried out by NHS dental practices in North Down in each of the last three years, and (ii) the reasons for these block de-registrations.
answer_text: The Head of Dental Services within the Strategic Planning and Performance Group of the Department of Health wrote to general dental practitioners on 9 May 2024 outlining a revised process to facilitate dental practitioners de-registering a large number of their health service patients i.e. a “block de-registration.” Prior to this date dental practices could not block de-register their patients as patients could only be de-registered on an individual basis. As such, there have not been any block de-registrations in the last three years.  Dental practitioners must give patients three months notice in writing when de-registering them.  They are not required to provide a reason to the SPPG or the BSO when de-registering a patient except in circumstances where they are requesting that the patient is de-registered with immediate effect.  SPPG is aware that approximately 2200 de-registrations were processed in 2022 in your constituency due to a practice in Donaghadee being unable to replace a practitioner who left the practice.Table 1 below outlines the number of patients de-registered from active health service dental practices in the North Down Assembly area in each of the past five years:Table 1: Number of Patient De-registrations from an active health service dentist in the North Down Assembly Area by Year1,2,3,4Year4Number of Patient De-registrations12019620208202180620222,3762023548Source: Family Practitioner Services Dental Payment System, Family Practitioner Services, HSC Business Services Organisation.  1 To de-register a patient, a dentist must submit a WCA 966 form to the BSO and give the patient 3 months’ notice. The count of de-registrations is based on the number of WCA 966 forms submitted and recorded on the FPS Dental Payment System. The variable used to signify the submissions of a WCA 966 form may also be used in other scenarios e.g. removing a registration from a dentist that should have been seen on referral rather than an initial or re-registration. It is not possible to specifically extract WCA 966 forms submitted, thus resulting in a possible overcount of true de-registrations.2 An active dentist has made an arrangement with the SPPG (previously the Health and Social Care Board) to provide health service dentistry.3 Private patients who have been de-registered are not included in this data. 4 All count figures represent de-registrations across each full calendar year (1st January to 31st December). The data presented is as of 24th April 2024.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any engagement his Department has had with Artemis Technologies in relation to (i) promotion of the proposed Belfast to Bangor ferry route; and (ii) a timescale for its introduction.
answer_text: My Department have met Artemis Technologies on its proposed Belfast to Bangor ferry route on a number of occasions. I have asked officials to continue to work with Artemis Technologies on procedural arrangements for the introduction of this innovative service and I have articulated my support for the project. These discussions are ongoing, and my officials met with Artemis Technologies on 16 May 2024 where they were provided with an update on progress. At this stage of development, the Department has no plans to promote the service and Artemis Technologies would be best placed to provide you with an update on timescales for the introduction of the proposed service.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any engagement her Department has had with Artemis Technologies in relation to (i) promotion of the proposed Belfast to Bangor ferry route; and (ii) a timescale for its introduction.
answer_text: The Belfast to Bangor 100% electric foiling passenger ferry is being developed and trialled by the Belfast Maritime Consortium, with £33m of UKRI Strength in Places funding secured in 2020.  The Belfast Maritime Consortium is led by Artemis Technologies Ltd and brings together a range of partners from across industry, academia and local government to collaborate across a number of innovation areas in the project.   Following an extensive R&D programme involving the consortium partners, I am pleased that Artemis has chosen to pilot the Ferry in the North of Ireland, providing an alternative transport option between Belfast and Bangor.  It is my understanding that the Ferry will be launched and undertaking sea trials over the summer period, with the pilot Ferry service to open the public over the autumn period.Invest NI is a Consortium member, acting mainly in an observing/supporting role as required and attends both formal quarterly project meetings and assists with PR, project development as appropriate.  Invest NI has an active engagement with Artemis and is working with the company on increasing the company’s exposure internationally, including the Ferry pilot, through utilising its presence in overseas markets, as well as discussing its commercialisation and scale up and support available.With regards to promotion of the Ferry, Invest NI, where appropriate, has promoted this significant investment project both through sector presentations to both local and international audiences, attendance at Trade Events and through social media posts as required.  With the Northern Ireland Coastal Powerhouse Summit, alongside the Maritime UK Awards coming to Belfast in early June, this is an excellent opportunity for the Belfast to Bangor pilot to be showcased.  Invest NI is sponsoring activity during this week and it is understood that Artemis is hosting promotion activity at its Belfast manufacturing facility.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) the number of electric vehicle charge points in North Down; and (ii) how many of these are currently out of order.
answer_text: The provision and operation of publicly accessible electric vehicle charge points is a commercially led operation. Responsibility for the maintenance and installation of those charge points rests solely with the charge point operators (CPOs). In this commercially led market, my officials continue to engage with EV owners and provide advice to CPOs on operational matters. My Department does not hold records of the number of publicly available EV charge points here. The British Government publishes periodic updates of the number of publicly available EV charge points by both Local Council area, and Parliamentary constituency which you may find helpful. These can be found in the link below.Zero emission and electric vehicles - GOV.UK (www.gov.uk)Latest statistics show that there are 577 publicly available EV charge points in the North. This represents a growth of 48% in the total number of available EV charge points here since April 2023. Relevant charge point operators are best placed to advise you of their plans for the installation of further charge points within your constituency and the position of their current installations.Local Councils have been able to avail of funding for the installation of charge points under the Office for Zero Emissions Vehicles (OZEV)  ‘On Street Residential Charge Point Scheme’, which my Department has match funded. Derry City and Strabane District Council made an application under this scheme in 2021-22, on behalf of nine Local Councils including Ards & North Down Borough Council, and are currently preparing a tender for charge point operators to provide 127 charge points across those Council areas. Ards & North Down Borough Council are best placed to advise on the number of additional charge points this will provide in the area.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for an update on the introduction of Charlotte’s Law.
answer_text: It remains my intention to include legislative provision for ‘Charlotte’s Law’ as part of a Sentencing Bill, which I plan to introduce next year.A key element of Charlotte’s Law will be a significant increase in the murderer’s life sentence tariff to reflect their ongoing failure to disclose critical information about the location of the victim’s remains.  This will be coupled with the opportunity for a proportionate reduction in their tariff should a post sentence disclosure be made by the prisoner.   Similar to ‘Helen’s Law’ in England and Wales, it will also require the Parole Commissioners to take the failure to disclose into account when considering the prisoner’s suitability for release on licence at the parole stage.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail which MOT centres were operating at full capacity in April 2024.
answer_text: The Driver & Vehicle Agency (DVA) currently has 15 operational vehicle test centres geographically spread across the North.  In April 2024, the DVA conducted 92,314 vehicle tests, which is the third highest number ever recorded in April.Taking account annual leave, bank holidays, special leave, sickness absence, training, reasonable adjustments, phased return, personalised hours, team briefings, maintenance and calibration of equipment etc; each test centre operated at full capacity, with all available resources being fully utilised.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for his assessment of the level of funding required to enable CAFRE to carry out the roles outlined in his Ministerial Statement on 14 May 2024.
answer_text: In my statement of 14 May I set out my priorities to enable the development of an economically and environmentally sustainable agri-food sector in Northern Ireland. The College of Agriculture Food and Rural Enterprise (CAFRE) will play a key role in educating those entering and those currently working in the agri-food sector, to implement the policies and strategies that benefit our climate and environment, while supporting it economically and socially. In a difficult financial position CAFRE will operate within budget and in line with these priorities to ensure that it contributes to my vision.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether he intends to implement the recommendations of the Independent Resilience Review of Agricultural Shows in Northern Ireland (August 2022).
answer_text: The Independent Resilience Review of Agricultural Shows (IRRAS) in Northern Ireland, published in June 2023, brought forward recommendations for both industry and government aimed at maximising the sustainability of agricultural shows in the years ahead including future funding options.Progress in evaluating the options proffered by the IRRAS will involve completion of a business case and Economic Impact Assessment which can demonstrate feasibility, regularity, propriety, and value for money.	Following the agreement of the 2024/25 budget by the Executive my Finance Division are currently preparing proposals in respect of that budget for my consideration. Any proposals will take account of DAERA’s statutory obligations and contractual commitments prior to consideration of discretionary expenditure such as support for agricultural shows.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for a breakdown of investment by Invest NI in North Down in the 2023-24 financial year.
answer_text: Invest NI reports its performance on a financial year basis with 2022-23, ending 31st March 2023, being the most recent year available.  Information relating to performance on a Parliamentary Constituency basis in 2023-24 is provisionally planned for release at the end of summer 2024.  The table below details the amount of assistance that Invest NI offered towards projects in the North Down Parliamentary Constituency in 2022-23, and the amount of total planned investment that this support has secured for the area.  Total planned investment is the combination of business investment and assistance offered by Invest NI in support of a businesses’ project.In addition to financial assistance, Invest NI also provides wider support to all businesses through advice, assistance or guidance from our staff and through nibusinessinfo.co.uk, our online business advice portal.Invest NI assistance offered and investment secured in North Down Parliamentary Constituency (2022-23)YearAssistance Offered £mTotal Planned Investment £m2022-230.532.57

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what action his Department is taking to improve road safety on the A2 Bangor to Belfast dual carriageway.
answer_text: Road safety is an extremely high priority for my Department and it is committed to working proactively to make our roads safer and to address the needs of all road users.Officials will review the sites where there are clusters of collisions on the A2 to identify if there are any common causation factors for these collisions which would allow an effective road safety measure to be introduced.Concerns regarding driver behaviour such as speeding should be directed to the PSNI using the non-emergency 101.One of the best ways to ensure the safety of our roads and fulfil our long-term goal of eliminating death and serious injury on roads by 2050 is by changing driver behaviour.  If driver attitudes towards speeding and careless driving do not change, our death toll, which has increased in the past year, will continue to rise

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any engagement he has had with the Minister for Infrastructure in relation to improving public transport links to the Ulster Hospital.
answer_text: The Minister for Health has had no recent engagement with the Minister for Infrastructure in relation to improving transport to the Ulster Hospital.  The South Eastern Trust has, however, met Department for Infrastructure (DfI) representatives several times over the last number of years to discuss public transport connectivity.On 19th April 2024 South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust (SEHSCT) met DfI representatives seeking detailed information for the DfI Eastern Transport Plan (ETP). This framework is being devised to support transport policy and investment decisions up until 2035 and is part of an overarching regional review of transport strategy. The Trust considered this meeting as very positive and it was clear that the aims and actions of both Departments are in very close alignment to improve health inequalities and promote healthier and more active lifestyles. SEHSCT used the opportunity to raise the idea of concessionary fares for all HSC staff and the ripple effect of this on better health outcomes for the whole population; i.e. in terms of reducing single occupancy vehicle use, encouraging more sustainable and active transport and reducing CO2 and particulate emissions.  As in the past, DfI officers agreed the concept had potential and saw the advantages of engaging with the 72,500 HSC employees to create meaningful modal shift recognising a common and desirable outcome for both Departments.SEHSCT has also convened a multi-agency parking and travel planning meeting, chaired by an elected representative with MLAs, councillors, PSNI, DfI and Translink.  This group seeks to foster joined up thinking on improving public transport connectivity and reducing private transport where possible.Minister Swann said to the Assembly in 2020 on the matter, “It is important that, …. we have seen good working between our Department and the Department for Infrastructure. It is also important that we see that work continue and develop in the public transport system to support our health and care workers and our health and care facilities. It is also important that the Executive give my colleague in Infrastructure the support that she needs with rural transport.” (Assembly, 2020)Furthermore, through the Trust’s multi-agency forum, Translink has been monitoring passenger usage and has not noticed any increase in demand for a bus service.  Translink is also not aware of any specific complaints made regarding the frequency of its services to the Ulster Hospital.  Translink continues to forward bus usage data to the Trust on a regular basis to monitor the uptake of the bus service and it has agreed to meet the Trust if a noticeable increase in demand occurs.For staff and patients who do avail of public transport to the Ulster Hospital, travel information is available on the publicly accessible Trust website which includes links to the Translink Travel Planner. There are also public display timetables at the main entrances of the Trust’s larger acute site

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail (i) what plans she has to improve the operation of the Planning Appeals Commission; and (ii) how she will help address the backlog faced by the Commission.
answer_text: The Planning Appeals Commission (PAC) is an independent tribunal body. Its operation is the responsibility of the Chief Commissioner. It would not be appropriate for me as Minister of Justice to comment on matters that encroach on that independence.PAC’s performance is included in its Annual Review prepared by the Chief Commissioner. The 1 April 2022 – 31 March 2023 Annual Review acknowledges that the timeliness of decision-making in appeal work fell short and includes details of outstanding caseloads. It is available at: https://www.pacni.gov.uk/publications/202223-annual-report. I understand that the 2023/2024 Annual Review will be published in Autumn 2024. The management of caseloads is the responsibility of the Chief Commissioner.Northern Ireland Courts and Tribunals Service (NICTS) is responsible for providing resources and services to PAC and is accountable for the use of these resources and associated expenditure, whilst at the same time respecting the operational independence of PAC.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance for an update on the sale of the Northern Ireland Civil Service buildings at (i) Netherleigh House; (ii) Clarence Court; and (iii) Victoria Hall.
answer_text: My department is progressing an ambitious review of its Office Estate with the aim of reducing the in-scope footprint by 40% by March 2028.With regard to (i) Netherleigh House and (ii) Clarence Court, initial bids have now been received for both buildings and officials are confident that these sites can be sold in the coming months. With regard to (iii) Victoria Hall, the building is on the market with a closing date for offers of 30 May 2024.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the number of MOT bookings refunded due to the introduction of Temporary Exemption Certificates.
answer_text: From 1 June 2024, private cars first registered between 1 June 2019 and 31 May 2020 or between 1 June 2017 and 31 May 2018, which have a valid MOT issued by the Driver and Vehicle Agency (DVA) will have a one-year Temporary Exemption Certificate (TEC) automatically applied.Vehicle licensing information shows that around 115,000 cars may be eligible for a TEC.There are just over 6,600 cars eligible for a TEC that already have a booked MOT appointment, which will be automatically cancelled and refunded.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education what action his Department is taking to make school uniforms more affordable.
answer_text: I intend to bring forward legislation to the Assembly to make the guidance to schools about their uniform policy statutory.  The key driver underpinning this action is to ensure that school uniforms are more affordable for parents.  In advance of this, my Department will be issuing a consultation on the areas to be covered in such statutory guidance.  The outcomes of this will enable the legislation and the associated guidance to both respect the rights of schools to ensure uniform requirements adequately reflect the ethos of their school, and also take sufficient account of the issue of affordability.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the proposed new school building project at Crawfordsburn Primary School.
answer_text: The new build school project is fully designed, planning approval has been granted, the additional land required to deliver the project has been acquired and Business Case approval is in place. The project will progress to building contractor procurement when funding is available.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy how her Department is raising awareness with local businesses of the availability and eligibility criteria of the Energy Efficiency Capital Grant.
answer_text: Invest NI has been working in partnership with my department to deliver the Energy Efficiency Capital Grant (EECG), which opened for applications on 29 April 2024. A wide range of delegates were invited to attend the EECG press launch at the Seagoe Hotel, Portadown on 8 May 2024 which helped raise awareness of the grant.  Delegates included local Councils, Chambers of Commerce, trade representative bodies, SMEs and government bodies such as Tourism NI. 	Invest NI has a dedicated marketing plan for the EECG that will be tailored to meet local business needs, with a built-in flexible approach that will address any regional imbalances. A range of social media activities alongside continued engagement with key stakeholders are scheduled over the coming months. Activities will be monitored, and the marketing approach will be adapted to ensure all business groups are informed of the opportunities the grant presents. 		To support businesses at the initial eligibility stage, businesses can access a comprehensive set of guidance notes available from the Invest NI website Energy Efficiency Capital Grant | Invest Northern Ireland (investni.com). Invest NI Technical Advisors are then available to provide advice and guidance to businesses on suitability of projects aligned with eligibility guidelines.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the actions his Department is taking to address the rise in whooping cough cases.
answer_text: In response to the rise in pertussis cases in Northern Ireland, the Public Health Agency have enhanced out-of-hours staffing to manage the increased notification of cases; provided resilience staff to support advice and actions for high risk close contacts of cases; developed training, guidance and Standard Operating Procedures for staff responding to pertussis cases; and provided support for schools experiencing high levels of cases. Regarding vaccination uptake the Public Health Agency have established a low uptake working group to take forward actions in relation to the falling uptake of childhood vaccinations. They have also engaged with maternity colleagues to review the current delivery of maternal pertussis vaccination and explore options to address falling uptake. The Public Health Agency has issued communications to raise awareness via letters to primary and secondary care, factsheets for midwives and refreshed public information leaflets for pregnancy along with press releases and continue to provide situational updates to the Department.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any engagement he has had with the Minister for Infrastructure in relation to addressing coastal erosion.
answer_text: I wrote to the Minister for Infrastructure on 22 April 2024 requesting that we meet to discuss a range of coastal management issues, including the next steps for the Coastal Forum. Minister John O’Dowd MLA has agreed to meet, and a suitable date is being arranged.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether he intends to extend his Department’s funding for the Northern Ireland Rural Development Programme.
answer_text: I do not intend to extend the NI Rural Development Programme 2014-2020. This programme was funded in part by EU monies which were available up until 31 December 2023. New policies and schemes tailored to the needs of our stakeholders are being developed and implemented to replace the old programme.	In addition, I am building a detailed case for future earmarked funding for agriculture, environment, rural and fisheries in conjunction with the Finance Minister recognising the importance of ensuring that the next funding package meets the needs of our environment, rural communities, farming and fishing businesses, along with needs in relation to our climate and ensuring a genuinely just transition.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail any discussions she has had with banks in relation to the rollout of a banking hub in North Down.
answer_text: I held a meeting on 1 May 2024 with senior representatives of the local banks and building societies, LINK and Cash Access UK, where I urged them to maintain access to vital banking services including action to expediate further roll out of banking hubs.It is disappointing the North currently has only one banking hub in Kilkeel, but I understand from that meeting there are plans for four new banking hubs to open here. This includes a hub in Comber, in the Ards and North Down Borough Council Area, Cash Access UK indicated it is due to open in early 2025. I will continue to press the sector to roll out banking hubs at pace. My officials are also engaging with the Consumer Council who collaborate with LINK in identifying further areas that should be considered for a banking hub here.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether additional bus connections will be scheduled for those travelling between Belfast train stations as a result of the closure of Great Victoria Street Train Station.
answer_text: As I outlined in my statement to the Assembly on 16th April 2024, my Department and Translink have been working closely to seek to minimise the impact of the opening of Belfast Grand Central Station. The disruptions to the wider public, business community and surrounding communities, have taken account of all public transport users, including allowing passengers with valid rail tickets to use, at no extra charge, the local Metro and Ulsterbus bus services from the City Hospital, Botanic, Lanyon Place and York Street Stations. Whilst the development of these mitigation measures has been informed by an extensive and detailed assessment using the best available models and data, the overall effectiveness of the measures will depend on the public changing their current travel patterns by choosing alternative modes and times for their journey or by taking a different route to avoid congested areas in the vicinity of the construction works.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail his Department’s spend on coastal erosion defences in each of the last five years.
