The initial phase of the marketing campaign will be to highlight the accessibility of the two routes on one visit for international holidaymakers, building on the appeal of these two iconic tourism products.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail his Department’s plans to review support available for those undertaking Postgraduate Certificate in Education courses.
answer_text: I have asked my officials to update me on work to date on a potential review of Higher Education Funding, as well as proposals to take it forward. Any review would be likely to consider support available to students. Any changes to support for those undertaking Postgraduate Certificates in Education would be made in consultation with the Department of Education.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure how much of the additional £8.1 million allocated for road maintenance will be allocated to North Down.
answer_text: The additional £8.1m funding for my Department’s Structural Maintenance Programme is to deliver additional resurfacing schemes and continue to address the poor condition of our road network. This funding has now been allocated across all four of my Department’s road divisions.Work has started on the planning and delivery of new schemes for delivery this financial year. However, the development of the programmes is on-going and delivery will be phased in the coming weeks. As such, a full breakdown is not yet available.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) how much of the £8.1 million allocated to road maintenance has been reallocated from other areas of his Department’s budget; and (ii) from what areas of the budget it has been reallocated from.
answer_text: Recognising the need for vital investment in road maintenance, I allocated an additional £8.1m to the Department’s structural maintenance programme which included £3.1m from internal re-allocations from various business areas across the Department including Active Travel grants where take up was less than anticipated.  The re-allocation will ensure effective and efficient management of the overall departmental capital budget and delivery of much needed resurfacing programmes.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the Independent Review of Special Educational Needs.
answer_text: IPSOS published their report on the Independent Review of Special Educational Needs (SEN) in May 2023.  The recommendations contained within the report are being addressed through the End to End (E2E) Review of SEN which the Department of Education (DE) and the Education Authority (EA) embarked on in early 2023.  The review examines the entire SEN system from a policy perspective to ensure delivery and outcomes are in line with objectives and policy intent. Phase one of the review is complete which involved scoping the review and establishing appropriate governance structures.  Phase two has commenced and primarily focuses on stakeholder engagement to ensure that all decisions and solutions are underpinned by meaningful engagement.  Both phases will be followed by the development of options (phase three) and the way forward which includes an implementation plan (phase four).

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for an update on the implementation of Charlotte’s Law.
answer_text: Officials in my Department have continued to develop the detail of the proposal working closely with the affected families and other stakeholders during the absence of the Assembly.  It remains my intention to bring ‘Charlotte’s Law’ forward during the current mandate as part of a Sentencing Bill.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail his Department's (i) local economic target for North Down; (ii) local economic strategy for North Down.
answer_text: Promoting regional economic balance is one of my four key objectives and I will bring forward a Sub-Regional Economic Action Plan this autumn. Priorities will be identified by local councils and targets will be co-designed at a sub-regional level. This work is already underway with Ards and North Down Borough Council.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what plans his Department has to revert the minimum intervention level for potholes from 50 millimetres back to 20 millimetres.
answer_text: My Department’s resource budget, which is used to deliver day-to-day maintenance activities such as patching and grass cutting, has been under pressure for a number of years as a result of Tory cuts and austerity. In response, my Department has had to reduce its routine road maintenance activities to a limited service, which prioritises delivery of essential services for maintaining public safety and protecting the transport network. Consequently, only the highest priority defects across the network are currently being repaired.The level of repairs that can be carried out in future financial years will depend on the 2024-25 final budget determination for my Department. Until such times as sufficient funding is made available, it will not be possible to change the current level of service. The importance of a well maintained road network cannot be understated but this requires investment. I will continue to seek the support of Executive colleagues to enable this to happen.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for an update on the progress of processing applications to the Troubles Permanent Disablement Payment Scheme.
To ask the Minister of Justice to detail her Department’s plans to increase diversity within the legal profession.
answer_text: While I am supportive of increasing diversity within the legal profession, that does not fall within my Department’s responsibilities.  The Law Society of Northern Ireland and The Bar of Northern Ireland are independent of the Department of Justice and are responsible for the regulation of solicitors and barristers, respectively.  The Northern Ireland Judicial Appointments Commission (NIJAC), which is responsible for appointment of members of the Judiciary in Northern Ireland, is an arm's length body of The Executive Office.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail his Department's plans to ensure that higher education courses provide value for money.
answer_text: My Department assesses value for money for both students and the Department itself by looking at a range of measures including relative cost, student experience and student outcomes.  To underpin this my Department works with the Quality Assurance Agency, an independent expert body, to monitor the quality of provision in the sector.  This approach is currently being reviewed with a view to  strengthening confidence among students, staff and the public that the service provision delivered has real value.Our annual participation in the National Student Survey is another gauge of the value proposition. The Survey, which is designed to elicit feedback on the student experience, showed that some four in five of final year undergraduates at our institutions were satisfied with the quality of their course.My Department continues to benchmark sectoral funding against comparators and, while we undoubtedly face budgetary challenges, I am pleased to note that the ‘funding gap’ between our own sector and that in England has been narrowing in recent years.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs what plans his Department has to address coastal erosion in North Down.
answer_text: Coastal Change Risk Management is not the responsibility of any department in Northern Ireland.  This legislative gap was recognised by previous Ministers, and I will be discussing this issue with the Minister for Infrastructure and Executive colleagues. Under the existing policy and legislative framework, my Department has no role in addressing coastal erosion.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the average time it takes the Department to review and repair a pothole after it has been reported on the online system, in each of the last three years.
answer_text: Following reports from the public of potholes it may be necessary for my officials to carry out a site investigation to enable a decision on whether the pothole meets our intervention criteria and the creation of an instructed surface defect record.  My Department receives a high volume of surface defect related enquiries, and regrettably it is not possible to investigate all enquiries immediately. It is likely that some reports will not be investigated until the next routine inspection of the area.If, after investigation, it is found that the reported pothole requires action, our current standards specify a range of response times for the repair of defects, depending on factors such as their severity and the volume of traffic on the road. They range from one calendar day for the most serious defects, to periods of five working days and four weeks for less serious defects. The least serious defects have to be repaired in the next work programme for that route. As such, the Department does not measure the time taken from a defect being reported on the online system to a repair as not all such reports require action.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail his Department’s capital funding allocation to (i) controlled sector schools; and (ii) integrated schools, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: The following table provides details of capital funding provided by my Department to (i) controlled sector schools: and (ii) integrated schools, in each of the last five financial years.School Types2018/19 Final Outturn Expenditure2019/20Final Outturn Expenditure2020/21Final Outturn Expenditure2021/22Final Outturn Expenditure2022/23Final Outturn Expenditure£m£m£m£m£mControlled Schools45.57553.10545.41262.36364.814Controlled Irish Medium (IM)0.7970000.008Controlled Integrated Schools3.0301.7741.9552.0512.802Controlled Sector Total49.40254.87947.36764.41467.624Grant Maintained Integrated Schools13.12312.0396.4836.12920.981Funding for schools in the controlled sector has been provided by the Education Authority.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the number of new grit boxes installed by his Department, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: My Department has provided new grit boxes as set out in the table below:YearNumber of salt boxes20194862020107202132202267202316

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether his Department intends to review the support available for teachers with students who have English as an additional language.
answer_text: Students who have English as an Additional Language (EAL) are supported via the Department’s Supporting Newcomer Pupils policy. 		The Department commenced a review of its Supporting Newcomer Pupils policy in October 2017 in response to the growing number and increasing diversity of newcomer pupils arriving in Northern Ireland.  In 2019, the Department held a public consultation on its review of the policy however, work was temporarily suspended in March 2020 due to the outbreak of the COVID-19 pandemic.  Work recommenced in 2022 and policy proposals were prepared for the consideration of an incoming Minister.		Officials will now build on the previous consultation and revisit policy proposals to take account of the current context, with a view to presenting proposals for my consideration.In support of school staff working with newcomer children with EAL the Education Authority’s Intercultural Education Service (IES) co-delivers an intercultural education course in partnership with Queens University Belfast (QUB) for both teachers and teaching assistants, accredited at OCN Level 1.  Over 350 school-based staff have completed this course since 2017 and the course will run again in September 2024.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department intends to match the Homecare Association’s Minimum Price for Homecare of £29.37.
answer_text: I recognise that increases to the national living wage, inflation and other increasing costs are having a significant impact on organisations in terms of the day to day operation of their business. My Department is engaging across the sector to understand the impact these costs have on the delivery of those services and work is ongoing to consider the regional rate that will be paid for the provision of domiciliary care by independent sector providers for 2024/25.We are engaging employers, unions and other sectoral partners through the Social Care Collaborative Forum and Social Care Fair Work Forum to address a wide range of issues relating to pay, terms and conditions and career progression.  The Department’s Fair Work Forum recommenced work in December and is due to meet again at the end of February.  The primary purpose of the Fair Work Forum is to build a robust and powerful case for funding and supporting Fair Work.  There is also an acknowledgement within the Forum of the need to raise the profile of Social Care in society, to give it the status and profile it needs to be properly acknowledged and supported.  The founding principle for the Fair Work Forum is the recognition that delivering better Fair Work outcomes will improve the lives of the entire social care workforce.In regard to matching the Homecare Association’s minimum price for homecare, I understand that in December 2023 the Homecare Association suggested a regional rate for homecare in Northern Ireland of £29.37.	However, even with any additional funding allocated to the NI Executive following the restoration of the political institutions, I expect the financial position will remain very challenging and difficult choices will need to be made on what can be delivered within the funding available.My Department will continue to work with independent sector providers, to try to ensure the sustainability of the organisations, and the sustainability of care to those who need it, when they need it.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education what action his Department is taking to encourage community use of school sporting facilities outside of school hours.
answer_text: Operational guidance for Community Use of Schools is set out in the Community Use of School Premises: A Guidance Toolkit for Schools document, published on the DE website. The toolkit contains a number of key principles and requirements regarding community use of schools. Departmental and Education Authority officials encourage and facilitate community use of schools within current policy and operational guidelines.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the review of the current home to school transport policy.
answer_text: A review of the Home to School Transport Policy commenced in 2018, however this was paused by the then Minister due to the pandemic and then suspended pending the outcome of The Independent Review of Education. My Department is currently considering the report published by the Independent Review of Education and there are no plans at present to initiate a new separate review of the policy.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether his Department plans to review the criteria for providing a mobile classroom to a school.
answer_text: Should an accommodation deficit be identified, or condition reports show that replacement of existing mobile accommodation is required, all options are considered within the associated Business Case.  This includes options for both traditional build and modular accommodation.  Only once the outcome of the Business Case is known will this identify if a modular build is the most effective and efficient option.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs what action his Department is taking to protect Northern Ireland’s seabird population.
answer_text: The Seabird Conservation Strategy is one of several high-profile marine conservation strategies and action plans that I will be considering over the coming months. I intend to consult on a draft Seabird Conservation Strategy for Northern Ireland during the second half of 2024.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy what action his Department is taking to improve superfast broadband access to areas not already covered by Project Stratum.
answer_text: Premises that are unable to access a superfast broadband service following Project Stratum will be considered for inclusion in Project Gigabit.  Project Gigabit is a £5 billion British Government programme that is expected to benefit premises that are unable to access gigabit-capable broadband services and are not part of suppliers’ commercial deployment plans. My Department has carried out an Open Market Review and a Public Review for Project Gigabit and at present 59,000 premises have been categorised as eligible for intervention under the project. A further 60,000 premises are categorised as Under Review, where reported commercial coverage claims have not been verified, or there are risks to delivery plans; these premises could be brought into the scope of Project Gigabit at a later date.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what action his Department is taking to improve access to GP appointments in North Down.
answer_text: As independent contractors, GPs manage the day to day running of their practices, including the use of technology, telephony and management of their appointment system within the constraints of their available workforce. Practices have embraced new ways of working, incorporating telephone triage and video consultations to compliment face to face consultations with increased remote working to ensure patients continue to access primary care services.GPs want anyone who has a health concern to feel reassured that they will be able to get an appointment and see a GP if necessary. If people have symptoms or an unexplained illness, or have any reason to be concerned, they should in the first instance contact their GP who will be able to provide advice.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what action his Department is taking to ensure Alzheimer’s patients will have access to lecanemab and donanemab in the future.
answer_text: At present, there are no UK marketing authorisations (licenses) in place for either lecanemab or donanemab. Granting a marketing authorisation is only the first stage in the medicines access pathway; following licensing, these treatments will be assessed for clinical and cost effectiveness by the National Institute for Health and Care Excellence (NICE). NICE is currently preparing to assess lecanemab and donanemab when marketing authorisations are granted, and once the NICE assessment has been made to recommend or not recommend the treatment my Department will carry out the endorsement process as set out in circular HSC (SQSD) 12/1113 available at: https://www.health-ni.gov.uk/sites/default/files/publications/health/doh-hsc-sqsd-12-22.pdf

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what action his Department is taking to address the problem of excess vaping, particularly among young people.
answer_text: I share the growing concerns in relation to increased recreational vaping amongst young people. Seeking to address such concerns, in 2022 my Department introduced regulations to prohibit the sale of nicotine inhaling products to under 18s (and proxy purchasing on behalf of someone under 18). However, it is clear that more action is needed.The Public Health Agency has recently taken forward research in schools to better understand the issues and has been working in partnership with community and voluntary sector partners to develop resources to support young people, parents, teachers and community leaders, and has commissioned vape training for professionals in healthcare, social care, education, youth work and others. These resources will be available in March. Departmental officials have been working closely with colleagues in GB to ensure NI inclusion in the recent consultation on smoking and vaping.  The consultation showed overwhelming support from NI for measures such as restrictions on retail displays and restrictions on flavours and packaging. As a result, I have expressed my support for NI inclusion in the planned UK Government Bill which aims to reduce the appeal of these devices to young people.Furthermore, a new Tobacco Control Strategy for Northern Ireland is in the early stages of development and this will also set strategic direction in relation to e-cigarettes and other emerging nicotine products.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what action his Department is taking to ensure people have access to a timely diagnosis for dementia.
answer_text: The Department acknowledges that dementia is one of the biggest challenges to our health and social care system. Northern Ireland is projected to have the largest increase in the number of people living with dementia across the UK by 2040, with the current figures of 22,700 set to rise to 43,000. This is due to the fact that the size of the older population in Northern Ireland is expected to increase much faster than the rest of the UK. The Department recognises the urgency with which individuals awaiting assessment will wish to be seen in order to understand their condition and to commence treatment.  The Department has issued a 9 week- target commissioning plan directive which sets out that people should have a first assessment following referral into Health and Social Care Trust memory services within that timescale.  At the end of January 2024 there were 2,299 people awaiting a first assessment with 1,421 of these waiting greater than 9 weeks.   Unfortunately, significant workforce pressures within many of the professional groups most crucial to delivering diagnosis have led to delays in assessments and diagnosis. These pressures are most severe within psychiatry of old age and learning disability, as well within as social work, nursing, psychology and other allied health professionals.  My Officials continue to engage with Trusts Dementia Leads and have drawn up a proposal to take forward a Regional Dementia Project Board. It is proposed that the project board will support the delivery of existing dementia services, as well as consider future service delivery models associated with new disease modifying therapies such as Lecanemab and Donanemab.The Regional Dementia Care Pathway published in 2018 offers a transformational service delivery model incorporating a primary care led approach working closely with secondary care dementia services to deliver assessment, diagnosis, treatment and management of dementia. It is acknowledged that implementation of the pathway will require substantial financial investment in the region of millions of pounds. At the current time there is no identified source of funding to implement the regional dementia care pathway.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what measures his Department is taking to ensure the long term stability of the Promote Day Opportunities Services in Bangor.
answer_text: I am aware that Action Mental Health (AMH) Promote provides a valuable service to enable people with learning disabilities to access diverse day activities and develop greater levels of independence. AMH informed SEHSCT in November 2023 that it would not be renewing its contract to deliver the Promote Day Opportunity Service in Bangor after 31 March 2024, due to wider financial pressures.SEHSCT have worked closely with the provider since June 2023 to better understand the challenges faced and to identify options that would enable continued delivery of services. Unfortunately, it is not feasible to continue the service with the current operating model. SEHSCT have identified an alternative provider to continue delivery of the service at the current site in Bangor. Throughout this process there has been and will continue to be engagement with service users, families and the providers.  The Department remains committed to the expansion of day opportunities for people with learning disabilities. Throughout 2023, the Department has led on an exercise to finalise a draft service model for learning disability, which includes several outcomes and actions to expand day services and short breaks in Northern Ireland. The current draft has been shared with key stakeholders to inform the next phase of this work and wider consultation.  In parallel to this work, the Department’s Strategic Planning and Performance Group are working closely with Trusts to undertake a financial review of adult learning disability services.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice how many convictions for the possession of cannabis were made in Northern Ireland, in each of the last 5 years.
answer_text: There is no specific offence for possession of cannabis.  Possession of cannabis is prosecuted as ‘possession of a class B drug’; however, convictions datasets do not hold information in relation to the specific drug involved, where convictions have been made for this offence.Information has, therefore, been provided for all cases involving ‘possession of a class B drug’ offence that were dealt with in the period 2018 to 2022, the most recent five years for which information is available.  In addition to convictions at courts, these cases are often dealt with by way of out of court diversionary disposal, so those figures have also been provided.  Convictions at courts and out-of-court diversionary disposals for ‘possession of a Class B drug’ offences, 2018 – 2022YearConvictionsDiversions20182,00683720192,04983020201,50970720212,03776420221,910740Note:1. Figures relate to cases prosecuted by PPS on behalf of PSNI or other bodies. 2. Figures for convictions relate to initial disposal at court only.  Results of appeals are not included. 3. Figures relate to defendants who were convicted at court, or received an out of court diversion for at least one count of the offence ‘possession of a class B drug’.4. Figures for diversionary disposals include Cautions, Informed Warnings and Youth Conference Plans.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for his Department’s plans to resurface the Sydenham bypass carriageway, in both directions.
answer_text: Resurfacing schemes are funded through my Department’s Structural Maintenance Programme; regrettably, for some time the available investment has not kept pace with what is needed to maintain all of our roads in a reasonable condition.While there are many roads that would benefit from investment, due to limited budget resources, resurfacing work is taken forward through the prioritisation of those deemed most in need for intervention.Details of the planned list of resurfacing schemes for the 2023/24 financial year are included in the Department’s Annual Report for Belfast City Council which can be found at; https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/sites/default/files/publications/infrastructure/dfi-roads-2023-annual-report-to-belfast-city-council.pdf  Work contained in the Annual Report is planned for completion in the current financial year subject to the ongoing availability of resources, capacity and subject to other events like severe weather that can impact programming.The programme for 2024/25 will be developed once future budgets are agreed.In the meantime, officials in my Department will continue to undertake regular inspections and repair defects in accordance with the Department’s policy.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice how many convictions for benefit fraud were made in Northern Ireland, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: Offences relating to benefit fraud may be prosecuted under the Social Security Administration (Northern Ireland) Act 1992 or the Tax Credits Act 2002. Information has been provided for cases that were dealt with in the period 2018 to 2022, the most recent five years for which information is available.Convictions at courts for offences related to the benefit fraud, 2018 – 2022YearConvictions201822020192102020902021113202261Note:1. Figures relate to cases prosecuted by PPS on behalf of PSNI or other Governmental bodies. 2. Figures relate to initial disposal at court only.  Results of appeals are not included. 3. Figures relate to cases dealt with at Crown Court and at magistrates’ courts.4. Figures relate to defendants who were convicted of at least one offence relating to benefit or tax credit fraud under the Social Security Administration (Northern Ireland) Act 1992 or the Tax Credits Act 2002.5. In addition to the convictions listed, there were 19 diversions issued for these offences during the period.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail his Department’s plans to make childcare more affordable.
answer_text: I outlined my plans to the Assembly on 12th February for the development and implementation of an ambitious Early Learning and Childcare Strategy.  However, I am conscious that it will take time to get the right model in place and I am also considering what short-term measures might be possible to address current pressures.  Over the coming weeks, I plan to meet parents, providers, and others with an interest in the strategy, to hear firsthand the issues which they are facing.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice what plans her Department has to introduce tougher sentencing for assaults on public servants.
answer_text: My Department’s review of sentencing policy in Northern Ireland, which resulted in 54 recommendations, examined the need to introduce new legislation in relation to sentencing for attacks on frontline workers. I have agreed that the seriousness of assaults on a range of frontline workers should be recognised in the magistrates’ courts with a higher maximum penalty than is currently available and that, in more serious cases, the fact that the victim is a frontline worker should become a statutory aggravating factor, meaning the courts will be obliged to specifically recognise emergency workers in their sentencing decisions.  The report on the way forward setting out my decisions was published on 21 April 2021 and can be seen online at Sentencing Policy Review - Way Forward Report.I intend to bring forward these changes in a Sentencing Bill during the current mandate.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail his Department’s long-term strategy for Northern Ireland’s energy security.
answer_text: Energy security is a key priority for the Department. The Energy Strategy ‘A Pathway to Net Zero’ sets out my department’s ambition to deliver an energy system that is secure, affordable and sustainable. Security of supply for electricity is a risk managed collaboratively by government, the Utility Regulator and SONI (the Electricity Transmission System Operator), to ensure that business and domestic consumers do not pay a higher price than needed, especially in these times of particular hardship. My officials work on an ongoing basis with the energy industry, both electricity and gas, and the Utility Regulator to review and monitor short, medium and long-term security of supply and ensure our changing energy system is robust and resilient.There is also regular corporation with the Department for Energy Security and Net Zero (DESNZ) and the Department of the Environment, Climate and Communications (DECC) to ensure mutual support on energy security through interconnection.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail his Department’s programme for weed spraying in 2024.
answer_text: The upcoming programme for grass cutting and weed spraying cannot be confirmed until a budget has been agreed for 2024-25.  However, it would be my intention for the Department to deliver a programme of weed spraying in line with previous years and in accordance with the current road maintenance standards. A copy of these can be found at:https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/sites/default/files/publications/infrastructure/road-maintenance-standards-for-safety-limited-service-in-2021-22-dem-160-21.pdf

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs what measures his Department is taking to address poor water quality around the North Down coastline.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education what action his Department is taking to make the C2K system accessible for those who use screen readers.
answer_text: As part of my department’s responsibilities under the Special Educational Needs and Disability Act 2016, the Education Authority currently provides a range of applications and technical solutions to support accessibility via the C2k Service. The Microsoft Ease of Access programme that relates to accessibility has several applications that can be utilised by a pupil on C2k managed devices, e.g. Magnifier, Narrator, Cursor and pointer. Additionally, a specialist accessibility programme for visually impaired users, called Fusion 2023, is available for use on C2k managed devices. This programme includes the ‘Zoomtext’ and ‘Job Access with Speech’ (JAWS) applications, which contain enhanced screen reader functionality. The new Education Information Solutions (EdIS) service provision which will replace the current C2k service will continue to support users with Accessibility and Special Educational needs.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy what action his Department is taking to support high street businesses.
answer_text: High streets are an important part of the fabric of local communities and local economies.  My department is taking a range of actions to support all businesses, including those located on the high street.  My department is working in partnership with Invest NI to deliver a new scaled phase of its Energy & Resource Efficiency Programme for Businesses to reduce waste and emissions whilst helping businesses take control of their energy use by lowering their energy demand.Support for high streets also cuts across the remits of a number of departments and I intend to work with Executive colleagues to consider the High Street Task Force recommendations to take this agenda forward.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail (i) the number of people in rates arrears; and (ii) the value of those arrears, in the Ards and North Down Council Area, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: The table shows the number of occupancies that have a balance greater than £0 at the end of each of the last 5 rating years and the value of those arrears, in the Ards and North Down Council Area.Rating YearNumber of Occupancies with a balance at End of Rating yearTotal value of arrears2018/195,380£8,160,8882019/205,128£7,919,3392020/216,409£10,016,7232021/226,515£9,875,2942022/235,951£11,423,034

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the average waiting time at the Ulster Hospital for an A&E patient to get a bed in each of the last five years.
answer_text: Information on the average waiting time for an A&E patient to get a bed as an inpatient is not available. However, information on the median and 95th percentile time spent in A&E from arrival to admission to hospital is published on an annual basis on Table 17 and Table 18 (pages 58 and 59) of the ‘Northern Ireland Hospital Statistics: Emergency Care’ report, which can be viewed at the link below:https://www.health-ni.gov.uk/sites/default/files/publications/health/hs-emergency-care-22-23.pdfConsideration should be given to changes to A&E service provision and designation when making comparisons over time, including changes during the COVID 19 pandemic and the provision of urgent and emergency care services.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure which schools in North Down will be included in the 20mph speed limit zoning for the next financial year.
answer_text: Part-time 20mph limits near schools are implemented based on assessments considering collision history, speed, traffic volume, infrastructure, and site constraints.  Schools have been assessed in accordance with DfI’s policy ‘Road Safety at Schools’ which can be viewed at: https://www.infrastructureni.gov.uk/publications/road-safety-schools-rsppg-e070)The program to date has been rolled out in tranches, with the most recent completed in November 2022.  Over the coming weeks, I will be considering my priorities, including the potential for a further tranche of the 20mph schools programme, in the context of the funding that is currently available to my Department.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for an update on the strengthening of anti-social behaviour laws.
answer_text: On 28 November 2023, the Department of Justice (DoJ), together with the Department for Communities (DfC), launched a joint public consultation on proposals to amend legislation to help tackle Anti-Social Behaviour (ASB).The consultation is seeking views on potential change to four pieces of legislation, with a view to ensuring the relevant authorities (Councils, Police and Social Housing Providers) have effective and proportionate powers to help address ASB and its effects within our communities. The proposals relate to:Anti-Social Behaviour Orders (ASBOs);Drinking in Public;Injunctions Against Anti-Social Behaviour; andAbsolute Grounds for Possession.DoJ are leading the considerations of legislative change to the provisions of the Anti-Social Behaviour (Northern Ireland) Order 2004 in relation to ASBOs and are working collaboratively with DfC as joint policy leads on proposals to address the issue of drinking in public, including review of the un-commenced provisions of the Criminal Justice (Northern Ireland) Order 2008. DfC are progressing the other proposals.The consultation closes on 4 March 2024, after which a final policy position and legislative proposals will be agreed.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs what measures are in place to improve the water quality at Ballyholme beach.
answer_text: I will be considering the proposals in the Third River Basin Management Plan shortly, with a view to bringing it to the Executive. The primary sources of pollution are agricultural runoff and wastewater.  Heavy rainfall events in 2023 also exacerbated the problems. I will be examining how we tackle pollution from agriculture through measures like the review of the Nutrient Action Programme. The proposals will also include working with NI Water to target wastewater infrastructure investment.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on the roll out of the Sub-Regional Stadia Funding for local football.
answer_text: The Sub-Regional Stadia Programme for football is an important flagship Executive commitment and I recognise the valuable contribution it can deliver for football and wider society. I recognise and share the deep frustration of many that this programme has not yet been delivered. Investment in local football grounds will help to support the legacy of the Euros and I look forward to progressing this programme and delivering football facilities that are modern, safe and meet the needs of our communities.My officials are working with a range of stakeholders to develop proposals. I have asked my officials to present me with proposals and options for consideration as a matter of priority.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on a new Health and Wellbeing Centre in North Down.
answer_text: A Primary Health and Care Centre for the Ards and North Down population is included in Tranche 2 of the Primary Care Infrastructure Development, Strategic Implementation Plan. Once this project becomes affordable within my Department’s capital budget, the South Eastern Trust will be invited to submit a business case.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail how much money his department has spent on vehicle damage claims on roads within the Ards and North Down Council area, in each of the last five financial years.
answer_text: The management information my Department holds in relation to claims can be found online at:https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/publications/road-asset-maintenance-directorate-management-information-roads-related-claims-period-20182019 Table 7 in the above link provides details of vehicle damage expenditure for the Ards and North Down Roads Section Office, which broadly aligns with the Ards and North Down Council area.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on plans to address the MOT backlog.
answer_text: In 2022-2023, the Driver & Vehicle Agency (DVA) conducted over 1.15 million vehicle tests which is the highest number ever recorded in a year.  This increase in capacity has been achieved by adopting a range of measures including the recruitment of additional vehicle examiners, the use overtime to provide cover for leave and offering vehicle test appointments on Sundays and bank holidays, when testing is not normally available.  Disappointingly, the number of vehicles that failed to attend a booked appointment in 2022-2023 was also the highest on record at 50,772.A further vehicle examiner recruitment competition is due to launch later this month to fill staff vacancies across the DVA’s network of test centres.  The DVA appreciates that, in some cases, customers may not be able to have their vehicles tested before their current MOT certificate expires and has taken steps to ensure that motorists are still able to use their vehicles when they are unable to secure an MOT appointment before their current MOT expires.  The latest information on MOTs is available on NI Direct at https://www.nidirect.gov.uk/articles/guide-booking-your-mot-vehicle-test and is also included as an advice note with every MOT reminder letter issued to customers.Vehicle test appointments are released on a daily basis, when resources become available, so customers are encouraged to regularly check the booking system as they may be able to change their existing appointment to a different test centre and time if a slot becomes available.  Information on how a customer can change, cancel or view their MOT appointment online is available on NI Direct at change, cancel or view MOT/vehicle test appointment.To further increase vehicle testing capacity, the DVA is investing in new test centres.  The construction of a new test centre at Hydebank, in Belfast, is nearing completion and will open later this year.  The construction contract for a second new test centre at Mallusk has also been awarded and this new test centre is expected to open in 2025.  Once fully operational, these two new centres will provide the capacity to test over 200,000 additional vehicles per annum, which will help the DVA meet the future demand for this service.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on plans to increase provision of NHS dentists in North Down.
answer_text: A key part of the access issues facing patients are the rates being paid to our dentists for treatment.  The absence of Executive Ministers has meant that it has not been possible to progress the recommended pay uplifts for staffing groups for 2023/24.  This is now my immediate priority, and I am meeting with Trade Unions in early course to take this forward. Officials have already been making arrangements in anticipation of funding being made available to ensure that uplifts can be implemented as quickly as possible.I know that an additional £95m has been invested in General Dental Services via the Financial Support Scheme and Rebuilding Support Scheme since April 2020. This came to an end in July 2023 due to the severe financial pressures arising from the Department’s 2023/24 budget settlement.A pilot scheme is currently running to provide appointments to unregistered patients who require emergency dental treatment during the working week. If a patient is not registered with a dentist, requires emergency dental treatment, and has been unable to access an appointment, they can contact their local Strategic Planning and Performance Group (SPPG) office for advice.I am also conscious of the progress that has been made in the rest of the UK on dental reform and I will be discussing these issues with officials as soon as possible, to ensure that the sector here in NI is supported in delivering important care to patients. More immediately, officials have been developing investment proposals to further support General Dental Services and I will consider them in early course.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the footpath and carriageway resurfacing programmes to be completed in North Down in this financial year.
answer_text: Unfortunately my officials were unable to gather together the information sought by the member in the short amount of time I had remaining in office.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure when Patton’s Lane, Gray’s Lane, and High Street in Holywood will be resurfaced.
answer_text: Unfortunately my officials were unable to gather together the information sought by the member in the short amount of time I had remaining in office.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what steps he has taken to increase the provision of NHS dentists in North Down.
answer_text: Access to routine dental care continues to be significantly affected by the COVID-19 pandemic, which has caused reduced activity and therefore reduced capacity in dental practices. In order to increase activity in General Dental Services, I introduced the Rebuilding Support Scheme (RSS), which rewards dentists based on treatments being provided.  The RSS has seen an increase in activity levels of around 16.5% over the first 5 months of 2022/23, and support will continue until the end of the financial year.Further, any patient who has an urgent or emergency dental need is able to make use of an Emergency Dental Clinic during weekends or Public Holidays. There the patient will be triaged remotely, and appropriate advice given or treatment arranged.To further help those in most need, on 3 October 2022, my Department commenced a scheme throughout Northern Ireland to provide access to unregistered patients who are experiencing an emergency or urgent dental condition.Support being provided to dental practices will remain under ongoing review.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the future of the Priory Surgery, Holywood and the Springhill Surgery, Bangor.
answer_text: The Strategic Planning and Performance Group (SPPG) have initiated a process to find a new contractor to take over the Priory & Springhill GMS Contract from 1st February 2023.  There have been a number of expressions of interest put forward.  SPPG officers are currently engaging with potential new contract holders and I remain hopeful that one will be successful.  Whilst I am hesitant to provide a timeframe, I can confirm that SPPG officers are working hard to find a solution that will ensure that the full range of GMS services are provided to all registered patients at Priory & Springhill Surgery from 1st February 2023.  Patients and the wider public will be kept informed of any developments.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail if all NI Housing Executive Offices are now fully reopened for public access following the Covid-19 pandemic.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Housing Executive.  I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“The Housing Executive can confirm that all offices have fully re-opened and operating as normal following the Covid-19 pandemic.”For any future queries relating to operational matters, you may wish to contact the Housing Executive directly.  Making direct contact with them will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on the Fundamental Review of Allocations: Modelling and Analysis of Waiting List Data.
answer_text: A research report on the Fundamental Review of Allocations published by the Housing Executive, can be found on their website (https://www.nihe.gov.uk/working-with-us/research/social-housing).Implementation of eighteen of the twenty proposals to change the Housing Selection Scheme is ongoing. It is being carried out on a four-phase approach by the Housing Executive with oversight from officials within my Department. Further work is being undertaken on the remaining two proposals.Stage One was implemented on 31 January 2023. Stage Two, containing the next five proposals, is almost complete. I will provide an update on this stage in due course.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what steps he has taken to address the backlog of MOTs.
answer_text: For the latest information on MOTs I would ask you to encourage your constituents to visit NI Direct at Book, view, change or cancel an MOT/ vehicle test | nidirect. This information is also included as an advice note with every MOT Reminder Letter issued to Driver & Vehicle Agency (DVA) customers.  The advice confirms if a customer gets to within five days of the expiry date of their vehicle tax and they have been unable to secure an MOT appointment, or their vehicle tax has expired, they should contact the DVA Customer Services team at dva.customerservices@infrastructure-ni.gov.uk, and they will do their best to get an urgent appointment for the vehicle.  To date, all customers who have contacted the DVA in these circumstances have been offered an earlier appointment slot.The DVA has steadily increased its vehicle testing capacity to pre-Covid levels by adopting a range of measures including the recruitment of additional vehicle examiners, the use of overtime to provide cover for leave and by offering vehicle test appointments on a Sunday and bank holidays, when testing is not normally available.  The DVA is also recruiting 24 new vehicle examiners, the first tranche are due to finish their training on 28 October and will then commence testing at their local test centre.   A further tranche of new vehicle examiners started their training on 12 October and the final tranche will commence training on 9 November.    To further increase vehicle testing capacity across its network, the DVA is investing in new test centres.  As a first step, the DVA is currently constructing a new test centre at Hydebank in Belfast, which is due to open in 2023.  A second new test centre at Mallusk, which is scheduled to open in 2024, is currently at the planning stage.  These two new centres will provide the capacity to test over 200,000 additional vehicles per annum, which will help meet the future demand for this service.The DVA is monitoring the number of applications received and tests carried out and expects waiting times to reduce over time through the actions they have taken to increase vehicle testing capacity across the network.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what plans she has to expand the criteria for the Affordable Warmth Scheme, given the cost of living crisis.
answer_text: The cost-of-living crisis is having major impact, particularly on those who are already struggling financially. I am deeply frustrated that the continued absence of a functioning Executive and agreed Executive budget prevents additional measures being introduced.	My Department will do all it can to support those experiencing hardship, and all options are being explored to provide both immediate and medium to longer term interventions.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance what plans he has to put in place a pay award for the Northern Ireland Civil Service and Arm’s Length Bodies, given that their current award expired on 1 August 2022.
answer_text: I refer you to the statement that I made on 11 October which sets out the position in relation to Civil Service pay:https://www.financeni.gov.uk/sites/default/files/publications/dfp/Finance%20Minister%20-Written%20Ministerial%20Statement%20-%202022-23%20Budgetary%20Position.pdf

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what steps she is taking to address fuel poverty.
answer_text: The cost-of-living crisis is having major impact, particularly on those who are already struggling financially. I am deeply frustrated that the continued absence of a functioning Executive and agreed Executive budget prevents additional measures being introduced.	My Department will do all it can to support those experiencing hardship, and all options are being explored to provide both immediate and medium to longer term interventions.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance what his Department is doing to introduce a financial support scheme, delivered via the existing Small Business Rates Relief Scheme, for small businesses who are struggling with rising energy bills.
answer_text: At the end of the last mandate I secured early delivery of a wide ranging Executive package of measures for business including the extension of the Small Business Rate Relief scheme in advance of the 2022/23 rating year. This was in addition to over £1 billion of support delivered to businesses by my Department during the pandemic. Given the ongoing absence of an Executive since February, it is not legally possible for me to now act unilaterally to direct additional budget allocations to new or extended policy measures that would incur additional expenditure.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy (i) for an update on the rollout of the Energy Strategy; (ii) to outline how other Executive Departments are working with your Department on the Strategy; and (iii) whether your Department and other Departments are on target to deliver against the action plan contained alongside the Energy Strategy.
answer_text: The Energy Strategy - The Path to Net Zero Energy was published in December 2021. Delivering the strategy requires collaboration across government with the Department for the Economy leading. DfI, DfC, DAERA, DoF and TEO participate in the Energy Strategy Programme Board, which is chaired by DfE’s Permanent Secretary, providing governance for the delivery of the energy strategy. Across the energy efficiency, power, heat, transport and energy systems delivery workstreams, there are working and steering groups with representation from across government, alongside appropriate representation from the business sector. The Energy Strategy Action Plan for 2022 has 22 key actions for priority attention during this calendar year, along the energy decarbonisation pathway to 2030 and 2050. DfE has committed to publishing a report in Q1 2023 on progress against this Action Plan and also intend to publish an Action Plan for 2023.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on how his Department is addressing the backlog of practical driving tests.
answer_text: The Driver and Vehicle Agency (DVA) has, and continues, to work hard to increase its capacity to help meet the high demand for driving tests for all customers.  The DVA has 94 full time and dual role staff capable of conducting driving tests across its network of test centres, and is in the process of completing an external recruitment competition for 16 new full time driving examiners.  To date 12 new driving examiners have been appointed from this competition and training will continue until all new driving examiner posts are filled.  In addition to the recruitment of additional examiners, the DVA continue to offer driving tests on a Saturday and at certain centres on Sundays and bank holidays, where it is suitable to do so without compromising the integrity of the test.  Overtime is also being used to rota off-shift dual role driving examiners to provide further capacity and to provide cover for scheduled driving tests, where due to a variety of unforeseen reasons such as sick absence or the requirement to self-isolate, driving examiners are unable to attend work. The DVA is releasing Category B (private car) driving test appointments five months in advance with all test centres set to release driving test appointments on the first working day of each month and additional test slots will also be released as resources continue to become available.Due to the constantly changing position, the DVA’s advice to customers is to keep checking the booking system for availability as additional appointment slots are added when resources become available.  If slots are not initially available at a customer’s preferred test centre, they may wish to consider booking a test at another centre.I remain committed to minimising disruption and ensuring DVA’s plans for the restoration of services are in hand.  However, all driving test services across these islands are experiencing high demand with longer than usual waiting times and I would ask customers for their continued patience.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on how his Department is addressing the backlog of MOT tests.
answer_text: For the latest information on MOTs I would ask you to encourage your constituents to visit NI Direct at Book, view, change or cancel an MOT/ vehicle test | nidirect. This information is also included as an advice note with every MOT Reminder Letter issued to DVA customers.  The advice confirms if a customer gets to within five days of the expiry date of their vehicle tax and they have been unable to secure an MOT appointment, or their vehicle tax has expired, they should contact the DVA Customer Services team at dva.customerservices@infrastructure-ni.gov.uk, and they will do their best to get an urgent appointment for the vehicle.  To date, all customers who have contacted the DVA in these circumstances have been offered an earlier appointment slot.The Driver and Vehicle Agency (DVA) has steadily increased its vehicle testing capacity to pre-Covid levels by adopting a range of measures including the recruitment of additional vehicle examiners, the use of overtime to provide cover for leave and by offering vehicle test appointments on a Sunday and bank holidays, when testing is not normally available.  The next tranche of new vehicle examiners are due to start their training later this month.  To further increase vehicle testing capacity across its network, the DVA is investing in new test centres.  As a first step, the DVA is currently constructing a new test centre at Hydebank in Belfast, which is due to open in 2023.  A second new test centre at Mallusk, which is scheduled to open in 2024, is currently at the planning stage.  These two new centres will provide the capacity to test over 200,000 additional vehicles per annum, which will help meet the future demand for this service.The DVA is monitoring the number of applications received and tests carried out and expects waiting times to reduce over time through the actions they have taken to increase vehicle testing capacity across the network.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on the Sub-Regional Stadia Programme for Soccer funding.
answer_text: The challenges created by not having a functioning Executive mean I cannot bring new proposals for endorsement and agreement as is required.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on progress of the regeneration of Bangor and Queen’s Parade.
answer_text: The Developer’s proposals for Queen’s Parade are currently being considered by the Ards and North Down Borough Council’s planning service. The Ards and North Down Borough Council is also currently developing its proposals for the regeneration of Bangor Waterfront, being funded by the  Executive under the Belfast Region City Deal programme.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the number of road fatalities in each of the last fifteen years.
answer_text: Details on the number of road fatalities in each of the last fifteen years can be found in Appendix 2 of the PSNI’s ‘Police Recorded Injury Road Traffic Collisions and Casualties 2021 Key Statistics Report’https://www.psni.police.uk/globalassets/inside-the-psni/our-statistics/road-traffic-collision-statistics/2021/2021-key-statistics-report.pdf

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether there are any plans to review the format of the driving test to be in line with Great Britain.
answer_text: I am aware of the changes to driver testing in GB.  Changes to testing arrangements have implications for road safety, and so there are no current plans to replicate these changes.Road safety will continue to be of paramount importance in all of our decisions.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any involvement her Department has had with the UK Government on its Levelling Up plan through the Ulster branch and IRFU to local rugby clubs.
answer_text: On 22 June, DfC provided Ulster Rugby with a letter of support for Ulster Rugby’s applications to the Levelling Up Fund, which seek to develop and refurbish existing rugby facilities here.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether an independent review will be carried out into the provision of services available for people living with autism.
answer_text: On 26th April 2022 legislation to amend the Autism Act (NI) 2011 received Royal Assent. The Autism (Amendment) Act (NI) 2022 will have implications for all government departments and the health and social care sector in relation to services and provision of support for autistic people and their families.  The requirements of the legislation will be introduced as actions for the next Autism Strategy 2023-2028 which is due for publication 2023.One of the major outworkings of the legislation will be the appointment of an Independent Autism Reviewer who will be responsible for monitoring the implementation and effectiveness of the autism strategy and submitting an annual report to the Assembly on the exercise of their functions.  Officials have commenced discussions with the Commissioner for Public Appointments regarding the development of the role and how this recruitment will be undertaken through the public appointments process.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what steps are being taken to improve services available to young people diagnosed with autism.
To ask the Minister of Health whether further funding is to be made available for the diagnosis of autism in young people.
answer_text: I am conscious of the challenges that growing waiting lists for assessments and support are placing on individuals and their families and can assure you that Trusts are working diligently to provide supports and interventions for individuals and families who are waiting for autism assessments.  Funding for service provision and support for children and adults is provided through a range of funding streams and programmes of care, based upon individual need.In the absence of an agreed budget for 2022-23 I'm having to carefully manage within the resources available to my Department and this has limited my ability to commit additional funding to services.  The Finance Minister wrote to departments in March, which involved DoF issuing departments with contingency planning envelopes for the 2022-23 financial year.  These envelopes provided departments with an assessment of the minimum funding they could reasonably expect for 2022-23 and allowed departments to plan for expenditure until such times as a Budget could be agreed. I have agreed an approach for my Department to proceed with opening allocations which will continue to fund activity in the first quarter of 2022/23 at previous levels while controlling spending in line with the advice from the Finance Minister. However I am very conscious of pressing demands for extra funding across many areas of health and social care. As Minister, I will continue to do the best I can to deliver for the public with the resources I have available, however funding elements of the health service on a month to month, or quarter to quarter, basis is not an efficient or effective way to build for the future.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance what role his Department has in the Homes for Ukraine 'Thank You' payments process.
answer_text: Responsibility for the Homes for Ukraine ‘Thank You’ payments to sponsors rests with The Executive Office. Some officials from my Department are assisting The Executive Office with establishing the processes to administer the payments.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on the review of the housing allocation points process.
answer_text: My Department has been working with the Housing Executive to implement 18 of the 20 proposals set out in the December 2020 Consultation Outcome Report on “A Fundamental Review of Social Housing Allocations”. Further work is ongoing for the remaining two proposals relating to intimidation points and interim accommodation points.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on the Sub-Regional Stadia Programme for Soccer.
answer_text: The challenges created by not having a functioning Executive means I cannot bring new proposals for endorsement and agreement as is required. I met with representatives from IFA and NIFL, and have agreed to work with them, and other stakeholders, to progress.I remain committed to moving this Programme forward
